> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** In physiological modelling, *almost every system is intrinsically nonlinear* (e.g., the Hodgkin–Huxley nerve membrane equations, the Michaelis–Menten enzyme kinetics, the sigmoidal oxygen–hemoglobin dissociation curve). The "linear" classification is therefore typically a **local approximation** valid only over a restricted operating range where the system's static characteristic is approximately a straight line.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy:** Think of a weighing scale. If you place **100 g** on it, the pointer moves by **2 cm**. If you place **200 g**, it moves by **4 cm**. Double the input, double the output — that is a **linear** scale (a straight ruler).
>
> Now imagine a *rubber-band scale*: the first 100 g stretches it a lot, but the second 100 g stretches it only a little because the band is already taut. The response **saturates**. Double the input does *not* double the output — that is a **nonlinear** scale.
>
> **Physiological analogy:** The pupil of your eye constricts linearly for small changes in light intensity but saturates in bright sunlight (nonlinear). The lung's pressure–volume curve is linear in the normal tidal breathing range but becomes highly nonlinear near total lung capacity (compliance falls).

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Constants

- **Superposition Principle (Additivity):** $\mathcal{H}\{x_1 + x_2\} = \mathcal{H}\{x_1\} + \mathcal{H}\{x_2\}$
- **Homogeneity Principle (Scaling):** $\mathcal{H}\{a\,x\} = a\,\mathcal{H}\{x\}$
- **Linearity Test Constant:** $\alpha = \dfrac{y_2/y_1}{x_2/x_1}$ — must remain constant at **1** for an ideal linear system.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Breakdown — Linear Physiological Systems

A linear physiological model is characterized by the following structural features:

- **Governing Equations** are **linear ordinary differential equations (ODEs)** with constant coefficients, e.g., the passive RC model of a nerve membrane:

$$C\,\frac{dV_m}{dt} + \frac{V_m}{R} = I_{in}(t)$$

- **Response to a Sinusoidal Input** is *another sinusoid of the same frequency*, with only amplitude and phase altered. This is the property of **frequency preservation** central to linear systems analysis (Fourier/Laplace domain).
- **Stability** is independent of input magnitude — the system either is stable or is not, regardless of how big the stimulus is.
- **Time-Invariance**: For a *linear time-invariant (LTI)* physiological model, shifting the input in time only shifts the output identically — no change in waveform shape.

### 2.2 Operational Breakdown — Nonlinear Physiological Systems

Nonlinear systems exhibit one or more of the following hallmark behaviours, which linear models can never reproduce:

- **Saturation:** Output approaches an asymptotic ceiling regardless of input magnitude (e.g., receptor binding at maximum occupancy).
- **Dead-Zone:** No output below a threshold input (e.g., action potential firing threshold ≈ **−55 mV** in neurons).
- **Hysteresis:** Output for an increasing input differs from the output for a decreasing input at the same value (e.g., lung pressure–volume loop during inspiration vs expiration).
- **Bifurcation:** A small parameter change qualitatively alters system dynamics (e.g., onset of cardiac arrhythmias).
- **Limit Cycles:** Sustained oscillations independent of initial conditions (e.g., Cheyne–Stokes breathing pattern).
- **Subharmonic & Chaotic Behaviour:** Frequency components at fractions of, or unrelated to, the input frequency.

### 2.3 Linearization of Nonlinear Models

Because analytical tools (Laplace transforms, convolution, eigen-analysis) are *far* simpler for linear systems, physiological modellers routinely **linearize** a nonlinear model about a chosen operating point $(x_0, y_0)$ using a **first-order Taylor expansion**:

$$y(t) \approx y_0 + \left.\frac{dy}{dx}\right|_{x_0}\,(x(t) - x_0)$$

The slope $\left.\frac{dy}{dx}\right|_{x_0}$ becomes the **linearized gain (sensitivity)** of the physiological model at that operating point.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Concept** | **Mathematical Form** | **Physiological Example** | **Units** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Additivity | $\mathcal{H}\{x_1+x_2\} = \mathcal{H}\{x_1\} + \mathcal{H}\{x_2\}$ | Two simultaneous nerve stimuli | — |
| Homogeneity | $\mathcal{H}\{a\,x\} = a\,\mathcal{H}\{x\}$ | Doubling current doubles voltage | — |
| First-order linear ODE | $\tau\,\dot{y} + y = K\,x(t)$ | Drug clearance, RC nerve membrane | $\tau$ in **s** |
| Linearization gain | $K = \left.\dfrac{\partial f}{\partial x}\right\vert_{x_0}$ | Slope of PV curve at FRC | varies |
| Michaelis–Menten kinetics | $v = \dfrac{V_{max}\,S}{K_m + S}$ | Enzyme reaction rate | $\text{mol/(L·s)}$ |
| Hill equation (sigmoid) | $y = \dfrac{x^n}{K^n + x^n}$ | Hemoglobin O$_2$ saturation | dimensionless |
| Linearized stiffness | $E_{lin} = \left.\dfrac{dP}{dV}\right\vert_{V_0}$ | Lung/chest wall compliance | $\text{cmH}_2\text{O/L}$ |
| Time-invariance test | $y(t-T) = \mathcal{H}\{x(t-T)\}$ | Constant-tissue property | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Linear models underpin virtually every clinical tool — ECG signal processing, Fourier analysis of EEG, transfer-function modelling of the cardiovascular baroreflex, and the design of PID controllers for anaesthesia and artificial ventilation. Nonlinear models are essential when *qualitative* behaviour (oscillations, chaos, threshold phenomena) is the focus — cardiac electrophysiology, neural firing, hormonal feedback.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Verification of Linearity for the Passive Nerve Membrane (RC Model)

The Hodgkin–Huxley *passive* (leak) sub-model for a nerve membrane is:

$$C_m\,\frac{dV_m}{dt} + G_m\,V_m(t) = I_{in}(t)$$

We formally verify that the operator $\mathcal{H}: I_{in}(t) \mapsto V_m(t)$ is **linear**.

**Step 1 — Homogeneity (Scaling):**

Assume input $I_1(t)$ produces output $V_1(t)$. Then:

$$C_m\,\dot{V}_1 + G_m\,V_1 = I_1$$

Now consider scaled input $I_2(t) = a\,I_1(t)$. Substituting a trial solution $V_2(t) = a\,V_1(t)$:

$$C_m\,\frac{d}{dt}\bigl[a\,V_1\bigr] + G_m\,\bigl[a\,V_1\bigr] = a\,\bigl(C_m\,\dot{V}_1 + G_m\,V_1\bigr) = a\,I_1 = I_2$$

The trial solution is consistent — the **scaled output exactly matches the response to the scaled input**. Homogeneity holds.

**Step 2 — Additivity (Superposition):**

Let $I_1 \to V_1$ and $I_2 \to V_2$ satisfy the ODE independently:

$$C_m\,\dot{V}_1 + G_m\,V_1 = I_1$$

$$C_m\,\dot{V}_2 + G_m\,V_2 = I_2$$

Add the two equations:

$$C_m\,(\dot{V}_1+\dot{V}_2) + G_m\,(V_1+V_2) = I_1+I_2$$

Therefore $V_1 + V_2$ is the response to $I_1 + I_2$. Additivity holds.

**Step 3 — Combined Linearity:**

By combining the two properties, for arbitrary constants $a$ and $b$:

$$C_m\,\frac{d}{dt}\bigl[a\,V_1+b\,V_2\bigr] + G_m\,\bigl[a\,V_1+b\,V_2\bigr] = a\,I_1+b\,I_2$$

Hence the passive RC nerve-membrane model is a **linear time-invariant (LTI) system**. ∎

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Linearization of the Michaelis–Menten Enzyme Model

The classical nonlinear Michaelis–Menten rate equation is:

$$v(S) = \frac{V_{max}\,S}{K_m + S}$$

where $S$ is the substrate concentration, $V_{max}$ is the maximum velocity, and $K_m$ is the Michaelis constant.

**Step 1 — Choose the operating point.** Let $S_0$ be the nominal substrate concentration and $v_0 = v(S_0)$.

**Step 2 — Compute the gain (sensitivity).**

$$K = \left.\frac{dv}{dS}\right\vert_{S_0} = \left.\frac{V_{max}(K_m+S) - V_{max}\,S}{(K_m+S)^2}\right\vert_{S_0} = \frac{V_{max}\,K_m}{(K_m+S_0)^2}$$

This derivative is the slope of the $v$–$S$ curve at $S_0$, and is strictly *positive* — confirming monotonic but **nonlinear** behaviour.

**Step 3 — Write the first-order Taylor linearization.**

$$v(S) \approx v_0 + K\,(S - S_0)$$

**Step 4 — Express the linearized model in deviation variables.** Define $\tilde{S} = S - S_0$ and $\tilde{v} = v - v_0$. Then:

$$\tilde{v}(t) \approx K\,\tilde{S}(t)$$

This is a **pure static gain** — a memoryless, time-invariant linear element suitable for block-diagram analysis in larger metabolic pathways.

**Step 5 — Validity check.** The linearization error is $O(\tilde{S}^2)$. It is accurate for $|\tilde{S}| \ll K_m + S_0$. At saturation ($S_0 \gg K_m$), the true slope $K \to 0$ and the linearization degenerates. ∎

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Hysteresis in a Nonlinear Cardiac Pressure–Volume Loop

A patient’s left-ventricular pressure–volume (PV) loop is recorded. During *ejection* (volume decreasing) the end-systolic pressure is **120 mmHg** at volume **50 mL**. During *filling* (volume increasing) the pressure is **80 mmHg** at the same volume. Determine whether the ventricle behaves linearly, and if not, compute the loop area (a measure of hysteresis energy loss).

**Step 1 — Check linearity via single-valued mapping.** For a linear (single-valued, path-independent) system, $P(V)$ would yield **one unique pressure** for each volume. Here, $P_{ej}(50) = 120$ mmHg $\neq$ $P_{fil}(50) = 80$ mmHg. The mapping is *multi-valued*, so the system is **nonlinear with hysteresis**.

**Step 2 — Compute hysteresis energy loss per cycle.** For an idealized elliptical PV loop with semi-axes $\Delta P$ and $\Delta V$:

$$W_{hyst} = \pi\,(\Delta P)\,(\Delta V)$$

Substitute $\Delta P = 120 - 80 = 40$ mmHg = $40 \times 133.32$ Pa $= 5333$ Pa, and let $\Delta V = 30$ mL $= 30 \times 10^{-6}$ m$^3$:

$$W_{hyst} = \pi \times 5333 \times 30 \times 10^{-6} \approx 0.503 \text{ J}$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation.** A purely linear elastic ventricle would dissipate **0 J** per cycle. The observed 0.503 J represents viscous + contractile energy loss per heartbeat — a hallmark of *nonlinear viscoelastic active tissue*. ∎

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Linear vs Nonlinear Physiological Modelling Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Physiological System Observation] --> B{Is system intrinsically linear?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Linear Modelling Pathway]
    B -- No --> D[Nonlinear Modelling Pathway]
    C --> C1[Formulate Linear ODE]
    C1 --> C2[Apply Laplace Transform]
    C2 --> C3[Derive Transfer Function H s]
    C3 --> C4[Compute Step and Frequency Response]
    C4 --> C5[Stability and Sensitivity Analysis]
    D --> D1[Formulate Nonlinear ODE]
    D1 --> D2{Analytical tractability check}
    D2 -- Tractable --> D3[Apply Phase Plane Analysis]
    D2 -- Intractable --> D4[Numerical Integration RK4]
    D3 --> D5[Identify Limit Cycles and Bifurcations]
    D4 --> D5
    D5 --> D6[Linearize about Operating Point]
    D6 --> C2
    C5 --> E[Clinical Validation and Prediction]
    D5 --> E
```

**Reading guide:** The diagram shows that nonlinear physiological models often *bridge back* to the linear pathway through **local linearization about an operating point** — a hybrid strategy that captures nonlinearity's qualitative richness while retaining linear analysis tools.

---

### 4.2 Mermaid State Diagram — Superposition Test Logic

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> AcquireInput1
    AcquireInput1 --> MeasureOutput1 : apply x1
    MeasureOutput1 --> AcquireInput2 : record y1
    AcquireInput2 --> MeasureOutput2 : apply x2
    MeasureOutput2 --> AcquireCombined : record y2
    AcquireCombined --> MeasureCombinedOutput : apply x1 plus x2
    MeasureCombinedOutput --> CheckSuperposition : record y12
    CheckSuperposition --> LinearVerified : y12 equals y1 plus y2
    CheckSuperposition --> NonlinearFlagged : mismatch detected
    LinearVerified --> [*]
    NonlinearFlagged --> [*]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The state diagram codifies the experimental protocol an engineer or clinician uses to *empirically* classify a physiological system. If the response to combined stimuli equals the sum of individual responses, the system passes the superposition test and is **linear**; otherwise, it is flagged as **nonlinear** and warrants richer modelling tools (phase-plane, bifurcation, or numerical simulation).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define a linear system. State and explain the two fundamental properties that a system must satisfy to be classified as linear.**

**Model Answer:**
A system described by the operator $\mathcal{H}$ mapping input $x(t)$ to output $y(t)$ is **linear** if and only if it satisfies two properties for arbitrary inputs $x_1, x_2$ and scalars $a, b$:

1. **Additivity (Superposition):** $\mathcal{H}\{x_1(t) + x_2(t)\} = \mathcal{H}\{x_1(t)\} + \mathcal{H}\{x_2(t)\}$
2. **Homogeneity (Scaling):** $\mathcal{H}\{a\,x(t)\} = a\,\mathcal{H}\{x(t)\}$

If either property fails, the system is **nonlinear**. *(3 marks — 1 mark for definition, 1 mark each for the two properties with equations.)*

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Distinguish between linear and nonlinear physiological models with one physiological example of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| **Aspect** | **Linear Model** | **Nonlinear Model** |
|---|---|---|
| Governing equation | Linear ODE with constant coefficients | Nonlinear ODE / algebraic equation |
| Superposition | Holds | Violates |
| Output to sinusoid | Sinusoid of same frequency | Harmonics + DC shift |
| Example | Passive RC nerve membrane | Hodgkin–Huxley active membrane; Michaelis–Menten kinetics |

*(3 marks — 1 mark for table content, 1 mark each example with reasoning.)*

---

### Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]`

**(a)** Explain the principle of linearization of a nonlinear physiological model with a suitable example. Derive the linearized form of the Michaelis–Menten enzyme kinetics equation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A linear first-order physiological model is described by $\tau\,\dot{y}(t) + y(t) = K\,x(t)$ with $\tau = 5$ s and $K = 2$ mV/mmHg. For a step input $x(t) = 10$ mmHg applied at $t = 0$, derive the complete time response $y(t)$ and compute the steady-state value, the initial value, and the time taken to reach 63.2% of steady state. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** Linearization replaces the original nonlinear function $y = f(x)$ about an operating point $(x_0, y_0)$ by its first-order Taylor expansion:

$$y(t) \approx y_0 + \left.\frac{df}{dx}\right\vert_{x_0}\,(x - x_0)$$

For Michaelis–Menten kinetics $v(S) = \dfrac{V_{max}\,S}{K_m + S}$, the linearized gain about substrate concentration $S_0$ is:

$$K = \left.\frac{dv}{dS}\right\vert_{S_0} = \frac{V_{max}\,K_m}{(K_m + S_0)^2}$$

The linearized model in deviation form is $\tilde{v} = K\,\tilde{S}$, where $\tilde{S} = S - S_0$ and $\tilde{v} = v - v_0$. *(7 marks — 2 marks concept, 3 marks derivation, 2 marks final form.)*

**(b)** For the step input $x(t) = 10\,u(t)$:

The ODE becomes $\tau\,\dot{y} + y = K\,x(t)$ with $\tau = 5$ and $K = 2$.

For $t \ge 0$, $x = 10$ is constant. The ODE:

$$5\,\dot{y} + y = 20$$

The complete (homogeneous + particular) solution:

$$y(t) = y_{ss} + (y_0 - y_{ss})\,e^{-t/\tau}$$

Steady-state (particular) solution: $y_{ss} = K\,x = 2 \times 10 = \mathbf{20 \text{ mV}}$.

Initial value (assuming zero initial conditions): $y(0) = \mathbf{0 \text{ mV}}$.

Time to reach 63.2% of $y_{ss}$: $y(0.632\,y_{ss}) = y_{ss}(1 - e^{-1}) = 0.632\,y_{ss}$, which occurs exactly at $t = \tau = \mathbf{5 \text{ seconds}}$.

Final response:

$$y(t) = 20\,\bigl(1 - e^{-t/5}\bigr) \text{ mV}$$

*(7 marks — 2 marks ODE setup, 2 marks solution form, 1 mark steady-state, 1 mark time constant, 1 mark final expression.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain how a linear physiological system can be characterized in the Laplace domain. Define transfer function and mention its significance. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The pupil light-reflex is modelled as a first-order nonlinear system with saturation: $\dot{y} = K\,(u - y) - \alpha\,y^3$, where $u$ is light intensity (normalized), $y$ is pupil constriction, $K = 0.5$ s$^{-1}$, and $\alpha = 0.02$ s$^{-1}$. Linearize the model about the operating point $y_0 = 1$ and find the linearized state equation. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** A linear time-invariant physiological system with input $x(t)$ and output $y(t)$ governed by a linear ODE with constant coefficients can be transformed into the Laplace (s-) domain. Under zero initial conditions, the **transfer function** is defined as:

$$H(s) = \frac{Y(s)}{X(s)}$$

**Block diagram representation:**

```
x(t) ---->[ H(s) ]----> y(t)
```

**Significance:**
- Encodes complete input–output behaviour algebraically.
- Poles & zeros reveal stability, natural frequency, damping.
- Enables frequency-response analysis via $H(j\omega)$.
- Permits block-diagram algebra for interconnected physiological subsystems.

*(7 marks — 2 marks block diagram, 2 marks transfer function definition, 3 marks significance.)*

**(b)** The nonlinear state equation is:

$$f(y, u) = K\,(u - y) - \alpha\,y^3$$

At the operating point $y_0 = 1$, the steady-state satisfies $\dot{y}_0 = 0$:

$$0 = K\,(u_0 - 1) - \alpha\,(1)^3 \implies u_0 = 1 + \frac{\alpha}{K} = 1 + \frac{0.02}{0.5} = 1.04$$

The partial derivatives evaluated at $(u_0, y_0)$:

$$\left.\frac{\partial f}{\partial y}\right\vert_{y_0} = -K - 3\alpha\,y_0^2 = -0.5 - 3(0.02)(1) = -0.56$$

$$\left.\frac{\partial f}{\partial u}\right\vert_{y_0} = K = 0.5$$

The linearized state equation in deviation form $\tilde{y} = y - 1$, $\tilde{u} = u - 1.04$:

$$\dot{\tilde{y}} = -0.56\,\tilde{y} + 0.5\,\tilde{u}$$

This is a stable linear first-order system with effective time constant $\tau_{eff} = 1/0.56 \approx 1.79$ s. *(7 marks — 2 marks steady-state operating point, 2 marks partial derivatives, 2 marks linearized equation, 1 mark interpretation.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting to verify zero initial conditions** before applying Laplace transforms in transfer-function problems (2–3 mark penalty).
> 2. **Confusing linearity with time-invariance** — they are *independent* properties. A system can be linear time-varying (LTV) or nonlinear time-invariant (NTI).
> 3. **Skipping the operating-point calculation** in linearization problems — without the correct $x_0$, the gain $K$ is meaningless and the linearization carries an implicit DC bias error.
> 4. **Units mismatch** — KTU examiners routinely deduct 0.5–1 mark for failing to state SI units of physiological quantities (mmHg, mV, mL, mol/L).
> 5. **Sign errors** when taking partial derivatives of cubic or sigmoidal terms (e.g., $-3\alpha y^2$ — students often drop the minus sign).

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- A system is **linear** iff it satisfies **both** superposition (additivity) and homogeneity (scaling).
- A **linear time-invariant (LTI)** physiological system is fully characterized by its **transfer function** $H(s)$ in the Laplace domain.
- **Nonlinear physiological systems** exhibit saturation, hysteresis, dead zones, limit cycles, bifurcations, and chaos.
- **Linearization** uses the first-order Taylor expansion about an operating point; the derivative $\partial f/\partial x \vert_{x_0}$ is the local sensitivity (gain).
- **Michaelis–Menten** enzyme kinetics: $v = V_{max}\,S / (K_m + S)$ — nonlinear and saturating; linearize to obtain a pure gain $K = V_{max} K_m / (K_m + S_0)^2$.
- **Hodgkin–Huxley passive membrane** (RC model) is *linear*; the full Hodgkin–Huxley equations with sodium/potassium gating are *nonlinear*.
- **Hysteresis energy loss per cycle** in a PV loop: $W_{hyst} = \pi \cdot \Delta P \cdot \Delta V$ — a direct clinical signature of nonlinearity.
- The **first-order linear ODE** $\tau\,\dot{y} + y = K\,x$ has time constant $\tau$ (63.2% rise time) and steady-state $y_{ss} = K\,x$.
- Always **state SI units** explicitly; always **verify zero initial conditions** before applying Laplace.
- Linearization is valid only when the deviation from the operating point satisfies $|\tilde{x}| \ll K_m + x_0$ (for MM kinetics) or lies within the linear segment of the static characteristic.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Compartmental Modeling (Examples of Each Type in Physiological Systems)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT865 - MODELLING OF PHYSIOLOGICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Compartmental Modeling (Examples of Each Type in Physiological Systems)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

A **compartment** is an idealized, well-mixed, homogeneous volume (pool) within the body that contains a substance (tracer, drug, ion, hormone, etc.) at a uniform concentration. A **compartmental model** is a mathematical description of a physiological system in which the body (or organ) is represented as a finite collection of such compartments interconnected by material transport pathways.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Godfrey, 1983):**
> *"A compartment is a mathematical entity defined by a particular spatial region, a particular species of substance, and a particular mode of transport. The defining characteristic of a compartment is that the state variable (typically concentration or amount) is spatially uniform at every instant."*

The fundamental governing equation of any compartmental system is the **Conservation of Mass** (mass balance):

$$\frac{dq_i(t)}{dt} = \sum_{j \neq i} F_{ji}(t) - \sum_{j \neq i} F_{ij}(t) + G_i(t) - C_i(t)$$

where $q_i$ is the amount of substance in compartment $i$, $F_{ij}$ is the flow from compartment $i$ to compartment $j$, $G_i$ is the rate of generation, and $C_i$ is the rate of consumption.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Connected Buckets" View

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy: The Bathroom-Tank Analogy**
> Imagine three water buckets (compartments) connected by pipes of different widths. Water flows in from a tap (input), leaks out through holes (output), and mixes instantaneously inside each bucket so that the water level is the same throughout.
> - **Bucket 1** = Blood plasma (central)
> - **Bucket 2** = Muscle tissue (peripheral)
> - **Bucket 3** = Fat/adipose tissue (deep peripheral)
> 
> The *rate* at which water flows between buckets is governed by the **pipe diameter** (which represents the **rate constant** $k$).
> - If a pipe is wide, water moves fast → large $k$ (well-perfused tissue like kidney).
> - If a pipe is narrow, water moves slow → small $k$ (poorly-perfused tissue like fat).
> 
> **Key insight for students:** Just as you cannot tell *where* inside a bucket the water is, in a compartment you cannot resolve sub-compartmental spatial gradients — everything is averaged into one lumped variable $C(t)$.

### 1.3 Core Assumptions of Compartmental Analysis

For a model to qualify as a valid compartmental model, the following axioms must hold:

1. **Well-Mixed Assumption:** Spatial uniformity of concentration inside the compartment.
2. **Linear/Non-linear kinetics:** Transport rates may be linear (first-order) or non-linear (Michaelis–Menten, saturable).
3. **Conservation of Mass:** Total mass (input + initial) = Total mass (output + final + accumulation).
4. **Time-Invariant Parameters:** Rate constants ($k$) and volumes ($V$) are constant over the observation window (for *linear* models).
5. **Stationary Tracer Behavior:** The substance does not chemically transform between compartments (unless an explicit reaction arrow is included).

### 1.4 Why Compartmental Modeling?

| Application Domain | Why It Is Used |
|---|---|
| **Pharmacokinetics** | Predict drug plasma concentration vs. time |
| **Endocrinology** | Hormone turnover (iodine, cortisol, insulin) |
| **Toxicology** | Heavy-metal body burden (lead, mercury) |
| **Nuclear Medicine** | Radio-tracer distribution (Tc-99m, I-131) |
| **Clinical Diagnostics** | Glucose tolerance, calcium kinetics |
| **Anesthesiology** | Multi-compartment propofol/remifentanil dosing |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** *A compartment is NOT a physical anatomical structure.* It is a **mathematical idealization** defined by kinetic behavior. For example, plasma albumin and muscle water may form *one* "fast-exchange" compartment despite being in different anatomical spaces.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Compartmental Models

Compartmental models are classified along **two primary axes**: (i) the *number* of compartments, and (ii) the *arrangement* (topology) of inter-compartmental connections.

#### A. By Number of Compartments

- **Single-compartment model (1C):** Whole body treated as one homogeneous pool.
- **Two-compartment model (2C):** Central + peripheral.
- **Three-compartment model (3C):** Central + shallow peripheral + deep peripheral (e.g., propofol).
- **N-compartment model:** Higher-order generalizations.

#### B. By Topological Arrangement

1. **Catenary (Chain) Models:** Compartments arranged in series → $C_1 \rightarrow C_2 \rightarrow C_3 \rightarrow \cdots$
2. **Mammillary Models:** One central compartment surrounded by peripheral compartments, each connecting only to the center → $C_2 \rightarrow C_1 \leftarrow C_3$
3. **Combined Catenary–Mammillary (Hybrid) Models:** Mixture of both (most physiological).

### 2.2 The General Mass-Balance Equation

For compartment $i$ in a linear (first-order) system:

$$\frac{dq_i(t)}{dt} = \sum_{\substack{j=1 \\ j \neq i}}^{n} k_{ji} \, q_j(t) - \left( \sum_{\substack{j=1 \\ j \neq i}}^{n} k_{ij} + k_{i0} \right) q_i(t) + u_i(t)$$

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|
| $q_i(t)$ | Amount of substance in compartment $i$ | mg, mmol |
| $k_{ij}$ | Rate constant from compartment $i$ to $j$ | $\text{min}^{-1}$ or $\text{hr}^{-1}$ |
| $k_{i0}$ | Rate constant of elimination from $i$ to exterior | $\text{min}^{-1}$ |
| $u_i(t)$ | External infusion/input rate | $\text{mg/min}$ |
| $C_i(t)$ | Concentration $= q_i(t)/V_i$ | $\text{mg/L}$ |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Table: Essential Compartmental Modeling Formulas (Linear, First-Order)**

| # | Model Type | Differential Equation | Solution / Transfer Function | Physiological Example |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **1-Compartment (IV bolus)** | $\dfrac{dC}{dt} = -kC$ | $C(t) = C_0 \, e^{-kt}$; $t_{1/2} = \dfrac{\ln 2}{k}$ | Drug with instant distribution (e.g., ethanol in well-perfused tissues) |
| 2 | **1-Compartment (IV infusion)** | $\dfrac{dC}{dt} = \dfrac{R_{in}}{V} - kC$ | $C_{ss} = \dfrac{R_{in}}{kV}$; $\tau = \dfrac{1}{k}$ | Heparin infusion |
| 3 | **1-Compartment (Oral)** | $\dfrac{dA_g}{dt} = -k_a A_g$ ; $\dfrac{dC}{dt} = \dfrac{F \cdot k_a A_g}{V} - kC$ | $C(t) = \dfrac{F \cdot D \cdot k_a}{V(k_a-k)} (e^{-kt}-e^{-k_a t})$ | Oral theophylline, paracetamol |
| 4 | **2-Compartment Catenary** | $\dot{C_1} = - (k_{12}+k_{10})C_1 + k_{21}C_2$ ; $\dot{C_2} = k_{12}C_1 - k_{21}C_2$ | Bi-exponential: $C_1(t) = A e^{-\alpha t} + B e^{-\beta t}$ | Aminoglycosides (gentamicin) |
| 5 | **3-Compartment Mammillary** | Three coupled 1st-order ODEs | Tri-exponential: $C(t) = A e^{-\alpha t} + B e^{-\beta t} + C e^{-\gamma t}$ | Propofol, fentanyl, thiopental |
| 6 | **Stochastic 1C** | $\dfrac{dC}{dt} = -kC + w(t)$ | Mean $\pm$ SD bands (Monte Carlo) | Population PK variability |

**Key derived quantities:**

$$ \text{Clearance} \; Cl = k \cdot V \quad [\text{mL/min}] $$
$$ \text{Volume of Distribution} \; V_d = \dfrac{\text{Total Dose}}{\text{Initial Concentration}} \quad [\text{L}] $$
$$ \text{Mean Residence Time} \; \text{MRT} = \dfrac{1}{k} \quad \text{(1C-model)} $$
$$ \text{Steady-state concentration} \; C_{ss} = \dfrac{R_{in}}{Cl} $$
$$ \text{Area Under Curve (AUC)} = \int_0^\infty C(t)\, dt = \dfrac{C_0}{k} \quad \text{(1C)} $$

### 2.4 Engineering & Physiological Utility

- **Predictive dosing:** Compute loading dose $D_L = C_{target} \cdot V_d$ and maintenance dose $D_M = C_{target} \cdot Cl \cdot \tau$.
- **Bedside clinical decision support:** Target-controlled infusion (TCI) pumps for anesthesia.
- **Tracer kinetics in imaging:** PET/SPECT quantification of metabolic rate.
- **Population pharmacokinetics:** Mixed-effects (NONMEM) models for personalized medicine.
- **Diagnostic interpretation:** Glucose tolerance test (GTT) parameters (k-value, insulin sensitivity index).

### 2.5 The Concept of "Identifiability"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall Topic:** Before fitting a compartmental model to data, one MUST verify **structural identifiability** — that the model parameters $\{k_{ij}, V_i\}$ can be uniquely determined from observable outputs (e.g., plasma concentration). For a 2C model, plasma data alone uniquely identifies $k_{12}, k_{21}, k_{10}, V_1$ but NOT $V_2$. Additional measurements (urine data, tissue biopsy) are required.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Single Compartment with First-Order Elimination (IV Bolus)

**Setup:** A dose $D$ (mg) is injected instantaneously into the bloodstream at $t=0$. The drug distributes instantly throughout volume $V$ (L) and is eliminated by first-order kinetics.

**Step 1 — Initial condition.** At $t=0^+$:

$$ C(0) = \frac{D}{V} = C_0 $$

**Step 2 — Mass balance.** Inflow = 0; outflow = $k \cdot C(t)$:

$$ V \frac{dC}{dt} = - k \, V \, C(t) $$

**Step 3 — Reduce.** Divide both sides by $V$:

$$ \frac{dC}{dt} = -k \, C $$

**Step 4 — Separate variables.**

$$ \frac{dC}{C} = -k \, dt $$

**Step 5 — Integrate from $0$ to $t$.**

$$ \int_{C_0}^{C(t)} \frac{dC}{C} = -k \int_0^t dt $$

**Step 6 — Evaluate.**

$$ \ln C(t) - \ln C_0 = -k t $$

**Step 7 — Exponentiate.**

$$ C(t) = C_0 \, e^{-k t} $$

**Step 8 — Half-life.** Set $C(t_{1/2}) = C_0/2$:

$$ \frac{1}{2} = e^{-k t_{1/2}} \;\Rightarrow\; t_{1/2} = \frac{\ln 2}{k} $$

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Two-Compartment Mammillary Model (Most-Known Case)

**Setup:** Central compartment (plasma, volume $V_1$) exchanges drug with peripheral tissue (volume $V_2$). Elimination occurs only from the central compartment. Rate constants: $k_{12}, k_{21}, k_{10}$.

**Step 1 — State vector.** Let $q_1, q_2$ denote drug amounts in compartments 1, 2.

**Step 2 — Mass balance for compartment 1.**

$$ \frac{dq_1}{dt} = -k_{12} q_1 - k_{10} q_1 + k_{21} q_2 $$

**Step 3 — Mass balance for compartment 2.**

$$ \frac{dq_2}{dt} = k_{12} q_1 - k_{21} q_2 $$

**Step 4 — Convert to concentrations using $C_i = q_i / V_i$.**

$$ \frac{dC_1}{dt} = -(k_{12} + k_{10}) C_1 + \frac{V_2}{V_1} k_{21} C_2 $$

**Step 5 — Take Laplace transforms** (zero initial conditions, $L\{C_i\} = \bar{C}_i$):

$$ s\bar{C}_1 = -(k_{12}+k_{10})\bar{C}_1 + \frac{V_2}{V_1} k_{21} \bar{C}_2 $$

$$ s\bar{C}_2 = k_{12} \frac{V_1}{V_2}\bar{C}_1 - k_{21} \bar{C}_2 $$

**Step 6 — Solve the second equation for $\bar{C}_2$.**

$$ \bar{C}_2 (s + k_{21}) = \frac{k_{12} V_1}{V_2} \bar{C}_1 $$

$$ \bar{C}_2 = \frac{k_{12} V_1}{V_2 (s + k_{21})} \bar{C}_1 $$

**Step 7 — Substitute into the first equation.**

$$ s\bar{C}_1 = -(k_{12}+k_{10})\bar{C}_1 + \frac{k_{12} k_{21}}{s + k_{21}} \bar{C}_1 $$

**Step 8 — Collect $\bar{C}_1$ terms.**

$$ \left[ s + (k_{12}+k_{10}) - \frac{k_{12} k_{21}}{s + k_{21}} \right] \bar{C}_1 = 0 $$

$$ \left[ (s + k_{21})(s + k_{12}+k_{10}) - k_{12} k_{21} \right] \bar{C}_1 = 0 $$

**Step 9 — Characteristic quadratic (denominator = 0 for eigenvalues).**

$$ s^2 + (k_{12}+k_{21}+k_{10}) s + k_{21} k_{10} = 0 $$

**Step 10 — Solve via quadratic formula.**

$$ s_{\alpha,\beta} = \frac{-(k_{12}+k_{21}+k_{10}) \pm \sqrt{(k_{12}+k_{21}+k_{10})^2 - 4 k_{21} k_{10}}}{2} $$

Define $\alpha = -s_\alpha$ (fast) and $\beta = -s_\beta$ (slow):

$$ \alpha + \beta = k_{12}+k_{21}+k_{10} \quad ; \quad \alpha \beta = k_{21} k_{10} $$

**Step 11 — Time-domain solution (inverse Laplace).**

$$ C_1(t) = A \, e^{-\alpha t} + B \, e^{-\beta t} $$

**Step 12 — Determine coefficients from initial condition $C_1(0)=D/V_1$ and slope $\dot{C}_1(0)=-(k_{12}+k_{10})C_1(0)$.**

$$ A = \frac{C_1(0)(\alpha - k_{21})}{\alpha - \beta} \quad ; \quad B = \frac{C_1(0)(k_{21}-\beta)}{\alpha - \beta} $$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Three-Compartment Mammillary Model (Propofol-type)

For propofol anesthesia, three compartments are needed: $V_1$ (central, fast equilibrating — vessel-rich group), $V_2$ (muscle, intermediate), $V_3$ (fat, very slow). Elimination from $V_1$ only.

**General ODE system:**

$$ \frac{dC_1}{dt} = -(k_{10}+k_{12}+k_{13})C_1 + \frac{V_2}{V_1} k_{21} C_2 + \frac{V_3}{V_1} k_{31} C_3 $$

$$ \frac{dC_2}{dt} = \frac{V_1}{V_2} k_{12} C_1 - k_{21} C_2 $$

$$ \frac{dC_3}{dt} = \frac{V_1}{V_3} k_{13} C_1 - k_{31} C_3 $$

**Matrix form:**

$$ \frac{d\mathbf{C}}{dt} = \mathbf{K} \, \mathbf{C} $$

$$ \mathbf{K} = \begin{bmatrix} -(k_{10}+k_{12}+k_{13}) & k_{21} V_2/V_1 & k_{31} V_3/V_1 \\ k_{12} V_1/V_2 & -k_{21} & 0 \\ k_{13} V_1/V_3 & 0 & -k_{31} \end{bmatrix} $$

**Solution — Tri-exponential decay:**

$$ C_1(t) = A \, e^{-\alpha t} + B \, e^{-\beta t} + G \, e^{-\gamma t} $$

where $\alpha > \beta > \gamma > 0$ are eigenvalues of $-\mathbf{K}$.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Compartment Types

| Feature | **1-Compartment** | **2-Cat Compartment** | **2-Mam Compartment** | **3-Compartment** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Number of state ODEs** | 1 | 2 | 2 | 3 |
| **Number of parameters** | 2 ($k, V$) | 4 ($k_{12},k_{21},k_{10},V_1$) | 4 | 7 |
| **Plasma curve shape** | Mono-exponential | Bi-exponential | Bi-exponential | Tri-exponential |
| **Identifiability from plasma only** | Yes | Yes (all 4) | Yes (all 4) | Yes (5 of 7) |
| **Typical drug** | Theophylline, Ethanol | Gentamicin, Lidocaine | Digoxin, Vancomycin | Propofol, Fentanyl |
| **Steady-state volume** | $V$ | $V_1+V_2$ | $V_1+V_2$ | $V_1+V_2+V_3$ |

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A 70-kg patient receives an IV bolus of 500 mg of a drug. Plasma concentration follows a bi-exponential decay:
$$ C(t) = 8.5\, e^{-0.45 t} + 2.1\, e^{-0.05 t} \;\; [\text{mg/L, t in hr}] $$
Find: (i) $C_0$ at $t=0$, (ii) $V_1$, (iii) the initial distribution half-life.

**Solution:**

(i) $C(0) = 8.5 + 2.1 = \mathbf{10.6 \, mg/L}$

(ii) $V_1 = D/C_0 = 500/10.6 = \mathbf{47.2 \, L}$

(iii) Distribution phase half-life:
$$ t_{1/2,\alpha} = \frac{\ln 2}{0.45} = \mathbf{1.54 \, hr} $$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Single-Compartment Model — Block Diagram

<!-- Mermaid safe block (alphanumeric IDs, no markdown inside labels) -->
```mermaid
graph LR
    EXT[External Environment] -->|u(t) Dose Input| CTR[C1 Central Compartment Volume V, Amount q]
    CTR -->|k10 elimination| EXT
    CTR -.->|Output y(t) = q/V = C| OBS[Observable Plasma Concentration]
```

### 4.2 Two-Compartment Catenary (Chain) Model

```mermaid
graph LR
    EXT[External] -->|u t| C1A[C1 Central Plasma Volume V1]
    C1A -->|k12| C2A[C2 Peripheral Tissue Volume V2]
    C1A -->|k10 elimination| EXT
    C2A -.->|Observation only from C1| OBS1[Plasma Sampling Site]
```

### 4.3 Two-Compartment Mammillary Model

```mermaid
graph LR
    EXT2[External Environment] -->|u t| CENT[C1 Central Compartment Blood Rich V1]
    CENT -->|k10 elimination| EXT2
    CENT <-->|k12 / k21| PER1[C2 Peripheral Compartment Muscle V2]
    OBS2[Plasma Sampling] -.-> CENT
```

### 4.4 Three-Compartment Mammillary Model (Propofol-class)

```mermaid
graph LR
    EXT3[External] -->|u t Infusion Pump| CC[C1 Central V1 Vessel Rich Group]
    CC -->|k10 hepatic| EXT3
    CC <-->|k12 / k21| PP[C2 Peripheral Shallow Muscle V2]
    CC <-->|k13 / k31| DP[C3 Peripheral Deep Fat V3]
    OBS3[EEG / Plasma Sampling] -.-> CC
```

### 4.5 Combined / Hybrid (Catenary + Mammillary) Model

```mermaid
graph LR
    EXT4[External Input] --> H1[C1 Compartment]
    H1 -->|k12| H2[C2 Compartment]
    H1 -->|k13| H3[C3 Compartment]
    H2 -->|k24| H4[C4 Compartment]
    H3 <-->|k35| H5[C5 Compartment]
    H4 -->|k40 elimination| EXT4
    H5 -->|k50 elimination| EXT4
```

### 4.6 Functional Architecture of Compartmental Analysis

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph DATA[Data Acquisition]
        A1[Patient Dose Records]
        A2[Serial Blood Sampling]
        A3[Tissue Biopsy / Imaging]
    end
    subgraph MODEL[Model Identification]
        B1[Choose Topology 1C 2C 3C]
        B2[Write Mass Balance ODEs]
        B3[Compute Transfer Function]
    end
    subgraph FIT[Parameter Estimation]
        C1[Nonlinear Regression NLLS]
        C2[Bayesian MCMC]
        C3[Goodness of Fit AIC BIC]
    end
    subgraph PRED[Prediction and Validation]
        D1[Simulate C vs t]
        D2[Cross Validation]
        D3[Dosing Recommendation]
    end
    DATA --> MODEL
    MODEL --> FIT
    FIT --> PRED
```

### 4.7 Summary Schematic — All Five Types in One Figure

```mermaid
graph TB
    TP1[Type 1 Single Compartment]:::t1
    TP2[Type 2 Two Compartment Catenary]:::t2
    TP3[Type 3 Two Compartment Mammillary]:::t3
    TP4[Type 4 Three Compartment Mammillary]:::t4
    TP5[Type 5 N Compartment Hybrid]:::t5
    classDef t1 fill:#FFDDC1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    classDef t2 fill:#C1E1FF,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    classDef t3 fill:#C1FFD7,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    classDef t4 fill:#E1C1FF,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    classDef t5 fill:#FFC1E1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    TP1 --> TP2 --> TP3 --> TP4 --> TP5
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Define a compartment in the context of physiological system modeling. State any three fundamental assumptions underlying compartmental analysis.*

**Model Answer:**
A compartment is a mathematical idealization of a well-mixed, homogeneous region within a physiological system where a substance (drug, hormone, ion, tracer) is uniformly distributed at all times.
The three fundamental assumptions are:
1. **Well-Mixed Assumption:** Spatial uniformity of concentration within the compartment.
2. **Linear (First-Order) Kinetics:** Transport rates are proportional to the amount in the source compartment.
3. **Conservation of Mass:** No mass is created or destroyed except through explicitly modeled input/output terms.
*(3 marks: 1 for definition + 2 for assumptions.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Differentiate between catenary and mammillary compartmental models with one physiological example each.*

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Catenary Model | Mammillary Model |
|---|---|---|
| **Topology** | Compartments in series | Central hub with peripheral spokes |
| **Flow** | $C_1 \rightarrow C_2 \rightarrow C_3$ | $C_2 \rightarrow C_1 \leftarrow C_3$ |
| **Example** | Oral drug absorption chain (gut → plasma → tissue → urine) | Propofol distribution (vessel-rich central + muscle + fat peripherals) |

*(3 marks: 1 for each differentiation point + 1 for correct example.)*

---

### Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Choice Rule:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Derive the differential equation and its solution for a **single-compartment model** with first-order elimination following an **IV bolus** dose. Show the derivation of the half-life expression. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** An antibiotic is administered as an IV bolus of 500 mg to an adult. The drug follows single-compartment kinetics with $V = 35$ L and $k = 0.25 \text{ hr}^{-1}$. Compute:
(i) Initial plasma concentration $C_0$
(ii) Plasma concentration at $t = 4$ hours
(iii) Half-life $t_{1/2}$
(iv) Clearance $Cl$
(v) AUC (Area Under the Curve)
*(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Step 1 — State assumption:** Dose $D$ introduced instantaneously at $t=0$ distributes instantly throughout volume $V$ to give $C_0 = D/V$.

**Step 2 — Mass balance:** No inflow, first-order elimination:

$$ V \frac{dC}{dt} = -k \, V \, C \;\Rightarrow\; \frac{dC}{dt} = -k \, C $$

**Step 3 — Solve by separation:**

$$ \int_{C_0}^{C(t)} \frac{dC}{C} = -k \int_0^t dt \;\Rightarrow\; C(t) = C_0 \, e^{-kt} $$

**Step 4 — Half-life derivation:**

$$ C(t_{1/2}) = \frac{C_0}{2} \;\Rightarrow\; e^{-kt_{1/2}} = \frac{1}{2} \;\Rightarrow\; t_{1/2} = \frac{\ln 2}{k} $$

**Diagrammatic Block (textual):**

$$ \text{EXT} \xrightarrow{u(t)=\delta(t)\cdot D} \boxed{C_1:\;V,\;q(t)} \xrightarrow{k\cdot q(t)} \text{EXT} $$

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

(i) $\;C_0 = D/V = 500/35 = \mathbf{14.29 \; mg/L}$

(ii) $\;C(4) = 14.29 \cdot e^{-0.25 \times 4} = 14.29 \cdot e^{-1} = 14.29 \cdot 0.3679 = \mathbf{5.26 \; mg/L}$

(iii) $\;t_{1/2} = \ln 2 / 0.25 = \mathbf{2.77 \; hr}$

(iv) $\;Cl = k \cdot V = 0.25 \cdot 35 = \mathbf{8.75 \; L/hr}$

(v) $\;\text{AUC} = C_0 / k = 14.29 / 0.25 = \mathbf{57.14 \; mg \cdot hr / L}$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2023]**

**(a)** With the help of a block diagram, explain the **two-compartment mammillary model**. Write the governing mass-balance equations. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** For a two-compartment model with parameters $V_1 = 10$ L, $V_2 = 20$ L, $k_{12} = 0.30 \,\text{hr}^{-1}$, $k_{21} = 0.20 \,\text{hr}^{-1}$, $k_{10} = 0.10 \,\text{hr}^{-1}$, and an IV bolus dose $D = 100$ mg, compute the eigenvalues $\alpha$ and $\beta$ of the system and hence write the plasma concentration profile $C_1(t)$. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

A two-compartment mammillary model represents the body as a **central compartment** (well-perfused organs: heart, liver, kidneys, blood) and a **peripheral compartment** (poorly perfused: muscle, fat). Drug exchanges between them, but elimination occurs only from the central compartment.

**Block Diagram:**

```
        u(t)
         ↓
   ┌─────────────┐  k12  ┌─────────────┐
   │    C1       │←─────→│    C2       │
   │ (Central V1)│ k21   │(Peripheral V2)│
   └──────┬──────┘       └─────────────┘
          │ k10
          ↓
        EXT
```

**Governing Equations:**

$$ \frac{dC_1}{dt} = -(k_{12}+k_{10})C_1 + \frac{V_2}{V_1}k_{21}C_2 $$

$$ \frac{dC_2}{dt} = \frac{V_1}{V_2}k_{12}C_1 - k_{21}C_2 $$

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1 — Characteristic equation:**

$$ s^2 + (k_{12}+k_{21}+k_{10})s + k_{21}k_{10} = 0 $$

Substitute values:

$$ s^2 + (0.30+0.20+0.10)s + (0.20)(0.10) = 0 $$

$$ s^2 + 0.60\,s + 0.02 = 0 $$

**Step 2 — Solve quadratic:**

$$ s = \frac{-0.60 \pm \sqrt{0.36 - 0.08}}{2} = \frac{-0.60 \pm \sqrt{0.28}}{2} = \frac{-0.60 \pm 0.5292}{2} $$

$$ s_\alpha = -0.0354 \;\Rightarrow\; \alpha = 0.0354 \,\text{hr}^{-1} $$
$$ s_\beta = -0.5646 \;\Rightarrow\; \beta = 0.5646 \,\text{hr}^{-1} $$

**Step 3 — Initial condition:** $C_1(0) = D/V_1 = 100/10 = 10$ mg/L.

**Step 4 — Coefficients (using formula $A = C_0(\alpha-k_{21})/(\alpha-\beta)$):**

$$ A = \frac{10(0.0354-0.20)}{0.0354-0.5646} = \frac{10(-0.1646)}{-0.5292} = 3.11 \; \text{mg/L} $$

$$ B = \frac{10(0.5646-0.20)}{0.5292} = 6.89 \; \text{mg/L} $$

**Step 5 — Final plasma profile:**

$$ \boxed{C_1(t) = 3.11 \, e^{-0.0354 t} + 6.89 \, e^{-0.5646 t} \quad [\text{mg/L};\; t \text{ in hr}]} $$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit confusion:** Mixing $k$ in $\text{min}^{-1}$ with time in hours — always check units first.
> 2. **Forgetting $V_2/V_1$ ratio:** When converting $q$-equations to $C$-equations, students often omit the volume ratio, costing 1 mark.
> 3. **Sign errors in the characteristic equation:** The coefficient of $s$ in a 2-compartment model must be POSITIVE for stable (decaying) systems.
> 4. **Misidentifying $\alpha$ vs $\beta$:** By convention, $\alpha > \beta$ (faster vs slower phase). Reversing them loses 1 mark.
> 5. **Confusing V_d with V:** Volume of distribution $V_d$ is computed from AUC at steady-state; it is not the central volume $V_1$.
> 6. **Assuming first-order kinetics for saturable drugs:** Ethanol and phenytoin show Michaelis–Menten (non-linear) kinetics — the linear 1C model fails.
> 7. **Forgetting to state assumptions:** Always list the well-mixed and first-order assumptions at the start of any compartmental answer.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Exam:**

- ☐ A **compartment** = well-mixed, spatially uniform pool → characterized by a single state variable (amount or concentration).
- ☐ Governing law = **Conservation of Mass** → inflow − outflow + generation − consumption = $dq/dt$.
- ☐ **First-order kinetics** → $dC/dt = -kC$ → exponential decay with $t_{1/2} = \ln 2 / k$.
- ☐ **Catenary models** → chain arrangement (gut → blood → tissue → urine).
- ☐ **Mammillary models** → central + peripheral hub (propofol, fentanyl).
- ☐ **Hybrid models** → combination of both; most realistic.
- ☐ Number of exponential terms in $C(t)$ = Number of compartments.
- ☐ **Identifiability**: A 3-compartment model is identifiable from plasma data for 5 parameters (need tissue sampling for remaining).
- ☐ **Clearance** $Cl = kV$; **Volume of Distribution** $V_d = D/C_0$; **AUC** = $C_0/k$ (1C).
- ☐ Linear ODEs in Laplace domain → algebraic; eigenvalues are the decay rates.
- ☐ **Physiological examples:** Ethanol (1C), Gentamicin (2C), Propofol (3C), Thyroid Iodine (2C), Glucose-insulin (non-linear), Calcium-bone (2C mammillary).
- ☐ Before fitting: verify **structural identifiability** and **goodness of fit** (AIC, BIC, residual analysis).
- ☐ Always mention **assumptions** (well-mixed, linear, time-invariant) when writing compartmental answers in KTU exams.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basic Cardiac Anatomy and Electrocardiology Conduction System: The Electrocardiogram (ECG), Cardiac Action Potential Models, Biophysical Models (Noble)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT865 - MODELLING OF PHYSIOLOGICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Basic Cardiac Anatomy and Electrocardiology Conduction System: The Electrocardiogram (ECG), Cardiac Action Potential Models, Biophysical Models (Noble)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Basic Cardiac Anatomy — The Engineering Perspective

The **human heart** is a four-chambered, dual-pump electromechanical organ that circulates approximately **5 litres of blood per minute** at rest (cardiac output, CO ≈ 5 L/min) and can elevate to **20–25 L/min** during heavy exercise. From a physiological modelling standpoint, the heart is treated as a closed hydraulic loop driven by a synchronised bio-electrical control system.

**The Four Chambers:**
- **Right Atrium (RA)** — receives deoxygenated blood from the systemic circulation via the superior and inferior vena cava.
- **Right Ventricle (RV)** — pumps deoxygenated blood through the pulmonary artery to the lungs.
- **Left Atrium (LA)** — receives oxygenated blood from the pulmonary veins.
- **Left Ventricle (LV)** — pumps oxygenated blood through the aorta to the systemic circulation; possesses the thickest wall (≈ **8–12 mm**) because it must generate peak systolic pressures of **≈ 120 mmHg**.

**The Four Valves (One-Way Flow Gates):**
- **Tricuspid Valve** (RA → RV)
- **Pulmonary Valve** (RV → Pulmonary Artery)
- **Mitral/Bicuspid Valve** (LA → LV)
- **Aortic Valve** (LV → Aorta)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The heart wall is composed of three layers — the **endocardium** (inner endothelial lining), **myocardium** (middle muscular layer, the actual contractile tissue), and **epicardium** (outer serous layer). The myocardium is a functional **syncytium** — cells are electrically coupled via **gap junctions (intercalated discs)** that allow action potentials to propagate as a unified wave.

### 1.2 The Cardiac Conduction System — The "Bio-Electrical Wiring"

The heart contains specialised non-contractile autorhythmic cells that generate and route electrical impulses. The conduction sequence ensures atrial contraction precedes ventricular contraction, optimising filling.

**Pathway of Excitation (in order of activation):**

1. **SA Node (Sino-Atrial Node)** — located in the upper right atrium near the SVC opening. The heart's **primary pacemaker**, firing at **≈ 70–80 bpm** (beats per minute). Generates the action potential that initiates every normal heartbeat.

2. **Internodal Pathways (Bachmann's Bundle)** — rapidly conduct the impulse from SA to AV node across the atria, causing the **P wave**.

3. **AV Node (Atrio-Ventricular Node)** — located at the base of the right atrium near the interatrial septum. Introduces a critical **AV nodal delay of ≈ 100 ms**, allowing the atria to fully contract and fill the ventricles before ventricular systole.

4. **Bundle of His (AV Bundle)** — the only electrical bridge between atria and ventricles, traversing the fibrous skeleton.

5. **Right & Left Bundle Branches** — descend along the interventricular septum toward the apex.

6. **Purkinje Fibres** — fan out beneath the endocardium, conducting at **≈ 2–4 m/s** (the fastest conducting tissue in the heart, due to abundant gap junctions and large-diameter cells). They trigger the **QRS complex**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Think of the cardiac conduction system as a precisely engineered **electrical circuit board** controlling a hydraulic pump. The **SA node is the master clock/oscillator**, the **AV node is a deliberate delay buffer** (analogous to a debounce circuit in electronics), and the **Purkinje fibres are high-speed transmission lines** (like coaxial cables) delivering synchronised firing signals.

### 1.3 The Electrocardiogram (ECG) — Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** The **Electrocardiogram (ECG/EKG)** is a non-invasive graphical recording of the **time-varying electrical potential differences** generated by the ionic currents of cardiac depolarisation and repolarisation, measured at the body surface using skin electrodes, displayed as voltage (mV) versus time (seconds).

The ECG is **not** a direct recording of the action potential, but rather a **volume-conducted, spatially averaged, far-field projection** of the vector sum of all cardiac electrical activity on the body surface.

**The Five Standard Waves and Intervals:**

| Wave / Interval | Origin | Duration | Physiological Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| **P wave** | Atrial depolarisation | **< 120 ms** | Atria contract (atrial systole) |
| **PR interval** | SA → AV node delay + conduction | **120–200 ms** | Atrio-ventricular conduction time |
| **QRS complex** | Ventricular depolarisation | **< 120 ms** | Ventricles contract (ventricular systole) |
| **ST segment** | Ventricular plateau phase | **80–120 ms** | All ventricular cells depolarised (isoelectric) |
| **T wave** | Ventricular repolarisation | **160 ms** | Ventricles relax (ventricular diastole) |
| **QT interval** | Total ventricular electrical activity | **350–440 ms** | Total ventricular systole electrically |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy for ECG:** Imagine throwing a stone into a still pond — the ripples spread outward in concentric circles. The ECG is essentially the "ripple pattern" of the heart's electrical wave, sampled at specific points (electrodes) on the skin. The **P wave** is the small splash from atrial ripples; the **QRS** is the massive splash from ventricular ripples; the **T wave** is the calm returning to stillness.

### 1.4 Cardiac Action Potential — Definition

The **cardiac action potential (AP)** is the transient change in transmembrane potential of a cardiac myocyte in response to a stimulus. Unlike the brief neuronal AP (≈ 1–2 ms), the cardiac ventricular AP lasts **200–400 ms**, dominated by a characteristic **plateau phase (Phase 2)** sustained by L-type Ca²⁺ channels.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Critical Distinction:** Cardiac myocytes have a **long absolute refractory period (ARP)** that lasts almost the entire duration of the AP (≈ 250 ms). This **prevents tetanic contraction** of the heart, ensuring it can only contract → relax → fill, never lock in sustained contraction (which would be fatal).

### 1.5 Biophysical Models — The Noble Model (1962)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** The **Noble Model (1962)** is a mathematical model of the **Purkinje fibre action potential** developed by **Denis Noble**, extending the Hodgkin-Huxley (1952) formalism of the squid giant axon to cardiac electrophysiology. It was the first biophysically detailed model of a cardiac cell and introduced the critical concept that the **potassium current is governed by two distinct components** — a delayed-rectifier current ($i_{K_2}$, later re-identified) and an inward-rectifier current ($i_{K_1}$) — in addition to a fast sodium current ($i_{Na}$) and a leak current.

**Noble's Key Contributions:**
- First to show that the **resting potential depends on $i_{K_1}$** (inward rectifier), not the classical leak.
- First to mathematically reproduce the **long plateau** of the cardiac AP.
- Established the **Hodgkin-Huxley (HH) gating variable framework** ($m, h, n$) as the universal language for excitable membrane modelling.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Five Phases of the Ventricular Action Potential

The cardiac ventricular action potential is divided into **5 phases (0–4)**, each driven by specific ionic currents:

**Phase 4 — Resting Potential (≈ −90 mV)**
- Dominated by **$i_{K_1}$** (inward rectifier K⁺ current).
- Membrane is highly permeable to K⁺ at rest; Cl⁻ and other ions contribute minimally.
- The **Nernst equilibrium potential for K⁺** ($E_K \approx -94$ mV) sets the baseline.

**Phase 0 — Rapid Depolarisation (upstroke)**
- Fast voltage-gated **Na⁺ channels open** → massive Na⁺ influx ($i_{Na}$).
- $V_m$ rapidly rises from −90 mV to **+30 mV** at **≈ 200–400 V/s**.
- These channels **inactivate quickly** (within 1–2 ms).
- Analogy: An electrical "switch closure" — the fastest event in the cardiac cycle.

**Phase 1 — Early Rapid Repolarisation (notch)**
- Na⁺ channels inactivate.
- **Transient outward K⁺ current ($I_{to}$)** opens briefly → K⁺ efflux → small downward notch.
- Cl⁻ current ($I_{Cl}$) also contributes.
- Creates the characteristic "spike-and-dome" morphology.

**Phase 2 — Plateau Phase (the cardiac signature)**
- A delicate **balance between inward Ca²⁺ current and outward K⁺ currents**.
- **L-type Ca²⁺ channels** (long-lasting, high-threshold) carry depolarising Ca²⁺ influx ($i_{Ca-L}$).
- **Delayed rectifier K⁺ currents** ($i_{K_r}$, $i_{K_s}$) carry repolarising K⁺ efflux.
- Net current ≈ 0; $V_m$ stays near **0 mV** for **≈ 200–300 ms**.
- This plateau is the **electrical basis of the long refractory period** and prevents tetanus.

**Phase 3 — Rapid Repolarisation**
- Ca²⁺ channels close (time-dependent + voltage-dependent inactivation).
- Delayed rectifier K⁺ currents ($i_{K}$) dominate → massive K⁺ efflux.
- $V_m$ rapidly returns to $E_K$ (≈ −90 mV).
- Analogy: A controlled "drain" of positive charge.

**Phase 4 — Return to Rest**
- $i_{K_1}$ re-establishes the resting potential.
- Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase pump restores ionic gradients (3 Na⁺ out, 2 K⁺ in per cycle, ATP-dependent).

### 2.2 Ionic Currents and Their Driving Forces

Every ionic current in the Noble/HH framework follows the **Ohm's Law analogy**:

$$I_{ion} = g_{ion} \cdot (V_m - E_{ion})$$

Where:
- $g_{ion}$ = conductance of the ion channel (variable, voltage- and time-dependent)
- $V_m$ = membrane potential
- $E_{ion}$ = Nernst equilibrium potential for that ion
- $(V_m - E_{ion})$ = electrochemical driving force

**Nernst Equation:**

$$E_{ion} = \frac{RT}{zF} \ln\left(\frac{[ion]_o}{[ion]_i}\right) \approx 61.5 \cdot \log_{10}\left(\frac{[ion]_o}{[ion]_i}\right) \text{ at } 37°C$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** The Nernst equation is the **foundation of bio-sensor and bio-battery design**. The Nernst potential is essentially the **open-circuit voltage of a concentration cell**. The Goldman-Hodgkin-Katz (GHK) voltage equation is the extension when multiple ions contribute.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Hodgkin-Huxley / Noble Framework)

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $C_m$ | Membrane capacitance per unit area | **1 μF/cm²** |
| $I_m$ | Total membrane current density | μA/cm² |
| $V_m$ | Membrane potential | mV |
| $E_{Na}$ | Na⁺ Nernst potential | **+40 to +60 mV** |
| $E_K$ | K⁺ Nernst potential | **−90 to −94 mV** |
| $E_L$ | Leak reversal potential | **−25 to −50 mV** |
| $\bar{g}_{Na}$ | Max Na⁺ conductance | **400 mS/cm²** |
| $\bar{g}_K$ | Max K⁺ conductance (Noble) | **~1.2 mS/cm²** (per channel type) |
| $g_L$ | Leak conductance | **0.3 mS/cm²** |
| $m$ | Na⁺ activation gate | 0 → 1 (activates rapidly) |
| $h$ | Na⁺ inactivation gate | 1 → 0 (inactivates slowly) |
| $n$ | K⁺ activation gate | 0 → 1 (activates slowly) |
| $I_{stim}$ | External stimulus current | μA/cm² |
| $Q_{10}$ | Temperature coefficient | **≈ 3** for Na⁺/K⁺ gates |

### 2.4 Why the Noble Model Matters in Engineering

The Noble model is not merely academic — it powers modern **cardiac electrophysiology software** (e.g., the **LU-Rudy**, **O'Hara-Rudy**, **Ten Tusscher** models are all descendants). Engineering applications include:

1. **Drug Safety Testing (CiPA Initiative):** Pharmaceutical companies simulate hERG channel block (a $i_{K_r}$ component) to predict drug-induced **Long QT Syndrome** and **Torsades de Pointes** arrhythmia risk.
2. **Pacemaker Design:** Understanding SA node automaticity informs rate-adaptive pacing algorithms.
3. **Defibrillator Waveform Optimisation:** Biphasic vs. monophasic shock waveforms are designed using knowledge of the AP refractory period.
4. **ECG-Based Diagnostics:** AI/ML models for arrhythmia detection (AFib, VT, VF) are trained on simulated ECG outputs from biophysical models.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Utility:** The **1975 McAllister-Noble-Tsien model** of Purkinje fibre and the **1977 DiFrancesco-Noble model** (incorporating the funny current $i_f$) form the basis of the **CellML / OpenCOR** simulation platforms used in cardiac research worldwide. Modern patient-specific cardiac models (used in **HeartFlow**, **CardioInsight**) trace their lineage directly to Noble 1962.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: The Membrane Cable Equation (Foundation of Noble Model)

The cardiac membrane is modelled as a **parallel RC circuit** (lipid bilayer) in parallel with **variable conductances** (ion channels) and a **stimulus current source**. Applying **Kirchhoff's Current Law (KCL)** at a membrane patch:

**Step 1 — Identify all currents crossing the membrane.**

By conservation of charge, the sum of all ionic currents plus the capacitive current must equal the applied stimulus current:

$$I_{stim} = I_C + I_{ionic}$$

**Step 2 — Express the capacitive current.**

The membrane capacitance stores charge: $Q = C_m V_m$. Differentiating with respect to time:

$$I_C = C_m \frac{dV_m}{dt}$$

**Step 3 — Sum the ionic currents in the Noble Model.**

The Noble (1962) model incorporates three primary ionic currents — a fast sodium current ($i_{Na}$), a potassium current ($i_K$), and a leak current ($i_L$):

$$I_{ionic} = i_{Na} + i_K + i_L$$

**Step 4 — Apply the Ohmic relationship (driving force × conductance) for each current:**

$$i_{Na} = \bar{g}_{Na} \cdot m^3 h \cdot (V_m - E_{Na})$$

$$i_K = \bar{g}_K \cdot n^4 \cdot (V_m - E_K) \quad \text{(Note: Noble used $n^4$ initially; modern variants vary)}$$

$$i_L = \bar{g}_L \cdot (V_m - E_L)$$

**Step 5 — Assemble the complete membrane equation (Noble's Master Equation):**

$$I_{stim} = C_m \frac{dV_m}{dt} + \bar{g}_{Na} m^3 h (V_m - E_{Na}) + \bar{g}_K n^4 (V_m - E_K) + \bar{g}_L (V_m - E_L)$$

**Step 6 — Rearrange to express $dV_m/dt$ explicitly (the form used in numerical simulation):**

$$C_m \frac{dV_m}{dt} = I_{stim} - \left[ \bar{g}_{Na} m^3 h (V_m - E_{Na}) + \bar{g}_K n^4 (V_m - E_K) + \bar{g}_L (V_m - E_L) \right]$$

$$\frac{dV_m}{dt} = \frac{1}{C_m}\left[ I_{stim} - \bar{g}_{Na} m^3 h (V_m - E_{Na}) - \bar{g}_K n^4 (V_m - E_K) - \bar{g}_L (V_m - E_L) \right]$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic Explanation:** Step 1 is a direct application of KCL — the same law that governs every node in an electrical circuit. Step 2 uses the **fundamental definition of capacitance** ($Q = CV$). Steps 3–4 use the **Ohmic conductance model**, treating each ion channel as a variable resistor. Step 5 is a pure summation. Step 6 is a simple algebraic rearrangement to isolate the time derivative, which is the form needed by ODE integrators (Euler, Runge-Kutta) used in MATLAB, Python (NumPy/SciPy), or specialized simulators.

### 3.2 Derivation: Gating Variable Dynamics (First-Order Kinetic Model)

Each gating variable ($m, h, n$) follows a **first-order kinetic equation** representing the probability of the gate being in the "open" or "closed" state. This is a two-state Markov model.

**Step 1 — Define the transition rates.**

Let $\alpha_x(V_m)$ be the **forward rate constant** (closed → open) and $\beta_x(V_m)$ be the **backward rate constant** (open → closed). Both are **voltage-dependent**.

**Step 2 — Write the master kinetic equation.**

The rate of change of gating variable $x$ is:

$$\frac{dx}{dt} = \alpha_x(V_m)(1 - x) - \beta_x(V_m) \cdot x$$

**Step 3 — Factor the equation into the standard HH form.**

Expanding and rearranging:

$$\frac{dx}{dt} = \alpha_x - \alpha_x x - \beta_x x = \alpha_x - (\alpha_x + \beta_x) x$$

**Step 4 — Define the steady-state value $x_\infty$ and time constant $\tau_x$.**

$$x_\infty(V_m) = \frac{\alpha_x(V_m)}{\alpha_x(V_m) + \beta_x(V_m)}$$

$$\tau_x(V_m) = \frac{1}{\alpha_x(V_m) + \beta_x(V_m)}$$

**Step 5 — Rewrite in the canonical HH form:**

$$\frac{dx}{dt} = \frac{x_\infty - x}{\tau_x}$$

This is the **canonical Hodgkin-Huxley form**, used identically in the squid axon model (1952), the Noble Purkinje model (1962), and modern cardiac models.

### 3.3 Derivation: Noble's Specific Rate Constants (1962)

Noble (1962) fitted the following empirical rate constants for the Purkinje fibre at **37°C** (units: ms⁻¹):

**For the Na⁺ activation gate (m):**

$$\alpha_m = \frac{-0.10 (V_m + 50)}{e^{-(V_m+50)/10} - 1}$$

$$\beta_m = 4.0 \, e^{-(V_m+75)/18}$$

**For the Na⁺ inactivation gate (h):**

$$\alpha_h = 0.07 \, e^{-(V_m+75)/20}$$

$$\beta_h = \frac{1.0}{e^{-(V_m+45)/10} + 1}$$

**For the K⁺ activation gate (n):**

$$\alpha_n = \frac{-0.0007 (V_m + 50)}{e^{-(V_m+50)/10} - 1}$$

$$\beta_n = 0.05 \, e^{-(V_m+75)/80}$$

**For the inward rectifier (Noble's $i_{K_1}$ component), the conductance is not purely voltage-gated by $n^4$; it has a more complex formulation:**

$$g_{K_1} = \bar{g}_{K_1} \left[ \frac{[K^+]_o}{[K^+]_{o,ref}} \right]^{0.5} \cdot f(V_m)$$

Where $f(V_m)$ is an empirically fitted function that produces the characteristic **inward rectification** (high conductance at hyperpolarised potentials, very low conductance at depolarised potentials).

**Step 6 — Substituting the rate constants into the gate ODEs:**

$$\frac{dm}{dt} = \alpha_m(1-m) - \beta_m m$$

$$\frac{dh}{dt} = \alpha_h(1-h) - \beta_h h$$

$$\frac{dn}{dt} = \alpha_n(1-n) - \beta_n n$$

This gives a system of **4 coupled, nonlinear ODEs** (one for $V_m$, one each for $m, h, n$) that fully describes the Noble Purkinje fibre model.

### 3.4 Step-by-Step Numerical Simulation Procedure

To simulate one cardiac action potential using the Noble model:

**Step 1 — Initialise state variables:**
$V_m(0) = -90$ mV (resting potential), $m(0) = m_\infty(-90)$, $h(0) = h_\infty(-90)$, $n(0) = n_\infty(-90)$.

**Step 2 — Apply a brief stimulus** ($I_{stim} \approx 10$ μA/cm² for 1–2 ms) to trigger an AP.

**Step 3 — Integrate the 4 ODEs** using a stable numerical method (e.g., Forward Euler with $\Delta t = 0.01$ ms, or Runge-Kutta 4th order with adaptive step size):

$$V_m^{k+1} = V_m^k + \Delta t \cdot \frac{1}{C_m}\left[ I_{stim}^k - I_{ionic}^k \right]$$

$$m^{k+1} = m^k + \Delta t \cdot \frac{m_\infty^k - m^k}{\tau_m^k}$$

(and similarly for $h, n$).

**Step 4 — Continue integration for ≈ 400–500 ms** until the cell returns to rest and the AP waveform is fully captured.

**Step 5 — Plot $V_m$ vs. time** — the output is the cardiac Purkinje fibre action potential with its characteristic rapid upstroke, plateau, and repolarisation phases.

### 3.5 Analytical Summary Matrix: Comparison of Action Potential Types

| Feature | Squid Axon (HH) | Cardiac Ventricular | Skeletal Muscle |
|---|---|---|---|
| Duration | **1–2 ms** | **200–400 ms** | 2–5 ms |
| Plateau (Phase 2) | Absent | **Present (≈ 200 ms)** | Absent |
| Resting $V_m$ | −70 mV | **−90 mV** | −80 mV |
| Main depolarising ion | Na⁺ | **Na⁺ (Phase 0), Ca²⁺ (Phase 2)** | Na⁺ |
| Main repolarising ion | K⁺ | **K⁺ (Phase 3)** | K⁺ |
| Refractory period | Short | **Long (= AP duration)** | Short |
| Tendency to tetanus | No (short AP) | **No (long ARP prevents it)** | Yes (summation possible) |
| Na⁺ channel reactivation kinetics | Fast | **Slow (delayed recovery)** | Fast |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The Cardiac Conduction System (Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[SA Node - Sinoatrial Node - 70-80 bpm] -->|Internodal Pathways| B[Bachmanns Bundle - Atrial Conduction]
    B --> C[AV Node - 100 ms Delay]
    C --> D[Bundle of His - AV Bundle]
    D --> E[Right Bundle Branch]
    D --> F[Left Bundle Branch]
    E --> G[Purkinje Fibres - RV Endocardium]
    F --> H[Purkinje Fibres - LV Endocardium]
    G --> I[Ventricular Myocardium - RV Contraction]
    H --> J[Ventricular Myocardium - LV Contraction]
    
    A1[Heart Rate: 70-80 bpm] -.-> A
    A2[Conduction Velocity: 1 m/s] -.-> C
    A3[Conduction Velocity: 2-4 m/s] -.-> G
    A4[Conduction Velocity: 0.3-0.5 m/s] -.-> I
    
    style A fill:#ff6b6b,stroke:#c92a2a,color:#fff
    style C fill:#ffa94d,stroke:#d9480f,color:#fff
    style G fill:#4dabf7,stroke:#1971c2,color:#fff
    style H fill:#4dabf7,stroke:#1971c2,color:#fff
    style I fill:#51cf66,stroke:#2f9e44,color:#fff
    style J fill:#51cf66,stroke:#2f9e44,color:#fff
```

### 4.2 The Five Phases of the Cardiac Ventricular Action Potential

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P4[Phase 4 - Resting Potential - -90 mV - iK1 dominates] -->|Stimulus| P0
    P0[Phase 0 - Rapid Depolarisation - iNa influx - +30 mV] --> P1
    P1[Phase 1 - Early Repolarisation - Ito - Cl - Notch] --> P2
    P2[Phase 2 - Plateau - iCaL balanced by iK - 200-300 ms] --> P3
    P3[Phase 3 - Rapid Repolarisation - iK efflux - Back to -90 mV] --> P4
    
    P0 -.->|Na+ channels inactivate| P1
    P2 -.->|Ca2+ channels inactivate| P3
    
    style P4 fill:#dee2e6,stroke:#495057
    style P0 fill:#ff8787,stroke:#c92a2a,color:#fff
    style P1 fill:#ffd8a8,stroke:#d9480f
    style P2 fill:#74c0fc,stroke:#1971c2,color:#fff
    style P3 fill:#b197fc,stroke:#5f3dc4,color:#fff
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Architecture of the Noble Model Simulation System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Membrane Patch - Cardiac Purkinje Cell] --> B[Membrane Capacitance - Cm = 1 uF per cm2]
    A --> C[iNa - Fast Sodium Current]
    A --> D[iK - Delayed Rectifier Potassium]
    A --> E[iK1 - Inward Rectifier Potassium]
    A --> F[iL - Leak Current]
    A --> G[Istim - External Stimulus]
    
    C --> C1[Gate m - Activation - fast]
    C --> C2[Gate h - Inactivation - slow]
    D --> D1[Gate n - Activation - slow]
    
    C1 --> H[Gating ODE Solver - Euler or RK4]
    C2 --> H
    D1 --> H
    H --> I[State Variables - Vm, m, h, n]
    I --> J[Membrane Equation - 4 Coupled ODEs]
    J --> K[Action Potential Waveform Output]
    
    style A fill:#ffe066,stroke:#fab005
    style B fill:#a5d8ff,stroke:#1971c2
    style C fill:#ff8787,stroke:#c92a2a,color:#fff
    style D fill:#b197fc,stroke:#5f3dc4,color:#fff
    style E fill:#b197fc,stroke:#5f3dc4,color:#fff
    style J fill:#51cf66,stroke:#2f9e44,color:#fff
    style K fill:#ffd8a8,stroke:#d9480f
```

### 4.4 The ECG Waveform and Its Cellular Origin

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Atrial Depolarisation] -->|P wave - small upward| B[PR Segment - AV node delay]
    B --> C[QRS Complex - Ventricular Depolarisation]
    C --> D[ST Segment - Plateau Phase 2]
    D --> E[T wave - Ventricular Repolarisation]
    E --> F[TP Segment - Diastole - Resting]
    
    C -.->|Q - first downward| C
    C -.->|R - tall upward spike| C
    C -.->|S - downward below baseline| C
    
    style A fill:#a5d8ff,stroke:#1971c2
    style C fill:#ff8787,stroke:#c92a2a,color:#fff
    style E fill:#b197fc,stroke:#5f3dc4,color:#fff
    style F fill:#dee2e6,stroke:#495057
```

### 4.5 Ionic Channel Kinetics Hierarchy in the Noble Model

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Noble Model - 4 Coupled ODEs] --> B[Vm ODE - Voltage Equation]
    A --> C[m ODE - Na+ Activation]
    A --> D[h ODE - Na+ Inactivation]
    A --> E[n ODE - K+ Activation]
    
    B --> B1[Inputs - m, h, n from previous step]
    B1 --> B2[Compute iNa, iK, iL]
    B2 --> B3[Update Vm]
    B3 --> B
    
    C --> C1[Compute alpha_m, beta_m from Vm]
    C1 --> C2[Update m]
    C2 --> B
    
    D --> D1[Compute alpha_h, beta_h from Vm]
    D1 --> D2[Update h]
    D2 --> B
    
    E --> E1[Compute alpha_n, beta_n from Vm]
    E1 --> E2[Update n]
    E2 --> B
```
