## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks = 6 Marks)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define the cardiac action potential. List its five phases with the dominant ionic current in each phase.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Definition:** The cardiac action potential is the transient change in transmembrane potential of a cardiac myocyte during depolarisation and repolarisation, lasting 200–400 ms in ventricular cells.

**Five Phases:**

| Phase | Name | Dominant Ionic Current | Approximate Voltage |
|---|---|---|---|
| Phase 0 | Rapid depolarisation | Fast Na⁺ influx ($i_{Na}$) | −90 mV → +30 mV |
| Phase 1 | Early repolarisation | Transient outward K⁺ ($I_{to}$) and Cl⁻ currents | +30 mV → 0 mV |
| Phase 2 | Plateau | L-type Ca²⁺ influx ($i_{Ca-L}$) balanced by K⁺ efflux ($i_K$) | ≈ 0 mV (sustained) |
| Phase 3 | Rapid repolarisation | Delayed rectifier K⁺ ($i_{K}$) | 0 mV → −90 mV |
| Phase 4 | Resting potential | Inward rectifier K⁺ ($i_{K_1}$) | −90 mV (stable) |

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Explain the role of the AV node in the cardiac conduction system. What is its conduction delay and why is it physiologically important?**

**Model Answer:**

The **AV node** is located at the base of the right atrium near the interatrial septum. It is the **only electrical pathway** between the atria and the ventricles, conducting the impulse from the atria to the Bundle of His.

**Conduction Delay: ≈ 100 ms** (ranges from 50–120 ms depending on autonomic tone).

**Physiological Importance:**
1. The delay allows the atria to **fully contract and complete ventricular filling** before the ventricles contract — maximising cardiac output by the Frank-Starling mechanism.
2. It acts as a **protective filter**, preventing excessively rapid atrial rates (e.g., during atrial fibrillation/flutter) from being conducted 1:1 to the ventricles, which would cause haemodynamic collapse.
3. It serves as a **secondary pacemaker** (junctional escape rhythm at 40–60 bpm) if the SA node fails.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** The AV node delay is analogous to a **traffic signal at an intersection** — it temporarily holds traffic to allow safe, sequential flow, preventing collisions (i.e., simultaneous atrial and ventricular contraction, which would be fatal).

### 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (ESE Module Choice — 14 Marks)

---

**Question A (14 Marks):**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question Paper Style]**

**(a)** With a neat labelled diagram, explain the various components of the cardiac conduction system. List the conduction velocities at each segment. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the membrane equation of the Hodgkin-Huxley / Noble model, identifying all ionic currents and their gating variables. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**(a) Cardiac Conduction System — Diagram and Components (7 marks):**

*(Refer to the Mermaid diagram in Section 4.1 for the flow structure.)*

| Component | Location | Conduction Velocity | Function |
|---|---|---|---|
| **SA Node** | Upper RA near SVC | ≈ 0.05 m/s | Primary pacemaker (70–80 bpm) |
| **Atrial Myocardium** | Atrial walls | ≈ 1 m/s | Atrial depolarisation (P wave) |
| **AV Node** | Lower RA, interatrial septum | **≈ 0.05 m/s** | 100 ms delay, secondary pacemaker |
| **Bundle of His** | Interventricular septum | ≈ 1 m/s | Sole AV conduction pathway |
| **Bundle Branches** | Septal sides | ≈ 2 m/s | Route impulse to apex |
| **Purkinje Fibres** | Subendocardial | **≈ 2–4 m/s** | Fastest conduction, ventricular activation |
| **Ventricular Myocardium** | Ventricular walls | ≈ 0.3–0.5 m/s | QRS complex, contraction |

**Significance of velocity gradient:** The slow AV node → fast His-Purkinje sequence ensures **atria contract first → delay → ventricles contract from apex upwards**, optimising ejection.

**(b) Derivation of the Noble/HH Membrane Equation (7 marks):**

*(Refer to the full derivation in Section 3.1.)*

**Key Final Equation:**

$$\frac{dV_m}{dt} = \frac{1}{C_m}\left[ I_{stim} - \bar{g}_{Na} m^3 h (V_m - E_{Na}) - \bar{g}_K n^4 (V_m - E_K) - \bar{g}_L (V_m - E_L) \right]$$

**Gating equations (canonical HH form):**

$$\frac{dx}{dt} = \frac{x_\infty - x}{\tau_x}, \quad x \in \{m, h, n\}$$

**Key Points to Mention in Exam:**
1. Cable equation from KCL.
2. Each ion channel modelled as a variable conductance in series with a Nernst battery.
3. Na⁺ current governed by **$m^3 h$** (3 activation gates, 1 inactivation gate).
4. K⁺ current governed by **$n^4$** (4 activation gates, no inactivation in the original Noble model).
5. Gates follow first-order voltage-dependent kinetics with $x_\infty$ and $\tau_x$.

---

**Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice:**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the electrocardiogram (ECG) waveform in detail. List all waves, intervals, and their normal durations, along with their cellular origin. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the features and significance of the **Noble (1962) model** of the cardiac Purkinje fibre. What were Noble's key contributions beyond the Hodgkin-Huxley framework? **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**(a) ECG Waveform — Detailed Explanation (7 marks):**

*(Refer to the ECG diagram in Section 4.4.)*

| Component | Duration | Cellular Origin | Clinical Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| **P wave** | < 120 ms | Atrial depolarisation (RA → LA) | Atrial enlargement if wide/tall |
| **PR interval** | 120–200 ms | SA → AV nodal conduction | 1st-degree AV block if > 200 ms |
| **QRS complex** | < 120 ms | Ventricular depolarisation (septum → apex → walls) | Bundle branch blocks if > 120 ms |
| **ST segment** | 80–120 ms | All ventricular cells in **Phase 2 plateau** (isoelectric) | Elevation → MI; depression → ischaemia |
| **T wave** | ≈ 160 ms | Ventricular repolarisation (Phase 3) | Inverted in ischaemia; peaked in hyperkalaemia |
| **QT interval** | 350–440 ms | Total ventricular electrical activity (dep + rep) | Prolonged in Long QT syndrome (risk of Torsades) |
| **U wave** | (Optional) | Purkinje fibre repolarisation or papillary muscle | Hypokalaemia if prominent |

**Einthoven's Triangle (3 Standard Limb Leads):**
- **Lead I:** LA (+) − RA (−); records leftward vector
- **Lead II:** LL (+) − RA (−); records inferior vector (most commonly used for rhythm)
- **Lead III:** LL (+) − LA (−); records inferior vector
- Relationship: **II = I + III** (Einthoven's Law)

**Vector Concept:** The ECG is the projection of the **cardiac vector** (the net dipole of all depolarised cells) onto the lead axis.

**(b) Noble (1962) Model — Features and Significance (7 marks):**

**Origin and Motivation:**
- Published in **1962** in *Journal of Physiology* by **Denis Noble** (Oxford University).
- Extended the **Hodgkin-Huxley (1952)** framework from the squid giant axon to the **cardiac Purkinje fibre** — the heart's high-speed conduction tissue.
- The Purkinje AP was chosen because its waveform was the most experimentally characterised cardiac AP at the time.

**Mathematical Form (summarised):**
$$C_m \frac{dV_m}{dt} = I_{stim} - i_{Na} - i_K - i_L$$

Where the K⁺ current was **decomposed into two components** (Noble's critical insight):
- $i_{K_2}$: a **delayed-rectifier-type** current (later identified as $i_{K_r}$ and $i_{K_s}$)
- $i_{K_1}$: an **inward-rectifier** current responsible for maintaining the resting potential

**Key Contributions Beyond Hodgkin-Huxley:**

1. **Two-component K⁺ current:** Showed for the first time that cardiac K⁺ currents are **heterogeneous** with distinct voltage- and time-dependence.

2. **Inward rectification ($i_{K_1}$):** Demonstrated that $i_{K_1}$ conductance is **high at hyperpolarised potentials** (maintaining rest) and **low at depolarised potentials** (enabling the plateau phase). This was a major departure from HH, which had only a single outward K⁺ current.

3. **Plateau reproduction:** First model to mathematically reproduce the **long cardiac plateau** (~200 ms) by balancing inward Ca²⁺-like currents with outward K⁺ currents.

4. **Repolarisation dynamics:** The $n^4$ K⁺ activation gate with its slow kinetics produced realistic **Phase 3 repolarisation**.

5. **Foundation for all subsequent cardiac models:** LU-Rudy, DiFrancesco-Noble (1985), Beeler-Reuter (1977), Luo-Rudy (1991, 1994), ten Tusscher (2004), and O'Hara-Rudy (2011) all descend from Noble 1962.

**Historical Impact:** The Noble model was the **first biophysically detailed computer simulation of a cardiac cell** and helped launch the field of **cardiac electrophysiology simulation**. Modern applications include the **FDA's CiPA (Comprehensive in vitro Proarrhythmia Assay)** initiative, which uses Noble-descended models to predict drug-induced arrhythmia risk.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Common Mistake #1:** Students often write the Hodgkin-Huxley **squid axon** equations ($i_Na = \bar{g}_{Na} m^3 h (V_m - E_{Na})$) and present them as the **Noble model** without noting the **decomposition of $i_K$ into $i_{K_1}$ + $i_{K_2}$**. This loses 2–3 marks. Always state the two-component K⁺ structure explicitly.
> 2. **Common Mistake #2:** In the ECG question, students often confuse **PR interval** (start of P to start of QRS) with **PR segment** (end of P to start of QRS). The interval is the **clinical measurement**; the segment is the isoelectric line.
> 3. **Common Mistake #3:** When asked to "derive" the membrane equation, students often jump to the final form without showing KCL, capacitance definition, and Ohmic substitution. Each of these is a **mandatory step** worth marks.
> 4. **Common Mistake #4:** Confusing **$E_K$** (Nernst potential, a constant) with **$g_K$** (conductance, a variable). Always differentiate between the **driving force** $(V_m - E_{ion})$ and the **conductance** $g_{ion}$.
> 5. **Common Mistake #5:** Failing to mention that the **AV node delay (100 ms)** is the **only delay in the conduction system** — failing to explain its physiological purpose (complete atrial emptying before ventricular systole) is a recurring error.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Heart anatomy:** 4 chambers (RA, RV, LA, LV); 4 valves; **myocardium** is the contractile layer; **endocardium** is the inner lining; **epicardium** is the outer layer.
- **Conduction system order:** **SA node → Atria → AV node (100 ms delay) → Bundle of His → Bundle branches → Purkinje fibres → Ventricular myocardium.**
- **SA node is the primary pacemaker** at 70–80 bpm. AV node is the secondary pacemaker at 40–60 bpm. Purkinje fibres are the tertiary at 20–40 bpm.
- **Purkinje fibre conduction velocity: 2–4 m/s** (fastest in the heart).
- **ECG waves (mnemonic: "Pee QRS T"):** **P wave** (atrial dep), **QRS** (vent dep), **T wave** (vent rep). The **atrial repolarisation wave is hidden inside the QRS**.
- **Einthoven's Law:** **Lead II = Lead I + Lead III**.
- **Cardiac AP duration: 200–400 ms** (vs. 1–2 ms in neurons) due to the **Phase 2 plateau** sustained by L-type Ca²⁺ channels.
- **Long absolute refractory period prevents tetanic contraction** of the heart — critical for the heart's pump function.
- **Noble (1962) model is the first biophysical cardiac model**, based on the Hodgkin-Huxley (1952) framework.
- **Master Noble equation:** $C_m \frac{dV_m}{dt} = I_{stim} - \bar{g}_{Na} m^3 h (V_m - E_{Na}) - \bar{g}_K n^4 (V_m - E_K) - \bar{g}_L (V_m - E_L)$.
- **Three ionic currents in original Noble:** $i_{Na}$ (fast Na⁺), $i_K$ (K⁺ delayed rectifier + inward rectifier components), $i_L$ (leak).
- **Three gating variables:** $m$ (Na⁺ activation, fast), $h$ (Na⁺ inactivation, slow), $n$ (K⁺ activation, slow).
- **Gating ODE canonical form:** $\frac{dx}{dt} = \frac{x_\infty - x}{\tau_x}$.
- **Nernst equation:** $E_{ion} = 61.5 \log_{10}([ion]_o / [ion]_i)$ mV at 37°C.
- **Standard values:** $E_{Na} \approx +40$ mV, $E_K \approx -94$ mV, $E_L \approx -25$ to $-50$ mV, $C_m = 1$ μF/cm².
- **Phase 0 is driven by $i_{Na}$; Phase 2 by $i_{Ca-L}$ + $i_K$; Phase 3 by $i_K$; Phase 4 by $i_{K_1}$.**
- **Inward rectification of $i_{K_1}$** is the biophysical basis for both the **resting potential** and the **plateau** — a Nobel-worthy insight by Noble.
- **Numerical simulation requires solving 4 coupled, nonlinear ODEs** using Euler or Runge-Kutta methods with $\Delta t \approx 0.01$ ms.
- **Real-world applications:** Drug safety (CiPA, hERG block), pacemaker design, defibrillator waveform engineering, AI/ML arrhythmia detection.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT865 - MODELLING OF PHYSIOLOGICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Noble Models, Reduced Cell Models, Mathematical Models of Cardiac Cell Arrangements: The Bidomain and Monodomain Models

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Noble Models (Cardiac Cellular Electrophysiology)

The **Noble Models** refer to a family of mathematical models of the cardiac action potential developed by **Denis Noble** beginning in **1962** (Purkinje fibre model) and subsequently refined in 1975, 1985, and 2002. These models are direct descendants of the **Hodgkin–Huxley (HH) framework** of 1952, but explicitly tailored for cardiac myocytes by incorporating additional ionic currents such as the **sodium–calcium exchange current**, **inward rectifier potassium current (I_K1)**, and a more accurate description of the **slow calcium current (I_s)**. The **Noble 1962 model** was the first mathematical model of a cardiac cell and is famous for correctly predicting the **long plateau phase** of the cardiac action potential — a feature that the original HH model failed to capture for neurons.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme High-Yield Concept:** The Noble 1962 model was a *paradigm shift* in cardiac electrophysiology. The key innovation was the introduction of an **anomalous (inward) rectifier K⁺ current** and a **Na⁺/Ca²⁺ exchange mechanism**, which together produce the long refractory plateau typical of ventricular and Purkinje cells.

## 1.2 Reduced Cell Models (Simplified Ionic Formulations)

A **Reduced Cell Model** is a lower-dimensional simplification of a high-fidelity ionic model. It uses fewer state variables (membrane voltage plus a small number of gating/recovery variables) while still reproducing the essential waveform of the action potential. Classic reduced cell models include:

- **FitzHugh–Nagumo (1961, 1962)** — a 2-variable simplification of the HH system.
- **McAllister–Noble–Tsien (1975)** — a 7-variable reduction of the Noble 1962 Purkinje model.
- **Beeler–Reuter (1977)** — 8 variables for ventricular myocytes.
- **Luo–Rudy Phase I (1991)** & **Luo–Rudy Phase II (1994)** — progressively more detailed ventricular models.
- **Courtemanche–Nattel–Vigmond (1998)** — atrial cell model.
- **Modern reduced models**: O'Hara–Rudy (2011) for human ventricle.

The mathematical hallmark of a reduced model is a smaller **state-space dimension** $n$, which yields faster integration: 
- **Full Noble 1962**: 4 state variables
- **Luo–Rudy II**: 12+ state variables
- **FitzHugh–Nagumo**: only **2** state variables

## 1.3 Mathematical Models of Cardiac Cell Arrangements

When cardiac myocytes are arranged in tissue, single-cell models must be coupled through a **continuum field description**. The two principal continuum frameworks are:

### (a) The Bidomain Model
The **bidomain model** (Tung, 1978; Geselowitz & Miller, 1973) treats cardiac tissue as **two interpenetrating but continuous domains**:

- **Intracellular domain** (the cytoplasm of all myocytes, with anisotropic conductivity tensor $\sigma_i$)
- **Extracellular domain** (the interstitial space, with anisotropic conductivity tensor $\sigma_e$)

A thin **sarcolemmal membrane** separates the two domains. Electrical coupling occurs via **gap junctions** (modeled in the intracellular domain) and through the membrane current $I_m$.

### (b) The Monodomain Model
The **monodomain model** is a mathematically simpler special case of the bidomain model in which the two conductivity tensors are assumed to be **proportional** ($\sigma_i = k \cdot \sigma_e$, where $k$ is a scalar constant). It is computationally cheaper but still captures anisotropic propagation in cardiac tissue.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy (Intuitive Overview):** Imagine a building with thousands of water tanks (the cardiac cells). The *Noble/Reduced cell models* describe the behaviour of **one tank** — how water flows in and out through its pipes. The *Bidomain model* describes **two interconnected pipe networks** in the whole building (one network inside the tanks, one outside), and the *Monodomain model* is the special case where the two pipe networks have the same shape — so we can collapse them into a single equivalent network and solve the problem much faster.

### 1.4 Why These Models Matter in Engineering

- **Defibrillator design** — requires accurate bidomain simulations to predict virtual electrode polarization.
- **ECG and body-surface potential mapping** — the bidomain model is the gold standard for forward simulation.
- **Cardiac arrhythmias** — re-entrant circuits and spiral waves in 2D/3D monodomain simulations explain tachycardia and fibrillation.
- **Drug safety pharmacology (CiPA initiative)** — reduced cell models are used for *in silico* drug screening on hERG channel effects.
- **Cardiac image-based electrophysiology** — patient-specific bidomain models personalize ablation therapy.

---

## 1.5 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics

| Symbol | Quantity | Standard Value |
|:------:|:---------|:---------------|
| $C_m$ | Membrane specific capacitance | **$\mathbf{1.0 \ \mu F/cm^2}$** |
| $\chi$ | Surface-to-volume ratio | **$\mathbf{2000 \ cm^{-1}}$** |
| $\sigma_i$ | Intracellular conductivity | **$\mathbf{0.17 \ S/m}$ (longitudinal) / 0.019 S/m (transverse)** |
| $\sigma_e$ | Extracellular conductivity | **$\mathbf{1.2 \ S/m}$ (longitudinal) / 0.24 S/m (transverse)** |
| $R_i$ | Intracellular axial resistivity | **$\mathbf{0.2 \ k\Omega \cdot cm}$** |
| $R_e$ | Extracellular axial resistivity | **$\mathbf{0.2 \ k\Omega \cdot cm}$** |
| $V_{rest}$ | Resting membrane potential | **$\mathbf{-85 \ mV}$** (Purkinje) / $\mathbf{-90 \ mV}$ (ventricular) |
| $V_{peak}$ | Peak action potential | **$\mathbf{+30 \ mV}$** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners frequently test these values. Memorize $C_m$ and $\chi$ — they appear in nearly every KTU 2024 scheme question on the bidomain equation.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Noble 1962 Model — Detailed Ionic Structure

The Noble 1962 model is a 4-variable Hodgkin–Huxley-type formulation:

$$C_m \frac{dV}{dt} = -(I_{Na} + I_K + I_{K1} + I_{leak}) + I_{stim}$$

where each ionic current follows the **GHK (Goldman–Hodgkin–Katz) voltage equation** or an Eyring-rate formulation. The four state variables are:

| Variable | Meaning |
|:--------:|:--------|
| $V$ | Membrane potential (mV) |
| $m$ | Na⁺ activation gate (fast) |
| $h$ | Na⁺ inactivation gate (slow) |
| $n$ | K⁺ activation gate |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why was the 1962 model revolutionary?** The original Hodgkin–Huxley model produced a brief action potential (~1 ms) characteristic of squid axon. Noble's insight was to introduce the **inward (anomalous) rectifier $I_{K1}$**, which is dominant at potentials negative to $-60$ mV. This current strongly stabilizes the resting potential but deactivates during depolarization, thereby **prolonging repolarization** and creating the **plateau** observed in cardiac cells.

## 2.2 Reduced Cell Models — Mathematical Construction

The general strategy of model reduction is to project the high-dimensional state space onto a low-dimensional manifold spanned by a few *aggregated* variables. Mathematically:

$$\frac{dV}{dt} = \mathcal{F}(V, w_1, w_2, \dots, w_n) \ \longrightarrow \ \frac{dV}{dt} = \mathcal{G}(V, w)$$

### 2.2.1 FitzHugh–Nagumo (2-Variable Reduced Model)

$$\frac{dV}{dt} = V - \frac{V^3}{3} - w + I_{stim}$$

$$\frac{dw}{dt} = \epsilon (V + \alpha - \beta w)$$

where $V$ is the membrane potential (analogous to $V_m$), $w$ is a slow recovery variable (analogous to $K^+$ gating), and $\epsilon \ll 1$ separates the time scales.

### 2.2.2 McAllister–Noble–Tsien Reduction (1975)

The MNT 1975 model reduced the full Noble 1962 dynamics to a tractable form by replacing the fast $Na^+$ current with an instantaneous activation function $m_\infty(V)$ and combining the $K^+$ currents. The result is a 7-variable model widely used in Purkinje fiber simulations.

## 2.3 The Bidomain Model — Theoretical Foundation

### 2.3.1 Assumptions
1. The cardiac tissue is treated as a **continuum** (length scales $\gg$ cellular dimensions).
2. The intracellular and extracellular spaces are **interpenetrating** and everywhere present.
3. The **sarcolemmal membrane** is treated as a thin 2D interface.
4. Quasi-static approximation: $\nabla \times \mathbf{E} = 0 \Rightarrow \mathbf{E} = -\nabla \phi$.

### 2.3.2 Governing Equations

Let $V_i$ and $V_e$ be the intracellular and extracellular potentials. Define the **transmembrane potential** $V_m = V_i - V_e$.

Applying **Ohm's law** in each domain:

$$\mathbf{J}_i = -\sigma_i \nabla V_i \quad \text{(intracellular current density)}$$

$$\mathbf{J}_e = -\sigma_e \nabla V_e \quad \text{(extracellular current density)}$$

Applying **current conservation** (no net accumulation in the bulk) and accounting for the membrane current leaving the intracellular space:

$$\nabla \cdot \mathbf{J}_i = -I_m \quad \text{and} \quad \nabla \cdot \mathbf{J}_e = I_m$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The opposite signs arise because current leaving the intracellular domain enters the extracellular domain across the membrane. $I_m$ is defined as **outward** (from inside to outside).

### 2.3.3 Membrane Current & Ionic Current

The membrane current density is given by:

$$I_m = \chi \left( C_m \frac{dV_m}{dt} + I_{ion}(V_m, \mathbf{w}) \right)$$

where $I_{ion}$ is the **total ionic current density** (sum of all ion channels, pumps, and exchangers), $\chi$ is the surface-to-volume ratio, and $\mathbf{w}$ is the vector of gating variables.

## 2.4 The Monodomain Model — Mathematical Simplification

The bidomain model comprises a **coupled system of two parabolic PDEs**. In the monodomain assumption, we set $\sigma_i = k \sigma_e$ (or equivalently, equal anisotropy ratios $\sigma_{iL}/\sigma_{iT} = \sigma_{eL}/\sigma_{eT}$). The two PDEs decouple, and the result is:

$$\chi \left( C_m \frac{\partial V_m}{\partial t} + I_{ion} \right) = \nabla \cdot \left( \sigma \nabla V_m \right)$$

where $\sigma = \sigma_i \sigma_e / (\sigma_i + \sigma_e)$ is the **effective (harmonic-mean) conductivity tensor**.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|:-:|:--------|:-----------|:------|
| 1 | $V_m = V_i - V_e$ | Definition of transmembrane potential | mV |
| 2 | $C_m \dfrac{dV_m}{dt} + I_{ion} = I_m / \chi$ | Membrane cable equation (cell) | $\mu A / cm^2$ |
| 3 | $\nabla \cdot (\sigma_i \nabla V_i) = -I_m$ | Intracellular bidomain equation | A/m³ |
| 4 | $\nabla \cdot (\sigma_e \nabla V_e) = +I_m$ | Extracellular bidomain equation | A/m³ |
| 5 | $\chi C_m \dfrac{\partial V_m}{\partial t} = \nabla \cdot (\sigma_i \nabla V_m) - \chi I_{ion}$ | Combined bidomain PDE | — |
| 6 | $\chi C_m \dfrac{\partial V_m}{\partial t} = \nabla \cdot \left( \dfrac{\sigma_i \sigma_e}{\sigma_i + \sigma_e} \nabla V_m \right) - \chi I_{ion}$ | Monodomain PDE | — |
| 7 | $I_{ion} = \sum_k g_k (V_m - E_k)$ | Linear ionic current (HH form) | $\mu A/cm^2$ |
| 8 | $g_k = \bar{g}_k \prod_j x_j^{p_j}$ | Conductance with gating variables | mS/cm² |
| 9 | $\sigma_i = \sigma_i^{eff} \mathbf{a}_f \mathbf{a}_f^T + \sigma_i^{cross} (\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{a}_f \mathbf{a}_f^T)$ | Anisotropic conductivity tensor | S/m |
| 10 | $v = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{\chi C_m} \cdot \dfrac{\sigma_{iL} \sigma_{eL}}{\sigma_{iL} + \sigma_{eL}} \cdot \dfrac{\partial V_m}{\partial t} \cdot \dfrac{1}{\partial V_m/\partial x}}$ | Conduction velocity expression | cm/s |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse the sign of $I_m$ in the bidomain equations. Memorize: **$\nabla \cdot (\sigma_i \nabla V_i) = -I_m$** and **$\nabla \cdot (\sigma_e \nabla V_e) = +I_m$**. The signs are determined by *current leaving* vs *entering* each domain.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application |
|:-------|:------------|
| **Implantable cardioverter defibrillators (ICD)** | Bidomain simulations predict extracellular potential gradients and virtual electrode formation during shocks. |
| **Ablation therapy planning** | Patient-specific monodomain models guide catheter placement for atrial fibrillation ablation. |
| **Drug safety screening (CiPA)** | Reduced cell models test hERG channel block by novel pharmaceuticals. |
| **ECG forward problem** | The bidomain model is the gold standard source model for body-surface potential mapping. |
| **Cardiac resynchronization therapy (CRT)** | Coupled electromechanical bidomain–mechanics models optimize biventricular pacing leads. |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Cable Equation to Bidomain Model

### Step 1: Start with a single cell

The classical **Hodgkin–Huxley cable equation** for a cylindrical cell of radius $a$ gives:

$$C_m \frac{\partial V_m}{\partial t} = \frac{1}{2\pi a R_i} \frac{\partial^2 V_m}{\partial x^2} - I_{ion}$$

where $R_i$ is the **axial resistivity** of the cytoplasm (units $\Omega \cdot cm$).

### Step 2: Generalize to a 3D continuum

Replace the second spatial derivative with a **Laplacian**, and express axial resistance in terms of the **conductivity tensor** $\sigma_i$:

$$C_m \frac{\partial V_m}{\partial t} = \frac{1}{\chi} \nabla \cdot (\sigma_i \nabla V_m) - I_{ion}$$

Multiplying both sides by $\chi$:

$$\chi C_m \frac{\partial V_m}{\partial t} = \nabla \cdot (\sigma_i \nabla V_m) - \chi I_{ion} \quad \text{(Equation A)}$$

### Step 3: Apply Kirchhoff's Current Law (KCL) at the membrane

The total membrane current per unit tissue volume equals $-(\nabla \cdot \mathbf{J}_i)$, where $\mathbf{J}_i$ is the intracellular current density. With $\mathbf{J}_i = -\sigma_i \nabla V_i$:

$$\nabla \cdot (\sigma_i \nabla V_i) = -I_m \quad \text{(Equation B)}$$

Similarly, the extracellular domain satisfies:

$$\nabla \cdot (\sigma_e \nabla V_e) = +I_m \quad \text{(Equation C)}$$

### Step 4: Express the membrane current

By definition, the membrane current density is:

$$I_m = \chi C_m \frac{\partial V_m}{\partial t} + \chi I_{ion} \quad \text{(Equation D)}$$

### Step 5: Eliminate $V_i$ using the definition $V_m = V_i - V_e$

Substitute $\nabla V_i = \nabla V_m + \nabla V_e$ into Equation B:

$$\nabla \cdot \left[ \sigma_i \left( \nabla V_m + \nabla V_e \right) \right] = -I_m$$

$$\nabla \cdot (\sigma_i \nabla V_m) + \nabla \cdot (\sigma_i \nabla V_e) = -I_m \quad \text{(Equation E)}$$

Add Equations C and E:

$$\nabla \cdot (\sigma_i \nabla V_m) + \nabla \cdot (\sigma_i \nabla V_e) + \nabla \cdot (\sigma_e \nabla V_e) = 0$$

$$\nabla \cdot (\sigma_i \nabla V_m) + \nabla \cdot \left[ (\sigma_i + \sigma_e) \nabla V_e \right] = 0$$

### Step 6: Solve for $V_e$ in terms of $V_m$ (the elliptic sub-problem)

$$\nabla \cdot \left[ (\sigma_i + \sigma_e) \nabla V_e \right] = -\nabla \cdot (\sigma_i \nabla V_m) \quad \text{(Equation F)}$$

This is an **elliptic PDE** for $V_e$ given $V_m$.

### Step 7: Substitute back to obtain the parabolic PDE for $V_m$

Using Equation D in Equation A (or, equivalently, replacing $I_m$ in Eq. C and combining), we get the full **bidomain parabolic system**:

$$\chi \left( C_m \frac{\partial V_m}{\partial t} + I_{ion}(V_m, \mathbf{w}) \right) = \nabla \cdot (\sigma_i \nabla V_m) + \nabla \cdot (\sigma_i \nabla V_e)$$

subject to the constraint (Equation F).

### Step 8: Reduce to the Monodomain equation under the proportional-conductivity assumption

Assume $\sigma_i = k \sigma_e$ for some scalar $k > 0$. Then:

$$\sigma_i + \sigma_e = (1 + k) \sigma_e \quad \text{and} \quad \sigma_i = k \sigma_e$$

Equation F becomes:

$$\nabla \cdot \left[ (1+k)\sigma_e \nabla V_e \right] = -\nabla \cdot (k \sigma_e \nabla V_m)$$

$$\nabla V_e = -\frac{k}{1+k} \nabla V_m + \text{(harmonic function)}$$

Under appropriate boundary conditions, the harmonic function vanishes, giving $\nabla V_e = -\frac{k}{1+k} \nabla V_m$.

Substitute into Equation E:

$$\nabla \cdot (\sigma_i \nabla V_m) - \frac{k}{1+k} \nabla \cdot (\sigma_i \nabla V_m) = -I_m$$

$$\frac{1}{1+k} \nabla \cdot (\sigma_i \nabla V_m) = -I_m$$

Now use Equation D: $I_m = \chi C_m \partial V_m/\partial t + \chi I_{ion}$:

$$\chi C_m \frac{\partial V_m}{\partial t} = \frac{1}{1+k} \nabla \cdot (\sigma_i \nabla V_m) - \chi I_{ion}$$

With $\sigma_i = k \sigma_e$:

$$\frac{\sigma_i}{1+k} = \frac{k \sigma_e}{1+k} = \frac{\sigma_i \sigma_e}{\sigma_i + \sigma_e}$$

So the **monodomain equation** is:

$$\chi C_m \frac{\partial V_m}{\partial t} = \nabla \cdot \left( \frac{\sigma_i \sigma_e}{\sigma_i + \sigma_e} \nabla V_m \right) - \chi I_{ion}$$

This is the desired result.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Conduction Velocity in the Monodomain Model

The **conduction velocity** $v$ of a propagating action potential wave can be derived using the **cable-theory asymptotic matching** technique.

### Step 1: One-dimensional monodomain equation

For a 1D cable with effective conductivity $\sigma$:

$$\chi C_m \frac{\partial V_m}{\partial t} = \sigma \frac{\partial^2 V_m}{\partial x^2} - \chi I_{ion}(V_m, \mathbf{w})$$

### Step 2: Travelling-wave ansatz

Assume a constant-propagation-speed travelling wave: $V_m = V_m(\xi)$ where $\xi = x - v t$:

$$\frac{\partial V_m}{\partial t} = -v \frac{dV_m}{d\xi}, \quad \frac{\partial^2 V_m}{\partial x^2} = \frac{d^2 V_m}{d\xi^2}$$

Substitute:

$$-\chi C_m v \frac{dV_m}{d\xi} = \sigma \frac{d^2 V_m}{d\xi^2} - \chi I_{ion}$$

### Step 3: Multiply both sides by $dV_m/d\xi$ and integrate

$$-\chi C_m v \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \left(\frac{dV_m}{d\xi}\right)^2 d\xi = \sigma \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \frac{dV_m}{d\xi} \frac{d^2 V_m}{d\xi^2} d\xi - \chi \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} I_{ion} \frac{dV_m}{d\xi} d\xi$$

The second integral on the right integrates to $\frac{1}{2}\sigma \left[(dV_m/d\xi)^2\right]_{-\infty}^{\infty} = 0$ (since $V_m$ is constant at $\pm \infty$).

### Step 4: Solve for $v$

$$v = \frac{-\chi \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} I_{ion}(V_m) \dfrac{dV_m}{d\xi} d\xi}{\chi C_m \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \left(\dfrac{dV_m}{d\xi}\right)^2 d\xi}$$

$$v = \frac{\chi \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} I_{ion}(V_m) dV_m}{\chi C_m \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \left(\dfrac{dV_m}{d\xi}\right)^2 d\xi}$$

This is the **FitzHugh–Rinzel expression** for conduction velocity. Numerically, in the human ventricle $v \approx 0.3 - 0.5$ m/s transversely and $0.6 - 1.0$ m/s longitudinally.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Models

| Property | Noble 1962 | FitzHugh–Nagumo | Luo–Rudy II | Bidomain | Monodomain |
|:---------|:-----------|:----------------|:------------|:---------|:-----------|
| **State variables** | 4 | 2 | 12 | Continuous (∞) | Continuous (∞) |
| **Spatial dimension** | 0 (single cell) | 0–2 | 0 (single cell) | 3D | 3D |
| **Membrane current detail** | $I_{Na}, I_K, I_{K1}, I_{leak}$ | Abstract $(V, w)$ | Full ion channels | Coupled PDEs | Single PDE |
| **Computational cost (relative)** | 1× | 0.3× | 10× | 1000× | 100× |
| **Captures anisotropic conduction** | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ | ✅ |
| **Captures plateau** | ✅ | Approximately | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ |
| **Used in defibrillation studies** | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ | ✅ | Limited |
| **KTU exam relevance** | High | High | Medium | Very High | Very High |

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** Compute the effective monodomain conductivity tensor in a ventricular tissue region where:
- $\sigma_{iL} = 0.17$ S/m, $\sigma_{iT} = 0.019$ S/m
- $\sigma_{eL} = 1.2$ S/m, $\sigma_{eT} = 0.24$ S/m

**Solution:**

The monodomain conductivity is $\sigma_{mono} = \sigma_i \sigma_e / (\sigma_i + \sigma_e)$ computed component-wise:

**Longitudinal component:**

$$\sigma_{mono,L} = \frac{0.17 \times 1.2}{0.17 + 1.2} = \frac{0.204}{1.37} \approx 0.1489 \ \text{S/m}$$

**Transverse component:**

$$\sigma_{mono,T} = \frac{0.019 \times 0.24}{0.019 + 0.24} = \frac{0.00456}{0.259} \approx 0.01761 \ \text{S/m}$$

**Anisotropy ratio:**

$$\rho = \frac{\sigma_{mono,L}}{\sigma_{mono,T}} = \frac{0.1489}{0.01761} \approx 8.45$$

This high anisotropy ratio ($\approx 8:1$) is what gives cardiac tissue its characteristic **fibre-elongated** wave propagation.

**Conduction velocity ratio:**

$$\frac{v_L}{v_T} = \sqrt{\frac{\sigma_{mono,L}}{\sigma_{mono,T}}} = \sqrt{8.45} \approx 2.91$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** Real Purkinje fibres can have anisotropy ratios up to 10:1, while ventricular myocardium typically shows 3:1 to 4:1. The numerical example above therefore represents an extreme case (likely Purkinje tissue).
