topics.title: Ionic Currents and Conductance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT865 - MODELLING OF PHYSIOLOGICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Ionic Currents and Conductance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Ionic Current ($I_{ion}$)** is the net rate of flow of a specific ionic species (Na⁺, K⁺, Ca²⁺, Cl⁻) across the cell membrane through selective ion channels, driven by the electrochemical gradient. It is measured in **Amperes (A)** or more commonly in physiological systems in **microamperes (μA)** or **picoamperes (pA)** for single-channel recordings.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Ionic Conductance ($g_{ion}$)** is a measure of how readily an ion channel permits the flow of ions. It is the reciprocal of resistance ($g = 1/R$) and is expressed in **Siemens (S)**. For a single channel, conductance is typically in **picosiemens (pS)**; for a whole cell, it is in **nanosiemens (nS)** or **microsiemens (μS)**.

The fundamental Ohm's Law analogue for an ion channel is:

$$I_{ion} = g_{ion} \times (V_m - E_{ion})$$

where:
- $V_m$ = membrane potential (mV)
- $E_{ion}$ = Nernst equilibrium/reversal potential for that ion (mV)
- $(V_m - E_{ion})$ = **electrochemical driving force** (mV)

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The Water-Pipe Analogy:** Imagine the cell membrane as a complex plumbing system with selective valves (ion channels). Each valve only lets through a specific type of "water" (ion). The **flow rate** of water is the **ionic current** ($I_{ion}$), and the **ease with which the valve opens** is the **conductance** ($g_{ion}$). A wider, fully-open valve = high conductance = lots of flow. A closed or partially open valve = low conductance = little flow. The **water pressure** difference between the two sides of the pipe is the **driving force** $(V_m - E_{ion})$.

**Plain-English Summary:**
- **Ionic Current** = the actual *amount* of ions moving per second through a channel.
- **Conductance** = the *capacity* of the channel to carry ions — its "permeability scaled by ion mobility."
- Conductance is a property of the **channel**; current depends on **both** the channel AND the driving force.

> [!TIP]
> **Key Insight:** A channel can have a high conductance, but if the driving force is zero (i.e., $V_m = E_{ion}$), the current is zero. Conversely, a small conductance with a large driving force can still produce a substantial current.

---

### 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Reference Values

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Faraday Constant | $F$ | **96,485** | C/mol |
| Gas Constant | $R$ | **8.314** | J/(mol·K) |
| Body Temperature | $T$ | **310** (37°C) | K |
| Room Temp | $T$ | **298** (25°C) | K |
| RT/F @ 37°C | — | **26.73** | mV |
| (RT/zF)·ln(10) @ 37°C | — | **61.5** | mV |
| Typical $E_{Na}$ | — | **+60** | mV |
| Typical $E_K$ | — | **−90** | mV |
| Typical $E_{Cl}$ | — | **−70** | mV |
| Typical $E_{Ca}$ | — | **+120** | mV |
| Resting $V_m$ | — | **−70** | mV |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Decomposition of Ionic Current Generation

The generation of an ionic current can be understood in **four hierarchical steps**:

1. **Electrochemical Gradient Formation**
   - Cells maintain asymmetric ionic concentrations via the Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase pump.
   - This creates both a **chemical concentration gradient** and an **electrical potential gradient**.
   - Combined → **electrochemical gradient**, quantified by the Nernst equation.

2. **Channel Gating (Open Probability)**
   - Voltage-gated channels have **activation gates** (m, n particles) and **inactivation gates** (h particle).
   - The fraction of channels open at any instant is the **open probability** $P_o$.
   - $P_o$ depends on $V_m$ and time.

3. **Single-Channel Conductance**
   - Each individual open channel has a characteristic conductance $g_{single}$ (~1–50 pS).
   - Determined by channel pore geometry and ion selectivity.

4. **Macroscopic Current Summation**
   - Total membrane current = sum over all $N$ channels: $I_{ion} = N \cdot P_o \cdot i_{single}$
   - Where $i_{single} = g_{single}(V_m - E_{ion})$ is the single-channel current.

---

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> This is your **high-yield reference table** for the examination hall. Memorize all entries.

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $I_{ion} = g_{ion}(V_m - E_{ion})$ | Ohmic relation for ionic current | A |
| 2 | $g_{ion} = 1/R_{ion}$ | Conductance = reciprocal of resistance | S |
| 3 | $E_{ion} = \dfrac{RT}{zF}\ln\dfrac{[ion]_o}{[ion]_i}$ | Nernst equation | V |
| 4 | $E_{ion}\vert_{37°C} = \dfrac{61.5}{z}\log_{10}\dfrac{[ion]_o}{[ion]_i}$ | Simplified Nernst (base-10) | mV |
| 5 | $g_{chord} = \dfrac{I_{ion}}{V_m - E_{ion}}$ | Chord conductance (whole I–V slope) | S |
| 6 | $g_{slope} = \dfrac{dI_{ion}}{dV_m}$ | Slope (differential) conductance | S |
| 7 | $I_{Na} = \bar{g}_{Na}\cdot m^3 h \cdot (V_m - E_{Na})$ | HH sodium current | A |
| 8 | $I_K = \bar{g}_K \cdot n^4 \cdot (V_m - E_K)$ | HH potassium current | A |
| 9 | $I_L = \bar{g}_L \cdot (V_m - E_L)$ | HH leak current | A |
| 10 | $I_{total} = N \cdot P_o \cdot g_{single}\cdot (V_m - E_{ion})$ | Microscopic-to-macroscopic link | A |
| 11 | $P_{Na}:P_K = 0.04:1$ (at rest) | Relative permeabilities (squid axon) | — |
| 12 | $V_{rest} \approx \dfrac{g_K E_K + g_{Na} E_{Na} + g_{Cl} E_{Cl}}{g_K + g_{Na} + g_{Cl}}$ | Weighted parallel-conductance model | mV |
| 13 | $\tau_m = \dfrac{1}{\alpha_m + \beta_m}$ | Gating time constant | s |
| 14 | $m_\infty = \dfrac{\alpha_m}{\alpha_m + \beta_m}$ | Steady-state gating variable | — |
| 15 | $\dfrac{dm}{dt} = \alpha_m(1-m) - \beta_m m$ | First-order gating kinetics | 1/s |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Distinguishing Chord vs. Slope Conductance:**
> - **Chord conductance** uses a *fixed reference* ($E_{ion}$): measures how much the channel passes at one $V_m$ value relative to its reversal potential.
> - **Slope conductance** is the *local slope* of the I–V curve: captures rectification (nonlinearity).
> - For a perfectly linear (ohmic) channel, chord = slope everywhere.

---

### 2.3 Engineering & Real-World Utility

> [!TIP]
> **Why this matters beyond the exam hall:**
> - **Neural Prosthetics & Brain-Computer Interfaces:** Electrode-tissue coupling is modeled using ionic conductance concepts.
> - **Pharmacology:** Drug action on ion channels (e.g., lidocaine blocking Na⁺ channels) is quantified by changes in $\bar{g}_{ion}$ or $P_o$.
> - **Cardiac Pacemaker Design:** Understanding how I_f (funny current) and I_K1 set the resting potential is critical for arrhythmia modeling.
> - **Biosensor & Patch-Clamp Engineering:** Amplifier design depends on the pS–nS conductance range.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Nernst Equation from Thermodynamic Equilibrium

**Start Point:** At equilibrium, the electrochemical potential of an ion is equal on both sides of the membrane.

$$\mu_{inside} = \mu_{outside}$$

**Step 1:** Write the electrochemical potential (chemical + electrical):

$$\mu_i^o + RT\ln[C]_i + zF V_i = \mu_i^o + RT\ln[C]_o + zF V_o$$

**Step 2:** The standard chemical potential $\mu_i^o$ cancels (same ion, same phase), and the electrical term moves to the right:

$$RT\ln[C]_i - RT\ln[C]_o = zF(V_o - V_i)$$

**Step 3:** Combine the logs:

$$RT\ln\left(\dfrac{[C]_i}{[C]_o}\right) = -zF(V_m)$$

where $V_m = V_i - V_o$ is the membrane potential (inside relative to outside).

**Step 4:** Solve for $V_m$:

$$V_m = -\dfrac{RT}{zF}\ln\left(\dfrac{[C]_i}{[C]_o}\right) = \dfrac{RT}{zF}\ln\left(\dfrac{[C]_o}{[C]_i}\right)$$

**Step 5:** This equilibrium potential is the **Nernst potential** $E_{ion}$:

$$\boxed{\,E_{ion} = \dfrac{RT}{zF}\ln\left(\dfrac{[ion]_o}{[ion]_i}\right)\,}$$

**Step 6:** Convert to base-10 logarithm and substitute numerical constants for $T = 310$ K:

$$E_{ion} = \dfrac{2.303 \cdot RT}{zF}\log_{10}\left(\dfrac{[ion]_o}{[ion]_i}\right) = \dfrac{61.5}{z}\log_{10}\left(\dfrac{[ion]_o}{[ion]_i}\right)\ \text{mV}$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Instantaneous I–V Relation & Chord Conductance

**Scenario:** A voltage-clamp experiment steps $V_m$ to various test potentials and measures the *instantaneous* (post-capacity-transient) current.

**Step 1:** By definition, the chord conductance from $E_{ion}$ to a test voltage $V$ is:

$$g_{chord}(V) = \dfrac{I_{ion}(V)}{V - E_{ion}}$$

**Step 2:** If the channel behaves ohmically (linear I–V), then $g_{chord}$ is constant and equal to the slope conductance. This is a **signature of an ohmic (non-rectifying) channel**.

**Step 3:** For the Hodgkin-Huxley Na⁺ channel, however, the *instantaneous* I–V is approximately linear (ohmic), but the **peak current** is highly nonlinear due to voltage-dependent activation $m$ and inactivation $h$. Thus:

$$I_{Na,peak} = \bar{g}_{Na}\cdot m_\infty^3(V)\cdot h_\infty(V) \cdot (V - E_{Na})$$

**Step 4:** Define the **voltage-dependent chord conductance** of Na⁺ as:

$$g_{Na}(V) = \dfrac{I_{Na,peak}}{V - E_{Na}} = \bar{g}_{Na}\cdot m_\infty^3(V)\cdot h_\infty(V)$$

**Step 5:** Plotting $g_{Na}(V)$ vs. $V$ gives the **conductance-voltage curve**, a direct measure of channel activation/inactivation. This curve is *bell-shaped* for Na⁺ because $m$ activates and $h$ inactivates with different voltage sensitivities.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Microscopic to Macroscopic Current Linkage

**Step 1:** A single open channel carries a current:

$$i_{single} = g_{single}\cdot (V_m - E_{ion})$$

**Step 2:** If there are $N$ channels in the membrane and the open probability is $P_o$, the expected number of open channels is $N \cdot P_o$.

**Step 3:** The total macroscopic current is the sum over all channels:

$$I_{ion} = (N \cdot P_o) \cdot g_{single} \cdot (V_m - E_{ion})$$

**Step 4:** Define the maximum conductance (all channels open): $\bar{g}_{ion} = N \cdot g_{single}$.

**Step 5:** Therefore:

$$\boxed{\,I_{ion} = \bar{g}_{ion}\cdot P_o \cdot (V_m - E_{ion})\,}$$

**Step 6:** For the Hodgkin-Huxley formalism, $P_o$ is factorized as $m^3 h$ (for Na⁺) or $n^4$ (for K⁺), giving the famous kinetic equations listed in the formula sheet.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Properties of Major Ionic Currents

| Current | Ion | Direction at Rest | Reversal $E_{ion}$ | Gating | Physiological Role |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $I_{Na}$ | Na⁺ | Inward | **+60 mV** | $m^3 h$ (fast) | Action potential upstroke |
| $I_K$ | K⁺ | Outward | **−90 mV** | $n^4$ (slow) | Repolarization |
| $I_{Ca,L}$ | Ca²⁺ | Inward | **+120 mV** | $d \cdot f$ | Cardiac plateau, muscle contraction |
| $I_{Cl}$ | Cl⁻ | Outward | **−70 mV** | (constitutive) | Stabilization of $V_{rest}$ |
| $I_f$ | Na⁺/K⁺ | Inward | **−30 mV** | HCN | Pacemaker depolarization |
| $I_{K1}$ | K⁺ | Outward | **−90 mV** | (inward rectifier) | Maintains resting $V_m$ |

---

### 3.5 Quantitative Numerical Example (KTU-style)

**Problem:** At 37°C, intracellular [K⁺] = 140 mM and extracellular [K⁺] = 5 mM. A single K⁺ channel has $g_{single} = 20$ pS. There are $N = 10^6$ such channels, with $P_o = 0.25$ at $V_m = -40$ mV. Compute the macroscopic $I_K$.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Compute $E_K$ using the simplified Nernst:

$$E_K = \dfrac{61.5}{1}\log_{10}\left(\dfrac{5}{140}\right) = 61.5 \times (-1.447) = -89.0\ \text{mV}$$

**Step 2:** Driving force:

$$V_m - E_K = -40 - (-89) = +49\ \text{mV}$$

**Step 3:** Single-channel current:

$$i_{single} = 20 \times 10^{-12} \times 49 \times 10^{-3} = 0.98\ \text{pA}$$

**Step 4:** Number of open channels:

$$N_{open} = 10^6 \times 0.25 = 2.5 \times 10^5$$

**Step 5:** Total macroscopic current:

$$I_K = 2.5 \times 10^5 \times 0.98 \times 10^{-12} = 0.245\ \text{nA}$$

**Step 6:** Note the **outward** sign (positive) because K⁺ flows out of the cell at this depolarized voltage (driving force is positive inside-out).

$$\boxed{\,I_K \approx 0.245\ \text{nA (outward)}\,}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block: Hierarchical Conductance-Current Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Cell Membrane] --> B[Lipid Bilayer]
    A --> C[Ion Channels]
    A --> D[Pumps and Exchangers]

    C --> E[Na plus Channel]
    C --> F[K plus Channel]
    C --> G[Ca2 plus Channel]
    C --> H[Cl minus Channel]

    E --> E1[g_single 20 pS]
    E --> E2[Open Prob m3h]
    E --> E3[N equals 1e6]
    E1 --> E4[I_Na macroscopic]
    E2 --> E4
    E3 --> E4

    F --> F1[g_single 20 pS]
    F --> F2[Open Prob n4]
    F --> F3[N equals 5e5]
    F1 --> F5[I_K macroscopic]
    F2 --> F5
    F3 --> F5

    G --> G1[g_single 10 pS]
    G --> G2[Open Prob d f]
    G1 --> G6[I_Ca macroscopic]
    G2 --> G6

    H --> H1[g_single 5 pS]
    H1 --> H2[I_Cl macroscopic]

    E4 --> OUT[Total Membrane Current I_m]
    F5 --> OUT
    G6 --> OUT
    H2 --> OUT

    D --> P1[Na K ATPase Pump]
    D --> P2[Na Ca Exchanger]
    P1 --> BG[Ionic Gradients Maintained]
    P2 --> BG
    BG --> E
    BG --> F
    BG --> G
    BG --> H
```

**Interpretation of the Flowchart:**
- The **left branch** (D, P1, P2, BG) shows the *active maintenance* of ionic gradients.
- The **middle branch** (C → E, F, G, H) shows the *passive flow* through selective channels.
- The **right branch** (E1–E4, F1–F5, etc.) shows how microscopic parameters ($g_{single}$, $P_o$, $N$) combine to give the macroscopic current.
- The final aggregation box **OUT** represents the total membrane current $I_m = I_{Na} + I_K + I_{Ca} + I_{Cl} + I_{pump} + I_{leak}$, which feeds into the cable/space-clamp equation of the Hodgkin-Huxley framework.

---

### 4.2 Block Diagram: I–V Relationship and Chord/Slope Conductance Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[V_m Test Voltage] --> B[Apply Step via Voltage Clamp]
    B --> C[Measure Instantaneous Current I_ion]
    C --> D{Is I-V Linear?}
    D -->|Yes| E[Ohmic Channel: g_chord equals g_slope]
    D -->|No| F[Rectifying Channel: g_chord differs from g_slope]
    E --> G[Draw Straight I-V line through E_ion]
    F --> H[Draw Nonlinear I-V curve through E_ion]
    G --> I[Slope equals g_slope equals 1 slash R]
    H --> J[Chord equals I slash V minus E_ion at chosen V]
```

**Interpretation:**
- The geometry of the I–V plot is the most direct visual diagnostic of channel rectification.
- The **reversal potential** $E_{ion}$ is the point where the curve crosses the voltage axis (I = 0).
- For an *inward rectifier* (e.g., $I_{K1}$), the I–V curve is steep for hyperpolarized voltages and shallow for depolarized ones — a hallmark of voltage-dependent block by intracellular Mg²⁺ and polyamines.

---

### 4.3 Signal-Flow Schematic: From Permeability to Macroscopic Current

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Step 1: Define Ion Concentrations] --> S2[Step 2: Compute E_ion via Nernst]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Define Channel Density N and g_single]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: Compute Open Probability P_o at V_m]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5: Evaluate Driving Force V_m minus E_ion]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6: Multiply g_bar times P_o times Driving Force]
    S6 --> S7[Step 7: Obtain I_ion]
    S7 --> S8[Step 8: Sum across all ions to get I_m]
    S8 --> S9[Step 9: Plug into HH Equation C dV slash dt equals minus I_m plus I_stim]
```

**Why this flowchart is high-yield:**
- This is the **exact algorithm** a KTU examiner expects you to follow when solving a problem like "Given the following concentrations and channel densities, find the peak Na⁺ current during a step to +20 mV."

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define ionic conductance and chord conductance. How are they related?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Ionic conductance ($g_{ion}$)** is the reciprocal of the resistance offered by an ion channel to the flow of a specific ionic species; it is a measure of the channel's ability to conduct ions, expressed in Siemens (S) **[1 mark]**.

> **Chord conductance ($g_{chord}$)** is defined as the slope of the line joining the operating point $(V_m, I_{ion})$ to the reversal point $(E_{ion}, 0)$ on the I–V curve: $g_{chord} = I_{ion}/(V_m - E_{ion})$ **[1 mark]**.

> **Relationship:** For an *ohmic* (linear) channel, chord conductance is constant and equal to the slope conductance $dI/dV$. For a *nonlinear* (rectifying) channel, chord conductance varies with $V_m$ and differs from the slope conductance. The chord conductance directly gives the macroscopic current via $I_{ion} = g_{chord}(V_m - E_{ion})$ **[1 mark]**.

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*State the Nernst equation for a univalent ion at 37°C. What is the significance of the Nernst potential?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Nernst equation at 37°C (T = 310 K):**
> $$E_{ion} = \dfrac{61.5}{z}\log_{10}\left(\dfrac{[ion]_o}{[ion]_i}\right)\ \text{mV}$$
> where $z$ is the valence of the ion and $[ion]_o$, $[ion]_i$ are the extracellular and intracellular concentrations **[1 mark]**.

> For a univalent ion ($z = 1$):
> $$E_{ion} = 61.5 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{[ion]_o}{[ion]_i}\right)\ \text{mV}$$ **[1 mark]**.

> **Significance:** The Nernst potential $E_{ion}$ is the membrane voltage at which there is **no net electrochemical driving force** for that ion, i.e., the chemical and electrical gradients are exactly balanced. It serves as the **reversal potential** for the ion's current: at $V_m = E_{ion}$, the current reverses direction. It is a fundamental input to the Hodgkin-Huxley current equation $I_{ion} = g_{ion}(V_m - E_{ion})$ **[1 mark]**.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **QUESTION A (Choice 1) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**(a)** Derive the Nernst equation from thermodynamic principles. Using the Nernst equation, compute the equilibrium potential for K⁺ at 37°C given $[K^+]_o = 4$ mM and $[K^+]_i = 150$ mM. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Explain instantaneous and steady-state ionic currents. A voltage-clamp experiment on a squid axon measures an instantaneous K⁺ current of 0.4 μA at $V_m = +20$ mV and 0.1 μA at $V_m = -40$ mV. Calculate the chord conductance of the K⁺ channel at each voltage and comment on whether the channel is ohmic. Take $E_K = -90$ mV. **[7 marks]**

---

**Model Solution to Q.A(a):**

**Step 1 — Setting up the equilibrium condition:** At electrochemical equilibrium, the Gibbs free energy of the ion is equal on both sides of the membrane **[1 mark]**.

$$\mu_i^o + RT\ln[C]_i + zFV_i = \mu_i^o + RT\ln[C]_o + zFV_o$$

**Step 2 — Simplification:** Standard chemical potentials cancel; rearrange to isolate the electrical potential difference $V_m = V_i - V_o$:

$$RT\ln\left(\dfrac{[C]_i}{[C]_o}\right) = -zFV_m$$ **[1 mark]**

**Step 3 — Final Nernst form:**

$$E_{ion} = V_m = \dfrac{RT}{zF}\ln\left(\dfrac{[ion]_o}{[ion]_i}\right)$$ **[1 mark]**

**Step 4 — Conversion to base-10 logarithm:**

$$E_{ion} = \dfrac{2.303\,RT}{zF}\log_{10}\left(\dfrac{[ion]_o}{[ion]_i}\right) = \dfrac{61.5}{z}\log_{10}\left(\dfrac{[ion]_o}{[ion]_i}\right)\ \text{mV at 37°C}$$ **[1 mark]**

**Step 5 — Numerical substitution for K⁺ ($z = +1$):**

$$E_K = 61.5 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{4}{150}\right) = 61.5 \times \log_{10}(0.02667)$$ **[1 mark]**

**Step 6 — Compute the log:**

$$\log_{10}(0.02667) = -1.574$$

**Step 7 — Final answer:**

$$E_K = 61.5 \times (-1.574) = -96.8\ \text{mV} \approx \mathbf{-97\ \text{mV}}$$ **[2 marks]**

---

**Model Solution to Q.A(b):**

**Step 1 — Definition of instantaneous current:** The *instantaneous* current is the ionic current measured **immediately** after a voltage step (i.e., after capacitive transients have settled but before significant channel gating has occurred). It reflects the *passive* ohmic behavior of the channels that are open at that moment **[1 mark]**.

**Step 2 — Definition of steady-state current:** The *steady-state* current is reached after all gating variables have settled to their asymptotic values ($m_\infty$, $h_\infty$, $n_\infty$). It represents the new equilibrium of channel activation/inactivation at the held voltage **[1 mark]**.

**Step 3 — Compute chord conductance at $V_m = +20$ mV:**

$$g_{chord}(+20) = \dfrac{I_K}{V_m - E_K} = \dfrac{0.4\ \mu A}{(+20) - (-90)} = \dfrac{0.4}{110} = 3.64\ \text{nS}$$ **[1 mark]**

**Step 4 — Compute chord conductance at $V_m = -40$ mV:**

$$g_{chord}(-40) = \dfrac{I_K}{V_m - E_K} = \dfrac{0.1\ \mu A}{(-40) - (-90)} = \dfrac{0.1}{50} = 2.00\ \text{nS}$$ **[1 mark]**

**Step 5 — Comment on ohmicity:** The chord conductances differ (3.64 nS vs. 2.00 nS), so the channel is **NOT purely ohmic** **[1 mark]**.

**Step 6 — Engineering interpretation:** A higher chord conductance at depolarized voltages is consistent with the **delayed-rectifier** behavior of K⁺ channels: the K⁺ conductance activates with depolarization due to the $n$ gate, so more channels are open at +20 mV than at -40 mV. Hence, although the *instantaneous* I–V is the operational definition, even at this fast time scale, the population of open channels varies with voltage **[2 marks]**.

---

#### **QUESTION B (Choice 2) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** With a neat labelled block diagram, explain the microscopic-to-macroscopic current linkage in ion channels. Show that $I_{ion} = \bar{g}_{ion}\cdot P_o \cdot (V_m - E_{ion})$. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** The Hodgkin-Huxley model gives $\bar{g}_{Na} = 120$ mS/cm², $m_\infty = 0.95$, $h_\infty = 0.05$ at $V_m = +30$ mV. Given $E_{Na} = +50$ mV, calculate the peak Na⁺ current density. Also calculate the number of Na⁺ ions entering per second through a 1 cm² patch of membrane. **[7 marks]**

---

**Model Solution to Q.B(a):**

**Step 1 — Define the single-channel current:** Each individual open ion channel passes a current given by Ohm's law:

$$i_{single} = g_{single}\cdot (V_m - E_{ion})$$

where $g_{single}$ is the single-channel conductance (pS range) and $(V_m - E_{ion})$ is the driving force **[1 mark]**.

**Step 2 — Introduce open probability $P_o$:** At any instant, only a fraction $P_o$ of the $N$ total channels are open. The expected number of open channels is $N \cdot P_o$ **[1 mark]**.

**Step 3 — Sum to get macroscopic current:** Since channels operate independently (a key assumption):

$$I_{ion} = N \cdot P_o \cdot g_{single} \cdot (V_m - E_{ion})$$ **[1 mark]**

**Step 4 — Define the maximum conductance:** When all channels are open ($P_o = 1$): $\bar{g}_{ion} = N \cdot g_{single}$ **[1 mark]**.

**Step 5 — Substitute and finalize:**

$$\boxed{\,I_{ion} = \bar{g}_{ion}\cdot P_o \cdot (V_m - E_{ion})\,}$$ **[1 mark]**

**Step 6 — Block diagram explanation (in text form for the model answer):** Draw three boxes in series: *(i) Channel population box* ($N$, $g_{single}$) → *(ii) Gating box* ($P_o$ from kinetic variables $m, h, n$) → *(iii) Driving force multiplier* $(V_m - E_{ion})$ → output $I_{ion}$. This is the standard HH "linear chain" of ionic current generation **[2 marks]**.

---

**Model Solution to Q.B(b):**

**Step 1 — Identify the HH sodium current equation:**

$$I_{Na} = \bar{g}_{Na}\cdot m^3 h \cdot (V_m - E_{Na})$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the steady-state values (at peak activation, $m \to m_\infty$, $h \to h_\infty$):**

$$I_{Na} = 120\ \text{mS/cm}^2 \times (0.95)^3 \times (0.05) \times ((+30) - (+50))\ \text{mV}$$ **[1 mark]**

**Step 3 — Compute the gating factor:**

$$(0.95)^3 \times 0.05 = 0.8574 \times 0.05 = 0.0429$$ **[1 mark]**

**Step 4 — Driving force:**

$$V_m - E_{Na} = +30 - (+50) = -20\ \text{mV}$$ **[1 mark]**

**Step 5 — Final current density:**

$$I_{Na} = 120 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{S/cm}^2 \times 0.0429 \times (-20 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{V})$$

$$I_{Na} = 120 \times 0.0429 \times (-20) \times 10^{-6}\ \text{A/cm}^2$$

$$I_{Na} = -102.96 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{A/cm}^2 \approx \mathbf{-103\ \mu A/cm^2}$$ **[1 mark]**

**Step 6 — Convert to ion flux (ions/second):** Use $I = (dQ/dt) = (zF)(dN/dt)$, so $dN/dt = I / (zF)$:

$$\dfrac{dN}{dt} = \dfrac{103 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{A/cm}^2}{1 \times 96{,}485\ \text{C/mol}} = 1.068 \times 10^{-9}\ \text{mol/(s·cm}^2)$$

**Step 7 — Convert to ions per second using Avogadro's number $N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{23}$:**

$$\dfrac{dN}{dt} = 1.068 \times 10^{-9} \times 6.022 \times 10^{23} = 6.43 \times 10^{14}\ \text{ions/(s·cm}^2)$$ **[1 mark]**

$$\boxed{\,\text{Approximately}\ 6.4 \times 10^{14}\ \text{Na}^+\ \text{ions flow inward per second per cm}^2.\,}$$ **[1 mark]**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Ionic Current Problems:**
> 1. **Sign of driving force:** Students frequently forget that $(V_m - E_{ion})$ can be **negative** (inward current) or **positive** (outward current). Carrying the sign through is essential — losing 1 mark here is the single most common error.
> 2. **Nernst equation base confusion:** Using $\ln$ instead of $\log_{10}$ without applying the 2.303 conversion factor is a guaranteed 2-mark deduction.
> 3. **HH gating exponent mistakes:** Na⁺ uses $m^3 h$ (NOT $m^3 + h$), K⁺ uses $n^4$ (NOT $n$). Always verify which current you are computing.
> 4. **Confusing $\bar{g}$ with $g$:** The bar denotes *maximum* (all channels open) conductance; without the bar, $g$ is the *actual* (voltage-dependent) conductance. Examiners test this distinction explicitly.
> 5. **Units mismatch:** Mixing mS with mV, or S with V, when units have not been normalized (e.g., mS·mV ≠ mA). Always convert to SI (S·V = A) before multiplying.
> 6. **Avogadro constant omission:** When asked for "number of ions," the step $N_A \times \text{moles}$ is mandatory. Skipping it costs 1 mark.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Ionic Currents and Conductance**

- ☐ **Ohm's Law for channels:** $I_{ion} = g_{ion}(V_m - E_{ion})$ — the central equation. Memorize it first.
- ☐ **Nernst equation** gives $E_{ion}$ from concentrations; at 37°C use the **61.5/z** mV shortcut with base-10 log.
- ☐ **Conductance ≠ current:** Conductance is a channel property; current also depends on the driving force.
- ☐ **Chord conductance** is measured from $E_{ion}$ to the operating point; **slope conductance** is the local $dI/dV$.
- ☐ **Inward currents** (Na⁺, Ca²⁺) **depolarize**; **outward currents** (K⁺, Cl⁻) **hyperpolarize or stabilize**.
- ☐ **Hodgkin-Huxley framework:** $I_{Na} = \bar{g}_{Na} m^3 h (V_m - E_{Na})$, $I_K = \bar{g}_K n^4 (V_m - E_K)$.
- ☐ **Microscopic-macroscopic link:** $I = N \cdot P_o \cdot g_{single} \cdot (V_m - E_{ion}) = \bar{g}_{ion} P_o (V_m - E_{ion})$.
- ☐ **Resting potential** is set mainly by $g_K$ and $E_K$ (weighted parallel-conductance model).
- ☐ **Driving force changes sign** at $E_{ion}$ — that is why $E_{ion}$ is called the *reversal potential*.
- ☐ **Convert units carefully:** mS × mV → μA; μA / (zF) → mol/s; mol/s × $N_A$ → ions/s.
- ☐ **Instantaneous** I–V ≈ ohmic; **steady-state** I–V shows the bell-shaped conductance-voltage curve due to gating.
- ☐ **Reversal potential is read off** the I–V curve at $I = 0$, and is the experimental gold-standard for identifying the permeant ion.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Equivalent Circuit Representation of a Patch of Cell Membrane
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT865 - MODELLING OF PHYSIOLOGICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Equivalent Circuit Representation of a Patch of Cell Membrane

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Equivalent Circuit Representation of a Patch of Cell Membrane** is an electrical analogue model in which the biophysical properties of a small, uniform section of the excitable cell membrane are abstracted into a lumped-parameter circuit. In this model:

- The **phospholipid bilayer** is represented as a **parallel-plate capacitor** (membrane capacitance, $C_m$).
- The **voltage-gated ion channels** (Na⁺, K⁺, Ca²⁺, Cl⁻) are represented as **variable conductances** ($g_{Na}, g_K, g_{Cl}, \dots$) in series with their respective **Nernst equilibrium potentials** ($E_{Na}, E_K, E_{Cl}, \dots$) acting as electromotive force (EMF) sources.
- The **leak channels** are represented as a fixed conductance $g_L$ in series with the leak Nernst potential $E_L$.

This representation, formalised by **Hodgkin and Huxley (1952)** for the squid giant axon, reduces the complex biophysics of an excitable membrane into a tractable network that obeys Kirchhoff's Current and Voltage Laws.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** This topic forms the **foundational building block** for the Hodgkin–Huxley framework, cable theory, and the derivation of the action potential. Expect direct questions on circuit topology, the role of $C_m$, and the form of the membrane current equation.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **water tank with a flexible rubber sheet stretched across its middle** (the membrane):

- The **rubber sheet** stores elastic energy when stretched — this is exactly what a capacitor does (stores electrical energy in an electric field). So the bilayer $\rightarrow$ **capacitor**.
- Imagine **small, controllable valves** poked through the rubber sheet. Each valve only allows *one specific ion* (Na⁺, K⁺, Cl⁻) to pass — these are the **ion channels** $\rightarrow$ **variable resistors/conductances**.
- Behind each valve is a **water-pressure difference** that pushes the ions across. This is the electrochemical gradient — represented as a **battery** (the Nernst potential).
- All these elements are arranged **in parallel** because they all sit across the same membrane patch.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Reference Metrics (Mammalian Cell Membrane):**
> - Specific membrane capacitance: $C_m \approx \mathbf{1\ \mu F/cm^2}$
> - Specific membrane resistance: $R_m \approx \mathbf{1000\ \Omega\cdot cm^2}$ (resting)
> - Resting membrane potential: $V_m \approx \mathbf{-70\ mV}$
> - Nernst potentials: $E_K \approx -90\ mV$, $E_{Na} \approx +60\ mV$, $E_{Cl} \approx -65\ mV$

### 1.3 Why This Model Works (Biophysical Justification)

The lipid bilayer is **virtually impermeable** to charged ions, but it is an excellent **dielectric** (insulator) sandwiched between two conductive ionic solutions (intracellular and extracellular fluids). This geometry is *textbook* for a parallel-plate capacitor. Embedded within this dielectric are **transmembrane proteins** (ion channels) that provide selective ionic pathways — modelled as resistors. The driving force for ion flow is the electrochemical gradient, modelled as a battery.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Construction of the Equivalent Circuit

**Step 1 — The Capacitive Element ($C_m$):**
The bilayer has thickness $d \approx 5\text{–}7\ \text{nm}$ and dielectric constant $\kappa \approx 3$ (for phospholipids). It separates two electrolytic solutions of conductivity $\sigma_i$ and $\sigma_o$. The resulting specific capacitance is:

$$C_m = \frac{\kappa \varepsilon_0}{d} \approx 1\ \mu F/cm^2$$

**Step 2 — The Ionic Current Branches (in parallel with $C_m$):**
For each ion type $X$, a branch is added consisting of:
- A **variable conductance** $g_X(t, V_m)$ representing channel gating.
- An **EMF source** $E_X$ (the Nernst potential) in series.

**Step 3 — The Leak Branch ($g_L$, $E_L$):**
A lumped, voltage-independent branch accounts for all non-gated pathways and imperfect selectivity.

**Step 4 — Kirchhoff's Current Law at the Node:**
The total transmembrane current $I_m$ splits into a **capacitive (displacement) current** and a sum of **ionic currents**.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Quantity** | **Symbol** | **Equation** | **Standard Value / Unit** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Membrane Capacitance | $C_m$ | $C_m = \kappa\varepsilon_0 / d$ | $1\ \mu F/cm^2$ |
| Ionic Current (ion $X$) | $I_X$ | $I_X = g_X (V_m - E_X)$ | $\mu A/cm^2$ |
| Nernst Potential | $E_X$ | $E_X = (RT/zF)\ln([X]_o/[X]_i)$ | mV |
| Total Membrane Current | $I_m$ | $I_m = C_m dV_m/dt + \sum_X I_X$ | $\mu A/cm^2$ |
| Resting Potential (GHK) | $V_{rest}$ | via Goldman equation | $\approx -70$ mV |
| Membrane Time Constant | $\tau_m$ | $\tau_m = R_m \cdot C_m$ | $1\text{–}10$ ms |
| Total Conductance | $g_{total}$ | $g_{total} = \sum_X g_X + g_L$ | mS/cm² |
| Membrane Resistance | $R_m$ | $R_m = 1/g_{total}$ | $\Omega\cdot cm^2$ |
| Driving Force | $D_X$ | $D_X = V_m - E_X$ | mV |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall Avoidance:** In the formula table, never confuse the driving force direction. The current $I_X$ flows *outward* (efflux) when $V_m > E_X$ for a cation, and *inward* when $V_m < E_X$ for $K^+$. Sign convention: **outward current is positive** (Hodgkin convention).

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

This model is the **cornerstone of neural engineering**:

1. **Neural Prosthetics & Cochlear Implants:** Circuit simulations of neurons require faithful membrane equivalents to predict stimulation thresholds.
2. **ECG / EEG Source Modelling:** Cardiac and neural field potentials are simulated using cable networks built from these patch equivalents.
3. **Pharmacology & Drug Discovery:** In silico channel blockers are tested by modulating $g_X$ in the circuit.
4. **Brain-Computer Interfaces (BCIs):** Electrode–tissue interface design uses these RC models to predict signal attenuation.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Membrane Capacitance from Physical Geometry

We treat the membrane as a parallel-plate capacitor of area $A$, plate separation $d$, and dielectric constant $\kappa$.

**Step 1:** Capacitance of a parallel plate capacitor:

$$C = \frac{\kappa \varepsilon_0 A}{d}$$

This is the standard textbook form of a parallel-plate capacitance.

**Step 2:** To get a *specific* (per unit area) capacitance, divide both sides by $A$:

$$C_m = \frac{C}{A} = \frac{\kappa \varepsilon_0}{d}$$

Dividing by $A$ converts the expression into the more useful per-area form.

**Step 3:** Substituting standard values: $\kappa \approx 3$, $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}\ F/m$, $d \approx 6\ nm = 6 \times 10^{-9}\ m$:

$$C_m = \frac{3 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12}}{6 \times 10^{-9}} = 4.427 \times 10^{-3}\ F/m^2$$

$$C_m \approx 0.01\ F/m^2 = 1\ \mu F/cm^2$$

The unit conversion $1\ F/m^2 = 10^{-4}\ F/cm^2 = 10^{-4} \times 10^{6}\ \mu F/cm^2$ yields $1\ \mu F/cm^2$.

### 3.2 Derivation: Nernst Equation (Battery EMF)

For an ion $X$ of charge $z$ at concentrations $[X]_i$ (inside) and $[X]_o$ (outside):

**Step 1:** Electrochemical equilibrium demands equal electrochemical potentials:

$$\mu_i + zFV_o = \mu_o + zFV_i \quad \Rightarrow \quad zF(V_i - V_o) = RT \ln\frac{[X]_o}{[X]_i}$$

Equating chemical plus electrical work on both sides gives the fundamental relation.

**Step 2:** Defining the Nernst potential $E_X = V_i - V_o$ (inside relative to outside):

$$\boxed{E_X = \frac{RT}{zF}\ln\frac{[X]_o}{[X]_i}}$$

This is the **reversal potential** at which no net ionic current flows through a perfectly selective channel.

**Step 3:** At body temperature $T = 310\ K$ (37 °C), $\frac{RT}{F} \approx 26.7\ mV$:

$$E_X \approx \frac{26.7}{z}\log_{10}\frac{[X]_o}{[X]_i}\ mV$$

Conversion from natural log to base-10 log introduces the factor $1/\ln 10$.

### 3.3 Derivation: The Total Membrane Current Equation

Applying Kirchhoff's Current Law at the inside of the membrane patch:

**Step 1:** Total current $I_m$ supplied externally equals the sum of all currents leaving the intracellular node:

$$I_m = I_C + I_{Na} + I_K + I_L$$

This is a direct application of KCL at the membrane node.

**Step 2:** Substituting the element equations:

$$I_m = C_m \frac{dV_m}{dt} + g_{Na}(V_m - E_{Na}) + g_K(V_m - E_K) + g_L(V_m - E_L)$$

Each ionic current is a linear (Ohmic) function of driving force, scaled by the appropriate conductance.

**Step 3:** Rearranging into the canonical Hodgkin–Huxley form:

$$\boxed{C_m \frac{dV_m}{dt} = -\left[g_{Na}(V_m - E_{Na}) + g_K(V_m - E_K) + g_L(V_m - E_L)\right] + I_m}$$

The minus sign on the ionic sum reflects the convention that ionic *outflow* opposes the externally injected current.

### 3.4 Derivation: Membrane Time Constant

At rest, the membrane behaves approximately as a passive RC parallel network.

**Step 1:** Replace the parallel combination of ionic conductances by an equivalent resting conductance $g_{rest} = 1/R_m$:

$$I_m = C_m \frac{dV_m}{dt} + \frac{V_m - V_{rest}}{R_m}$$

All three ionic branches collapse into a single linear resistor in parallel with the capacitor.

**Step 2:** Apply a small step current pulse of amplitude $I_0$ and let $\Delta V = V_m - V_{rest}$:

$$C_m \frac{d(\Delta V)}{dt} + \frac{\Delta V}{R_m} = I_0$$

The shift $\Delta V$ is governed by a first-order linear ODE.

**Step 3:** The homogeneous solution decays exponentially with the **membrane time constant**:

$$\boxed{\tau_m = R_m C_m}$$

The solution of the first-order ODE is $\Delta V(t) = I_0 R_m (1 - e^{-t/\tau_m})$, identifying $\tau_m = R_m C_m$ as the characteristic decay time.

**Step 4:** Numerical estimate: $R_m = 1000\ \Omega\cdot cm^2$, $C_m = 1\ \mu F/cm^2$:

$$\tau_m = 10^3 \times 10^{-6} = 10^{-3}\ s = 1\ ms$$

This shows that mammalian membranes integrate inputs on a millisecond timescale — a fundamental property of neuronal signalling.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Equivalent Circuit of a Membrane Patch

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeA[Intracellular Fluid] --> nodeB[Membrane Capacitor Cm]
    nodeC[Extracellular Fluid] --> nodeB
    nodeA --> nodeD[Variable Conductance gNa]
    nodeC --> nodeE[Nernst Battery ENa]
    nodeD --- nodeE
    nodeA --> nodeF[Variable Conductance gK]
    nodeC --> nodeG[Nernst Battery EK]
    nodeF --- nodeG
    nodeA --> nodeH[Leak Conductance gL]
    nodeC --> nodeI[Nernst Battery EL]
    nodeH --- nodeI
    nodeB -.parallel.-> nodeD
    nodeB -.parallel.-> nodeF
    nodeB -.parallel.-> nodeH
    nodeA --> nodeJ[Injected Current Im]
    nodeC --> nodeJ
```

**Interpretation of the schematic:**

- The **top horizontal rail** represents the **extracellular fluid** (taken as ground).
- The **bottom rail** represents the **intracellular fluid** (at potential $V_m$).
- The **four vertical branches** between the rails are:
  1. **$C_m$** (membrane capacitor — lipid bilayer).
  2. **$g_{Na} + E_{Na}$** (sodium channel branch).
  3. **$g_K + E_K$** (potassium channel branch).
  4. **$g_L + E_L$** (leak branch).
- The injected current $I_m$ is applied between intra- and extracellular nodes.

### 4.2 Functional Architecture Flow — Current Partitioning in the Membrane

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start([Injected Current Im]) --> kcl{KCL at Membrane Node}
    kcl --> icap[Capacitive Branch: Ic = Cm dVm/dt]
    kcl --> ion1[Sodium Branch: INa = gNa Vm - ENa]
    kcl --> ion2[Potassium Branch: IK = gK Vm - EK]
    kcl --> ion3[Leak Branch: IL = gL Vm - EL]
    icap --> charge[Charges Membrane Capacitor]
    ion1 --> nflux[Na+ Ion Flux Across Channel]
    ion2 --> kflux[K+ Ion Flux Across Channel]
    ion3 --> lflux[Passive Leak Flux]
    charge --> vout([Membrane Voltage Vm t])
    nflux --> vout
    kflux --> vout
    lflux --> vout
```

This flowchart visualises how the externally injected current $I_m$ is *partitioned* by Kirchhoff's Current Law into four parallel pathways, and how each pathway contributes to either the time-derivative of $V_m$ (capacitive) or the steady-state ion fluxes (resistive).

### 4.3 Equivalent Circuit Reduction — From Multi-Branch to Two-Element RC

```mermaid
graph LR
    orig[Multi-Branch Equivalent] --> red1[Sum Ionic Conductances]
    red1 --> red2[Combine Nernst Batteries]
    red2 --> final[Single RC Parallel Network]
    final --> r1[Equivalent Resistance Rm]
    final --> c1[Capacitance Cm Unchanged]
    style orig fill:#f9e79f
    style final fill:#a9dfbf
```

This block diagram captures the **circuit reduction principle**: the entire multi-branch membrane circuit can be reduced, at any *instant* in time, to a **simple parallel RC** with $R_m = 1/\sum_X g_X$ and $C_m$ unchanged.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PEEBT865]**
*What is the biophysical basis for representing the lipid bilayer of a cell membrane as a capacitor in an equivalent circuit?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The lipid bilayer of the cell membrane consists of two layers of phospholipid molecules (thickness $d \approx 5\text{–}7\ \text{nm}$, dielectric constant $\kappa \approx 3$) sandwiched between two conductive electrolyte solutions (intracellular and extracellular fluids). This geometry is identical to a **parallel-plate capacitor**, with the lipid core acting as the dielectric. Since ions cannot cross the hydrophobic core, charge accumulation occurs on either side when a voltage is applied, leading to a measurable capacitance of about $C_m \approx 1\ \mu F/cm^2$, calculated from $C_m = \kappa\varepsilon_0/d$. This capacitance stores electrical energy and accounts for the **displacement current** during changes in membrane potential. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, PEEBT865]**
*In the equivalent circuit of a membrane patch, what does the variable conductance $g_{Na}$ represent physically, and what is the role of the battery $E_{Na}$ in series with it?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **$g_{Na}$ (variable conductance):** Physically represents the **collective openness of voltage-gated sodium channels** embedded in the membrane patch. It is *time- and voltage-dependent* because the channels open/close in response to changes in $V_m$ (activation and inactivation gates). Mathematically, $g_{Na} = \bar{g}_{Na}\, m^3 h$, where $m$ and $h$ are gating variables. **(1.5 marks)**
- **$E_{Na}$ (battery):** Represents the **Nernst equilibrium potential for Na⁺** (about +60 mV), the reversal potential at which no net Na⁺ current flows. It encodes the **electrochemical driving force** for Na⁺ across the membrane. The product $g_{Na}(V_m - E_{Na})$ gives the actual ionic current. **(1.5 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full ESE Module Question (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, PEEBT865, Module 3]**

**(a)** Draw the complete equivalent circuit representation of a small patch of excitable cell membrane, clearly labelling all elements (capacitance, ionic conductances, Nernst batteries). State the **physical origin** of each element. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Starting from Kirchhoff's Current Law, derive the **total membrane current equation** for the circuit drawn in part (a). Identify each term and state its physical meaning. **(7 marks)**

##### **Model Solution (a) — Circuit Diagram & Origin of Elements (7 marks)**

**Diagram (to be drawn in exam):**

```
         (Extracellular, ground)
              |     |     |     |     |
             Cm   gNa   gK    gL    Im
              |     |     |     |     |
         (Intracellular, Vm)
```

Each ionic branch contains the variable conductance *in series* with its Nernst battery.

**Tabular Justification (5 marks for the table content + 2 marks for the circuit):**

| **Element** | **Physical Origin** |
|---|---|
| $C_m$ (Capacitor) | Phospholipid bilayer acting as dielectric between two ionic solutions |
| $g_{Na}$ (Variable Resistor) | Collective conductance of voltage-gated Na⁺ channels |
| $E_{Na}$ (Battery) | Nernst potential for Na⁺ (electrochemical gradient) |
| $g_K$ (Variable Resistor) | Collective conductance of voltage-gated K⁺ channels |
| $E_K$ (Battery) | Nernst potential for K⁺ |
| $g_L$ (Fixed Resistor) | Non-gated "leak" channels (Cl⁻, others) |
| $E_L$ (Battery) | Reversal potential of the leak current |
| $I_m$ (Current Source) | Externally injected stimulus current |

All four parallel branches ($C_m, g_{Na}\!-\!E_{Na}, g_K\!-\!E_K, g_L\!-\!E_L$) are connected between the intra- and extracellular nodes. **(7 marks)**

##### **Model Solution (b) — Derivation of Membrane Current Equation (7 marks)**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Apply Kirchhoff's Current Law at the intracellular node (the only node where the four branches meet):

$$I_m = I_C + I_{Na} + I_K + I_L$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Substitute the constitutive relation for each element. The capacitor obeys $I_C = C_m\, dV_m/dt$. Each ionic branch gives $I_X = g_X(V_m - E_X)$ (Ohm's law in the Hodgkin convention):

$$I_m = C_m \frac{dV_m}{dt} + g_{Na}(V_m - E_{Na}) + g_K(V_m - E_K) + g_L(V_m - E_L)$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Rearrange to the canonical Hodgkin–Huxley form by moving the ionic currents to the right-hand side:

$$C_m \frac{dV_m}{dt} = -\left[g_{Na}(V_m - E_{Na}) + g_K(V_m - E_K) + g_L(V_m - E_L)\right] + I_m$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Identify each term:
- $C_m\, dV_m/dt$: **displacement (capacitive) current** — charges/discharges the membrane.
- $g_{Na}(V_m - E_{Na})$: Na⁺ ionic current; driving force is $V_m - E_{Na}$.
- $g_K(V_m - E_K)$: K⁺ ionic current; driving force is $V_m - E_K$.
- $g_L(V_m - E_L)$: leak current; passive.
- $I_m$: externally applied current.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students frequently **drop the negative sign** when rearranging, or **omit the leak term** ($g_L, E_L$). Full marks require all four branches explicitly and the correct sign convention. Also, do **not** confuse $I_m$ (current source) with $C_m$ (capacitance) — they have entirely different units.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

**[KTU University Exam – May 2023, PEEBT865, Module 3]**

**(a)** Define the **Nernst equation** and derive the Nernst potential for K⁺ given $[K^+]_i = 140\ mM$ and $[K^+]_o = 5\ mM$ at $T = 310\ K$. Use $R = 8.314\ J/(mol\cdot K)$, $F = 96,485\ C/mol$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is the **membrane time constant**? Derive it from first principles using the equivalent circuit, and compute its value for a typical mammalian axon ($R_m = 2000\ \Omega\cdot cm^2$, $C_m = 1\ \mu F/cm^2$). Briefly comment on its physiological significance. **(7 marks)**

##### **Model Solution (a) — Nernst Equation (7 marks)**

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Definition. The Nernst equation gives the **reversal potential** of a permeable ion — the membrane voltage at which there is no net ionic flux due to a balance between chemical (concentration) and electrical gradients.

$$E_X = \frac{RT}{zF} \ln \frac{[X]_o}{[X]_i}$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Substitute numerical values for K⁺ ($z = +1$, $[K^+]_o = 5\ mM$, $[K^+]_i = 140\ mM$, $T = 310\ K$):

$$E_K = \frac{8.314 \times 310}{1 \times 96485} \ln\frac{5}{140}$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Evaluate:

$$E_K = \frac{2577.34}{96485} \times \ln(0.03571)$$

$$E_K = 0.02671\ V \times (-3.332)$$

$$E_K \approx -0.0890\ V = -89.0\ mV$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Result $E_K \approx \mathbf{-89\ mV}$, which is close to the standard textbook value of $-90$ mV. **(7 marks)**

##### **Model Solution (b) — Membrane Time Constant (7 marks)**

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Definition. The membrane time constant $\tau_m$ is the characteristic time over which the membrane voltage responds (charges or discharges) to a step current input. It is the product of the membrane resistance and capacitance.

**Step 2 (3 marks):** Derivation. Apply a small step current $I_0$ to the simplified parallel-RC equivalent (sum of all ionic conductances = $1/R_m$):

$$I_0 = C_m \frac{dV_m}{dt} + \frac{V_m}{R_m}$$

The solution to this first-order linear ODE is:

$$V_m(t) = I_0 R_m \left(1 - e^{-t/\tau_m}\right)$$

with the characteristic decay time:

$$\boxed{\tau_m = R_m C_m}$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Numerical computation:

$$\tau_m = 2000 \times 1 \times 10^{-6} = 2 \times 10^{-3}\ s = \mathbf{2\ ms}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Physiological significance. The time constant sets the **temporal resolution** of the neuron's response — shorter $\tau_m$ means faster signalling, while a longer $\tau_m$ enables temporal summation of synaptic inputs. **(7 marks)**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Common mistakes in part (a): (i) **swapping $[X]_o$ and $[X]_i$** in the log argument; (ii) using the wrong **sign of $z$**; (iii) forgetting to convert from $V$ to $mV$ in the final step. In part (b), students often confuse the time constant $\tau_m$ with the **length constant $\lambda$** — they are *not* the same. $\lambda$ depends on the axon's axial and membrane resistances and determines spatial spread, while $\tau_m$ governs temporal spread.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Membrane as parallel RC network** with multiple parallel branches.
- **$C_m \approx 1\ \mu F/cm^2$** is a near-universal constant across cell types.
- **Four canonical branches**: $C_m$, Na⁺ branch, K⁺ branch, leak branch — all in **parallel**.
- **Nernst equation** $E_X = (RT/zF)\ln([X]_o/[X]_i)$ gives each ionic battery.
- **Ionic current** $I_X = g_X(V_m - E_X)$ — conductance $\times$ driving force.
- **Total current equation**: $I_m = C_m dV_m/dt + \sum_X g_X(V_m - E_X)$.
- **Sign convention**: outward current is **positive** (Hodgkin convention).
- **Time constant** $\tau_m = R_m C_m$ — sets the speed of voltage change.
- **Driving force** changes sign at $V_m = E_X$ — this is the basis of **anode-break excitation** and **current reversal** experiments.
- **Cable theory** (Module 3 extension): adds the **axial resistance** $r_i$ along the intracellular core, leading to the **length constant** $\lambda = \sqrt{R_m / r_i}$ and the **cable equation**:

$$\tau_m \frac{\partial V}{\partial t} = \lambda^2 \frac{\partial^2 V}{\partial x^2} - V$$

- **Exam heuristic**: Whenever the question asks to "draw and explain the equivalent circuit", always include (i) the diagram, (ii) the **table mapping each element to its biophysical origin**, and (iii) the **KCL-based equation** — this trio almost guarantees full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Resting Membrane Potential
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT865 - MODELLING OF PHYSIOLOGICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Resting Membrane Potential

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Resting Membrane Potential (RMP)** is the steady-state, time-invariant electrical potential difference that exists across the plasma membrane of a living cell when the cell is **not** actively generating an action potential or receiving synaptic input. By convention, it is defined as the intracellular potential relative to the extracellular potential (ground), i.e., $V_m = V_i - V_o$.

For a typical mammalian neuron, the standard accepted value is **−70 mV** (i.e., the cytoplasm is electrically negative with respect to the extracellular fluid). For a squid giant axon (the classical Hodgkin–Huxley preparation), the value is approximately **−60 mV to −75 mV**.

The RMP arises from three coupled physico-chemical mechanisms:
1. **Asymmetric transmembrane ion concentration gradients** maintained by the $\text{Na}^+/\text{K}^+$-ATPase pump.
2. **Differential membrane permeability** to specific ionic species (selectivity of ion channels).
3. **The electrochemical driving force** acting on each permeant ion.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Constants for the Human Body (T = 37 °C = 310 K):**
> - Universal Gas Constant, $R = 8.314 \; \text{J} \cdot \text{mol}^{-1} \cdot \text{K}^{-1}$
> - Faraday's Constant, $F = 96{,}485 \; \text{C} \cdot \text{mol}^{-1}$
> - Nernst Slope Factor, $\dfrac{2.303 \, RT}{F} = 61.5 \; \text{mV}$ (at 37 °C)
> - Body Temperature, $T = 310 \; \text{K}$

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Ion Battery"

Imagine the cell membrane as a **selectively permeable concrete dam** between two reservoirs of water containing dissolved salts:
- The **upper reservoir (intracellular space)** has a high concentration of $\text{K}^+$ ions and large impermeable anions (proteins, organic phosphates).
- The **lower reservoir (extracellular space)** is rich in $\text{Na}^+$ and $\text{Cl}^-$.
- The dam wall contains **small sluice gates (ion channels)** that let $\text{K}^+$ leak out easily, $\text{Cl}^-$ somewhat, and $\text{Na}^+$ only poorly.
- A **hydraulic pump (the $\text{Na}^+/\text{K}^+$-ATPase)** constantly pumps $\text{K}^+$ back uphill and $\text{Na}^+$ back out, sustaining the imbalance.

The result is a **standing pressure difference (voltage)** across the dam wall — this is the resting membrane potential. Just as a hydraulic battery stores potential energy, the cell stores electrochemical potential energy in its ion gradients, ready to drive currents during an action potential.

## 1.3 Why RMP is Foundational for Physiological Modelling

RMP is the **DC operating point** of every excitable cell. All subsequent bioelectric phenomena — the action potential, synaptic transmission, sensory transduction, cardiac excitation, and electroencephalographic (EEG) signals — are *deviations* from this baseline. In the **equivalent circuit model**, RMP is the sum of ionic Nernst batteries weighted by their conductances, plus the direct electrogenic contribution of the pump.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Operational Layers of RMP

### Layer 1 — The Concentration Gradients (Established by the Pump)
The $\text{Na}^+/\text{K}^+$-ATPase actively transports **3 $\text{Na}^+$ out** and **2 $\text{K}^+$ in** per ATP hydrolyzed. This is a **electrogenic** pump (net export of +1 charge per cycle), directly contributing a small hyperpolarizing component.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Typical Mammalian Neuronal Ion Concentrations (mM):**
> | Ion | Intracellular | Extracellular |
> |---|---|---|
> | $\text{K}^+$ | 140 | 5 |
> | $\text{Na}^+$ | 10 | 145 |
> | $\text{Cl}^-$ | 4 | 120 |
> | $\text{Ca}^{2+}$ | 0.0001 | 1.2 |
> | $\text{A}^-$ (proteins) | 138 | 0 |

The $\text{K}^+$ gradient is ~28-fold outward, the $\text{Na}^+$ gradient is ~14.5-fold inward, and the $\text{Cl}^-$ gradient is ~30-fold inward. **All three ions are far from electrochemical equilibrium** at RMP.

### Layer 2 — Selective Permeability (Channels as Tunable Resistors)
At rest, the membrane is most permeable to $\text{K}^+$ (leak channels), moderately to $\text{Cl}^-$, and very poorly to $\text{Na}^+$. Typical resting permeability ratios (squid axon):

$$P_K : P_{Na} : P_{Cl} = 1.0 : 0.04 : 0.45$$

Because $\text{K}^+$ permeability dominates, the resting potential is "pulled" toward the $\text{K}^+$ Nernst potential.

### Layer 3 — The Electrochemical Driving Force
For each ion, the net driving force is the difference between the membrane voltage and the ion's equilibrium potential:

$$\text{Driving Force}_X = V_m - E_X$$

- For $\text{K}^+$: $-70 - (-90) = +20$ mV → $\text{K}^+$ tends to **leave** the cell.
- For $\text{Na}^+$: $-70 - (+60) = -130$ mV → $\text{Na}^+$ tends to **enter** strongly.
- For $\text{Cl}^-$: $-70 - (-70) = 0$ mV → $\text{Cl}^-$ is **at equilibrium** in many neurons.

The steady state is reached when charge separation (membrane capacitance) produces a field that exactly balances the diffusive leak — i.e., **net ionic current = 0**.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All equations below are absolutely essential for KTU ESE 2024 questions on RMP. Memorize the symbols, signs, and physiological numerical values.

| # | Equation | Description | Key Variables |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E_X = \dfrac{2.303 \, RT}{zF} \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{[X]_o}{[X]_i}\right)$ | **Nernst Equation** — equilibrium potential of ion $X$ | $z$ = valence, $[X]_o$, $[X]_i$ = extra/intra conc. |
| 2 | $E_X = -\dfrac{2.303 \, RT}{zF} \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{[X]_i}{[X]_o}\right)$ | Alternate form (same result) | — |
| 3 | $\dfrac{2.303 \, RT}{F} \bigg\vert_{T=310\,K} = 61.5 \; \text{mV}$ | **Nernst slope** at body temperature | Universal constant |
| 4 | $J_X = -D \dfrac{d[X]}{dx} - \dfrac{zF D [X]}{RT} \dfrac{dV}{dx}$ | **Nernst-Planck Equation** — flux under gradient + field | $D$ = diffusivity, $J$ = flux (mol·m⁻²·s⁻¹) |
| 5 | $V_m = \dfrac{RT}{F} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{P_K[K^+]_o + P_{Na}[Na^+]_o + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_i}{P_K[K^+]_i + P_{Na}[Na^+]_i + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_o}\right)$ | **Goldman-Hodgkin-Katz (GHK) Voltage Equation** | $P_X$ = permeability |
| 6 | $\dfrac{[K^+]_i}{[K^+]_o} = \dfrac{[Cl^-]_o}{[Cl^-]_i} = r$ | **Donnan Equilibrium** ratio | For impermeant anions on inside |
| 7 | $E_{Donnan} = \dfrac{RT}{F} \ln r$ | Donnan potential | Same form as Nernst for monovalent |
| 8 | $I_{pump} = \dfrac{1}{3} I_{Na}^{leak}$ | Pump current balance (steady state) | Electrogenic condition |
| 9 | $V_m = \dfrac{g_K E_K + g_{Na} E_{Na} + g_{Cl} E_{Cl}}{g_K + g_{Na} + g_{Cl}}$ | **Chord-conductance form** of GHK | $g$ = chord conductance |
| 10 | $C_m = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d} \approx 1 \; \mu F/\text{cm}^2$ | **Membrane capacitance** | $d$ ~ 5–7 nm bilayer |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mnemonic for Nernst Sign Convention:**
> - Inside negative ion ($\text{K}^+$) → ratio $\dfrac{[X]_o}{[X]_i} < 1$ → log negative → $E_X$ negative.
> - Inside positive ion ($\text{Na}^+$) → ratio > 1 → log positive → $E_X$ positive.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Application of RMP Modelling |
|---|---|
| **Medical Devices** | ECG, EEG, EMG acquisition; cardiac defibrillators; cochlear & retinal implants |
| **Pharmacology** | Patch-clamp screening of cardio/neurotoxic drug candidates on ion channels |
| **Neural Engineering** | Deep brain stimulators; computational models (Hodgkin–Huxley, FitzHugh–Nagumo) |
| **Biosensors** | Ion-selective electrodes; bioimpedance spectroscopy for tissue characterization |
| **Tissue Engineering** | Designing scaffolds with controlled membrane potentials for organoid maturation |
| **AI/ML in Medicine** | Feature extraction from RMP dynamics for seizure prediction and arrhythmia detection |

The RMP, in essence, is the **bioelectric DC bias** of life — a critical reference point without which no electrophysiological measurement or model has meaning.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Nernst Equation (from Electrochemical Potential)

### Step 1 — Define the Electrochemical Potential
For an ion $X$ with charge $z$, the **electrochemical potential** in an aqueous phase is:

$$\overline{\mu}_X = \mu^0_X + RT \ln [X] + z F V$$

where $\mu^0_X$ is the standard chemical potential, $[X]$ is the molar concentration, and $V$ is the local electric potential. The first term after $\mu^0_X$ captures the **chemical (osmotic) part**, and the second captures the **electrical part**.

### Step 2 — Write the Difference Across the Membrane

$$\Delta \overline{\mu}_X = \overline{\mu}_X^{(i)} - \overline{\mu}_X^{(o)} = RT \ln \frac{[X]_i}{[X]_o} + z F (V_i - V_o)$$

The standard term $\mu^0_X$ cancels because it is the same in both compartments (same solvent, same temperature).

### Step 3 — Apply the Equilibrium Condition
At **electrochemical equilibrium**, there is **no net flow** of ion $X$ across the membrane. Thermodynamically, this means the electrochemical potential difference is zero:

$$\Delta \overline{\mu}_X = 0$$

Hence:

$$RT \ln \frac{[X]_i}{[X]_o} + z F (V_i - V_o) = 0$$

### Step 4 — Solve for the Equilibrium Voltage

$$z F (V_i - V_o) = -RT \ln \frac{[X]_i}{[X]_o}$$

$$E_X \equiv V_i - V_o = -\frac{RT}{zF} \ln \frac{[X]_i}{[X]_o}$$

### Step 5 — Convert Natural Log to Base-10
Using $\ln y = 2.303 \log_{10} y$:

$$\boxed{E_X = -\frac{2.303 \, RT}{zF} \log_{10} \frac{[X]_i}{[X]_o} = +\frac{2.303 \, RT}{zF} \log_{10} \frac{[X]_o}{[X]_i}}$$

This is the **Nernst Equation** (1888). It tells us the membrane voltage at which the diffusive tendency of an ion to move down its concentration gradient is exactly opposed by the electrical drift.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Equilibrium Potentials at 37 °C

Given the standard neuronal concentrations:

| Ion | $[X]_i$ (mM) | $[X]_o$ (mM) | $z$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\text{K}^+$ | 140 | 5 | +1 |
| $\text{Na}^+$ | 10 | 145 | +1 |
| $\text{Cl}^-$ | 4 | 120 | −1 |

### Potassium Equilibrium Potential $E_K$

$$E_K = -\frac{61.5}{+1} \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{140}{5}\right) = -61.5 \times \log_{10}(28)$$

$$\log_{10}(28) = 1.4472$$

$$E_K = -61.5 \times 1.4472 = -89.0 \; \text{mV}$$

### Sodium Equilibrium Potential $E_{Na}$

$$E_{Na} = -61.5 \times \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{10}{145}\right) = -61.5 \times (-1.161)$$

$$E_{Na} = +71.4 \; \text{mV}$$

### Chloride Equilibrium Potential $E_{Cl}$ (note $z = -1$)

$$E_{Cl} = -\frac{61.5}{-1} \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{4}{120}\right) = +61.5 \times \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{4}{120}\right)$$

$$\log_{10}(4/120) = \log_{10}(0.0333) = -1.477$$

$$E_{Cl} = +61.5 \times (-1.477) = -90.8 \; \text{mV}$$

## 3.3 Derivation of the Nernst-Planck Equation

The **Nernst-Planck equation** describes ion flux under simultaneous concentration and electrical gradients in a continuous medium.

### Step 1 — Fick's First Law (Diffusion)

$$J_{\text{diff}} = -D \, \frac{d[X]}{dx}$$

where $D$ is the diffusion coefficient (m²/s). The minus sign indicates flow from high to low concentration.

### Step 2 — Drift Flux due to Electric Field
An ion with charge $z$ in an electric field $E = -dV/dx$ experiences a force $F_{\text{elec}} = -zF \, dV/dx$. The drift velocity is $v = u \, E$ where $u$ is electrophoretic mobility. Drift flux is:

$$J_{\text{drift}} = [X] \cdot v = [X] \cdot u \left(-\frac{dV}{dx}\right) = -u[X] \frac{dV}{dx}$$

### Step 3 — Apply the Nernst-Einstein Relation
This links mobility to diffusivity through thermal energy:

$$D = \frac{u \, RT}{zF} \quad \Rightarrow \quad u = \frac{zF D}{RT}$$

### Step 4 — Substitute $u$ into the Drift Term

$$J_{\text{drift}} = -\frac{zF D}{RT} [X] \frac{dV}{dx}$$

### Step 5 — Sum the Two Contributions (assuming linearity / Nernst-Planck)

$$\boxed{J_X = -D \frac{d[X]}{dx} - \frac{z F D [X]}{RT} \frac{dV}{dx}}$$

This is the **Nernst-Planck equation**. It is the foundation of all electrodiffusion models of membrane transport.

## 3.4 Derivation of the Goldman-Hodgkin-Katz (GHK) Voltage Equation

The GHK equation extends Nernst-Planck to multiple ions using the **constant-field assumption**: the electric field is taken as constant (linear potential drop) across the membrane thickness $a$.

### Step 1 — Set Up the 1-D Membrane Geometry
Let $x = 0$ be the outside, $x = a$ be the inside, and the potential vary linearly as $V(x) = V_i + (V_o - V_i)(x/a) \cdot (-1)$. Define $V = V_m = V_i - V_o$.

The constant-field condition gives $dV/dx = -V/a$ (independent of $x$).

### Step 2 — Solve Nernst-Planck Across the Membrane
Rearrange the Nernst-Planck equation into a linear first-order ODE in $[X]$:

$$J = -D \frac{d[X]}{dx} + \frac{zF D [X]}{RT} \frac{V}{a}$$

Multiplying both sides by the integrating factor $\exp(-zFV x / (RT a))$ and integrating from $x=0$ to $x=a$:

$$J = P_X \cdot zF \cdot \frac{[X]_i \exp(zFV/RT) - [X]_o}{\exp(zFV/RT) - 1}$$

where $P_X = D_X / a$ is the **permeability** (m/s).

### Step 3 — Apply the Constant-Field GHK Form
For monovalent ions, the current carried by ion $X$ is $I_X = zF J_X$. At **steady-state RMP**, **net membrane current must be zero** (charge conservation, no net charging of $C_m$):

$$I_K + I_{Na} + I_{Cl} = 0$$

For a cation, $z = +1$; for an anion, $z = -1$, so the sign of the current reverses.

### Step 4 — Substitute and Rearrange
Carrying out the algebra (shown in expanded form below) and solving for $V$:

$$V_m = \frac{RT}{F} \ln \frac{P_K [K^+]_o + P_{Na} [Na^+]_o + P_{Cl} [Cl^-]_i}{P_K [K^+]_i + P_{Na} [Na^+]_i + P_{Cl} [Cl^-]_o}$$

### Step 5 — Key Physical Interpretation
- If $P_{Na} = P_{Cl} = 0$, the equation reduces to the **Nernst equation for $\text{K}^+$** (membrane permeable to $\text{K}^+$ only).
- The membrane potential is a **permeability-weighted average** of the Nernst potentials of the permeant ions.
- Increasing $P_{Na}$ (e.g., during excitation) shifts $V_m$ toward $E_{Na}$ (~ +60 mV) — this is depolarization.

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example — RMP via GHK

Using the squid axon parameters (Hodgkin & Huxley, 1952):

$$P_K = 1.0, \quad P_{Na} = 0.04, \quad P_{Cl} = 0.45$$

Substitute the concentrations into the GHK equation:

$$V_m = 61.5 \times \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{(1.0)(5) + (0.04)(145) + (0.45)(4)}{(1.0)(140) + (0.04)(10) + (0.45)(120)}\right)$$

Compute numerator:

$$5.0 + 5.8 + 1.8 = 12.6$$

Compute denominator:

$$140.0 + 0.4 + 54.0 = 194.4$$

Ratio:

$$\frac{12.6}{194.4} = 0.06481$$

Logarithm:

$$\log_{10}(0.06481) = -1.1883$$

Final result:

$$V_m = 61.5 \times (-1.1883) = -73.1 \; \text{mV}$$

This matches the experimentally measured RMP of a squid giant axon (≈ −70 mV), confirming the validity of the GHK model.

## 3.6 Donnan Equilibrium Derivation (Brief)

Consider a membrane permeable to $\text{K}^+$ and $\text{Cl}^-$ but **impermeable** to an intracellular anion $\text{A}^-$.

### Step 1 — Charge Neutrality on Each Side
Inside: $[K^+]_i = [Cl^-]_i + [A^-]_i$
Outside: $[K^+]_o = [Cl^-]_o$

### Step 2 — Equality of Electrochemical Potentials
At equilibrium:

$$RT \ln \frac{[K^+]_i}{[K^+]_o} + F(V_i - V_o) = 0$$

$$RT \ln \frac{[Cl^-]_i}{[Cl^-]_o} - F(V_i - V_o) = 0$$

### Step 3 — Combining
Adding the two equations and using $[A^-]_i = [K^+]_i - [Cl^-]_i$ yields the **Donnan ratio**:

$$\frac{[K^+]_i}{[K^+]_o} = \frac{[Cl^-]_o}{[Cl^-]_i} = r$$

The Donnan potential across such a membrane is:

$$E_{Donnan} = \frac{RT}{F} \ln r$$

This explains the RMP contribution from **impermeant intracellular anions** (proteins, organic phosphates), even without active pumping.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Block-Level Model of RMP Generation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Cell Membrane Lipid Bilayer] --> B[Selective Permeability]
    A --> C[Na K ATPase Pump]
    A --> D[Membrane Capacitance Cm]
    A --> E[Ion Channels Variable Resistors]

    C -->|3 Na out| F[Active Ion Gradients]
    C -->|2 K in| F

    E --> G[K Leak Channel gK]
    E --> H[Na Leak Channel gNa]
    E --> I[Cl Channel gCl]

    F --> J[Chemical Driving Force]
    B --> K[Electrical Driving Force]

    G --> L[Nernst Battery EK approx minus 90 mV]
    H --> M[Nernst Battery ENa approx plus 60 mV]
    I --> N[Nernst Battery ECl approx minus 70 mV]

    J --> O[GHK Voltage Equation]
    K --> O
    L --> O
    M --> O
    N --> O

    C --> P[Electrogenic Component minus 4 to minus 10 mV]

    O --> Q[Resting Membrane Potential minus 70 mV]
    P --> Q

    Q --> R[Action Potential Excitation]
    Q --> S[Synaptic Integration]
    Q --> T[Biosignal Acquisition ECG EEG]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Equivalent Circuit Model (Hodgkin-Huxley Style)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    EXT[Extracellular Ground] --> RNa[Variable Resistor gNa]
    RNa --> BNa[Nernst Battery ENa]
    BNa --> INT[Intra Cellular Node]

    EXT --> RK[Variable Resistor gK]
    RK --> BK[Nernst Battery EK]
    BK --> INT

    EXT --> RCl[Variable Resistor gCl]
    RCl --> BCl[Nernst Battery ECl]
    BCl --> INT

    INT -.->|in parallel| Cm[Fixed Capacitor Cm about 1 uF per cm2]
    Cm --> EXT

    PUMP[Na K Pump Current Source] -->|Ipump| INT
    EXT --> PUMP

    INT --> OUT[Output Vm measured]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The block-level architecture above mirrors the standard **Hodgkin-Huxley equivalent circuit**, which is the foundation of the **Modelling of Physiological Systems** course. Each ion channel is modelled as a variable resistor (gating kinetics $\alpha, \beta$) in series with its Nernst battery; the lipid bilayer appears as a parallel capacitor $C_m$. The $\text{Na}^+/\text{K}^+$ pump is a current source $I_{pump}$.

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Permeability-Weighted Potential Concept

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    EK1[E_K = minus 90 mV] --> WEIGHT1[Weight P_K = 1.0]
    ENa1[E_Na = plus 60 mV] --> WEIGHT2[Weight P_Na = 0.04]
    ECl1[E_Cl = minus 70 mV] --> WEIGHT3[Weight P_Cl = 0.45]

    WEIGHT1 --> SUM[Weighted Average]
    WEIGHT2 --> SUM
    WEIGHT3 --> SUM

    SUM --> RMP[Resting Vm = minus 70 mV]
```

This diagram visualizes the central insight of the GHK equation: **the RMP is a weighted mean of the individual Nernst potentials**, where the weights are the relative permeabilities. Since $P_K$ dominates at rest, the RMP is pulled strongly toward $E_K$.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## **PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)**

### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
Define the **resting membrane potential (RMP)** of a cell. State its typical value for a mammalian neuron and identify the three principal ions responsible for establishing it.

**Model Answer (Concise, KTU Key-Point Format):**

> **RMP:** The steady-state electrical potential difference across the plasma membrane of a cell in the absence of excitation, defined as $V_m = V_i - V_o$.
> **Typical value:** **−70 mV** (interior negative with respect to exterior).
> **Three principal ions:** $\text{K}^+$, $\text{Na}^+$, $\text{Cl}^-$.
> The RMP arises because (i) the $\text{Na}^+/\text{K}^+$-ATPase maintains concentration gradients and (ii) the membrane is most permeable to $\text{K}^+$ at rest, so $V_m$ lies closest to $E_K \approx -90$ mV.

### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
State the **Nernst equation** for an ion and compute the **Nernst (equilibrium) potential for $\text{K}^+$** at 37 °C, given $[K^+]_i = 140$ mM and $[K^+]_o = 5$ mM.

**Model Answer:**

> **Nernst Equation:**
>
> $$E_K = \frac{2.303 \, RT}{zF} \log_{10} \frac{[K^+]_o}{[K^+]_i}$$
>
> At $T = 310$ K, the slope factor is $\dfrac{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 310}{96485} = 61.5$ mV.
>
> $$E_K = 61.5 \times \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{5}{140}\right) = 61.5 \times \log_{10}(0.03571)$$
>
> $$\log_{10}(0.03571) = -1.4472$$
>
> $$\boxed{E_K = 61.5 \times (-1.4472) = -89.0 \; \text{mV}}$$

---

## **PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each — KTU Internal Choice Pattern)**

---

### **Question A: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
**(a)** Starting from the concept of electrochemical potential, **derive the Nernst equation** for the equilibrium potential of an ion across a biological membrane. State clearly all assumptions used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Calculate the **Nernst potentials** for $\text{K}^+$, $\text{Na}^+$, and $\text{Cl}^-$ at body temperature using the standard concentrations: $[K^+]_i = 140, [K^+]_o = 5$; $[Na^+]_i = 10, [Na^+]_o = 145$; $[Cl^-]_i = 4, [Cl^-]_o = 120$ mM. Hence explain why the RMP lies closest to $E_K$. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution (a):**

1. **Electrochemical potential** of an ion $X$ of valence $z$ in aqueous solution at potential $V$:

$$\overline{\mu}_X = \mu^0_X + RT \ln [X] + zFV$$

2. **Difference across the membrane:**

$$\Delta \overline{\mu}_X = RT \ln \frac{[X]_i}{[X]_o} + zF(V_i - V_o)$$

3. **Equilibrium condition** — no net flux, so $\Delta \overline{\mu}_X = 0$:

$$RT \ln \frac{[X]_i}{[X]_o} = -zF(V_i - V_o)$$

4. **Solve for $V \equiv V_i - V_o$:**

$$E_X = -\frac{RT}{zF} \ln \frac{[X]_i}{[X]_o} = +\frac{2.303 \, RT}{zF} \log_{10} \frac{[X]_o}{[X]_i}$$

5. **Assumptions:**
   - System is at thermodynamic equilibrium (no net flux of $X$).
   - Temperature uniform and constant.
   - The membrane is permeable **only** to ion $X$.
   - Concentrations at the membrane surface equal bulk concentrations (no unstirred layer effects).

#### **Model Solution (b):**

Using $\dfrac{2.303RT}{F} = 61.5$ mV:

**For $\text{K}^+$ ($z = +1$):**

$$E_K = -61.5 \times \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{140}{5}\right) = -61.5 \times 1.447 = -89.0 \; \text{mV}$$

**For $\text{Na}^+$ ($z = +1$):**

$$E_{Na} = -61.5 \times \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{10}{145}\right) = -61.5 \times (-1.161) = +71.4 \; \text{mV}$$

**For $\text{Cl}^-$ ($z = -1$):**

$$E_{Cl} = -\frac{61.5}{-1} \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{4}{120}\right) = +61.5 \times (-1.477) = -90.8 \; \text{mV}$$

**Why RMP is closest to $E_K$:**
The resting membrane is **~25 times more permeable to $\text{K}^+$** than to $\text{Na}^+$. According to the chord-conductance weighted equation:

$$V_m = \frac{g_K E_K + g_{Na} E_{Na} + g_{Cl} E_{Cl}}{g_K + g_{Na} + g_{Cl}}$$

The dominant weight $g_K$ pulls $V_m$ toward $E_K$ ($-90$ mV). However, the small but finite $g_{Na}$ (and the electrogenic pump) shifts $V_m$ slightly positive from $E_K$ to the observed value of $\approx -70$ mV. **Hence the RMP lies between $E_K$ and $E_{Na}$, but much closer to $E_K$.**

---

### **Question B: [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
**(a)** With the aid of a **neat diagram**, explain the **Goldman-Hodgkin-Katz (GHK) voltage equation**. State the constant-field assumption and the role of ionic permeabilities. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a squid giant axon, the intracellular and extracellular ion concentrations (mM) are: $K^+ = 140 / 5$, $Na^+ = 10 / 145$, $Cl^- = 4 / 120$. If the relative permeabilities are $P_K : P_{Na} : P_{Cl} = 1.0 : 0.04 : 0.45$, **calculate the resting membrane potential** at 37 °C using the GHK equation. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution (a):**

**GHK Voltage Equation:**

$$V_m = \frac{RT}{F} \ln \frac{P_K [K^+]_o + P_{Na} [Na^+]_o + P_{Cl} [Cl^-]_i}{P_K [K^+]_i + P_{Na} [Na^+]_i + P_{Cl} [Cl^-]_o}$$

**Constant-Field Assumption (Goldman, 1943):** The electric field within the membrane is **uniform**, i.e., the potential drops **linearly** across the membrane thickness $a$. This is equivalent to assuming $dV/dx = \text{constant}$, which simplifies the integration of the Nernst-Planck equation.

**Role of Permeabilities:** The equation is a **permeability-weighted** average of the Nernst potentials. If $P_{Na} = P_{Cl} = 0$, then $V_m = E_K$. As $P_{Na}$ increases (e.g., during the rising phase of an action potential), the membrane is depolarized toward $E_{Na} \approx +60$ mV.

**Diagram (Equivalent Circuit):**

```
   Outside ──[g_K]──┬──[E_K]──┐
                     │          │
   Outside ──[g_Na]─┼──[E_Na]─┤── Inside (V_m)
                     │          │
   Outside ──[g_Cl]─┴──[E_Cl]──┘
                  ║
                [C_m]
                  ║
              Outside (ground)
```

**Limitations:** The GHK equation is valid only at steady state, does not account for active transport, and assumes constant field — violated when currents are large.

#### **Model Solution (b):**

Substitute the given values into the GHK equation:

$$V_m = 61.5 \times \log_{10} \left( \frac{(1.0)(5) + (0.04)(145) + (0.45)(4)}{(1.0)(140) + (0.04)(10) + (0.45)(120)} \right)$$

**Numerator (outside terms):**

$$5.0 + 5.8 + 1.8 = 12.6$$

**Denominator (inside terms):**

$$140.0 + 0.4 + 54.0 = 194.4$$

**Ratio:**

$$\frac{12.6}{194.4} = 0.0648$$

**Logarithm:**

$$\log_{10}(0.0648) = -1.188$$

**Final calculation:**

$$\boxed{V_m = 61.5 \times (-1.188) = -73.1 \; \text{mV}}$$

This is in excellent agreement with the experimentally measured squid axon RMP (≈ −70 mV).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — How Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Sign error in Nernst equation:** Forgetting that $\text{Cl}^-$ has $z = -1$ flips the sign. Always substitute $z$ explicitly; do not assume $z = +1$.
> 2. **Inverted concentration ratio:** Writing $\dfrac{[X]_i}{[X]_o}$ instead of $\dfrac{[X]_o}{[X]_i}$ in the positive form causes an answer of opposite sign.
> 3. **Wrong temperature:** Using 25 °C (298 K) instead of 37 °C (310 K) gives slope 59.2 mV instead of 61.5 mV — a 4% error and loss of **partial marks**.
> 4. **Confusing Nernst with GHK:** Nernst gives equilibrium for *one* ion; GHK gives RMP for *multiple* ions. Many students mix them.
> 5. **Forgetting the pump contribution:** RMP is **not** purely diffusive; the electrogenic $\text{Na}^+/\text{K}^+$ pump adds **−4 to −10 mV**. GHK alone gives ≈ −73 mV; the pump brings it closer to −70 mV.
> 6. **Units mismatch:** Concentrations must be in the **same units** on both sides of the log; ratios are dimensionless.

---

## **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Resting Membrane Potential**

### **A. Foundational Facts**
- RMP is a **DC voltage** across the plasma membrane, defined as $V_m = V_i - V_o$, measured in **millivolts (mV)**.
- Typical neuronal RMP: **−70 mV** (squid axon: −60 to −75 mV).
- The interior is **negative** with respect to the exterior.

### **B. Three Required Conditions for RMP**
1. Asymmetric ion concentration gradients (set up by $\text{Na}^+/\text{K}^+$-ATPase).
2. Differential membrane permeability (mainly to $\text{K}^+$ at rest).
3. A selectively permeable membrane (lipid bilayer + ion channels).

### **C. Equations You MUST Memorize**
1. **Nernst equation** (single ion equilibrium): $E_X = \dfrac{61.5}{z} \log_{10} \dfrac{[X]_o}{[X]_i}$ (at 37 °C).
2. **Nernst-Planck equation** (flux under gradient + field).
3. **GHK voltage equation** (multi-ion steady-state RMP).
4. **Chord-conductance form** of RMP.
5. **Donnan ratio** for impermeant anion systems.

### **D. Standard Numerical Values (Memorize)**
- $E_K \approx -90$ mV
- $E_{Na} \approx +60$ mV
- $E_{Cl} \approx -70$ mV
- $E_{Ca} \approx +120$ mV
- $P_K : P_{Na} : P_{Cl} = 1.0 : 0.04 : 0.45$ (squid axon)
- Pump stoichiometry: **3 Na⁺ out, 2 K⁺ in per ATP** (electrogenic)
- $C_m \approx 1 \; \mu F/\text{cm}^2$

### **E. Conceptual Insights**
- RMP is **not** a single Nernst potential; it is a **weighted average** dominated by the most permeant ion.
- The membrane is a leaky capacitor — at RMP, leak currents and pump current are balanced.
- RMP sets the **operating point** for all bioelectric signals: action potentials, postsynaptic potentials, receptor potentials.
- Destroying the pump (e.g., with ouabain) causes RMP to decay slowly toward **0 mV** as gradients equilibrate.
- The Goldman equation is a **steady-state, constant-field** approximation — valid for low-to-moderate voltages; not applicable during action potentials.

### **F. Common Exam Traps**
- "RMP is generated by the $\text{Na}^+/\text{K}^+$ pump alone" — **FALSE**. The pump maintains gradients; the **diffusion potentials** (Nernst/GHK) generate the voltage.
- "RMP equals $E_K$" — **FALSE**; it lies between $E_K$ and $E_{Na}$, shifted by the finite $P_{Na}$ and pump electrogenicity.
- "$\text{Cl}^-$ is at equilibrium in every neuron" — **FALSE** in some neurons; in cortical neurons, $E_{Cl}$ is more positive than RMP, making $\text{Cl}^-$ inhibitory.
- Confusing the **resting potential** (RMP) with the **threshold potential** (~ −55 mV) or the **after-hyperpolarization** potential.

> **End of Topic Notes — Resting Membrane Potential (Module 3)**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT865 - MODELLING OF PHYSIOLOGICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
