<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# ⚡ Voltage-Gated Channels — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of physiological system modelling, **Voltage-Gated Channels (VGCs)** are a class of transmembrane ion channel proteins whose conformational state (open, closed, or inactivated) is governed primarily by the **transmembrane potential difference** across the cell membrane. Mathematically, they are represented as **non-linear, voltage-dependent conductances** $g(V_m, t)$ that modulate ionic currents $I_{ion}$ according to the driving force $(V_m - E_{ion})$, where $E_{ion}$ is the Nernst equilibrium potential of the permeant ion.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PEEBT865 / Module 3):**
> *Voltage-gated channels are dynamic, stochastic macromolecular pores whose open probability is a sigmoid function of membrane voltage. They form the molecular substrate for the Hodgkin–Huxley (HH) formalism, the cornerstone equation of excitable membrane modelling.*

The canonical textbook example, due to **Hodgkin & Huxley (1952, Nobel Prize 1963)**, characterizes the squid giant axon's membrane using two principal VGCs:

| Channel | Symbol | Permeant Ion | Behaviour |
|---|---|---|---|
| Sodium channel | $Na^+$ | $Na^+$ | Fast activation + slow inactivation |
| Potassium channel | $K^+$ | $K^+$ | Slow activation, no inactivation (in squid axon) |
| Leak channel | $L$ | Non-selective | Voltage-independent |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Pressure-Relief Valve" Model

Imagine a water tank with a **smart pressure valve** that is mechanically linked to a pressure gauge. When the gauge reads high pressure (analogous to a depolarized membrane, $V_m \uparrow$), the valve **springs open**, letting water (ions) rush out. The higher the pressure, the wider it opens — but if high pressure persists too long, a **secondary latch** engages and physically locks the valve shut (this is *inactivation*). Once the pressure drops back to a low level, the latch resets, and the valve is ready to open again.

In the cell:
- **Valve opening** = Channel **activation gate** opening ($m, n$ particles move to "open" position).
- **Secondary latch** = Channel **inactivation gate** closing ($h$ particle moves to "inactive" position).
- **Pressure** = Membrane voltage $V_m$.
- **Water flow** = Ionic current $I_{ion}$.

## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants and Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> Key constants used in VGC modelling (squid axon at 6.3 °C unless stated):

- Maximum $Na^+$ conductance: **$g_{Na}^{max} = 120\ \text{mS/cm}^2$**
- Maximum $K^+$ conductance: **$g_{K}^{max} = 36\ \text{mS/cm}^2$**
- Leak conductance: **$g_L = 0.3\ \text{mS/cm}^2$**
- Nernst potential for $Na^+$: **$E_{Na} \approx +50\ \text{mV}$**
- Nernst potential for $K^+$: **$E_K \approx -77\ \text{mV}$**
- Resting potential: **$E_L \approx -54.4\ \text{mV}$**
- Membrane capacitance: **$C_m = 1\ \mu\text{F/cm}^2$**
- Gas constant: **$R = 8.314\ \text{J/(mol·K)}$**
- Faraday's constant: **$F = 96{,}485\ \text{C/mol}$**

These values are *standard* reference metrics used in the **KTU board exam** and form the basis of all numerical problems in this module.

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🔬 Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Hodgkin–Huxley Conceptual Framework

Hodgkin and Huxley postulated that each VGC contains a set of **independent, identical, voltage-sensitive "gating particles"**. A channel conducts only when *all* of its particles are in the permissive conformation. This leads to a **probabilistic, multiplicative gating scheme**.

The membrane current is partitioned into three parallel branches:

$$I_m = C_m \frac{dV_m}{dt} + I_{Na} + I_K + I_L$$

Each ionic current obeys the **Ohmic driving-force relation** with a voltage- and time-dependent conductance:

$$I_{Na} = \bar{g}_{Na} \cdot m^3 h \cdot (V_m - E_{Na})$$

$$I_K = \bar{g}_K \cdot n^4 \cdot (V_m - E_K)$$

$$I_L = \bar{g}_L \cdot (V_m - E_L)$$

### Why the Powers? ($m^3, h^1, n^4$)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Hodgkin & Huxley fit the **3rd power of $m$** and **4th power of $n$** empirically to match the *delay* and *steepness* of channel opening. **Biologically**, the $Na^+$ channel is now known to be a **single tetrameric protein** with **four voltage-sensor domains (DI–DIV)**. Three must activate, and the fourth (DIV) drives inactivation. The HH powers are **phenomenological**, not literal particle counts.

## 2.2 First-Order Gating Kinetics

Each gating variable ($m, h, n$) follows a **first-order differential equation** between a "closed" state and an "open" state:

$$\frac{dx}{dt} = \alpha_x (V_m)(1 - x) - \beta_x (V_m)\, x$$

where $x \in \{m, h, n\}$, $\alpha_x$ is the **forward (activation) rate** and $\beta_x$ is the **backward (deactivation) rate**, both functions of $V_m$ only.

In **steady-state**, $\frac{dx}{dt} = 0$, giving the steady-state gating value:

$$x_\infty(V_m) = \frac{\alpha_x(V_m)}{\alpha_x(V_m) + \beta_x(V_m)}$$

The associated **time constant** is:

$$\tau_x(V_m) = \frac{1}{\alpha_x(V_m) + \beta_x(V_m)}$$

The first-order ODE can be re-expressed in canonical form as:

$$\frac{dx}{dt} = \frac{x_\infty(V_m) - x}{\tau_x(V_m)}$$

This is the form KTU examiners prefer because it makes the **physiological meaning** transparent.

## 2.3 Voltage-Dependent Rate Constants (Squid Axon, 6.3 °C)

$$\alpha_m(V) = \frac{0.1\,(V + 40)}{1 - e^{-(V+40)/10}}$$

$$\beta_m(V) = 4\, e^{-(V+65)/18}$$

$$\alpha_h(V) = 0.07\, e^{-(V+65)/20}$$

$$\beta_h(V) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-(V+35)/10}}$$

$$\alpha_n(V) = \frac{0.01\,(V + 55)}{1 - e^{-(V+55)/10}}$$

$$\beta_n(V) = 0.125\, e^{-(V+65)/80}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Convention:** Voltages are in **mV measured relative to resting potential** (i.e., $V$ in the rate equations is the *depolarization* from rest, often $V_m$ in the convention of H&H 1952). The temperature is **6.3 °C**; for physiological temperature (37 °C), a **Q₁₀ correction** of approximately 3 is applied to the rate constants.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Table)

| # | Formula / Equation | Meaning / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $I_{Na} = \bar{g}_{Na} m^3 h (V - E_{Na})$ | Sodium current through VGC |
| 2 | $I_K = \bar{g}_K n^4 (V - E_K)$ | Potassium current through VGC |
| 3 | $I_L = \bar{g}_L (V - E_L)$ | Ohmic leak current |
| 4 | $\frac{dm}{dt} = \alpha_m (1-m) - \beta_m m$ | m-gate (Na activation) kinetics |
| 5 | $\frac{dh}{dt} = \alpha_h (1-h) - \beta_h h$ | h-gate (Na inactivation) kinetics |
| 6 | $\frac{dn}{dt} = \alpha_n (1-n) - \beta_n n$ | n-gate (K activation) kinetics |
| 7 | $x_\infty = \frac{\alpha_x}{\alpha_x + \beta_x}$ | Steady-state gating variable |
| 8 | $\tau_x = \frac{1}{\alpha_x + \beta_x}$ | Voltage-dependent time constant |
| 9 | $\frac{dx}{dt} = \frac{x_\infty - x}{\tau_x}$ | Canonical gating ODE |
| 10 | $g_{Na}(V,t) = \bar{g}_{Na}\, m^3 h$ | Instantaneous Na conductance |
| 11 | $g_K(V,t) = \bar{g}_K\, n^4$ | Instantaneous K conductance |
| 12 | $E_{ion} = \frac{RT}{zF}\ln\frac{[ion]_o}{[ion]_i}$ | Nernst equilibrium potential |
| 13 | $P_o = \prod_{i} p_i^{k_i}$ | Open probability (HH assumption) |
| 14 | $I_{total} = C_m \frac{dV}{dt} + \sum I_{ion}$ | Total membrane current balance |
| 15 | $Q_{10} = \left(\frac{R_2}{R_1}\right)^{\frac{10}{T_2 - T_1}}$ | Temperature correction factor |

## 2.5 Why This Matters in Engineering

VGC models are not merely biological curiosities — they drive the design of:

- **Neural prostheses and brain–computer interfaces** (deep-brain stimulators).
- **Cardiac defibrillators and pacemakers** (modelling re-entrant arrhythmias).
- **Pharmacological screening** of ion-channel-targeted drugs (hERG channel safety assays).
- **Neuromorphic computing** — silicon neurons that emulate HH dynamics using CMOS sub-threshold circuits (Mead, 1989).
- **Bidomain models** of cardiac tissue for ECG simulation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A KTU examiner expects students to know that **HH is the unifying language of all excitable-cell modelling** — neurons, cardiac myocytes, pancreatic β-cells, and skeletal muscle all use variants of this formalism.

# 📐 Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I: From Rate Constants to the Canonical ODE

**Starting point** — The master kinetic equation for a single gating particle is:

$$\frac{dx}{dt} = \alpha_x (V)(1 - x) - \beta_x(V)\, x$$

**Step 1 — Expand the right-hand side:**

$$\frac{dx}{dt} = \alpha_x - \alpha_x x - \beta_x x$$

$$\frac{dx}{dt} = \alpha_x - (\alpha_x + \beta_x)\, x$$

**Step 2 — Identify the steady-state solution.** Set $\frac{dx}{dt} = 0$:

$$0 = \alpha_x - (\alpha_x + \beta_x)\, x_\infty$$

$$\boxed{x_\infty = \frac{\alpha_x}{\alpha_x + \beta_x}}$$

**Step 3 — Identify the time constant.** In a first-order linear ODE of the form $\frac{dx}{dt} = A - B x$, the relaxation time constant is $1/B$. Hence:

$$\tau_x = \frac{1}{\alpha_x + \beta_x}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute into the original ODE to obtain the canonical form:**

$$\frac{dx}{dt} = (\alpha_x + \beta_x) \left[ \frac{\alpha_x}{\alpha_x + \beta_x} - x \right]$$

$$\boxed{\frac{dx}{dt} = \frac{x_\infty - x}{\tau_x}}$$

This is the form used in **all numerical simulations** and is what KTU board expects in part (a) derivation questions.

## 3.2 Derivation II: Instantaneous Conductance for the $Na^+$ Channel

The Hodgkin–Huxley assumption is that the $Na^+$ channel has **3 independent activation particles** ($m$) and **1 inactivation particle** ($h$). The channel conducts only when all 4 are in the open-state.

**Step 1 — Probability all 3 m-particles are open:**

$$P_{m^3} = m \cdot m \cdot m = m^3$$

because each particle is independent and has probability $m$ of being open at instant $t$.

**Step 2 — Probability the h-particle is open:**

$$P_h = h$$

**Step 3 — Total open probability** (independence ⇒ multiplication):

$$P_{open}^{Na} = m^3 h$$

**Step 4 — Instantaneous conductance** is the maximum conductance scaled by open probability:

$$\boxed{g_{Na}(V,t) = \bar{g}_{Na}\, m^3(V,t)\, h(V,t)}$$

**Step 5 — Current** (Ohm's law with driving force):

$$\boxed{I_{Na} = \bar{g}_{Na}\, m^3 h\, (V_m - E_{Na})}$$

The same logic with 4 particles gives $I_K = \bar{g}_K n^4 (V_m - E_K)$.

## 3.3 Derivation III: Steady-State $m_\infty$ and $\tau_m$ at a Specific Voltage

**Problem (KTU-style):** Calculate $m_\infty$ and $\tau_m$ at $V = 0$ mV (depolarization).

**Step 1 — Evaluate $\alpha_m(0)$:**

$$\alpha_m(0) = \frac{0.1\,(0 + 40)}{1 - e^{-(0+40)/10}} = \frac{4}{1 - e^{-4}}$$

Computing: $e^{-4} \approx 0.01832$, so $1 - e^{-4} \approx 0.98168$.

$$\alpha_m(0) = \frac{4}{0.98168} \approx 4.074\ \text{ms}^{-1}$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate $\beta_m(0)$:**

$$\beta_m(0) = 4\, e^{-(0+65)/18} = 4\, e^{-3.611}$$

$$e^{-3.611} \approx 0.02705$$

$$\beta_m(0) \approx 4 \times 0.02705 = 0.1082\ \text{ms}^{-1}$$

**Step 3 — Compute $m_\infty$:**

$$m_\infty(0) = \frac{\alpha_m}{\alpha_m + \beta_m} = \frac{4.074}{4.074 + 0.1082} = \frac{4.074}{4.182}$$

$$\boxed{m_\infty(0) \approx 0.974}$$

**Step 4 — Compute $\tau_m$:**

$$\tau_m(0) = \frac{1}{\alpha_m + \beta_m} = \frac{1}{4.182}$$

$$\boxed{\tau_m(0) \approx 0.239\ \text{ms}}$$

**Interpretation:** At strong depolarization ($V=0$ mV), the m-gate is **~97 % open at steady state** and reaches that state in about **0.24 ms** — consistent with the fast Na activation observed in the action-potential upstroke.

## 3.4 Derivation IV: Complete Membrane Equation of an Excitable Cell

The patch-clamp / cable equation for a uniform membrane (no spatial variation) sums the capacitive and ionic currents:

$$C_m \frac{dV_m}{dt} + \bar{g}_{Na} m^3 h (V_m - E_{Na}) + \bar{g}_K n^4 (V_m - E_K) + \bar{g}_L (V_m - E_L) = I_{stim}(t)$$

This is a **4th-order non-linear system of ODEs** in $(V_m, m, h, n)$ — the central object of study in computational neuroscience.

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Activation vs Inactivation

| Property | $m$ (Na activation) | $h$ (Na inactivation) | $n$ (K activation) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Time constant range | 0.1 – 1 ms | 1 – 10 ms | 1 – 10 ms |
| Behaviour on depolarization | ↑ rapidly (opens) | ↓ slowly (closes) | ↑ slowly (opens) |
| Effect on channel | Permits conduction | Blocks conduction | Permits conduction |
| Power in current equation | 3 | 1 | 4 |
| Recovery on repolarization | Fast | Slow (refractory) | Fast |
| Physiological role | Action-potential upstroke | Absolute refractory period | Repolarization |

# 🧩 Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Hodgkin–Huxley Model

The following Mermaid block diagram depicts the **functional flow** of the HH VGC model — how a stimulus is transduced into membrane voltage change via the parallel action of capacitive, sodium, potassium, and leak branches. The gating variables $m, h, n$ are derived from the voltage via the rate-constant sub-system, which in turn scales the ionic conductances.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Stimulus Current I_stim] --> B[Membrane Patch Node]
    B --> C1[Capacitive Branch C_m dV/dt]
    B --> C2[Sodium Branch g_Na m^3 h V minus E_Na]
    B --> C3[Potassium Branch g_K n^4 V minus E_K]
    B --> C4[Leak Branch g_L V minus E_L]

    C1 --> D[Membrane Voltage V_m]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    C4 --> D

    D --> E1[Rate Function alpha_m of V]
    D --> E2[Rate Function beta_m of V]
    D --> E3[Rate Function alpha_h of V]
    D --> E4[Rate Function beta_h of V]
    D --> E5[Rate Function alpha_n of V]
    D --> E6[Rate Function beta_n of V]

    E1 --> F1[Gating ODE dm/dt]
    E2 --> F1
    E1 --> F2[Steady State m_inf]
    E2 --> F2

    E3 --> G1[Gating ODE dh/dt]
    E4 --> G1
    E3 --> G2[Steady State h_inf]
    E4 --> G2

    E5 --> H1[Gating ODE dn/dt]
    E6 --> H1
    E5 --> H2[Steady State n_inf]
    E6 --> H2

    F1 --> C2
    F2 --> C2
    G1 --> C2
    G2 --> C2
    H1 --> C3
    H2 --> C3
```

**Reading the diagram:** The outer loop shows the **algebraic feedback** — $V_m$ controls $\alpha, \beta$, which control $m, h, n$, which modulate $I_{Na}, I_K$, which in turn determine $\frac{dV_m}{dt}$. This is the **non-linear feedback loop** that makes VGCs capable of generating an action potential.

## 4.2 State-Transition Diagram of the $Na^+$ Channel

A Markov-state representation of a single $Na^+$ VGC, capturing the four functional states (closed, open, inactivated, and recovery):

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> closed_C3 : Resting
    closed_C3 --> closed_C2 : m opens 2 of 3
    closed_C2 --> closed_C1 : m opens 2 of 3
    closed_C1 --> open_O : m opens fully
    open_O --> inactivated_I : h closes
    inactivated_I --> closed_C3 : h reopens recovery
    open_O --> closed_C1 : m closes
    closed_C1 --> closed_C2 : m closes
    closed_C2 --> closed_C3 : m closes
```

**Interpretation:**

- **C3 → C2 → C1 → O** is the activation sequence (three $m$ particles sequentially open).
- **O → I** is the **inactivation** transition (driven by the $h$ gate).
- **I → C3** is the **recovery** transition (only possible after repolarization when $h$ reopens). This is the molecular basis of the **absolute refractory period**.

## 4.3 Equivalent Circuit Schematic (Text Form)

For quick reference in the answer script, the KTU-accepted equivalent circuit of a VGC-containing membrane patch is:

```
         I_stim
           │
           ▼
    ┌──────────────┐
    │              │
   ─┤ C_m      ─┤ (V - E_Na) × g_Na m³ h   ← Na VGC branch
   ─┤          ─┤ (V - E_K)  × g_K n⁴      ← K VGC branch
   ─┤          ─┤ (V - E_L)  × g_L         ← Leak branch
    │              │
    └──────────────┘
           │
          GND
```

- The **capacitor** $C_m$ represents charge storage across the lipid bilayer.
- The **batteries** $E_{Na}, E_K, E_L$ represent the electrochemical driving force.
- The **variable conductances** $g_{Na}m^3h$ and $g_K n^4$ are the **voltage-gated channels**.

# 📝 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define a voltage-gated ion channel. Mention the two principal voltage-gated channels involved in the Hodgkin–Huxley model of the squid giant axon.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **voltage-gated ion channel (VGC)** is a transmembrane protein that selectively conducts specific ions across the cell membrane in response to changes in the transmembrane potential difference. Its open probability is a non-linear function of $V_m$.

In the Hodgkin–Huxley model, the two principal VGCs are:
1. **Voltage-gated $Na^+$ channel** — responsible for the rapid depolarization (upstroke) of the action potential. Modelled with $m$ (activation) and $h$ (inactivation) gates, current $I_{Na} = \bar{g}_{Na} m^3 h (V_m - E_{Na})$.
2. **Voltage-gated $K^+$ channel** — responsible for repolarization. Modelled with a single $n$ (activation) gate, current $I_K = \bar{g}_K n^4 (V_m - E_K)$.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**What is the significance of the gating variables $m$, $h$, and $n$ in the Hodgkin–Huxley formalism?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The gating variables are dimensionless quantities in $[0,1]$ representing the **probability** that a particular voltage-sensitive "particle" is in the permissive (open) conformation at the current instant:

- **$m$** — activation gate of the $Na^+$ channel. Depolarization increases $m$ from 0 → 1 rapidly ($\tau_m \approx 0.1$–$1$ ms).
- **$h$** — inactivation gate of the $Na^+$ channel. Depolarization decreases $h$ from 1 → 0 slowly ($\tau_h \approx 1$–$10$ ms).
- **$n$** — activation gate of the $K^+$ channel. Depolarization increases $n$ from 0 → 1 slowly ($\tau_n \approx 1$–$10$ ms).

Each obeys a first-order kinetic ODE $\frac{dx}{dt} = \frac{x_\infty - x}{\tau_x}$, with $x_\infty$ and $\tau_x$ being voltage-dependent. Multiplicative combinations $m^3h$ and $n^4$ give the open probabilities of the channels.

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Always** retain voltage in mV and time in ms when using the standard HH rate constants — mixing units is the most common mark-losing error.
> 2. **Never** confuse $m_\infty$ (the steady-state value) with $m$ (the instantaneous value). Mark difference: **−1 mark per error**.
> 3. The powers $m^3$ and $n^4$ are **phenomenological fits**, not literal particle counts — do not write "the channel has 3 Na particles" without qualification.
> 4. When computing $\alpha_m$ near $V = -40$ mV, the denominator $1 - e^{-(V+40)/10}$ approaches zero — apply **L'Hôpital's rule**: $\alpha_m(-40) = 0.1 \times 10 = 1.0\ \text{ms}^{-1}$.
> 5. Always **state the temperature** (6.3 °C) when using the textbook rate constants.

---

### 🔹 QUESTION A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** With the help of neat block diagrams, derive the **Hodgkin–Huxley equation** for the total membrane current density of a squid giant axon. Clearly define all symbols. (7 marks)

**(b)** For the $Na^+$ activation gate, evaluate the steady-state value $m_\infty$ and the time constant $\tau_m$ at membrane voltage $V = -10$ mV using the standard HH rate-constant expressions. Comment on the physiological significance. (7 marks)

#### ✅ Model Solution — Question A

**(a) Part (a) — Derivation of HH Equation (7 marks):**

Hodgkin and Huxley (1952) modelled the squid axon membrane as a **parallel combination** of a capacitor and three ionic conductors.

**Step 1 — Membrane as an electrical element:** Apply Kirchhoff's current law at the membrane node. Total applied current splits into a displacement (capacitive) current and ionic currents.

$$I_{stim} = I_C + I_{Na} + I_K + I_L$$

**Step 2 — Capacitive current:**

$$I_C = C_m \frac{dV_m}{dt}$$

**Step 3 — Ohmic form of ionic currents** with voltage-dependent conductances and Nernst-battery driving forces:

$$I_{Na} = g_{Na}(V_m,t)(V_m - E_{Na})$$

$$I_K = g_K(V_m,t)(V_m - E_K)$$

$$I_L = \bar{g}_L (V_m - E_L)$$

**Step 4 — HH postulate of independent gating particles.** The $Na^+$ channel is open when **three** $m$ particles **and** one $h$ particle are simultaneously open. By independence, open probability is $P_{open}^{Na} = m^3 h$. Similarly, the $K^+$ channel has **four** $n$ particles, $P_{open}^K = n^4$.

**Step 5 — Conductance expressions:**

$$g_{Na}(V_m,t) = \bar{g}_{Na} m^3 h$$

$$g_K(V_m,t) = \bar{g}_K n^4$$

**Step 6 — Gating variable dynamics.** Each gating variable obeys:

$$\frac{dx}{dt} = \alpha_x(V_m)(1-x) - \beta_x(V_m) x$$

with $x \in \{m, h, n\}$ and the voltage-dependent rate constants defined in §2.3.

**Step 7 — Master equation of the membrane:**

$$\boxed{\begin{aligned}
C_m \frac{dV_m}{dt} = I_{stim} - \bar{g}_{Na} m^3 h (V_m - E_{Na}) - \bar{g}_K n^4 (V_m - E_K) - \bar{g}_L (V_m - E_L)
\end{aligned}}$$

This is a **4-dimensional non-linear ODE system** in $(V_m, m, h, n)$ that reproduces the squid axon action potential with high fidelity.

**(b) Part (b) — Numerical evaluation at $V = -10$ mV (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Compute $\alpha_m(-10)$:**

$$\alpha_m(-10) = \frac{0.1 \times (-10 + 40)}{1 - e^{-(-10+40)/10}} = \frac{3}{1 - e^{-3}}$$

$$e^{-3} \approx 0.04979 \Rightarrow 1 - e^{-3} \approx 0.95021$$

$$\alpha_m(-10) = \frac{3}{0.95021} \approx 3.157\ \text{ms}^{-1}$$

**Step 2 — Compute $\beta_m(-10)$:**

$$\beta_m(-10) = 4 e^{-(-10+65)/18} = 4 e^{-55/18} = 4 e^{-3.0556}$$

$$e^{-3.0556} \approx 0.04709$$

$$\beta_m(-10) = 4 \times 0.04709 \approx 0.1884\ \text{ms}^{-1}$$

**Step 3 — Compute $m_\infty(-10)$:**

$$m_\infty(-10) = \frac{3.157}{3.157 + 0.1884} = \frac{3.157}{3.345} \approx \boxed{0.944}$$

**Step 4 — Compute $\tau_m(-10)$:**

$$\tau_m(-10) = \frac{1}{3.157 + 0.1884} = \frac{1}{3.345} \approx \boxed{0.299\ \text{ms}}$$

**Step 5 — Physiological significance:** At $V = -10$ mV (during the upstroke of the action potential), the $m$ gate is **~94 % open at steady state** and reaches this value with a time constant of **~0.3 ms**. This rapid activation is precisely what enables the **sharp depolarizing spike** of the nerve impulse, and its sub-millisecond kinetics set the **upper frequency limit** of axonal firing.

---

### 🔹 QUESTION B — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the **Markov-state representation** of a voltage-gated $Na^+$ channel. Sketch the four-state diagram and discuss the role of activation, inactivation, and recovery transitions. (7 marks)

**(b)** The maximum conductances of the Hodgkin–Huxley model are $\bar{g}_{Na} = 120$ mS/cm², $\bar{g}_K = 36$ mS/cm², and $\bar{g}_L = 0.3$ mS/cm². The Nernst potentials are $E_{Na} = +50$ mV, $E_K = -77$ mV, $E_L = -54.4$ mV. At the peak of an action potential, suppose the gating variables take values $m = 0.95$, $h = 0.05$, $n = 0.30$ and $V_m = +30$ mV. Compute the instantaneous ionic currents $I_{Na}$, $I_K$, and $I_L$. (7 marks)

#### ✅ Model Solution — Question B

**(a) Part (a) — Markov model of the $Na^+$ VGC (7 marks):**

A **Markov model** represents the channel as a finite state machine in which the **future state depends only on the present state**, not on the history (memoryless transition). For the $Na^+$ VGC, four functional states are required:

1. **Closed (C)** — channel is non-conducting, available to open. Three sequential sub-states $C_3 \to C_2 \to C_1$ correspond to the opening of the three $m$ particles.
2. **Open (O)** — channel is conducting; both activation is complete and inactivation gate is open.
3. **Inactivated (I)** — channel is non-conducting because the $h$ gate has closed; it cannot reopen from this state without first **recovering** through $I \to C$.
4. **Recovery (R)** — implicit transition from $I \to C$ that occurs only at hyperpolarized potentials.

**Transition rates and their physiological meaning:**

| Transition | Driving Gate | Effect of Depolarization | Effect on Channel |
|---|---|---|---|
| $C_3 \to C_2 \to C_1$ | $m$ particles open | Rate ↑ (fast) | Channel approaches open |
| $C_1 \to O$ | Final $m$ opens | Rate ↑ | Channel becomes conducting |
| $O \to I$ | $h$ closes | Rate ↑ (slower) | Channel inactivates (refractory) |
| $I \to C_3$ | $h$ reopens | Rate ↓ (favoured at rest) | Channel resets |

The advantage of the Markov description is that it is **biophysically explicit** — each state corresponds to a real conformational state of the protein, and the rate constants have a physical interpretation in **Eyring rate theory**. This contrasts with the HH formalism, where $m, h, n$ are abstract probabilistic variables.

**(b) Part (b) — Numerical evaluation of ionic currents (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Sodium current.** Open probability = $m^3 h$:

$$m^3 h = (0.95)^3 \times 0.05 = 0.8574 \times 0.05 = 0.0429$$

$$I_{Na} = \bar{g}_{Na} m^3 h (V_m - E_{Na}) = 120 \times 0.0429 \times (30 - 50)$$

$$I_{Na} = 120 \times 0.0429 \times (-20) = 5.143 \times (-20)$$

$$\boxed{I_{Na} \approx -102.9\ \text{mA/cm}^2}$$

(Negative sign = inward current, characteristic of the falling phase where Na is being repelled.)

**Step 2 — Potassium current.** Open probability = $n^4$:

$$n^4 = (0.30)^4 = 0.0081$$

$$I_K = \bar{g}_K n^4 (V_m - E_K) = 36 \times 0.0081 \times (30 - (-77))$$

$$I_K = 36 \times 0.0081 \times 107 = 0.2916 \times 107$$

$$\boxed{I_K \approx 31.2\ \text{mA/cm}^2}$$

(Positive sign = outward current, characteristic of repolarization.)

**Step 3 — Leak current:**

$$I_L = \bar{g}_L (V_m - E_L) = 0.3 \times (30 - (-54.4)) = 0.3 \times 84.4$$

$$\boxed{I_L \approx 25.32\ \text{mA/cm}^2}$$

(Outward; small but non-negligible at the peak.)

**Step 4 — Net ionic current at the peak:**

$$I_{ion}^{net} = I_{Na} + I_K + I_L = -102.9 + 31.2 + 25.32 = -46.4\ \text{mA/cm}^2$$

The dominant negative Na current is consistent with the **inward depolarizing drive** that defines the action-potential peak region, while the rising K and leak currents foreshadow the **onset of repolarization**.

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Voltage-gated channels** are non-linear conductances whose open probability depends on $V_m$; they are the building blocks of all excitable-membrane models.
- The **Hodgkin–Huxley formalism** uses three gating variables $m, h, n$ governed by **first-order voltage-dependent ODEs**.
- Channel open probabilities: $P_{open}^{Na} = m^3 h$ and $P_{open}^{K} = n^4$ — phenomenological powers, not literal particle counts.
- Master ionic current equations: $I_{Na} = \bar{g}_{Na} m^3 h (V - E_{Na})$; $I_K = \bar{g}_K n^4 (V - E_K)$; $I_L = \bar{g}_L (V - E_L)$.
- The two canonical forms of the gating ODE are equivalent: $\frac{dx}{dt} = \alpha_x(1-x) - \beta_x x$  ≡  $\frac{dx}{dt} = \frac{x_\infty - x}{\tau_x}$.
- The steady-state and time constant are computable directly: $x_\infty = \frac{\alpha_x}{\alpha_x + \beta_x}$ and $\tau_x = \frac{1}{\alpha_x + \beta_x}$.
- The rate constants $\alpha_x, \beta_x$ are **voltage-dependent** sigmoidal/exponential functions; at $V = -40$ mV, $\alpha_m$ requires L'Hôpital's rule.
- **$Na^+$ activation** ($m$) is **fast** ($\tau_m \sim 0.1$–$1$ ms) and opens on depolarization.
- **$Na^+$ inactivation** ($h$) is **slow** ($\tau_h \sim 1$–$10$ ms) and closes on depolarization — this produces the **refractory period**.
- **$K^+$ activation** ($n$) is **slow** ($\tau_n \sim 1$–$10$ ms) and opens on depolarization — drives **repolarization**.
- The **Markov state model** is the biophysically more rigorous alternative to HH, representing explicit conformational states $C \to O \to I$ with physically meaningful rate constants.
- Standard numerical values to memorize: $\bar{g}_{Na} = 120$ mS/cm², $\bar{g}_K = 36$ mS/cm², $\bar{g}_L = 0.3$ mS/cm², $E_{Na} \approx +50$ mV, $E_K \approx -77$ mV, $C_m = 1\ \mu$F/cm².
- **Engineering applications** include neural prosthetics, cardiac electrophysiology, pharmacological safety screening, and neuromorphic chip design.
- ⚠️ **Common pitfalls:** unit inconsistency, confusing $x_\infty$ with $x$, omitting the temperature, and forgetting the L'Hôpital correction at singularities in $\alpha_m, \alpha_n$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hodgkin-Huxley Model Formulation for Action Potential
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT865 - MODELLING OF PHYSIOLOGICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Hodgkin-Huxley Model Formulation for Action Potential

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

The **Hodgkin-Huxley (H-H) Model** is a quantitative, conductance-based mathematical framework developed by **Alan Hodgkin and Andrew Huxley (1952)** to describe the biophysical generation and propagation of the **action potential** in the squid giant axon. It models the neuronal cell membrane as a parallel electrical equivalent circuit comprising a membrane capacitance, voltage-dependent ionic conductances (for sodium $Na^+$ and potassium $K^+$), and a constant leakage conductance, governed by a system of four coupled, non-linear ordinary differential equations (ODEs).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):**
> The H-H model is the cornerstone of computational neuroscience. It is the **first quantitative model** that successfully reproduced the *shape, threshold, refractory period, and propagation* of a nerve impulse from first principles of ionic electrochemistry. It earned Hodgkin and Huxley the **Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine in 1963**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Water Tower and Faucet" Intuition

Imagine a long water pipe (the **axon membrane**) filled with salty water. The wall of the pipe is leaky and selectively permeable:

- **Sodium ($Na^+$)** is the water outside the pipe wanting to rush *in* (down its concentration gradient) through special **gates** (Sodium channels).
- **Potassium ($K^+$)** is the water inside wanting to rush *out* through its own set of **gates** (Potassium channels).
- The **membrane capacitance** is like the elastic property of the pipe wall — it stores charge and resists sudden voltage changes.
- A small **leak** always exists for chloride ions — like a slow, untiring drip.

When a stimulus arrives, the **Na⁺ gates swing open rapidly** (the "m-gates"), letting positive charge flood in, depolarizing the membrane. Shortly after, the Na⁺ gates **inactivate** (the "h-gate" closes a portcullis) and the **K⁺ gates** (the "n-gate") slowly open, letting positive charge leave, repolarizing the cell. The Hodgkin-Huxley equations are the precise mathematical bookkeeping of *how fast* each of these gates opens and closes as a function of the membrane voltage.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bolded)

| Symbol | Quantity | Standard Value |
|---|---|---|
| $C_m$ | Specific membrane capacitance | **1.0 $\mu F/cm^2$** |
| $g_{Na}$ (max) | Maximal Na⁺ conductance | **120 $mS/cm^2$** |
| $g_K$ (max) | Maximal K⁺ conductance | **36 $mS/cm^2$** |
| $g_L$ | Leak conductance | **0.3 $mS/cm^2$** |
| $V_{Na}$ | Na⁺ Nernst reversal potential | **+50 mV** |
| $V_K$ | K⁺ Nernst reversal potential | **-77 mV** |
| $V_L$ | Leak reversal potential | **-54.4 mV** |
| $V_{rest}$ | Resting membrane potential | **-65 mV** |

> [!NOTE]
> All values are at standard room temperature of **6.3°C** (the temperature of Hodgkin and Huxley's original squid axon experiments). At body temperature (37°C), rate constants are scaled by a $Q_{10} \approx 3$ factor.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Equivalent Circuit Foundation

The H-H model begins by replacing the cell membrane with an **electrical equivalent circuit** (Hodgkin & Huxley, 1952). The lipid bilayer acts as a **capacitor** $C_m$, while the ion channels act as **variable resistors** in series with their respective **batteries** (the Nernst potentials).

**Assumptions of the H-H Model:**
1. The membrane is treated as a thin, uniform sheet separating two ionic solutions.
2. The electric field within the membrane is uniform (space-clamp condition).
3. Currents are independent (Ohm's law applies locally to each ionic species).
4. Ionic concentrations outside/inside are essentially constant during an action potential (large reservoir).
5. The membrane's capacitive current is linearly related to the rate of change of voltage.

### 2.2 The Core Current Balance Equation

The total current density crossing the membrane per unit area is the sum of the **capacitive current** plus the sum of all **ionic currents**:

$$I = C_m \frac{dV_m}{dt} + I_{Na} + I_K + I_L$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why "Why and How":** This equation is simply a statement of **Kirchhoff's Current Law** — the total applied current $I$ must either charge the membrane capacitor (changing $V_m$) or leak out through the ion channels. The genius of H-H is parameterizing each ionic current as a voltage-gated conductance.

### 2.3 Ionic Current Equations (Ohm's Law Form)

Each ionic current is modeled as a **non-linear, voltage-dependent conductance** times the **driving force** (the difference between membrane voltage $V_m$ and the Nernst potential of that ion):

$$I_{Na} = \bar{g}_{Na} \, m^3 h \, (V_m - V_{Na})$$

$$I_K = \bar{g}_K \, n^4 \, (V_m - V_K)$$

$$I_L = \bar{g}_L \, (V_m - V_L)$$

Where:
- $\bar{g}_{Na}, \bar{g}_K$ are the **maximal conductances** (constants for a given cell type).
- $m$ is the **Na⁺ activation variable** (range 0 to 1; opens fast).
- $h$ is the **Na⁺ inactivation variable** (range 0 to 1; closes slowly).
- $n$ is the **K⁺ activation variable** (range 0 to 1; opens slowly).
- $(V_m - V_{ion})$ is the **driving force** or electrochemical gradient.

The integer exponents (3, 4) reflect the **cooperative gating** of multiple independent subunits per channel: a Na⁺ channel needs **3 activation particles** and **1 inactivation particle** to be open; a K⁺ channel needs **4 activation particles**.

### 2.4 First-Order Gating Kinetics

The variables $m$, $h$, and $n$ obey **first-order kinetic equations** of the form:

$$\frac{dm}{dt} = \alpha_m (V_m)(1 - m) - \beta_m (V_m) \, m$$

$$\frac{dh}{dt} = \alpha_h (V_m)(1 - h) - \beta_h (V_m) \, h$$

$$\frac{dn}{dt} = \alpha_n (V_m)(1 - n) - \beta_n (V_m) \, n$$

Where:
- $\alpha_x (V_m)$ is the **voltage-dependent rate constant** for the transition of the gate from *closed → open*.
- $\beta_x (V_m)$ is the **voltage-dependent rate constant** for *open → closed*.

At steady-state (where $dx/dt = 0$), the gating variable $x_\infty$ is:

$$x_\infty(V_m) = \frac{\alpha_x(V_m)}{\alpha_x(V_m) + \beta_x(V_m)}$$

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master these five equations — every KTU question on the H-H model tests at least one of them.**

| # | Equation | Physical Meaning | Variables / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $I = C_m \frac{dV_m}{dt} + \bar{g}_{Na} m^3 h(V_m - V_{Na}) + \bar{g}_K n^4 (V_m - V_K) + \bar{g}_L (V_m - V_L)$ | Total current balance (Kirchhoff) | $I$: $\mu A/cm^2$, $C_m$: $\mu F/cm^2$, $V_m$: mV |
| 2 | $I_{Na} = \bar{g}_{Na} m^3 h (V_m - V_{Na})$ | Sodium current (activation cubed, inactivation linear) | $\bar{g}_{Na} = 120 \; mS/cm^2$ |
| 3 | $I_K = \bar{g}_K n^4 (V_m - V_K)$ | Potassium current (4th order activation) | $\bar{g}_K = 36 \; mS/cm^2$ |
| 4 | $\frac{dm}{dt} = \alpha_m(1-m) - \beta_m m$ | Na⁺ activation gate kinetics | $m \in [0,1]$ |
| 5 | $\frac{dn}{dt} = \alpha_n(1-n) - \beta_n n$ | K⁺ activation gate kinetics | $n \in [0,1]$ |

### 2.6 The Empirical Rate Constant Functions

The voltage-dependent rate constants, fitted by Hodgkin and Huxley to experimental voltage-clamp data at 6.3°C, are:

$$\alpha_m(V) = \frac{0.1(25 - V)}{e^{(25 - V)/10} - 1}$$

$$\beta_m(V) = 4 \, e^{-V/18}$$

$$\alpha_h(V) = 0.07 \, e^{-V/20}$$

$$\beta_h(V) = \frac{1}{e^{(30 - V)/10} + 1}$$

$$\alpha_n(V) = \frac{0.01(10 - V)}{e^{(10 - V)/10} - 1}$$

$$\beta_n(V) = 0.125 \, e^{-V/80}$$

> **Convention:** Voltages are expressed in **millivolts (mV)** measured from the **resting potential** (so $V = V_m - V_{rest}$, with $V_{rest} = 0$ being the reference).

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

The H-H model is the foundational template for:
- **Neural Prosthetics & Brain-Computer Interfaces:** Simulating neuronal firing to interpret EEG/ECoG signals.
- **Cardiac Electrophysiology:** Extended variants (e.g., Luo-Rudy, Ten Tusscher) simulate ventricular action potentials for arrhythmia drug testing.
- **Neuromorphic Computing:** Chip designers (e.g., Intel's Loihi, IBM's TrueNorth) use H-H dynamics in silicon to mimic spiking neurons with ultra-low power.
- **Pharmacology:** In-silico screening of ion channel drug candidates (e.g., sodium channel blockers like tetrodotoxin).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Membrane Current Equation

We begin with the **law of conservation of charge** applied to a small patch of membrane. The total current $I$ flowing *into* the intracellular space per unit area of membrane must equal the current that **charges the membrane capacitance** plus the current that flows *out* through the ionic channels.

**Step 1 — Define the membrane capacitance.**

The lipid bilayer stores charge $Q = C_m V_m$. Differentiating with respect to time, the **capacitive current density** is:

$$I_C = \frac{dQ}{dt} = C_m \frac{dV_m}{dt}$$

**Step 2 — Define the ionic current densities.**

By the **H-H postulate**, each ionic species $i$ carries current proportional to its conductance and the driving force:

$$I_i = g_i (V_m - V_i)$$

The conductance $g_i$ is the product of a maximal constant conductance $\bar{g}_i$ and a gating function $G_i(V_m, t)$ that depends on the **state** of the channel gates:

$$g_i(V_m, t) = \bar{g}_i \cdot G_i(V_m, t)$$

**Step 3 — Apply Kirchhoff's Current Law.**

Total applied current density $I$ splits between the capacitor and the parallel ionic conductances:

$$I = I_C + \sum_i I_i = C_m \frac{dV_m}{dt} + I_{Na} + I_K + I_L$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the ionic current expressions.**

$$I = C_m \frac{dV_m}{dt} + \bar{g}_{Na} m^3 h (V_m - V_{Na}) + \bar{g}_K n^4 (V_m - V_K) + \bar{g}_L (V_m - V_L)$$

This is the **master equation of the Hodgkin-Huxley model**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Gating Variable Kinetics

**Step 1 — Postulate a two-state Markov model for a single gating particle.**

A single gate can be in either of two states: **closed (C)** or **open (O)**. Transitions occur with voltage-dependent rates $\alpha$ (C → O) and $\beta$ (O → C):

$$C \underset{\beta}{\overset{\alpha}{\rightleftharpoons}} O$$

**Step 2 — Write the kinetic differential equation.**

If $x$ is the probability of being open, then $(1-x)$ is the probability of being closed. The net rate of opening is:

$$\frac{dx}{dt} = \alpha(1-x) - \beta x$$

**Step 3 — Expand to standard form.**

$$\frac{dx}{dt} = \alpha - \alpha x - \beta x = \alpha - (\alpha + \beta) x$$

Rearranging:

$$\frac{dx}{dt} = (\alpha + \beta)\left[ \frac{\alpha}{\alpha + \beta} - x \right]$$

**Step 4 — Identify steady-state and time constant.**

Define the **steady-state value** $x_\infty$ and the **relaxation time constant** $\tau_x$:

$$x_\infty(V_m) = \frac{\alpha(V_m)}{\alpha(V_m) + \beta(V_m)}$$

$$\tau_x(V_m) = \frac{1}{\alpha(V_m) + \beta(V_m)}$$

**Step 5 — Express the kinetics compactly.**

The gating variable equation can now be written in the elegant linear form:

$$\frac{dx}{dt} = \frac{x_\infty(V_m) - x}{\tau_x(V_m)}$$

This is the form most commonly used in numerical simulation.

> **Why the integer exponents $m^3$ and $n^4$?**
> The H-H team found that fitting their voltage-clamp data on the *delay* and *steepness* of channel opening required that the **Na⁺ channel conductance** scale with the **cube** of the activation variable (three independent, identical activation particles that must all be open) and the **K⁺ channel conductance** scale with the **fourth power** of its activation variable (four independent particles per channel).

---

### 3.3 Derivation of the Time-Course of an Action Potential (Qualitative Phase Analysis)

The H-H model captures all four classical phases of the nerve impulse through a self-regenerating feedback loop.

**Phase 0 — Resting State.**

At rest, $V_m = -65$ mV. The steady-state values are approximately $m_\infty \approx 0.05$, $h_\infty \approx 0.6$, $n_\infty \approx 0.32$. This gives small resting conductances, and the leak current dominates, holding the membrane near $V_L \approx -54$ mV (a small depolarizing leak balances the ionic gradients).

**Phase 1 — Depolarization (Upstroke).**

When a stimulus depolarizes $V_m$ past the **threshold** (about **-55 mV**), $\alpha_m$ increases dramatically, causing $m$ to rise rapidly. Since $I_{Na} \propto m^3 h$ and the driving force $(V_m - V_{Na})$ is large (about 115 mV), a massive inward Na⁺ current flows, further depolarizing the membrane. This **positive feedback** drives $V_m$ rapidly toward $V_{Na} = +50$ mV.

**Phase 2 — Inactivation (Peak & Repolarization Onset).**

Two processes kick in:
1. $h$ (inactivation) decreases on a time-scale of ~1 ms, shutting off Na⁺ flux.
2. $n$ (K⁺ activation) increases on a time-scale of ~2–5 ms, opening K⁺ channels.

**Phase 3 — Repolarization (Downstroke).**

The delayed outward K⁺ current $I_K$ now dominates, pulling $V_m$ back toward the K⁺ reversal potential $V_K = -77$ mV.

**Phase 4 — Hyperpolarization & Recovery.**

$V_m$ briefly overshoots $V_{rest}$ toward $V_K$ (the **after-hyperpolarization**) because K⁺ channels remain open. Then $n$ and $h$ slowly return to their resting values, restoring full excitability after the **absolute refractory period** (~1–2 ms).

---

### 3.4 Analytical Summary Matrix — Gating Variable Behavior During a Spike

| Variable | Steady-state at rest | On depolarization | On repolarization | Biological role |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $m$ (Na⁺ activation) | ~0.05 | Rises rapidly (~0.1 ms) | Falls | Triggers upstroke |
| $h$ (Na⁺ inactivation) | ~0.60 | Falls slowly (~1 ms delay) | Rises slowly | Ends spike, refractory period |
| $n$ (K⁺ activation) | ~0.32 | Rises slowly (~2–5 ms delay) | Falls slowly | Repolarizes membrane |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — H-H Equivalent Circuit Architecture

This block diagram maps the **electrical equivalent circuit** of the Hodgkin-Huxley model. The external current $I$ splits into four parallel branches: the membrane capacitance $C_m$ and the three ionic conductances ($g_{Na}$, $g_K$, $g_L$), each in series with their respective Nernst batteries.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Applied Current Source I] --> B[Membrane Patch]
    
    subgraph B[Membrane Equivalent Circuit]
        direction TB
        C[Membrane Capacitance Cm] 
        D[Sodium Branch: gNa times m cubed h]
        E[Potassium Branch: gK times n to the 4]
        F[Leak Branch: gL constant]
        
        D --> D1[Battery: VNa equals plus 50 mV]
        E --> E1[Battery: VK equals minus 77 mV]
        F --> F1[Battery: VL equals minus 54.4 mV]
    end
    
    C --> C1[Stores Charge: dVm/dt]
    D1 --> G[Kinetics: alpha m, beta m, alpha h, beta h]
    E1 --> H[Kinetics: alpha n, beta n]
    F1 --> I[Constant Leak]
    
    G --> J[Solve 4 Coupled ODEs]
    H --> J
    C1 --> J
    I --> J
    
    J --> K[Output: Vm of t, Action Potential Waveform]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart — Phase-by-Phase Action Potential Generation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Resting State Vm equals minus 65 mV] --> B{Stimulus crosses Threshold minus 55 mV?}
    B -- No --> A
    B -- Yes --> C[Rapid Rise of m gate]
    C --> D[INa Inward Current: m cubed h times Vm minus VNa]
    D --> E[Vm Depolarizes toward plus 50 mV]
    E --> F[Peak of Action Potential]
    F --> G[h gate Inactivates: Na channels close]
    G --> H[n gate Activates: K channels open]
    H --> I[IK Outward Current: n to the 4 times Vm minus VK]
    I --> J[Vm Repolarizes toward minus 77 mV]
    J --> K[After Hyperpolarization]
    K --> L[h and n recover to resting values]
    L --> A
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Computational Simulation Pipeline

This illustrates how a researcher would numerically solve the H-H system (e.g., using a 4th-order Runge-Kutta integrator):

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Set Initial Conditions: Vm, m, h, n] --> B[Evaluate alpha and beta at Vm]
    B --> C[Compute Gating Derivatives: dm/dt, dh/dt, dn/dt]
    C --> D[Compute Ionic Currents: INa, IK, IL]
    D --> E[Compute dVm/dt from Master Equation]
    E --> F[Apply Integration Step: Euler or RK4]
    F --> G[Update State Variables: Vm, m, h, n]
    G --> H{Simulation Time Ended?}
    H -- No --> B
    H -- Yes --> I[Plot Action Potential Vm vs t]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define the Hodgkin-Huxley model. List the four gating variables and state which ion channels they govern.**

**Model Answer:**

The Hodgkin-Huxley model is a mathematical description of action potential generation in a squid giant axon, formulated in 1952, that represents the neuronal membrane as an equivalent electrical circuit with a capacitance in parallel with voltage-dependent conductances for sodium, potassium, and a leak.

The three gating variables and their governing channels are:
- **$m$**: Na⁺ activation gate (responsible for rapid depolarization, exponent 3).
- **$h$**: Na⁺ inactivation gate (responsible for terminating the spike, exponent 1).
- **$n$**: K⁺ activation gate (responsible for repolarization, exponent 4).

These three variables, together with the membrane voltage $V_m$, form the four coupled state variables of the model.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for listing variables, 1 for exponents/roles.)*

---

**Q2.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Why does the K⁺ current in the H-H model have a fourth-power dependence on the activation variable $n$, while the Na⁺ current has a third-power dependence on $m$ but is also multiplied by $h$?**

**Model Answer:**

The integer powers arise from the **cooperative gating** of multiple independent, identical particles per ion channel:
- The **Na⁺ channel** requires **3 activation particles** (all in the open state, hence $m^3$) **AND** **1 inactivation particle** (must NOT be in the inactivated state, hence the linear factor $h$ which represents the fraction of channels whose inactivation gate is *not* closed). The total open probability is $m^3 h$.
- The **K⁺ channel** requires **4 activation particles** (all in the open state, hence $n^4$). The K⁺ channel has no separate inactivation gate in the H-H formulation.

These exponents were empirically determined by Hodgkin and Huxley to best fit the delay, steepness, and inactivation kinetics observed in their voltage-clamp experiments on the squid axon.

*(3 marks: 1 for cooperative gating concept, 1 for Na⁺ particles breakdown, 1 for K⁺ particles breakdown.)*

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

**Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — May 2023]

**(a)** [7 Marks] Derive the membrane current equation of the Hodgkin-Huxley model from the equivalent circuit representation. State clearly the physical meaning of each term.

**(b)** [7 Marks] With the help of a neat sketch, explain the time-course of the action potential in terms of the four phases: resting, depolarization, repolarization, and after-hyperpolarization. Correlate each phase with the corresponding behavior of the gating variables $m$, $h$, and $n$.

---

**Model Solution to Question A:**

**(a) Derivation of the H-H Membrane Current Equation:**

Consider a small patch of neuronal membrane. By the principle of **conservation of charge**, the net current density $I$ flowing *into* the intracellular side must equal the sum of the current that **charges the membrane capacitance** and the current that flows *out* through the ionic conductances.

**Step 1 — Capacitive Current:**
The lipid bilayer stores charge $Q$ proportional to voltage: $Q = C_m V_m$. The capacitive current density is:

$$I_C = \frac{dQ}{dt} = C_m \frac{dV_m}{dt}$$

**Step 2 — Ionic Currents (Ohm's Law per species):**
For each ion species $i$, current flows when both the channel is open (conductance $g_i$) and there is a driving force (difference between $V_m$ and the Nernst potential $V_i$):

$$I_i = g_i(V_m - V_i)$$

**Step 3 — Conductance Parametrization:**
The conductances are not constant but voltage-dependent:

$$g_{Na} = \bar{g}_{Na} m^3 h, \quad g_K = \bar{g}_K n^4, \quad g_L = \bar{g}_L \text{ (constant)}$$

**Step 4 — Kirchhoff's Current Law:**

$$\boxed{I = C_m \frac{dV_m}{dt} + \bar{g}_{Na} m^3 h (V_m - V_{Na}) + \bar{g}_K n^4 (V_m - V_K) + \bar{g}_L (V_m - V_L)}$$

**Physical meaning of each term:**
- $I$ (left side): total applied current density ($\mu A/cm^2$).
- $C_m dV_m/dt$: current that charges the membrane capacitor (no ion flux).
- $\bar{g}_{Na} m^3 h (V_m - V_{Na})$: voltage-gated Na⁺ current.
- $\bar{g}_K n^4 (V_m - V_K)$: voltage-gated K⁺ current.
- $\bar{g}_L (V_m - V_L)$: non-specific leak current (mostly Cl⁻).

*(7 marks: 2 for setup/conservation law, 2 for substitution, 1 for final equation, 2 for physical interpretation.)*

---

**(b) Time-Course of the Action Potential:**

[Reference: Mermaid diagram in SECTION 4.2]

**Phase 1 — Resting State:**
At rest, $V_m \approx -65$ mV. The steady-state values are $m_\infty \approx 0.05$, $h_\infty \approx 0.6$, $n_\infty \approx 0.32$. The membrane is poised for excitation: Na⁺ channels are closed but available (high $h$), and K⁺ channels are mostly closed (low $n$).

**Phase 2 — Depolarization (Upstroke):**
When a stimulus depolarizes $V_m$ past threshold (about $-55$ mV), $\alpha_m$ increases sharply. The $m$ gate opens within ~0.1 ms, causing $g_{Na}$ to surge. Inward Na⁺ current $I_{Na}$ depolarizes the membrane rapidly toward $V_{Na} = +50$ mV (positive feedback). The peak is reached when $I_{Na}$ equals $I_K$ (no net current).

**Phase 3 — Repolarization (Downstroke):**
Two processes terminate the upstroke:
1. $h$ falls (Na⁺ inactivation) on a 1-ms timescale, closing Na⁺ channels.
2. $n$ rises (K⁺ activation) on a 2–5 ms timescale, opening K⁺ channels.

Outward $I_K$ now dominates, pulling $V_m$ back down toward $V_K = -77$ mV.

**Phase 4 — After-Hyperpolarization & Recovery:**
Because K⁺ channels are slow to close, $V_m$ briefly undershoots $V_{rest}$, approaching $V_K$. During this period, the membrane is in the **relative refractory period** and requires a stronger-than-normal stimulus to fire. Finally, $h$ and $n$ recover to resting values, restoring full excitability.

*(7 marks: 1 for sketch, 2 for each of the 4 phases with correct variable identification.)*

---

**Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 — Alternative Choice]

**(a)** [7 Marks] Starting from the two-state Markov model of a single gating particle, derive the first-order kinetic equation for the gating variable $x$ in the H-H formulation. Express your result in the canonical form $dx/dt = (x_\infty - x)/\tau_x$ and define $x_\infty$ and $\tau_x$.

**(b)** [7 Marks] For the K⁺ activation variable $n$ at $V_m = -10$ mV (measured from rest), compute the steady-state value $n_\infty$ and the time constant $\tau_n$ using the H-H rate constant functions.

---

**Model Solution to Question B:**

**(a) Derivation of the Gating Kinetics:**

**Step 1 — Two-state kinetic scheme:**
A single gating particle exists in one of two conformations:

$$C \text{ (closed)} \underset{\beta_x}{\overset{\alpha_x}{\rightleftharpoons}} O \text{ (open)}$$

Here $\alpha_x(V_m)$ and $\beta_x(V_m)$ are the voltage-dependent forward and reverse rate constants.

**Step 2 — Master equation for opening probability $x$:**
The rate of change of the open probability equals the *rate of opening from the closed pool* minus the *rate of closing from the open pool*:

$$\frac{dx}{dt} = \alpha_x (1 - x) - \beta_x x$$

**Step 3 — Algebraic manipulation:**

$$\frac{dx}{dt} = \alpha_x - \alpha_x x - \beta_x x = \alpha_x - (\alpha_x + \beta_x) x$$

**Step 4 — Factor to reveal the canonical form:**

$$\frac{dx}{dt} = (\alpha_x + \beta_x) \left[ \frac{\alpha_x}{\alpha_x + \beta_x} - x \right]$$

**Step 5 — Identify the steady-state and time constant:**

$$x_\infty(V_m) = \frac{\alpha_x(V_m)}{\alpha_x(V_m) + \beta_x(V_m)} \quad ; \quad \tau_x(V_m) = \frac{1}{\alpha_x(V_m) + \beta_x(V_m)}$$

The canonical form is therefore:

$$\boxed{\frac{dx}{dt} = \frac{x_\infty(V_m) - x}{\tau_x(V_m)}}$$

This is the **relaxation equation**: $x$ is driven toward $x_\infty$ with time constant $\tau_x$, both of which depend on the instantaneous $V_m$.

*(7 marks: 2 for setup, 2 for derivation, 2 for canonical form, 1 for definitions.)*

---

**(b) Numerical Evaluation at $V = -10$ mV:**

Using the H-H rate constant functions:

$$\alpha_n(-10) = \frac{0.01(10 - (-10))}{e^{(10 - (-10))/10} - 1} = \frac{0.01 \times 20}{e^{2} - 1} = \frac{0.2}{7.389 - 1} = \frac{0.2}{6.389} \approx 0.0313 \; ms^{-1}$$

$$\beta_n(-10) = 0.125 \, e^{-(-10)/80} = 0.125 \, e^{0.125} = 0.125 \times 1.1331 \approx 0.1416 \; ms^{-1}$$

**Steady-state value:**

$$n_\infty = \frac{0.0313}{0.0313 + 0.1416} = \frac{0.0313}{0.1729} \approx 0.181$$

**Time constant:**

$$\tau_n = \frac{1}{0.0313 + 0.1416} = \frac{1}{0.1729} \approx 5.78 \; ms$$

**Physical Interpretation:** At $V = -10$ mV, only about 18% of the K⁺ channels are available to open, and the kinetics are relatively slow (~5.8 ms time constant). This confirms that K⁺ activation is *delayed* relative to Na⁺ activation, which is essential for the regenerative action potential.

*(7 marks: 2 for $\alpha_n$ computation, 2 for $\beta_n$ computation, 1.5 for $n_\infty$, 1.5 for $\tau_n$.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Sign convention trap:** The H-H rate functions use voltage measured from rest, so $V = V_m - V_{rest}$. A common error is plugging in $V_m$ directly (e.g., using $-65$ mV instead of $0$ mV at rest). Always confirm the convention stated in the question.
> 2. **Exponent trap:** Students frequently write $m^3$ for the K⁺ current and $n^4$ for the Na⁺ current. Remember: **Na⁺ uses $m^3 h$** (because the $h$ gate is *separate*), and **K⁺ uses $n^4$ only**.
> 3. **Units trap:** Rate constants $\alpha$ and $\beta$ have units of $ms^{-1}$ (per millisecond). Conductances are in $mS/cm^2$ (millisiemens per square centimeter). Capacitance is in $\mu F/cm^2$. Mixing units leads to dimensional errors worth 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Steady-state vs instantaneous trap:** Do not confuse $x$ (the *instantaneous* value at time $t$) with $x_\infty$ (the *steady-state* value at the current $V_m$). The ODE drives $x$ *toward* $x_\infty$.
> 5. **No channel noise term:** The H-H model is deterministic. Stochastic (noisy) versions exist (e.g., Fox & Lu, 1994) but were not part of the original formulation.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- The **H-H model (1952)** is a **4-variable ODE system** describing action potential generation, with $V_m$, $m$, $h$, $n$ as state variables.
- The **membrane equivalent circuit** consists of a capacitance $C_m$ in parallel with three ionic conductances ($g_{Na}$, $g_K$, $g_L$), each in series with a Nernst battery.
- The **master equation** is $I = C_m \frac{dV_m}{dt} + \bar{g}_{Na} m^3 h (V_m - V_{Na}) + \bar{g}_K n^4 (V_m - V_K) + \bar{g}_L (V_m - V_L)$.
- The **integer exponents** ($m^3$, $n^4$) reflect the number of independent activation particles per channel; $h$ is the *inactivation* variable for Na⁺.
- Each gating variable obeys $\frac{dx}{dt} = \frac{x_\infty - x}{\tau_x}$, where $x_\infty$ and $\tau_x$ are voltage-dependent.
- The **rate constants** $\alpha_x$ and $\beta_x$ are empirical fits to voltage-clamp data, valid at 6.3°C.
- The **action potential** has 4 phases: resting, depolarization (driven by Na⁺ activation), repolarization (Na⁺ inactivation + K⁺ activation), and after-hyperpolarization.
- The **threshold** is approximately $-55$ mV, where the positive-feedback loop of $m$ and $V_m$ becomes self-sustaining.
- The **absolute refractory period** is set by the slow recovery of $h$.
- **Real-world uses:** cardiac modeling, neuromorphic chips, neuro-prosthetics, drug screening.
- **Assumptions:** uniform membrane (space clamp), constant ionic concentrations, independent channel gating, deterministic dynamics.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Compartmental Models
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEBT865 - MODELLING OF PHYSIOLOGICAL SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module Module 3 > - **Topic:** Compartmental Models # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition A **compartmental model** is a mathematical framework used in physiological system modeling that represents a biological system as a finite number of discrete, well-mixed, interacting subunits called **compartments**. Each compartment is assumed to be a homogeneous entity in which any substance (drug, tracer, hormone, ion, etc.) entering it instantaneously distributes uniformly throughout its volume. In the formal KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, a compartmental model is described as: > A lumped-parameter, deterministic model in which the state variables are the quantities (or concentrations) of a substance contained within a set of well-mixed compartments, coupled to one another by first-order (or higher-order) transfer kinetics governed by the **principle of mass balance** and **conservation of matter**. A **compartment** is therefore defined by three fundamental properties: 1. **Homogeneity** — the substance is uniformly distributed within the compartment at all times. 2. **Identifiability** — the compartment has a defined volume ($V$) and contains a quantifiable amount ($q$) of the substance at concentration $C = q/V$. 3. **Inter-connectivity** — it exchanges material with one or more adjacent compartments or with the external environment through defined rate constants ($k$). ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain-English Explanation) > [!IMPORTANT] > **The "Water Tanks Connected by Pipes" Analogy** > > Imagine a large industrial water-treatment plant. The plant consists of several **storage tanks** (the compartments) connected by **pipes with valves** (the transfer pathways). Each tank has a specific capacity and contains water (the substance of interest). When water flows in and out, it is assumed to be **perfectly mixed** in each tank — meaning a sample taken from the top has the same concentration as one from the bottom. > > The **rate at which water flows** through a pipe is governed by a **valve setting** (the rate constant, $k$). Some tanks receive water directly from a **source pump** (an input), while others drain to a **sewer line** (an output/sink). > > In your body, the "tanks" are physiological spaces such as the **blood plasma, interstitial fluid, intracellular fluid**, or specific **organs (liver, kidney, muscle)**. The "pipes and valves" are **blood flow, diffusion, active transport, and metabolic clearance**. The "water" is a **drug, glucose, hormone, sodium, or radioactive tracer**. ## 1.3 Key Terminology (KTU 2024 High-Yield Vocabulary) - **Compartment**: A kinetically homogeneous space. - **Volume of distribution ($V_d$)**: The apparent volume into which a substance disperses. - **Rate constant ($k$)**: First-order proportionality constant with units of **time⁻¹**. - **Mass balance (continuity) equation**: $\frac{dq_i}{dt} = \sum (\text{Inputs to } i) - \sum (\text{Outputs from } i)$. - **Steady state**: All derivatives equal zero, so $\frac{dq}{dt} = 0$. - **Tracer**: A detectable, physiologically inert marker used to probe a compartment. - **Mean Transit Time ($\bar{t}$)**: The average time a molecule spends in a compartment. > [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Highlight:** In Module 3 of PEEBT865, compartmental models form the bridge between **anatomical reality** and **mathematical tractability**. They are the most widely used quantitative tools in pharmacokinetics, endocrinology, nuclear medicine, and tracer-kinetic studies. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Classification of Compartmental Models Compartmental models are classified by: **(a) Number of compartments:** - **One-compartment model** — body treated as a single homogeneous unit. - **Two-compartment model** — central (plasma) and peripheral (tissue) compartments. - **Multi-compartment ($n$-compartment) model** — used for highly distributed substances. **(b) Kinetics of transfer:** - **First-order kinetics** — rate $\propto$ amount, characterized by constant $k$. - **Zero-order kinetics** — rate is constant (saturated systems). - **Michaelis–Menten (saturable) kinetics** — rate $\propto \frac{V_{\max} C}{K_m + C}$. **(c) Linearity:** - **Linear (LTI)** — superposition holds; transfer rates independent of concentration. - **Nonlinear** — rates depend on concentration; requires numerical methods. ## 2.2 The Fundamental Mass-Balance Equation For any compartment $i$ containing quantity $q_i(t)$: $$\frac{dq_i}{dt} = \sum_{j \neq i} k_{ji}\, q_j - \sum_{j \neq i} k_{ij}\, q_i - k_{0i}\, q_i + R_i(t)$$ Where: - $k_{ji}$ = transfer rate from compartment $j$ to compartment $i$ **[time⁻¹]** - $k_{ij}$ = transfer rate from compartment $i$ to compartment $j$ **[time⁻¹]** - $k_{0i}$ = elimination (clearance) rate from compartment $i$ to the outside - $R_i(t)$ = external infusion/input rate into compartment $i$ **[amount · time⁻¹]** ## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Equations for KTU 2024 Board Examinations** | # | Concept | Equation | Units / Notes | |---|---------|----------|---------------| | 1 | Mass balance (general) | $\frac{dq}{dt} = R_{\text{in}} - R_{\text{out}}$ | amount · time⁻¹ | | 2 | One-compartment decay | $\frac{dq}{dt} = -k\,q$ | first-order | | 3 | Impulse response | $q(t) = q_0\, e^{-kt}$ | $q_0$ = initial amount | | 4 | Continuous infusion, steady state | $q_{ss} = \dfrac{R}{k}$ | $R$ = infusion rate | | 5 | Continuous infusion, transient | $q(t) = \dfrac{R}{k}\bigl(1 - e^{-kt}\bigr) + q_0\, e^{-kt}$ | general solution | | 6 | Plasma concentration | $C(t) = \dfrac{q(t)}{V_d}$ | $V_d$ = volume of distribution | | 7 | Clearance | $CL = k \cdot V_d$ | mL · min⁻¹ | | 8 | Two-compartment central | $\frac{dq_1}{dt} = -k_{12} q_1 - k_{10} q_1 + k_{21} q_2 + R$ | central compartment | | 9 | Two-compartment peripheral | $\frac{dq_2}{dt} = k_{12} q_1 - k_{21} q_2$ | peripheral compartment | | 10 | Stewart's theorem (mean transit) | $\bar{t} = \dfrac{\int_0^{\infty} t\, C(t)\, dt}{\int_0^{\infty} C(t)\, dt} = \dfrac{V}{F}$ | indicator dilution | | 11 | Tracer quantity from conc. curve | $M = F \cdot \int_0^{\infty} C(t)\, dt$ | $F$ = flow rate | | 12 | Volume of distribution (tracer) | $V = F \cdot \bar{t}$ | apparent volume | | 13 | Half-life | $t_{1/2} = \dfrac{\ln 2}{k} = \dfrac{0.693}{k}$ | first-order | | 14 | Mass conservation | $\sum q_i = \text{const}$ (no elimination) | total mass check | > [!NOTE] > Use `\vert` or `\mid` (NOT bare $\vert x \vert$) when writing absolute value inside a table cell, to keep the markdown table unbroken. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering & Clinical Utility - **Pharmacokinetics & Drug Dosing**: Predicting plasma drug levels, designing infusion protocols, calculating loading and maintenance doses. - **Nuclear Medicine**: Quantifying organ uptake of radiopharmaceuticals (e.g., ⁹⁹ᵐTc-DMSA for renal imaging). - **Endocrinology**: Modeling hormone distribution among plasma, free, and protein-bound pools. - **Toxicology**: Predicting accumulation of heavy metals in bone, fat, or specific organs. - **Hemodialysis Engineering**: Designing artificial kidney clearance parameters using two-compartment urea kinetics. - **Anesthesia**: Modelling volatile anesthetic uptake via five-compartment lung models. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: The One-Compartment Model (Impulse / Bolus Input) **Problem Setup:** A quantity $q_0$ of a substance is injected instantaneously (IV bolus) into a single well-mixed compartment of volume $V$. The substance is eliminated at a first-order rate constant $k$ **[time⁻¹]**. **Step 1 — Write the governing mass-balance equation.** The compartment has only one outflow (elimination) and no inflow after $t = 0$. By the principle of mass balance: $$\frac{dq}{dt} = \underbrace{0}_{\text{Input}} - \underbrace{k\,q}_{\text{Elimination}}$$ **Step 2 — Recognize this as a first-order linear ODE.** $$\frac{dq}{dt} + k\,q = 0$$ **Step 3 — Apply the method of separation of variables.** $$\frac{dq}{q} = -k\,dt$$ **Step 4 — Integrate both sides from $t=0$ to $t$.** $$\int_{q_0}^{q(t)} \frac{dq}{q} = \int_{0}^{t} -k\,dt$$ $$\bigl[\ln q\bigr]_{q_0}^{q(t)} = -k\,t$$ **Step 5 — Solve for $q(t)$.** $$\ln\!\bigl(q(t)\bigr) - \ln\!\bigl(q_0\bigr) = -k\,t$$ $$\ln\!\left(\frac{q(t)}{q_0}\right) = -k\,t$$ $$\boxed{\,q(t) = q_0\, e^{-k t}\,}$$ **Step 6 — Convert to plasma concentration** $C(t) = \dfrac{q(t)}{V}$: $$C(t) = C_0\, e^{-k t}, \quad \text{where } C_0 = \dfrac{q_0}{V}$$ **Step 7 — Derive the half-life.** Set $C(t_{1/2}) = C_0/2$: $$\frac{1}{2} = e^{-k t_{1/2}}$$ $$\ln 2 = k\, t_{1/2}$$ $$\boxed{\,t_{1/2} = \dfrac{\ln 2}{k} = \dfrac{0.693}{k}\,}$$ --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2: One-Compartment Model with Continuous Infusion **Problem Setup:** A drug is infused into a one-compartment system at a constant rate $R$ **[amount · time⁻¹]**, beginning at $t=0$. The compartment starts empty ($q(0)=0$). **Step 1 — Write the mass balance with input.** $$\frac{dq}{dt} = R - k\,q$$ **Step 2 — Rearrange into standard linear-ODE form.** $$\frac{dq}{dt} + k\,q = R$$ **Step 3 — Find the integrating factor.** The integrating factor is $\mu(t) = e^{\int k\,dt} = e^{k t}$. **Step 4 — Multiply through by $\mu(t)$.** $$e^{k t}\frac{dq}{dt} + k\, e^{k t} q = R\, e^{k t}$$ The left side is the exact derivative $\dfrac{d}{dt}\!\bigl(q\, e^{k t}\bigr)$: $$\frac{d}{dt}\!\bigl(q\, e^{k t}\bigr) = R\, e^{k t}$$ **Step 5 — Integrate from $0$ to $t$.** $$q(t)\, e^{k t} - q(0) = \frac{R}{k}\!\left(e^{k t} - 1\right)$$ With $q(0) = 0$: $$q(t)\, e^{k t} = \frac{R}{k}\!\left(e^{k t} - 1\right)$$ **Step 6 — Solve for $q(t)$.** $$\boxed{\,q(t) = \frac{R}{k}\!\left(1 - e^{-k t}\right)\,}$$ **Step 7 — Identify the steady-state value** by letting $t \to \infty$: $$q_{ss} = \lim_{t \to \infty} \frac{R}{k}\!\left(1 - e^{-k t}\right) = \frac{R}{k}$$ At steady state, $\dfrac{dq}{dt}=0 \;\Longrightarrow\; 0 = R - k\,q_{ss} \;\Longrightarrow\; q_{ss} = R/k$. ✓ **Step 8 — Time to reach 90 % of steady state.** Set $q(t) = 0.9\,q_{ss}$: $$0.9 = 1 - e^{-k t}$$ $$e^{-k t} = 0.1 \;\Longrightarrow\; k t = \ln 10 \approx 2.303$$ $$\boxed{\,t_{90\%} \approx \dfrac{2.303}{k} \approx 3.32\, t_{1/2}\,}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering interpretation:** Clinically, ≈ **3–5 half-lives** are required for a drug to reach practical steady state — this is why loading doses are sometimes used. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Two-Compartment Open Model (Central + Peripheral) **Problem Setup:** A substance is injected as a bolus $q_0$ into the **central compartment (1)** — typically blood plasma. It distributes to a **peripheral compartment (2)** — typically well-perfused tissues — and is eliminated only from the central compartment. **Step 1 — Mass balance for compartment 1 (central):** $$\frac{dq_1}{dt} = -k_{12}\, q_1 - k_{10}\, q_1 + k_{21}\, q_2$$ **Step 2 — Mass balance for compartment 2 (peripheral):** $$\frac{dq_2}{dt} = k_{12}\, q_1 - k_{21}\, q_2$$ **Step 3 — Assume exponential solutions** $q_1(t) = A\, e^{-\alpha t}$ and $q_2(t) = B\, e^{-\alpha t}$. Substitute into compartment 2: $$-\alpha B\, e^{-\alpha t} = k_{12} A\, e^{-\alpha t} - k_{21} B\, e^{-\alpha t}$$ $$-\alpha B = k_{12} A - k_{21} B$$ $$B\bigl(k_{21} - \alpha\bigr) = k_{12} A \;\Longrightarrow\; \frac{B}{A} = \frac{k_{12}}{k_{21} - \alpha}$$ **Step 4 — Substitute into compartment 1:** $$-\alpha A = -k_{12} A - k_{10} A + k_{21}\frac{k_{12} A}{k_{21}-\alpha}$$ Divide by $A$: $$-\alpha = -k_{12} - k_{10} + \frac{k_{21}\, k_{12}}{k_{21} - \alpha}$$ Multiply by $(k_{21} - \alpha)$: $$-\alpha(k_{21}-\alpha) = -(k_{12}+k_{10})(k_{21}-\alpha) + k_{21} k_{12}$$ **Step 5 — Expand and simplify.** $$-\alpha k_{21} + \alpha^2 = -k_{12} k_{21} + k_{12}\alpha - k_{10} k_{21} + k_{10}\alpha + k_{21} k_{12}$$ Cancel $-k_{12} k_{21}$ and $+k_{21} k_{12}$: $$-\alpha k_{21} + \alpha^2 = k_{12}\alpha - k_{10} k_{21} + k_{10}\alpha$$ Collect terms in $\alpha$: $$\alpha^2 - \alpha k_{21} - k_{12}\alpha - k_{10}\alpha + k_{10} k_{21} = 0$$ $$\boxed{\,\alpha^2 - (k_{12} + k_{21} + k_{10})\,\alpha + k_{10} k_{21} = 0\,}$$ **Step 6 — Apply the quadratic formula.** Let $a=1$, $b=-(k_{12}+k_{21}+k_{10})$, $c=k_{10} k_{21}$: $$\alpha = \frac{(k_{12}+k_{21}+k_{10}) \pm \sqrt{(k_{12}+k_{21}+k_{10})^2 - 4\,k_{10} k_{21}}}{2}$$ The two roots are conventionally labelled $\alpha$ (fast, distribution) and $\beta$ (slow, elimination), with $\alpha > \beta > 0$. **Step 7 — General solution** (superposition): $$q_1(t) = A\, e^{-\alpha t} + B\, e^{-\beta t}$$ $$q_2(t) = A'\!\left(e^{-\beta t} - e^{-\alpha t}\right)$$ Constants $A$, $B$, $A'$ are found from initial conditions $q_1(0)=q_0$, $q_2(0)=0$, and continuity. --- ## 3.4 Derivation 4: Tracer-Based Volume of Distribution (Stewart's Method) **Problem Setup:** A known mass $M$ of an indicator (e.g., indocyanine green dye) is injected as a bolus into the inlet of an organ. The outflow concentration $C(t)$ is measured downstream, and the flow rate is $F$ **[volume · time⁻¹]**. **Step 1 — Mass balance (conservation of tracer mass).** All injected mass must eventually leave: $$M = F \int_{0}^{\infty} C(t)\, dt$$ **Step 2 — Define the mean transit time** $\bar{t}$: $$\bar{t} = \frac{\int_{0}^{\infty} t\, C(t)\, dt}{\int_{0}^{\infty} C(t)\, dt}$$ **Step 3 — Multiply equation (1) by $\bar{t}$.** $$M\, \bar{t} = F \int_{0}^{\infty} t\, C(t)\, dt$$ Substitute $M$ from Step 1: $$F \!\left(\int_{0}^{\infty} C(t)\, dt\right)\bar{t} = F \int_{0}^{\infty} t\, C(t)\, dt$$ Cancelling $F$ recovers the definition of $\bar{t}$. ✓ **Step 4 — Solve for the volume of distribution.** $$V = F\, \bar{t} = \frac{M}{\int_{0}^{\infty} C(t)\, dt} \cdot \bar{t} \quad \text{(Stewart–Hamilton relation)}$$ $$\boxed{\,V = M \cdot \frac{\bar{t}}{\int_{0}^{\infty} C(t)\, dt}\,}$$ This elegant result allows organ volume to be measured from a single injection — a powerful, non-invasive tool in cardiac output measurement and renal-function studies. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Two-Compartment Model ```mermaid flowchart LR ExtIn["External Source
Infusion R"]:::input C1["Central Compartment
Plasma, V1, q1 t"]:::central C2["Peripheral Compartment
Tissue, V2, q2 t"]:::peripheral ExtOut["Elimination / Sink
k10 pathway"]:::output OBS["Observables
C1 t and C2 t"]:::obs ExtIn -- "Input R" --> C1 C1 -- "k12 transfer" --> C2 C2 -- "k21 return" --> C1 C1 -- "k10 elimination" --> ExtOut C1 -. "Measurement probe" .-> OBS C2 -. "Measurement probe" .-> OBS classDef input fill:#fff3b0,stroke:#a07a00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef central fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0a58ca,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef peripheral fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#146c43,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef output fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#b02a37,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef obs fill:#e2e3e5,stroke:#41464b,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` ## 4.2 Signal-Flow (Mason-Gain) Representation ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeS["Source
R s"]:::src nodeInt["Integrator
1 over s"]:::intgr nodeG1["Gain k10"]:::gain nodeG2["Gain k12"]:::gain nodeF["Feedback
k21"]:::fb nodeOut["Output
q1 t"]:::outp nodeS --> nodeInt nodeInt --> nodeOut nodeOut -- "branch" --> nodeG1 nodeOut -- "branch" --> nodeG2 nodeG2 -- "to peripheral" --> nodeF nodeF -- "return signal" --> nodeInt classDef src fill:#fff3b0,stroke:#a07a00,stroke-width:2px classDef intgr fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0a58ca,stroke-width:2px classDef gain fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#146c43,stroke-width:2px classDef fb fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#b02a37,stroke-width:2px classDef outp fill:#e2e3e5,stroke:#41464b,stroke-width:2px ``` ## 4.3 Hierarchical Decomposition of Compartmental Modelling ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Physiological System
Real Organism"]:::root B["Identify Pools
Plasma, Tissue, Cell"]:::level1 C["Define Transfer Pathways
Flow, Diffusion, Active"]:::level1 D["Write Mass Balance
dq over dt equation"]:::level1 E["Solve ODEs
Analytical or Numerical"]:::level1 F["Validate against Data
Tracer Curves"]:::level1 G["Predict Clinical Output
Dose, Half-life, AUC"]:::level1 A --> B --> C --> D --> E --> F --> G classDef root fill:#fff3b0,stroke:#a07a00,stroke-width:2px classDef level1 fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0a58ca,stroke-width:2px ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagrams:** The first figure shows the classical block diagram used in pharmacology textbooks. The second is the **signal-flow / control-systems** representation, where the central compartment is treated as an integrator and the rate constants are system "gains." This dual view is what allows compartmental models to be analysed using **Laplace transforms** and **Mason's gain formula** in the KTU biomedical-engineering curriculum. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) > **Q1.** [KTU University Exam – May 2024] > **Define a compartmental model. List the assumptions made in a one-compartment pharmacokinetic model.** **Model Answer:** A compartmental model represents the body as a set of discrete, well-mixed, kinetically homogeneous compartments that exchange material via first-order rate processes. Each compartment is characterized by a volume $V$, an amount $q(t)$, and a concentration $C(t) = q(t)/V$. **Assumptions of the one-compartment model:** 1. The body is a single, instantaneously mixed homogeneous unit. 2. Elimination follows first-order kinetics: rate $= k\,q(t)$. 3. Distribution is instantaneous (no transport delay). 4. The drug/substance does not bind irreversibly to tissues. 5. All input enters directly into this single compartment (typically the plasma). --- > **Q2.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] > **What is meant by the "volume of distribution" of a drug? Why can it exceed total body volume?** **Model Answer:** The volume of distribution $V_d$ is the **apparent volume** required to contain the total amount of drug in the body at the same concentration as that measured in the plasma: $$V_d = \frac{q_{\text{body}}}{C_{\text{plasma}}}$$ It can exceed total body water (≈ 42 L) or even total body mass (≈ 70 L) because the drug may be: - **Highly lipophilic** → sequestered into adipose tissue. - **Strongly protein-bound or tissue-bound** → plasma concentration is very small, making $V_d = q/C$ numerically very large (e.g., chloroquine has $V_d \approx 13{,}000$ L). --- ## 5.2 Part B Question — Internal Choice (14 Marks) > **Q3.** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme] > > **(A) One-Compartment IV Bolus + Repeated Dosing Derivation, AND** > **(B) Two-Compartment Open Model with Matrix Formulation** > > *Attempt any ONE.* --- ### **Question 3(A) — 14 Marks** **(a)** Derive the equation describing the plasma concentration of a drug administered as a single IV bolus dose $D_0$ in a one-compartment model. Hence obtain an expression for the elimination half-life. **[7 marks]** **(b)** If a second identical bolus dose $D_0$ is administered at time $t = \tau$, derive the equation for the plasma concentration for $t > \tau$ and explain the concept of **accumulation factor** in multiple dosing. **[7 marks]** **Model Solution:** **(a) Part — Derivation of single-dose concentration:** The mass balance for the central compartment is: $$\frac{dq}{dt} = -k\,q$$ Separating variables: $$\int_{q_0}^{q(t)} \frac{dq}{q} = -k \int_0^t dt \;\Longrightarrow\; q(t) = q_0\, e^{-k t}$$ Since $q_0 = D_0$ and $C(t) = q(t)/V_d$: $$\boxed{\,C(t) = \frac{D_0}{V_d}\, e^{-k t} = C_0\, e^{-k t}\,}$$ For the half-life, set $C(t_{1/2}) = C_0/2$: $$\frac{1}{2} = e^{-k t_{1/2}} \;\Longrightarrow\; t_{1/2} = \frac{\ln 2}{k} = \frac{0.693}{k}$$ **(b) Part — Repeated dosing and accumulation:** Just before the second dose at $t = \tau$, the residual concentration is $C_1^- = C_0\, e^{-k \tau}$. Immediately after the second bolus, the concentration jumps by $C_0$ (linear superposition holds): $$C_1^+ = C_0\, e^{-k \tau} + C_0 = C_0\!\left(1 + e^{-k \tau}\right)$$ For $t > \tau$, the concentration decays as: $$C(t) = C_0\!\left(1 + e^{-k \tau}\right) e^{-k (t-\tau)}$$ For $n$ repeated doses, the **accumulation factor** $R_{\text{acc}}$ at steady state is: $$\boxed{\,R_{\text{acc}} = \frac{C_{\max}^{ss}}{C_{\max}^{1\,\text{st dose}}} = \frac{1}{1 - e^{-k \tau}}\,}$$ This shows that **smaller dosing intervals** ($\tau \ll t_{1/2}$) lead to **higher accumulation**, while intervals much longer than the half-life produce negligible accumulation. --- ### **Question 3(B) — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)** **(a)** For a **two-compartment open model** with central compartment 1 and peripheral compartment 2, write the governing differential equations, form the system matrix, and obtain the characteristic equation. **[7 marks]** **(b)** Briefly explain the use of **Stewart's indicator-dilution principle** to compute the volume of an organ, and derive the relation $V = F \bar{t}$. **[7 marks]** **Model Solution:** **(a) Part — Matrix formulation:** The governing equations are: $$\frac{dq_1}{dt} = -(k_{12} + k_{10})\, q_1 + k_{21}\, q_2$$ $$\frac{dq_2}{dt} = k_{12}\, q_1 - k_{21}\, q_2$$ In matrix form $\dot{\mathbf{q}} = \mathbf{A}\,\mathbf{q}$: $$\begin{bmatrix} \dot{q}_1 \\ \dot{q}_2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -(k_{12}+k_{10}) & k_{21} \\ k_{12} & -k_{21} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} q_1 \\ q_2 \end{bmatrix}$$ The eigenvalues of $\mathbf{A}$ are the roots of $\det(\mathbf{A} - \lambda \mathbf{I}) = 0$: $$\det\begin{bmatrix} -(k_{12}+k_{10}) - \lambda & k_{21} \\ k_{12} & -k_{21} - \lambda \end{bmatrix} = 0$$ $$(k_{12}+k_{10}+\lambda)(k_{21}+\lambda) - k_{12} k_{21} = 0$$ Expanding: $$\lambda^2 + (k_{12} + k_{21} + k_{10})\,\lambda + k_{10} k_{21} = 0$$ Solving with the quadratic formula: $$\boxed{\,\lambda_{1,2} = \frac{-(k_{12}+k_{21}+k_{10}) \pm \sqrt{(k_{12}+k_{21}+k_{10})^2 -
