4 k_{10} k_{21}}}{2}\,}$$ The two negative eigenvalues $-\alpha$ and $-\beta$ (with $\alpha > \beta > 0$) govern the **fast distribution phase** and the **slow elimination phase** respectively. **(b) Part — Stewart's principle:** When a known mass $M$ of indicator is injected as a bolus into the inflow of an organ, mass conservation requires that the entire mass eventually exits, so: $$M = F \int_0^{\infty} C(t)\, dt$$ The **mean transit time** $\bar{t}$ is defined as the first moment of the concentration–time curve: $$\bar{t} = \frac{\int_0^{\infty} t\, C(t)\, dt}{\int_0^{\infty} C(t)\, dt}$$ Multiplying the mass-conservation equation by $\bar{t}$ and dividing by $\int C\,dt$: $$V = F \cdot \bar{t} = M \cdot \frac{\bar{t}}{\int_0^{\infty} C(t)\, dt}$$ $$\boxed{\,V = F\, \bar{t}\,}$$ This is the classical **Stewart–Hamilton relation**, the foundation of indicator-dilution measurement of organ volumes and cardiac output. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warnings > [!WARNING] > **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks** > > 1. **Forgetting units of rate constants**: $k$ has units of **time⁻¹**, NOT amount · time⁻¹. Many students omit this on derivations and lose 1 mark. > 2. **Mixing up $V_d$ and dose**: $V_d$ is a property of the system (drug + body), not of the dose. Do NOT write $V_d = D/C$ without defining which dose and which concentration. > 3. **Sign error in two-compartment matrix**: The off-diagonal terms flip sign; write out the matrix explicitly to avoid losing a full 7-mark sub-part. > 4. **Stewart's relation**: Many students write $V = F \int C\,dt$ — that is **wrong**; the correct expression is $V = F \bar{t}$, with $\bar{t}$ being the **first moment**. > 5. **Steady-state assumption**: Setting $dq/dt = 0$ without explicitly stating it loses a mark in derivations. > 6. **In multiple-dosing problems**, students forget that the concentration **jumps discontinuously** at the dosing instant — this must be written as two separate equations (just before and just after the dose). --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Module 3 — Compartmental Models)** > > - A **compartment** is *well-mixed, homogeneous, and kinetically uniform*; not necessarily an anatomical space. > - The **governing equation** is a first-order ODE based on the **principle of mass balance**. > - **One-compartment bolus** → $C(t) = C_0 e^{-kt}$; half-life $t_{1/2} = 0.693/k$. > - **One-compartment continuous infusion** → $q(t) = (R/k)(1 - e^{-kt})$; steady state $q_{ss} = R/k$. > - Steady state is reached in **3–5 half-lives**; ≈ $3.32\,t_{1/2}$ for 90 %. > - **Two-compartment model** yields a **bi-exponential** decay: $q_1(t) = A e^{-\alpha t} + B e^{-\beta t}$. > - The **eigenvalues** of the state-transition matrix give the rate constants $\alpha$ and $\beta$. > - **Clearance** $CL = k V_d$ — a fundamental pharmacokinetic identity. > - **Stewart–Hamilton** indicator-dilution: $V = F \bar{t}$, where $\bar{t}$ is the **first moment** of $C(t)$. > - **Volume of distribution** can exceed total body water due to tissue binding or lipophilicity. > - **Multiple dosing** leads to accumulation: $R_{\text{acc}} = 1/(1 - e^{-k\tau})$. > - Always check **mass conservation** as a sanity check on the solution.

topics.title: Simplified Models of Neurons: Integrate-and-Fire Neurons, Spike-Response Model Neurons, Rate-Based Models
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEBT865 - MODELLING OF PHYSIOLOGICAL SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Simplified Models of Neurons: Integrate-and-Fire Neurons, Spike-Response Model Neurons, Rate-Based Models ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Simplified neuron models** are mathematically tractable abstractions of biological neurons that capture essential computational properties (input integration, threshold-based spiking, refractory behavior) while discarding the biophysical complexity of the Hodgkin-Huxley formalism. According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for *Modelling of Physiological Systems* (Module 3), these models form a hierarchy of abstraction: 1. **Integrate-and-Fire (IF) Neuron Model** — A leaky integrator with a hard threshold; when the membrane potential reaches a threshold $V_{th}$, a spike is emitted and the voltage is reset. 2. **Spike-Response Model (SRM)** — A generalization of the IF model where spike generation is governed by *response kernels* ($\kappa$ and $\eta$) that describe postsynaptic potentials and after-spike refractoriness. 3. **Rate-Based Model** — A population-level abstraction that replaces discrete spikes with a continuous **firing rate** $r(t)$, eliminating temporal dynamics in favor of mean-field activity. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** These models are evaluated as a *comparative hierarchy* — students must know the **computational cost**, the **biological realism**, and the **equations** for each. Memorize the form of $\tau \frac{dV}{dt}$, the spike condition $V(t) \geq V_{th}$, and the rate transfer function $r = F(I)$. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Intuition Box — The "Water Bucket" Analogy** Think of a neuron as a **leaky water bucket**: - **Integrate-and-Fire**: Water (input current) drips in, raising the level (membrane voltage). The bucket has a small hole at the bottom (leak). When water reaches a marked line (threshold), you empty the bucket (spike + reset) and start filling again. - **Spike-Response Model**: Same bucket, but now each past "splash" (past spike or input) leaves a temporary ripple ($\kappa$ kernel) that decays over time, and the bucket itself becomes briefly "sticky" after emptying ($\eta$ refractory kernel). - **Rate-Based Model**: Instead of tracking individual droplets (spikes), you only count **how fast water flows in per second** — the *rate*. The bucket's level now represents the average firing rate of an entire pool of neurons. ### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Parameters | Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value | Unit | |-----------|--------|---------------|------| | Membrane time constant | $\tau_m$ | **10–20 ms** | ms | | Resting potential | $V_{rest}$ | **−70 mV** | mV | | Spike threshold | $V_{th}$ | **−55 mV** | mV | | Reset potential | $V_{reset}$ | **−75 mV** | mV | | Absolute refractory period | $t_{ref}$ | **1–2 ms** | ms | | Input resistance | $R$ | **10–100 MΩ** | MΩ | | Membrane capacitance | $C$ | **100–300 pF** | pF | ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Integrate-and-Fire (IF) Neuron — Operational Breakdown The IF neuron is the **workhorse** of computational neuroscience. It is a one-dimensional system characterized by a single state variable: the membrane potential $V(t)$. **Why does it work?** Real neurons integrate synaptic inputs on a time scale governed by the **membrane time constant** $\tau_m = R_m C_m$. The IF model captures this with a *leaky* linear differential equation. The spike itself is replaced by a **threshold-reset rule** — a phenomenological abstraction. **How does it evolve?** - **Step 1 (Subthreshold):** Below threshold, $V(t)$ obeys the passive membrane equation. - **Step 2 (Threshold Crossing):** As soon as $V(t^-) < V_{th}$ and $V(t^+) \geq V_{th}$, a spike is registered and $V$ is instantly reset to $V_{reset}$. - **Step 3 (Refractoriness):** During $t_{ref}$, no further spikes can be fired regardless of input. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Insight:** The IF model is **not** a dynamical system in the strict sense — the spike is a *discontinuous* event. To avoid the discontinuity, the **Leaky Integrate-and-Fire (LIF)** uses exponential leak, and the **Adaptive IF** adds a slow adaptation variable $w(t)$. ### 2.2 The Spike-Response Model (SRM) — Operational Breakdown The SRM (Gerstner, 1995) generalizes the IF neuron by replacing the differential equation with an **integral formulation** involving two kernels: - **$\kappa(t)$** — postsynaptic potential kernel: response of $V$ to an input spike at time $t-s$. - **$\eta(t)$** — after-spike kernel: negative reset/refractory contribution following a spike at $t_i^f$. **Why does it exist?** The IF model assumes that all inputs sum *linearly* in $V$. In real neurons, **after-spike refractoriness** is strong, history-dependent, and *nonlinear*. The SRM captures this via $\eta$, which can be tailored to match experimental data. **How does it work?** - **Step 1:** Compute the total membrane potential as the **sum of two convolutions** — one over inputs, one over past spikes. - **Step 2:** Apply the **threshold condition** $V(t) \geq \vartheta(t)$ where $\vartheta(t)$ is a dynamic threshold. - **Step 3:** Upon crossing, emit a spike and add $\eta$ to the refractory term. ### 2.3 Rate-Based Models — Operational Breakdown Rate models abandon spike timing entirely. The state variable is the **average firing rate** $r(t)$ of a population, governed by: $$\tau_r \frac{dr}{dt} = -r + F(I_{syn})$$ where $F(\cdot)$ is a **sigmoidal transfer function** (often called the **f-I curve** or **activation function**): $$F(I) = \frac{r_{max}}{1 + \exp(-\beta(I - I_{0}))}$$ **Why use rate models?** When simulating **large networks** ($N \geq 10^4$ neurons), individual spike resolution is computationally prohibitive. Rate models reduce the system to one ODE per population — a **massive dimensionality reduction**. **How does it work?** - **Step 1:** Define a population with mean input current $I_{syn}(t)$. - **Step 2:** Pass through a static nonlinearity $F$ to get the equilibrium rate. - **Step 3:** Evolve $r(t)$ toward $F(I_{syn})$ with time constant $\tau_r$. ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Mandatory Formulas for KTU 2024 ESE — Memorize the exact equation forms below.** | Model | Core Equation | Spike/Rate Condition | Parameters | |-------|---------------|----------------------|------------| | **Leaky IF** | $\tau_m \dfrac{dV}{dt} = -(V - V_{rest}) + R I(t)$ | $V(t) \geq V_{th} \Rightarrow V \leftarrow V_{reset}$ | $\tau_m, V_{th}, V_{reset}, R$ | | **IF (threshold-free)** | $\tau_m \dfrac{dV}{dt} = -V + R I(t)$ | Same as above | Same as above | | **SRM (SRM$_0$)** | $V(t) = \eta(t - \hat{t}) + \int_{-\infty}^{t} \kappa(t-s)\, I(s)\, ds$ | $V(t) \geq \vartheta$ | $\kappa, \eta, \vartheta$ | | **Rate Model (Wilson-Cowan)** | $\tau_r \dfrac{dr}{dt} = -r + F\bigl(w \cdot r + I_{ext}\bigr)$ | Steady-state $r^{\star} = F(I)$ | $\tau_r, w, \beta, I_0$ | | **Sigmoidal f-I curve** | $F(I) = \dfrac{r_{max}}{1 + e^{-\beta(I - I_0)}}$ | $r \in [0, r_{max}]$ | $r_{max}, \beta, I_0$ | | **IF firing rate (mean drive)** | $r = \left[ t_{ref} - \tau_m \ln\!\left( \dfrac{V_{th} - R I \mu}{V_{reset} - R I \mu} \right) \right]^{-1}$ | Valid for $I > I_{th}$ | Derived from F-I | > [!NOTE] > **No pipe symbols in cells.** Where absolute value or norm appears, render as $\vert \cdot \vert$. ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility | Application Domain | Use of Simplified Neuron Models | |--------------------|--------------------------------| | **Neuromorphic Engineering (Intel Loihi, IBM TrueNorth)** | IF and SRM cores implemented in silicon for low-power spiking neural networks | | **Brain–Computer Interfaces (BCIs)** | Rate models used for decoding motor intent from population firing rates | | **Cochlear Implant Signal Processing** | SRM-style kernels model auditory nerve fiber responses to electrical stimulation | | **Epileptic Seizure Modeling** | LIF neurons in large networks reproduce seizure-like wave propagation | | **Spiking Deep Learning (STDP-based)** | IF neurons form the substrate for spike-timing-dependent plasticity hardware | ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Leaky Integrate-and-Fire Membrane Equation We start from the **passive membrane equation** combining the lipid bilayer capacitance $C$ and the leakage resistance $R$: $$C \frac{dV}{dt} = -\frac{V - V_{rest}}{R} + I(t)$$ **Conversion logic:** We want to express the equation in standard *leaky* form with the time constant $\tau_m = RC$ explicitly visible. Divide both sides by $C$: $$\frac{dV}{dt} = -\frac{V - V_{rest}}{RC} + \frac{I(t)}{C}$$ Substitute $\tau_m = RC$ and absorb $R/C$ structure: $$\tau_m \frac{dV}{dt} = -(V - V_{rest}) + R I(t)$$ To obtain the **canonical textbook form** (resting potential = 0), let $v = V - V_{rest}$: $$\tau_m \frac{dv}{dt} = -v + R I(t)$$ **Final canonical form of the LIF neuron:** $$\boxed{\;\tau_m \frac{dv}{dt} = -v + R I(t), \qquad v(t) \geq V_{th} \Rightarrow v \leftarrow V_{reset}\;}$$ ### 3.2 Derivation: Analytical Firing Rate of the IF Neuron (Constant Input $I = I_0$) **Goal:** Derive the mean firing rate $r = 1/T$ where $T$ is the **interspike interval** for constant input $I_0 > I_{th} = V_{th}/R$. **Step 1 — Solve the ODE.** With $I(t) = I_0$ constant and $v(0) = V_{reset}$, the LIF equation becomes: $$\tau_m \frac{dv}{dt} = -v + R I_0$$ This is a first-order linear ODE with constant forcing. The integrating factor is $e^{t/\tau_m}$. **Step 2 — Apply integrating factor.** Multiply both sides: $$\frac{d}{dt}\!\left[ v(t) e^{t/\tau_m} \right] = \frac{R I_0}{\tau_m} e^{t/\tau_m}$$ **Step 3 — Integrate from $0$ to $t$:** $$v(t) e^{t/\tau_m} - v(0) = R I_0 \left( e^{t/\tau_m} - 1 \right)$$ **Step 4 — Solve for $v(t)$:** $$v(t) = V_{reset} e^{-t/\tau_m} + R I_0 \left( 1 - e^{-t/\tau_m} \right)$$ **Conversion logic:** This is the standard solution: the voltage rises from $V_{reset}$ toward the asymptotic value $R I_0$ with exponential relaxation time $\tau_m$. **Step 5 — Find time $T$ to reach threshold.** Set $v(T) = V_{th}$: $$V_{th} = V_{reset} e^{-T/\tau_m} + R I_0 \left( 1 - e^{-T/\tau_m} \right)$$ **Step 6 — Rearrange to isolate the exponential.** Group the $e^{-T/\tau_m}$ terms: $$V_{th} - R I_0 = (V_{reset} - R I_0) e^{-T/\tau_m}$$ Therefore: $$e^{-T/\tau_m} = \frac{V_{th} - R I_0}{V_{reset} - R I_0}$$ **Step 7 — Solve for $T$:** Taking the natural logarithm: $$T = \tau_m \ln\!\left( \frac{R I_0 - V_{th}}{R I_0 - V_{reset}} \right)$$ (assuming $R I_0 > V_{th}$, which is the suprathreshold condition). **Step 8 — Incorporate the refractory period.** The neuron cannot fire again during $t_{ref}$, so the effective ISI is $T + t_{ref}$. The mean firing rate is therefore: $$\boxed{\;r = \frac{1}{T + t_{ref}} = \frac{1}{t_{ref} + \tau_m \ln\!\left( \dfrac{R I_0 - V_{th}}{R I_0 - V_{reset}} \right)}\;}$$ **Asymptotic check:** As $I_0 \to \infty$, $T \to 0$ and $r \to 1/t_{ref}$ — the maximum firing rate is set by the refractory period. As $I_0 \to I_{th}^+$, $T \to \infty$ and $r \to 0$ — the rate vanishes at threshold. This matches the **Type-I excitability** behavior of cortical pyramidal neurons. ### 3.3 Derivation: Spike-Response Model (SRM$_0$) Membrane Potential **Goal:** Show that the SRM$_0$ equation **reduces** to the LIF equation under specific kernel choices — proving the SRM is a strict generalization. The SRM$_0$ defines the membrane potential as: $$V(t) = \eta(t - \hat{t}) + \int_{0}^{\infty} \kappa(s)\, I(t - s)\, ds$$ where $\hat{t}$ is the time of the last spike. **Step 1 — Choose exponential kernels.** For an LIF-equivalent SRM, set: $$\eta(t) = -V_{th} \exp\!\left(-\frac{t}{\tau_m}\right), \qquad \kappa(s) = \frac{R}{\tau_m} \exp\!\left(-\frac{s}{\tau_m}\right) H(s)$$ where $H(s)$ is the Heaviside step function. **Step 2 — Differentiate $V(t)$ with respect to $t$** (to convert integral to ODE form): $$\frac{dV}{dt} = \eta'(t - \hat{t}) + \int_{0}^{\infty} \kappa(s)\, \frac{dI}{dt}(t-s)\, ds$$ **Step 3 — Compute $\eta'$:** Differentiate the exponential reset kernel: $$\eta'(t - \hat{t}) = \frac{V_{th}}{\tau_m} \exp\!\left(-\frac{t - \hat{t}}{\tau_m}\right) = -\frac{1}{\tau_m} \eta(t - \hat{t})$$ **Step 4 — Differentiate the integral using Leibniz rule.** Let $u = t - s$, then $du = -ds$: $$\int_{0}^{\infty} \kappa(s)\, \frac{dI}{dt}(t-s)\, ds = \frac{d}{dt} \int_{0}^{\infty} \kappa(s) I(t-s) ds - \kappa(0) I(t) + \int_{0}^{\infty} \kappa'(s) I(t-s) ds$$ **Step 5 — Combine and simplify.** After combining the reset and input terms and using $\kappa'(s) = -\frac{1}{\tau_m}\kappa(s)$: $$\tau_m \frac{dV}{dt} = -V + R I(t) + \text{(boundary terms from the spike at } \hat{t}\text{)}$$ **Conversion logic:** This recovers the LIF equation **plus** a discontinuous jump condition at $t = \hat{t}$ that enforces the reset. Thus: $$\boxed{\;\text{SRM}_0 \;\xrightarrow[\kappa(s) = (R/\tau_m) e^{-s/\tau_m}]{\eta(t) = -V_{th} e^{-t/\tau_m}}\; \text{Leaky Integrate-and-Fire}\;}$$ This shows the SRM is a **strict superset** of the LIF. ### 3.4 Derivation: Rate Model Equilibrium and Linear Stability The rate equation: $$\tau_r \frac{dr}{dt} = -r + F(w r + I_{ext})$$ **Step 1 — Find fixed point** $r^{\star}$ by setting $dr/dt = 0$: $$r^{\star} = F(w r^{\star} + I_{ext})$$ This is a **self-consistency equation** — the rate equals the output of the activation function evaluated at the *recurrent input*. **Step 2 — Linearize around $r^{\star}$** by setting $r(t) = r^{\star} + \delta r(t)$ with $\vert \delta r \vert \ll 1$: $$\tau_r \frac{d(\delta r)}{dt} = -\delta r + F'(w r^{\star} + I_{ext}) \cdot w \cdot \delta r$$ $$\tau_r \frac{d(\delta r)}{dt} = \left[ w F'(I_{syn}^{\star}) - 1 \right] \delta r$$ **Step 3 — Stability criterion.** Solutions are $\delta r(t) = A e^{\lambda t}$ where: $$\lambda = \frac{w F'(I_{syn}^{\star}) - 1}{\tau_r}$$ **Step 4 — Stability interpretation:** $$\boxed{\;\text{Stable if } w F'(I_{syn}^{\star}) < 1 \qquad \text{Unstable (oscillatory network) if } w F'(I_{syn}^{\star}) > 1\;}$$ **Conversion logic:** This is the **Hopf bifurcation** condition for neural mass models. In neural engineering, this is used to predict **pathological synchronization** (e.g., Parkinson's tremor, epileptic seizures). ### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix | Property | IF / LIF | SRM | Rate-Based | |----------|----------|-----|------------| | State dimension | 1 ($V$) | 1 ($V$) but history-dependent | 1 ($r$) per population | | Spike timing | Explicit | Explicit | Not represented | | Refractoriness | Constant $t_{ref}$ | Adaptive kernel $\eta(t)$ | Implicit in $F$ saturation | | Computational cost (per neuron) | Low | Low | Very low | | Biological realism | Low | Moderate | Low | | Network scalability ($N \geq 10^4$) | Good | Good | Excellent | | Analytically tractable | Yes (linear) | Yes (integral) | Yes (1 ODE/pop) | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Hierarchy of Simplified Neuron Models (Mermaid Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart TD A0["Biological Neuron
Hodgkin Huxley Model"] --> B0["Simplified Models"] B0 --> C0["Spike Based Models"] B0 --> D0["Rate Based Models"] C0 --> E0["Integrate and Fire IF"] C0 --> F0["Spike Response Model SRM"] E0 --> G0["Perfect Integrate and Fire"] E0 --> H0["Leaky Integrate and Fire LIF"] E0 --> I0["Adaptive Integrate and Fire"] H0 --> J0["Exponential Integrate and Fire EIF"] F0 --> K0["SRM0 Linear Subthreshold"] F0 --> L0["SRM1 Nonlinear with Adaptation"] D0 --> M0["Wilson Cowan Model"] D0 --> N0["Hopfield Rate Model"] D0 --> O0["Amari Neural Field Model"] E0 --> P0["Output Single Spike Train"] F0 --> Q0["Output Single Spike Train"] M0 --> R0["Output Mean Firing Rate"] N0 --> R0 O0 --> R0 ``` ### 4.2 Functional Architecture of the Leaky Integrate-and-Fire Neuron ```mermaid flowchart LR inp["Input Current I of t"] --> sum["Summing Junction"] leak["Leak Term minus V over tau m"] --> sum sum --> ode["Integration tau m dV by dt equals minus V plus R I"] ode --> comp{"V greater than V threshold"} comp -- No --> fb["Feedback to Leak Term"] comp -- Yes --> spike["Emit Spike at Time t f"] spike --> reset["Reset V to V reset"] reset --> ref["Apply Refractory Period t ref"] ref --> fb spike --> out["Output Spike Train"] ``` ### 4.3 Functional Flow of the Spike-Response Model ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Incoming Input Current I of t"] --> B["Convolve with Kernel kappa"] B --> C["Compute Input Contribution V input"] D["Past Spike Times t f"] --> E["Evaluate Refractory Kernel eta"] E --> F["Compute Refractory Contribution V reset"] C --> G["Sum Membrane Potential V of t"] F --> G G --> H{"V of t greater than threshold theta"} H -- No --> I["Continue Integration"] H -- Yes --> J["Emit New Spike"] J --> K["Record Spike Time t f"] K --> D J --> L["Output Spike Train to Downstream Neurons"] ``` ### 4.4 Rate-Based Population Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR popA["Population A
Rate rA"] --> synW["Synaptic Weight wAB"] synW --> popB["Population B
Rate rB"] extI["External Input I ext"] --> popB popB --> fcurve["f-I Curve F of I"] fcurve --> dyn["Dynamics tau r drB by dt equals minus rB plus F"] dyn --> popB popB --> out["Output Population Rate rB of t"] ``` ### 4.5 Spike Generation Process in the IF Neuron (Detailed State Machine) ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> Resting Resting: V below V threshold Resting: Membrane integrates I of t Resting --> ThresholdCrossed: V reaches V threshold ThresholdCrossed: Spike emitted at time t f ThresholdCrossed: V reset to V reset ThresholdCrossed --> Refractory Refractory: Absolute refractory period t ref Refractory: No spike possible Refractory --> Resting: After t ref elapses Resting --> [*]: Simulation ends ``` > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Note:** The state machine above is exactly how neuromorphic hardware (e.g., Intel Loihi) implements the IF neuron in digital logic — each state is a register, and transitions are governed by comparators. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** Define the Leaky Integrate-and-Fire (LIF) neuron model. Write its governing differential equation and explain the role of the membrane time constant $\tau_m$. **Model Answer:** The Leaky Integrate-and-Fire neuron is a simplified point-neuron model in which the subthreshold membrane potential $v(t)$ evolves according to a linear first-order ODE with a leakage term: $$\tau_m \frac{dv}{dt} = -v + R I(t)$$ When $v(t)$ crosses the threshold $V_{th}$, a spike is emitted and the voltage is instantaneously reset to $V_{reset}$, followed by an absolute refractory period of duration $t_{ref}$. **Role of $\tau_m$:** The membrane time constant $\tau_m = R C$ sets the timescale of integration. A *large* $\tau_m$ produces strong temporal summation (memory of past inputs); a *small* $\tau_m$ makes the neuron respond quickly but with weak integration. For cortical pyramidal neurons, $\tau_m \approx$ **10–20 ms**. *(3 marks)* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]** What is the Spike-Response Model? How does it differ from the basic Integrate-and-Fire model? **Model Answer:** The Spike-Response Model (SRM) is an integral-form generalization of the IF neuron in which the membrane potential is computed as a sum of two convolutions: $$V(t) = \eta(t - \hat{t}) + \int_{0}^{\infty} \kappa(s)\, I(t-s)\, ds$$ Here, $\kappa(s)$ is the **postsynaptic potential kernel** (response to an input) and $\eta(t)$ is the **after-spike kernel** (refractory reset). **Key differences from the basic IF:** - The IF uses a *fixed* reset value and *constant* refractory period; the SRM allows the reset and refractoriness to be **shaped** by arbitrary kernels $\eta(t)$. - The SRM captures **spike-frequency adaptation** and **history-dependent thresholds** naturally; the IF cannot. - The SRM is an **integral** formulation (no ODE to integrate), making it easier to fit to electrophysiological data. *(3 marks)* --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU ESE Format:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** and typically has sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Both alternatives are **completely independent questions** — students answer either the full OR the full. --- #### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question Paper Module 3]** **(a)** Derive the analytical expression for the mean firing rate of a Leaky Integrate-and-Fire neuron driven by a constant input current $I_0 > I_{th}$. Show all steps clearly. (7 marks) **(b)** Compare the Integrate-and-Fire neuron with the Spike-Response Model. Highlight three specific biological phenomena that the SRM captures but the IF cannot. (7 marks) **Model Solution (a):** Given the LIF equation with $I(t) = I_0$ constant: $$\tau_m \frac{dv}{dt} = -v + R I_0, \quad v(0) = V_{reset}$$ The integrating-factor solution yields: $$v(t) = V_{reset} e^{-t/\tau_m} + R I_0 \left(1 - e^{-t/\tau_m}\right)$$ Setting $v(T) = V_{th}$: $$V_{th} = V_{reset} e^{-T/\tau_m} + R I_0 \left(1 - e^{-T/\tau_m}\right)$$ Solving for $T$: $$T = \tau_m \ln\!\left( \frac{R I_0 - V_{th}}{R I_0 - V_{reset}} \right)$$ Incorporating the absolute refractory period $t_{ref}$, the mean firing rate is: $$\boxed{\;r = \frac{1}{T + t_{ref}} = \left[ t_{ref} + \tau_m \ln\!\left( \frac{R I_0 - V_{th}}{R I_0 - V_{reset}} \right) \right]^{-1}\;}$$ This expression is valid for $R I_0 > V_{th}$. As $I_0 \to I_{th}^+$, $T \to \infty$ and $r \to 0$; as $I_0 \to \infty$, $T \to 0$ and $r \to 1/t_{ref}$. **Model Solution (b):** | Phenomenon | Why IF Fails | Why SRM Succeeds | |------------|--------------|------------------| | **Spike-frequency adaptation** | IF has constant $t_{ref}$ | $\eta(t)$ has slow negative component | | **Threshold accommodation** | Fixed $V_{th}$ | Dynamic threshold $\vartheta(t)$ | | **Postsynaptic potential shape** | Exponential assumed via ODE | Arbitrary $\kappa(s)$ from data | The SRM uses kernels to capture **history dependence**, while the IF relies on instantaneous rules only. *(14 marks total)* --- #### Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Supplementary Model Paper]** **(a)** Explain the rate-based neuron model (Wilson–Cowan type). Derive the stability condition for the population firing rate equilibrium. (7 marks) **(b)** With the aid of a block diagram, explain the functional architecture of a neuromorphic hardware implementation of the Leaky Integrate-and-Fire neuron. (7 marks) **Model Solution (a):** The Wilson–Cowan rate model describes a population's mean firing rate $r(t)$ via: $$\tau_r \frac{dr}{dt} = -r + F(w r + I_{ext})$$ where $F$ is the sigmoidal f-I curve: $$F(I) = \frac{r_{max}}{1 + \exp[-\beta(I - I_0)]}$$ **Equilibrium analysis:** Setting $dr/dt = 0$ gives $r^{\star} = F(w r^{\star} + I_{ext})$. Linearizing $r = r^{\star} + \delta r$: $$\tau_r \frac{d(\delta r)}{dt} = \left[ w F'(I_{syn}^{\star}) - 1 \right] \delta r$$ **Stability condition:** $$\boxed{\;w F'(I_{syn}^{\star}) < 1 \;(\text{stable}) \quad ; \quad w F'(I_{syn}^{\star}) > 1 \;(\text{unstable, Hopf bifurcation})\;}$$ When $w F'(I_{syn}^{\star}) > 1$, the network undergoes sustained oscillations — the neural-mass analog of epileptic rhythms. **Model Solution (b):** The neuromorphic LIF hardware consists of four functional blocks (see Section 4.2): 1. **Integrator block** — a capacitor charged by synaptic current source $I(t)$. 2. **Comparator block** — continuously compares $V(t)$ with $V_{th}$. 3. **Reset and refractory block** — upon threshold crossing, discharges capacitor to $V_{reset}$ and holds for $t_{ref}$. 4. **Spike-output block** — emits a digital pulse to downstream cores. Each block is implemented in CMOS analog or digital logic, enabling **$10^6$** such neurons per chip with power consumption **$\sim$ mW** — orders of magnitude more efficient than CPU/GPU simulation. *(14 marks total)* --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Simplified Neuron Model Questions:** > > 1. **Forgetting the refractory period** in the LIF firing-rate derivation — costs **2 marks** easily. Always include $t_{ref}$ in the denominator. > 2. **Sign error in the leakage term:** The correct form is $-v$ (or $-(V - V_{rest})$), NOT $+v$. Examiners specifically look for this. > 3. **Mixing threshold definitions:** Some textbooks use $\vartheta$ as a *dynamic* threshold (SRM); others use a *fixed* $V_{th}$ (IF). Do **not** interchange them in the same answer. > 4. **Drawing an empty Mermaid diagram** without arrows or proper labels in part-(b) architecture questions — full **3 marks** deduction. > 5. **Forgetting to specify units** for $\tau_m$, $R$, $C$ — examiners often deduct **0.5–1 mark** per missing unit in numerical sub-questions. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must Memorize Before KTU ESE** - **LIF Equation:** $\tau_m \dfrac{dv}{dt} = -v + R I(t)$ ; reset rule $v \to V_{reset}$ at threshold. - **Threshold Condition:** $v(t) \geq V_{th}$ triggers spike; followed by absolute refractory period $t_{ref}$. - **IF Firing Rate (constant input):** $r = \left[ t_{ref} + \tau_m \ln\!\left( \dfrac{R I_0 - V_{th}}{R I_0 - V_{reset}} \right) \right]^{-1}$. - **SRM Equation:** $V(t) = \eta(t - \hat{t}) + \int_{0}^{\infty} \kappa(s)\, I(t-s)\, ds$. - **SRM Kernels:** $\kappa$ = postsynaptic kernel ; $\eta$ = refractory kernel. - **Rate Model ODE:** $\tau_r \dfrac{dr}{dt} = -r + F(w r + I_{ext})$. - **Sigmoidal f-I Curve:** $F(I) = \dfrac{r_{max}}{1 + e^{-\beta(I - I_0)}}$. - **Stability Criterion:** $w F'(I_{syn}^{\star}) < 1$ (stable); $> 1$ (Hopf, oscillatory). - **Hierarchy:** Hodgkin-Huxley $\supset$ SRM $\supset$ LIF $\supset$ IF; rate models are *orthogonal* abstraction. - **Numerical Defaults:** $\tau_m \approx$ 10–20 ms; $V_{th} \approx$ −55 mV; $V_{rest} \approx$ −70 mV; $t_{ref} \approx$ 1–2 ms. - **Engineering Uses:** Neuromorphic chips (Loihi, TrueNorth), BCIs, cochlear implants, seizure modeling, spiking deep learning. - **Pitfall Awareness:** Always include $t_{ref}$; never confuse dynamic $\vartheta$ with fixed $V_{th}$; watch the sign on the leak term.

topics.title: Dynamical System Analysis: Types of Fixed Points
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT865 - MODELLING OF PHYSIOLOGICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Dynamical System Analysis: Types of Fixed Points

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Dynamical Systems in Physiology

A **dynamical system** is a mathematical description of how a set of state variables (such as membrane potential, blood glucose concentration, or cell population) evolves with time under a set of governing rules (ordinary or partial differential equations). In physiological modelling, every state variable $x(t)$ describes a measurable biological quantity whose rate of change depends on its own current value and the values of other interacting variables.

Formally, a continuous-time, autonomous dynamical system in $n$ dimensions is written as:

$$\dot{\mathbf{x}}(t) = \mathbf{F}(\mathbf{x}), \quad \mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^{n}, \quad t \in \mathbb{R}$$

where $\mathbf{x} = [x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n]^{T}$ is the state vector and $\mathbf{F} : \mathbb{R}^{n} \to \mathbb{R}^{n}$ is a smooth (typically Lipschitz continuous) vector field. Physiological examples include the Hodgkin-Huxley nerve membrane equations, the van der Pol cardiac pacemaker model, and the minimal Bergman model of glucose–insulin regulation.

### 1.2 Fixed Points — The Heartbeat of Steady State

A **fixed point** (also called an **equilibrium point**, **critical point**, or **stationary point**) of the dynamical system $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{F}(\mathbf{x})$ is any state $\mathbf{x}^{\ast}$ such that:

$$\mathbf{F}(\mathbf{x}^{\ast}) = \mathbf{0}$$

At a fixed point, all rates of change vanish simultaneously, so the system, once there, remains there forever. In a physiological context, a fixed point corresponds to a **homeostatic steady state** — the resting membrane potential of a neuron, the equilibrium glucose concentration in blood, or the equilibrium population of a cell colony.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A fixed point does **not** mean the system is "off" or "dead". It means the system is in *dynamic balance*, with production and clearance rates exactly cancelling out.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — A Ball on a Landscape

Imagine a hilly landscape where every point on the ground represents a possible physiological state, and the height represents the "potential" of the system. A **fixed point** is any point where the slope is exactly zero (a hilltop, a valley floor, or a flat mountain pass).

| Landscape Feature | Mathematical Type | Physiological Meaning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Valley floor (lowest point) | **Stable node / spiral** | A robust homeostatic state; perturbations decay |
| Hilltop | **Unstable node** | A pathological threshold; small noise pushes system away |
| Mountain pass (saddle) | **Saddle point** | An unstable equilibrium in one direction, stable in another |
| Bowl-shaped basin | **Stable spiral** | Homeostasis reached via decaying oscillations |
| Flat plateau | **Degenerate / line of fixed points** | Indifferent equilibrium — system does not return to a unique state |

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Summary:** The classification of fixed points tells us whether the body, after a small disturbance (a skipped heartbeat, a meal, a brief hypoxia), returns to its healthy equilibrium, oscillates while doing so, or escapes into pathology.

### 1.4 Linearization — Why Local Behaviour Dominates

For non-linear physiological models (which are the vast majority), it is generally impossible to solve the equations analytically. However, **Hartman–Grobman theorem** guarantees that near a hyperbolic fixed point, the non-linear system behaves qualitatively like its linear approximation:

$$\dot{\mathbf{x}} \approx J(\mathbf{x}^{\ast}) \, \delta \mathbf{x}, \quad \delta \mathbf{x} = \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{x}^{\ast}$$

where $J(\mathbf{x}^{\ast})$ is the **Jacobian matrix** evaluated at the fixed point. Hence, classifying the fixed points reduces to analyzing the eigenvalues of a constant matrix — a tractable linear-algebra problem.

### 1.5 Key Engineering / Physiological Metrics

- **Time constant $\tau$**: characteristic time to return to equilibrium (in **seconds** for neural firing, **minutes** for glucose, **hours** for hormonal cycles).
- **Natural frequency $\omega_{n}$**: $\omega_{n} = \sqrt{\Delta}$ rad/s, governing oscillation rate.
- **Damping ratio $\zeta$**: $\zeta = \tau / (2\sqrt{\Delta})$, telling us whether return is over-damped, critically damped, or under-damped.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The General 2D Autonomous System

The pedagogical workhorse of physiological phase-plane analysis is the planar system:

$$\dot{x} = f(x, y), \qquad \dot{y} = g(x, y)$$

To classify the fixed point $(x^{\ast}, y^{\ast})$, we linearize around it using the Jacobian:

$$J(x^{\ast}, y^{\ast}) = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{\partial f}{\partial x} & \dfrac{\partial f}{\partial y} \\[2mm] \dfrac{\partial g}{\partial x} & \dfrac{\partial g}{\partial y} \end{bmatrix}_{(x^{\ast}, y^{\ast})}$$

Let the Jacobian at equilibrium be the constant matrix:

$$A = \begin{bmatrix} a & b \\ c & d \end{bmatrix}$$

### 2.2 Eigenvalue Equation and the Two Scalar Invariants

The characteristic polynomial of $A$ is:

$$\det(A - \lambda I) = 0 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \lambda^{2} - \tau \lambda + \Delta = 0$$

with the two crucial invariants:

$$\boxed{\;\tau = \operatorname{tr}(A) = a + d\;}, \qquad \boxed{\;\Delta = \det(A) = ad - bc\;}$$

The eigenvalues are:

$$\lambda_{1,2} = \frac{\tau \pm \sqrt{\tau^{2} - 4\Delta}}{2} = \frac{\tau}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{\tau}{2}\right)^{2} - \Delta}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why $\tau$ and $\Delta$?** They completely determine local qualitative behaviour. Every entry $(a,b,c,d)$ of the Jacobian is mapped to a single point $(\Delta, \tau)$ in the *trace–determinant plane*, where classification becomes a one-glance geometric exercise.

### 2.3 The Trace–Determinant Classification — A Master Table

| Region in $(\Delta, \tau)$ plane | Eigenvalues $\lambda_{1,2}$ | Fixed Point Type | Stability | Phase Portrait |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $\Delta > 0,\ \tau < 0,\ \tau^{2} > 4\Delta$ | Real, distinct, both $< 0$ | **Stable Node (Sink)** | Asymptotically stable | Trajectories flow in along slowest eigendirection |
| $\Delta > 0,\ \tau > 0,\ \tau^{2} > 4\Delta$ | Real, distinct, both $> 0$ | **Unstable Node (Source)** | Unstable | Trajectories flow out along slowest eigendirection |
| $\Delta > 0,\ \tau < 0,\ \tau^{2} < 4\Delta$ | Complex, $\operatorname{Re}\lambda < 0$ | **Stable Spiral (Focus)** | Asymptotically stable | Inward rotating spirals |
| $\Delta > 0,\ \tau > 0,\ \tau^{2} < 4\Delta$ | Complex, $\operatorname{Re}\lambda > 0$ | **Unstable Spiral (Focus)** | Unstable | Outward rotating spirals |
| $\Delta < 0$ | Real, opposite signs | **Saddle Point** | Unstable | Hyperbolic trajectories (in one direction, out the other) |
| $\Delta > 0,\ \tau = 0,\ \tau^{2} = 4\Delta$ | Purely imaginary $\pm i\omega$ | **Center** | Lyapunov stable (not asymptotically) | Closed orbits |
| $\Delta = 0$ | At least one zero eigenvalue | **Degenerate / Line / Star** | Indeterminate (higher-order analysis needed) | Whole line or higher-order manifold |

### 2.4 Stability Theorems (Lyapunov Linearization Method)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Theorem (Lyapunov indirect method):** If all eigenvalues of $A = J(\mathbf{x}^{\ast})$ have strictly negative real parts, then $\mathbf{x}^{\ast}$ is **locally asymptotically stable**. If any eigenvalue has a positive real part, $\mathbf{x}^{\ast}$ is **unstable**.

For a 2D system, this reduces to the simple Routh–Hurwitz conditions:

$$\Delta > 0 \quad \text{AND} \quad \tau < 0 \;\;\Longleftrightarrow\;\; \text{asymptotic stability of } \mathbf{x}^{\ast}$$

### 2.5 Physiological Engineering Utility

- **Pacemaker cells (SA node):** the van der Pol oscillator has an unstable spiral fixed point; the system cannot rest, it perpetually cycles — exactly the rhythmic property of cardiac cells.
- **Neural resting potential:** a stable node at $V \approx -70$ mV; injected current perturbations decay exponentially.
- **Disease threshold (e.g., spiral wave re-entry):** in cardiac electrophysiology, an unstable spiral fixed point in the FitzHugh–Nagumo model predicts the onset of lethal arrhythmias.
- **Glycaemic homeostasis:** the Bergman minimal model has a stable spiral representing the joint equilibrium of glucose and insulin dynamics.

### 2.6 Higher-Dimensional Systems (Brief Note)

For $n \geq 3$, the trace–determinant plane generalises to the **Routh–Hurwitz criteria**: a real polynomial $\lambda^{n} + a_{1}\lambda^{n-1} + \dots + a_{n}$ has all roots with negative real parts iff all leading principal minors of the associated Hurwitz matrix are positive. This is rarely examined in KTU exams but conceptually extends the 2D theory.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Jacobian Linearization of a 2D System

**Starting system (non-linear):**
$$\dot{x} = f(x, y), \qquad \dot{y} = g(x, y)$$

**Step 1:** Introduce small perturbations around the fixed point:
$$x = x^{\ast} + \xi, \qquad y = y^{\ast} + \eta$$

with $\vert \xi \vert \ll 1$, $\vert \eta \vert \ll 1$.

**Step 2:** Substitute into the right-hand side:
$$\dot{\xi} = f(x^{\ast} + \xi, y^{\ast} + \eta)$$
$$\dot{\eta} = g(x^{\ast} + \xi, y^{\ast} + \eta)$$

**Step 3:** Expand $f$ and $g$ using a 2D Taylor series up to first order:
$$\dot{\xi} \approx f(x^{\ast}, y^{\ast}) + \frac{\partial f}{\partial x}\bigg\vert_{\ast}\xi + \frac{\partial f}{\partial y}\bigg\vert_{\ast}\eta$$
$$\dot{\eta} \approx g(x^{\ast}, y^{\ast}) + \frac{\partial g}{\partial x}\bigg\vert_{\ast}\xi + \frac{\partial g}{\partial y}\bigg\vert_{\ast}\eta$$

**Step 4:** Apply the equilibrium condition $f(x^{\ast}, y^{\ast}) = g(x^{\ast}, y^{\ast}) = 0$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\dot{\xi} &= f_{x}^{\ast}\,\xi + f_{y}^{\ast}\,\eta \\
\dot{\eta} &= g_{x}^{\ast}\,\xi + g_{y}^{\ast}\,\eta
\end{aligned}$$

In matrix form:

$$\begin{bmatrix} \dot{\xi} \\ \dot{\eta} \end{bmatrix} = \underbrace{\begin{bmatrix} f_{x} & f_{y} \\ g_{x} & g_{y} \end{bmatrix}_{\ast}}_{J(x^{\ast},y^{\ast})} \begin{bmatrix} \xi \\ \eta \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 5:** Recognize this as a linear constant-coefficient system $\dot{\mathbf{z}} = A\mathbf{z}$, whose solution is $\mathbf{z}(t) = e^{At}\mathbf{z}(0)$. The matrix exponential is fully determined by the eigenvalues $\lambda_{1,2}$ of $A$.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Eigenvalue Formula from Trace and Determinant

For $A = \begin{bmatrix} a & b \\ c & d \end{bmatrix}$:

**Step 1:** The characteristic equation $\det(A - \lambda I) = 0$ gives:
$$(a - \lambda)(d - \lambda) - bc = 0$$

**Step 2:** Expand:
$$ad - a\lambda - d\lambda + \lambda^{2} - bc = 0$$
$$\lambda^{2} - (a+d)\lambda + (ad - bc) = 0$$

**Step 3:** Identify $\tau = a+d$ and $\Delta = ad-bc$:
$$\lambda^{2} - \tau \lambda + \Delta = 0$$

**Step 4:** Solve using the quadratic formula:
$$\lambda_{1,2} = \frac{\tau \pm \sqrt{\tau^{2} - 4\Delta}}{2}$$

**Step 5:** The sign of the **discriminant** $\tau^{2} - 4\Delta$ determines whether eigenvalues are real (positive discriminant) or complex conjugate (negative discriminant).

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Worked Physiological Example

Consider a simplified **glucose–insulin regulatory model** (Bergman minimal form, abstract):

$$\dot{G} = -a G - b I + G_{in}$$
$$\dot{I} = c G - d I$$

with constants $a = 2$, $b = 3$, $c = 1$, $d = 1$, and constant glucose infusion $G_{in} = 6$.

**Step 1 — Find the fixed point.** Set $\dot{G} = \dot{I} = 0$:

$$\begin{aligned}
-2G^{\ast} - 3I^{\ast} + 6 &= 0 \\
G^{\ast} - I^{\ast} &= 0 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; G^{\ast} = I^{\ast}
\end{aligned}$$

Substituting:
$$-2G^{\ast} - 3G^{\ast} + 6 = 0 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; -5G^{\ast} + 6 = 0 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; G^{\ast} = \frac{6}{5} = 1.2$$

So $\mathbf{x}^{\ast} = (1.2,\, 1.2)$.

**Step 2 — Compute the Jacobian.** Since the system is already linear, the Jacobian is constant:

$$J = \begin{bmatrix} -2 & -3 \\ 1 & -1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the invariants.**
$$\tau = -2 + (-1) = -3$$
$$\Delta = (-2)(-1) - (-3)(1) = 2 + 3 = 5$$

**Step 4 — Check the discriminant.**
$$\tau^{2} - 4\Delta = 9 - 20 = -11 < 0$$

So eigenvalues are complex conjugate.

**Step 5 — Compute the eigenvalues.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\lambda_{1,2} &= \frac{\tau \pm \sqrt{\tau^{2} - 4\Delta}}{2} \\
&= \frac{-3 \pm \sqrt{-11}}{2} \\
&= -\frac{3}{2} \pm i\,\frac{\sqrt{11}}{2}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 — Classify.**
- Real part: $-3/2 < 0$ → decay.
- Imaginary part: $\sqrt{11}/2 \neq 0$ → oscillation.

⇒ **Stable Spiral (Focus)**. The system returns to glycaemic equilibrium via damped oscillations — exactly the post-prandial glucose–insulin response observed clinically.

**Step 7 — Compute engineering metrics.**
$$\omega_{n} = \sqrt{\Delta} = \sqrt{5} \approx 2.236 \text{ rad (per model time unit)}$$
$$\zeta = \frac{\tau}{2\sqrt{\Delta}} = \frac{-3}{2\sqrt{5}} \approx -0.671$$

Since $\vert\zeta\vert < 1$, the response is **under-damped** — confirming the spiral classification.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Saddle Point Verification

For $A = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & -1 \end{bmatrix}$:
$$\tau = 0, \qquad \Delta = -1, \qquad \tau^{2} - 4\Delta = 4$$
$$\lambda_{1} = 1, \qquad \lambda_{2} = -1$$

Since $\Delta < 0$, this is a **saddle point** — one stable direction ($y$-axis) and one unstable direction ($x$-axis). Such a saddle appears, for instance, at the threshold of action-potential firing in reduced neuronal models.

---

### 3.5 Summary Matrix of Analysis Procedure

| Step | Operation | Tool | Output |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Identify state variables | Model inspection | $\mathbf{x} = [x_1, \dots, x_n]^{T}$ |
| 2 | Solve $\mathbf{F}(\mathbf{x}^{\ast}) = 0$ | Algebra / numerics | Fixed points $\mathbf{x}^{\ast}$ |
| 3 | Compute Jacobian $J(\mathbf{x}^{\ast})$ | Partial derivatives | Constant matrix $A$ |
| 4 | Compute $\tau, \Delta$ | Trace & determinant | Scalar invariants |
| 5 | Compute eigenvalues | $\lambda^{2} - \tau\lambda + \Delta = 0$ | $\lambda_{1}, \lambda_{2}$ |
| 6 | Apply classification table | Trace–determinant plane | Type + stability |
| 7 | Map to physiology | Domain knowledge | Biological interpretation |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Fixed Point Analysis Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Physiological Model Equations] --> B[Identify State Vector x]
    B --> C[Solve F(x*) = 0]
    C --> D[Locate Fixed Points x*]
    D --> E[Compute Jacobian J at x*]
    E --> F[Extract Trace and Determinant]
    F --> G{Compute Discriminant tau^2 - 4 Delta}
    G -->|Greater than zero| H[Real Eigenvalues Branch]
    G -->|Less than zero| I[Complex Eigenvalues Branch]
    G -->|Equal to zero| J[Center or Degenerate Branch]
    H --> K{Delta Greater than zero}
    K -->|Yes| L[Tau Less than zero: Stable Node]
    K -->|No| M[Saddle Point]
    L --> N[Final Classification Output]
    M --> N
    I --> O{Tau Sign}
    O -->|Negative| P[Stable Spiral Focus]
    O -->|Positive| Q[Unstable Spiral Focus]
    P --> N
    Q --> N
    J --> N
    N --> R[Physiological Interpretation]
```

### 4.2 Trace–Determinant Plane — Visual Classification Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Trace Determinant Plane tau vs Delta] --> B[Delta Greater than zero Region]
    A --> C[Delta Less than zero Region]
    A --> D[Delta Equal to zero Line]
    B --> E[Parabola tau^2 equals 4 Delta Boundary]
    E --> F[Above Parabola: Real Eigenvalues]
    E --> G[Below Parabola: Complex Eigenvalues]
    F --> H[Tau Negative: Stable Node]
    F --> I[Tau Positive: Unstable Node]
    G --> J[Tau Negative: Stable Spiral]
    G --> K[Tau Positive: Unstable Spiral]
    C --> L[Saddle Point always Unstable]
    D --> M[Degenerate or Line of Fixed Points]
    H --> N[Classification Complete]
    I --> N
    J --> N
    K --> N
    L --> N
    M --> N
```

### 4.3 Phase Portrait Reference Grid — Six Canonical Types

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph NODE_TYPES["SIX CANONICAL FIXED POINT TYPES"]
    A1[Stable Node - All trajectories flow inward - Real negative eigenvalues]
    A2[Unstable Node - All trajectories flow outward - Real positive eigenvalues]
    A3[Saddle Point - In along one direction out along another - Real opposite sign eigenvalues]
    A4[Stable Spiral - Inward rotating trajectories - Complex negative real part]
    A5[Unstable Spiral - Outward rotating trajectories - Complex positive real part]
    A6[Center - Closed orbits around fixed point - Pure imaginary eigenvalues]
    end
    A1 --> PHY1[Glucose insulin post prandial recovery]
    A2 --> PHY2[Pathological runaway state]
    A3 --> PHY3[Neuronal firing threshold]
    A4 --> PHY4[Cardiac pacemaker limit cycle collapse]
    A5 --> PHY5[Fibrillation onset]
    A6 --> PHY6[Conservative oscillation no damping]
```

### 4.4 Block Diagram — Linearization Around a Fixed Point

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IN[Nonlinear System dot x equals F of x] --> FP[Fixed Point x star: F x star equals zero]
    FP --> JAC[Compute Jacobian J of F at x star]
    JAC --> LIN[Linearized System dot z equals J times z]
    LIN --> EIG[Eigenvalue Computation lambda squared minus tau lambda plus Delta equals zero]
    EIG --> CLASS[Classification via Trace Determinant Plane]
    CLASS --> OUT[Local Qualitative Behaviour]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Guide:** Each block in the diagrams above corresponds to a step in the analytical matrix of Section 3.5. Following the pipeline from the model equation to physiological interpretation is the canonical KTU exam-style answer structure for any question on fixed points.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Define a fixed point of a dynamical system. With a physiological example, explain what it represents.**

**Model Answer:**

A fixed point (or equilibrium point) of an autonomous dynamical system $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{F}(\mathbf{x})$ is a state $\mathbf{x}^{\ast}$ satisfying $\mathbf{F}(\mathbf{x}^{\ast}) = \mathbf{0}$. At this state, every rate of change is zero, so the system remains there indefinitely once reached.

**Physiological example:** In the Hodgkin–Huxley model of a squid giant axon, the resting membrane potential $V^{\ast} \approx -70$ mV is a stable fixed point of the 4-dimensional system $(\dot{V}, \dot{n}, \dot{m}, \dot{h}) = \mathbf{F}(V,n,m,h)$. It represents the homeostatic equilibrium between the inward leak currents and the outward sodium–potassium pump current.

---

#### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**Distinguish between a stable node, a stable spiral, and a center.**

**Model Answer:**

All three have trajectories that do not escape to infinity, but they differ in the nature of the eigenvalues of the Jacobian:

- **Stable node:** real, distinct, both negative eigenvalues. Trajectories approach the fixed point along curved monotonic paths without rotation.
- **Stable spiral (focus):** complex conjugate eigenvalues with negative real part. Trajectories spiral inward, exhibiting damped oscillations.
- **Center:** purely imaginary eigenvalues $\pm i\omega$. Trajectories form closed orbits — neither attracted nor repelled (Lyapunov stable but not asymptotically stable).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question B-A** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** For the 2D non-linear system
$$\dot{x} = x - y - x(x^{2} + y^{2}), \qquad \dot{y} = x + y - y(x^{2} + y^{2})$$
find all fixed points and classify each using linearization.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Using the trace–determinant plane, explain how one can determine the type and stability of any fixed point of a planar linear system. Mention at least four distinct regions and their biological significance.

---

#### **Model Solution to B-A(a)**

**Step 1 — Find fixed points.** Setting $\dot{x} = \dot{y} = 0$:

$$\begin{aligned}
x - y - x(x^{2} + y^{2}) &= 0 \;\;\text{...(i)} \\
x + y - y(x^{2} + y^{2}) &= 0 \;\;\text{...(ii)}
\end{aligned}$$

Add (i) and (ii): $2x - 2x(x^{2}+y^{2}) = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; x(1 - (x^{2}+y^{2})) = 0$.

Subtract (ii) from (i): $-2y + 2y(x^{2}+y^{2}) = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; y((x^{2}+y^{2}) - 1) = 0$.

Solving simultaneously gives either $(x,y) = (0,0)$ or $x^{2} + y^{2} = 1$.

**Fixed points:** the origin $(0,0)$ and the entire unit circle $x^{2}+y^{2}=1$ (a continuum, hence degenerate).

**Step 2 — Compute Jacobian.**

$$J = \begin{bmatrix} 1 - (3x^{2} + y^{2}) & -1 - 2xy \\ 1 - 2xy & 1 - (x^{2} + 3y^{2}) \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate at origin.**
$$J(0,0) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$
$$\tau = 2, \qquad \Delta = 1 - (-1) = 2$$
$$\tau^{2} - 4\Delta = 4 - 8 = -4 < 0$$

Eigenvalues: $\lambda = 1 \pm i$. Positive real part and complex → **Unstable Spiral (Focus)** at the origin.

**Step 4 — Evaluate on the unit circle** (e.g. at $(1,0)$):
$$J(1,0) = \begin{bmatrix} -2 & -1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$
$$\tau = -2, \qquad \Delta = 0 - (-1) = 1$$
$$\tau^{2} - 4\Delta = 4 - 4 = 0$$

This is the **degenerate center/spiral boundary case**. Higher-order analysis (the non-linear terms matter here) shows the unit circle is a **stable limit cycle**: trajectories starting outside spiral inward, those inside spiral outward, all converging onto the circle.

---

#### **Model Solution to B-A(b)**

The trace–determinant plane plots $\Delta = \det A$ on the horizontal axis and $\tau = \operatorname{tr}A$ on the vertical axis. The characteristic equation $\lambda^{2} - \tau\lambda + \Delta = 0$ partitions this plane into qualitatively distinct regions.

**Region 1 — Stable node:** $\Delta > 0$, $\tau < 0$, $\tau^{2} > 4\Delta$. Both eigenvalues real and negative. *Biological:* robust homeostasis, e.g., thermoregulation around $37^{\circ}\text{C}$.

**Region 2 — Unstable node:** $\Delta > 0$, $\tau > 0$, $\tau^{2} > 4\Delta$. Both eigenvalues real and positive. *Biological:* runaway tumour cell proliferation in unchecked cancer models.

**Region 3 — Saddle point:** $\Delta < 0$. Real eigenvalues of opposite sign. *Biological:* the firing threshold of a neuron (Hodgkin–Huxley in reduced form).

**Region 4 — Stable spiral:** $\Delta > 0$, $\tau < 0$, $\tau^{2} < 4\Delta$. Complex conjugate with negative real part. *Biological:* damped glucose-insulin oscillations returning to glycaemic set-point after a meal.

**Region 5 — Center boundary:** $\tau = 0$, $\Delta > 0$, $\tau^{2} = 4\Delta$. Purely imaginary eigenvalues. *Biological:* conservative oscillation, e.g., undamped pacemaker rhythm (idealized).

**Biological significance:** the plane lets us predict, without solving the ODEs, whether a physiological system will recover (stable regions), fail (unstable regions), or require non-linear analysis (degenerate/center boundary).

---

#### **Question B-B (Alternative Internal Choice)** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Consider the linearized physiological model:
$$\dot{x} = -3x + y, \qquad \dot{y} = -x - y$$
where $x$ denotes deviation of blood calcium from set-point and $y$ denotes parathyroid hormone deviation. Find the fixed point, classify it, and compute the natural frequency and damping ratio.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain Hartman–Grobman theorem. Why is it central to the analysis of non-linear physiological models?

---

#### **Model Solution to B-B(a)**

**Step 1 — Fixed point.** Set RHS to zero: $-3x + y = 0 \Rightarrow y = 3x$; substitute: $-x - 3x = 0 \Rightarrow x = 0$. So $\mathbf{x}^{\ast} = (0, 0)$ — the physiological homeostatic set-point.

**Step 2 — Jacobian.**
$$J = \begin{bmatrix} -3 & 1 \\ -1 & -1 \end{bmatrix}$$
$$\tau = -4, \qquad \Delta = (-3)(-1) - (1)(-1) = 3 + 1 = 4$$
$$\tau^{2} - 4\Delta = 16 - 16 = 0$$

Eigenvalues (repeated): $\lambda_{1,2} = \tau/2 = -2$.

**Step 3 — Classification.** $\tau < 0$, $\Delta > 0$, $\tau^{2} = 4\Delta$ → **Stable degenerate node (star / improper node)**. All trajectories approach $(0,0)$ tangent to the eigenvector corresponding to $\lambda = -2$. The system returns monotonically (critically damped).

**Step 4 — Engineering metrics.**
$$\omega_{n} = \sqrt{\Delta} = \sqrt{4} = 2 \text{ rad per unit time}$$
$$\zeta = \frac{\tau}{2\sqrt{\Delta}} = \frac{-4}{2 \cdot 2} = -1$$

$\zeta = 1$ in magnitude → **critically damped**: fastest possible return without overshoot. Physiologically ideal for a tightly regulated variable like calcium.

---

#### **Model Solution to B-B(b)**

**Hartman–Grobman Theorem (statement):** Let $\mathbf{x}^{\ast}$ be a hyperbolic fixed point of a $C^{1}$ system $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{F}(\mathbf{x})$ (i.e., no eigenvalue of $J(\mathbf{x}^{\ast})$ lies on the imaginary axis). Then there exists a homeomorphism $h$ defined on a neighbourhood $U$ of $\mathbf{x}^{\ast}$ that maps orbits of the non-linear system onto orbits of its linearization $\dot{\mathbf{z}} = J(\mathbf{x}^{\ast})\mathbf{z}$, preserving orientation.

**Why it is central to physiological modelling:**

1. **Analytical tractability:** Most physiological models (Hodgkin–Huxley, FitzHugh–Nagumo, Noble cardiac model) are non-linear and analytically unsolvable, yet locally behave like linear systems.

2. **Classification transfer:** The trace–determinant classification computed on the linearization is *qualitatively* correct for the original non-linear system near equilibrium.

3. **Stability predictions:** Lyapunov's indirect method relies entirely on this theorem — a stable linearization implies local asymptotic stability of the non-linear equilibrium.

4. **Caveat — non-hyperbolic cases:** When eigenvalues are zero or purely imaginary (degenerate, center, Hopf bifurcation onset), the theorem *fails*. Such cases occur in critical physiological transitions — e.g., onset of periodic breathing, transition from sinus rhythm to tachyarrhythmia.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students commonly lose marks on this topic:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "stable" with "asymptotically stable".** A center is stable (Lyapunov) but not asymptotically stable. Worth **1–2 marks** if missed.
> 2. **Forgetting the Jacobian is evaluated AT the fixed point**, not at a generic point. If $J$ is computed at $(x,y)$ instead of $(x^{\ast}, y^{\ast})$, the entire classification is wrong. **Loss: up to 4 marks.**
> 3. **Incorrect sign of the discriminant.** Students often write $\tau^{2} + 4\Delta$ instead of $\tau^{2} - 4\Delta$. Always re-derive from $\lambda^{2} - \tau\lambda + \Delta = 0$.
> 4. **Not stating the Routh–Hurwitz conditions explicitly** ($\Delta > 0$ AND $\tau < 0$). Examiners reward these two crisp inequalities.
> 5. **Missing physiological interpretation.** A purely mathematical answer earns only ~70% of marks. Always close with the biological meaning — e.g., "this stable spiral represents damped glycaemic recovery after a meal."
> 6. **For units / dimensions.** When asked for $\omega_n$ or $\zeta$, mention "per second" or "dimensionless" respectively.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- A **fixed point** $\mathbf{x}^{\ast}$ of $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{F}(\mathbf{x})$ satisfies $\mathbf{F}(\mathbf{x}^{\ast}) = \mathbf{0}$; it represents a physiological steady state.
- Local behaviour near $\mathbf{x}^{\ast}$ is governed by the **Jacobian** $J(\mathbf{x}^{\ast})$ and its **eigenvalues** $\lambda_{1,2}$.
- The two scalar invariants — **trace $\tau = \operatorname{tr}A$** and **determinant $\Delta = \det A$** — fully determine the type and stability of any planar fixed point.
- **Six canonical types**: stable node, unstable node, saddle, stable spiral, unstable spiral, center. Plus **degenerate cases** when $\Delta = 0$ or $\tau^{2} = 4\Delta$.
- **Routh–Hurwitz condition**: $\Delta > 0$ AND $\tau < 0 \Leftrightarrow$ asymptotic stability (2D).
- **Hartman–Grobman theorem** guarantees that classification from the linearization applies to the original non-linear system locally (provided no eigenvalue lies on the imaginary axis).
- **Physiological anchors** to remember: van der Pol → cardiac pacemaker; Hodgkin–Huxley → neuronal resting potential; Bergman → glucose–insulin stable spiral; FitzHugh–Nagumo → saddle = neuronal firing threshold.
- **Engineering metrics**: $\omega_{n} = \sqrt{\Delta}$, $\zeta = \tau / (2\sqrt{\Delta})$; $\vert\zeta\vert < 1$ → under-damped (spiral), $\vert\zeta\vert \geq 1$ → over-damped (node), $\zeta = 0$ → center.
- Always conclude with a **biological interpretation** to capture full KTU marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Phase Plane Analysis: Nullclines, Hopf Bifurcation and Limit Cycles, Van der Pol Oscillator
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT865 - MODELLING OF PHYSIOLOGICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Phase Plane Analysis: Nullclines, Hopf Bifurcation and Limit Cycles, Van der Pol Oscillator

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE 4 — PHASE PLANE ANALYSIS, HOPF BIFURCATION, LIMIT CYCLES & VAN DER POL OSCILLATOR

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Phase Plane Analysis — Formal Definition

**Phase Plane Analysis** is a graphical method used to study the qualitative behaviour of **second-order autonomous dynamical systems** of the form

$$\dot{x} = f(x, y)$$
$$\dot{y} = g(x, y)$$

by plotting the system's trajectory in the two-dimensional state space $(x, y)$ — known as the **phase plane**. Each point in the plane represents a unique state, and the curve traced as the system evolves is called a **trajectory** or **orbit**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** Phase-plane analysis is the *primary qualitative tool* for studying nonlinear physiological oscillators (cardiac pacemakers, neural firing, respiratory rhythm) where analytical closed-form solutions are typically impossible.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Hilly Terrain Picture"

Imagine a **marble rolling on a hilly landscape** under gravity. The marble's position $(x, y)$ on the terrain corresponds to the state of the system. Hills are **unstable** equilibria (the marble rolls off), valleys are **stable** equilibria (the marble settles), and **saddle points** are passes between two valleys. A **limit cycle** is like a *circular groove* in the terrain — once the marble enters it, it rolls around forever, neither spiralling in nor flying out. This is exactly how a heart pacemaker cell oscillates: it is not at rest, yet it never diverges — it self-sustains a periodic rhythm.

### 1.3 Key Terminology

| Term | Symbol | Meaning |
|------|--------|---------|
| State variables | $x, y$ | Independent quantities fully describing the system |
| Phase trajectory | $\Gamma$ | Curve traced by $(x(t), y(t))$ in phase space |
| Fixed point | $(x^*, y^*)$ | Where $f = g = 0$ — system is at rest |
| Nullcline | $f(x,y)=0$ or $g(x,y)=0$ | Curve where one derivative vanishes |
| Trajectory direction | Arrows | Determined by sign of $\dot{x}, \dot{y}$ |
| Limit cycle | $\gamma$ | Isolated closed orbit — periodic solution |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why phase plane (and not time-domain) plots?**
> In physiological modelling, we often care about *stability and rhythmicity* more than the exact time course. The phase plane reveals **at a glance** whether oscillations exist, whether they are stable, and how they emerge/disappear as parameters change — information that time-domain plots hide.

### 1.4 Why This Matters in Physiology

The human body is a **nonlinear oscillator factory**: the heart (cardiac SA node), neurons (Hodgkin–Huxley, FitzHugh–Nagumo), respiratory centres, pancreatic $\beta$-cells, and circadian genes *all* generate self-sustained rhythms. Phase plane analysis lets us **predict, classify, and tune** these rhythms mathematically.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Construction of the Phase Portrait — Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Identify the system** as a 2-D autonomous ODE.
2. **Find the nullclines** — set $\dot{x}=0$ and $\dot{y}=0$. Each nullcline is a curve.
3. **Locate fixed points** — intersections of the two nullclines satisfy $f=g=0$.
4. **Linearise** at each fixed point via the Jacobian matrix
$$J(x^*, y^*) = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{\partial f}{\partial x} & \dfrac{\partial f}{\partial y} \\[6pt] \dfrac{\partial g}{\partial x} & \dfrac{\partial g}{\partial y} \end{bmatrix}_{(x^*,y^*)}$$
5. **Compute eigenvalues** $\lambda_{1,2}$ of $J$. They classify the fixed point (node, saddle, spiral, centre).
6. **Determine trajectory direction** by checking the sign of $\dot{x}, \dot{y}$ in each region partitioned by nullclines.
7. **Identify closed orbits** — limit cycles — by analytical theorems (Poincaré–Bendixson, Liénard) or by direct simulation.
8. **Interpret physiologically** — what does the oscillation correspond to?

## 2.2 Classification of Linearised Fixed Points

Let $\tau = \text{tr}(J) = \lambda_1+\lambda_2$ and $\Delta = \det(J) = \lambda_1 \lambda_2$.

| Case | Discriminant $D = \tau^2 - 4\Delta$ | Behaviour | Type |
|------|-------------------------------------|-----------|------|
| $\Delta < 0$ | any | Saddle (one $\lambda>0$, one $\lambda<0$) | **Saddle** |
| $\Delta > 0$, $\tau^2 > 4\Delta$, $\tau < 0$ | + real | Both $\lambda<0$ | **Stable Node** |
| $\Delta > 0$, $\tau^2 > 4\Delta$, $\tau > 0$ | + real | Both $\lambda>0$ | **Unstable Node** |
| $\Delta > 0$, $\tau^2 < 4\Delta$, $\tau < 0$ | – real | Complex, $\Re \lambda<0$ | **Stable Spiral** |
| $\Delta > 0$, $\tau^2 < 4\Delta$, $\tau > 0$ | – real | Complex, $\Re \lambda>0$ | **Unstable Spiral** |
| $\tau = 0$, $\Delta > 0$ | – real | Pure imaginary $\lambda=\pm i\omega$ | **Centre** (linear) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Hopf bifurcation** occurs precisely at the boundary $\tau = 0, \Delta > 0$. Crossing this boundary in parameter space can give birth to a **limit cycle** — a sustained nonlinear oscillation.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Meaning / Use |
|---|------------------|-----------|----------------|
| 1 | 2-D autonomous system | $\dot{x}=f(x,y), \ \dot{y}=g(x,y)$ | Canonical form for phase plane |
| 2 | $x$-nullcline | $f(x,y)=0$ | $\dot{x}=0$ — vertical motion only |
| 3 | $y$-nullcline | $g(x,y)=0$ | $\dot{y}=0$ — horizontal motion only |
| 4 | Jacobian | $J=\begin{bmatrix}f_x & f_y \\ g_x & g_y\end{bmatrix}$ | Linearisation matrix |
| 5 | Eigenvalue equation | $\lambda^2 - \tau \lambda + \Delta = 0$ | Characteristic polynomial |
| 6 | Eigenvalues | $\lambda_{1,2} = \dfrac{\tau \pm \sqrt{\tau^2 - 4\Delta}}{2}$ | Determine fixed-point type |
| 7 | Hopf condition | $\tau(\mu_c)=0, \ \Delta(\mu_c)>0$ | Birth of limit cycle |
| 8 | Hopf frequency | $\omega_c = \sqrt{\Delta(\mu_c)}$ | Natural oscillation frequency |
| 9 | Van der Pol equation | $\ddot{x} - \mu(1-x^2)\dot{x} + x = 0$ | Canonical relaxation oscillator |
| 10 | Van der Pol state form | $\dot{x}=y, \ \dot{y}=\mu(1-x^2)y - x$ | Phase plane form |
| 11 | Liénard system | $\dot{x}=y - F(x), \ \dot{y}=-x$ | With $F'(x)=f(x)$ |
| 12 | Limit cycle condition (Liénard) | $F(a) < 0$ for $0<x<a$, $F(x)\to \infty$ | Guarantees *one* stable cycle |
| 13 | Energy of oscillator | $E = \tfrac{1}{2}(\dot{x}^2 + x^2)$ | $dE/dt = \mu(1-x^2)\dot{x}^2$ |
| 14 | Supercritical Hopf amplitude | $r = \sqrt{\mu/\alpha_1}$ | $\alpha_1$ from normal-form cubic |
| 15 | Period (Van der Pol, $\mu\ll 1$) | $T \approx 2\pi\left(1 - \dfrac{\mu^2}{16}\right)$ | Slow-growth correction |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Engineering Utility**
> - **Biomedical:** Designing artificial pacemakers, predicting arrhythmia, modelling $\beta$-cell bursting in diabetes.
> - **Circadian biology:** Light-input Hopf bifurcations explain jet-lag and shift-work disorders.
> - **Neural prosthetics:** FitzHugh–Nagumo (a cousin of Van der Pol) explains action-potential thresholds.

## 2.4 The Three Components in Detail

### A. Nullclines — "Skeleton of the Portrait"

- **$x$-nullcline** ($f=0$): the velocity in the $x$-direction is zero, so trajectories cross this curve *vertically*.
- **$y$-nullcline** ($g=0$): the velocity in the $y$-direction is zero, so trajectories cross this curve *horizontally*.
- **Fixed points** are intersections — there, *both* velocities vanish, so the trajectory momentarily stops.

In the four regions formed by the two nullclines, the signs of $(\dot{x}, \dot{y})$ determine the *flow direction* by simple quadrant analysis.

### B. Hopf Bifurcation — "Birth of an Oscillator"

A **Hopf bifurcation** is a *local* bifurcation in which a pair of complex-conjugate eigenvalues $\lambda_{1,2} = \alpha(\mu) \pm i\omega(\mu)$ of the Jacobian crosses the imaginary axis as a parameter $\mu$ passes through a critical value $\mu_c$:

$$\alpha(\mu_c) = 0, \quad \omega(\mu_c) = \omega_c \neq 0$$

- **Supercritical Hopf** ($\mu > \mu_c$): an attracting limit cycle of radius $r \sim \sqrt{\mu - \mu_c}$ is *born*. Physiological example: a healthy pacemaker entering self-sustained firing.
- **Subcritical Hopf** ($\mu < \mu_c$): an *unstable* limit cycle exists, and the system jumps to a large-amplitude attractor — model of a **pathological** transition (e.g., seizure onset in neural models).

### C. Limit Cycles — "Self-Sustained Loops"

A **limit cycle** is an *isolated* closed orbit in the phase plane. Isolation distinguishes it from a centre (which has a continuum of nested closed orbits).

| Type | Trajectories near the cycle | Stability |
|------|----------------------------|-----------|
| **Stable** | Spiral *inward* from outside, *outward* from inside | Attracting |
| **Unstable** | Spiral *outward* from outside, *inward* from inside | Repelling |
| **Semi-stable** | Spiral inward on one side, outward on the other | Half-stable |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Poincaré–Bendixson Theorem:** If a trajectory of a 2-D continuous system is *confined to a closed bounded region containing no fixed points*, then it must approach a limit cycle. This theorem is the workhorse for *proving* limit-cycle existence without solving the ODE.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Nullclines and Linearisation of a 2-D System

Consider the system
$$\dot{x} = 1 - x - x y \quad \text{(1)}$$
$$\dot{y} = x y - y \quad \text{(2)}$$

**Step 1: Find the nullclines.**

$x$-nullcline ($1 - x - xy = 0$) $\Rightarrow y = \dfrac{1-x}{x}$ (for $x \neq 0$).

$y$-nullcline ($xy - y = 0$) $\Rightarrow y(x - 1) = 0$, so $y=0$ **or** $x=1$.

**Step 2: Find fixed points.**

- Subcase A: $y=0 \Rightarrow 1 - x = 0 \Rightarrow (x^*, y^*) = (1, 0)$.
- Subcase B: $x=1 \Rightarrow y = 0$ (same fixed point).
- Special: $x=0$ from $x$-nullcline formula? Substitute $x=0$ in (1) gives $1 = 0$, no solution.

So **one fixed point**: $P_1 = (1, 0)$.

**Step 3: Compute the Jacobian.**

$$J(x,y) = \begin{bmatrix} -1 - y & -x \\ y & x - 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4: Evaluate at $P_1 = (1, 0)$.**

$$J(1,0) = \begin{bmatrix} -1 & -1 \\ 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 5: Eigenvalues.**

Characteristic equation: $\det(J - \lambda I) = (-1-\lambda)(0-\lambda) - 0 = 0$, giving

$$\lambda(\lambda + 1) = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \lambda_1 = 0, \ \lambda_2 = -1$$

**Step 6: Interpret.** A zero eigenvalue indicates a **non-hyperbolic fixed point** — borderline case where linearisation alone is insufficient. Higher-order analysis is required.

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — A System Exhibiting a Hopf Bifurcation

Consider
$$\dot{x} = \mu x - y - x(x^2 + y^2) \quad \text{(3)}$$
$$\dot{y} = x + \mu y - y(x^2 + y^2) \quad \text{(4)}$$

**Step 1: Convert to polar coordinates** $x = r\cos\theta, y = r\sin\theta$.

Using $\dot{r} = \dfrac{x\dot{x} + y\dot{y}}{r}$ and $\dot{\theta} = \dfrac{x\dot{y} - y\dot{x}}{r^2}$:

**Compute $x\dot{x} + y\dot{y}$:**

$$x\dot{x} = \mu x^2 - xy - x^2(x^2+y^2)$$
$$y\dot{y} = xy + \mu y^2 - y^2(x^2+y^2)$$

Adding:

$$x\dot{x} + y\dot{y} = \mu(x^2 + y^2) - (x^2+y^2)^2 = r^2(\mu - r^2)$$

Therefore

$$\dot{r} = r(\mu - r^2) \quad \text{(5)}$$

**Step 2: Compute $x\dot{y} - y\dot{x}$:**

$$x\dot{y} = x^2 + \mu xy - xy(x^2+y^2)$$
$$y\dot{x} = \mu xy - y^2 - xy(x^2+y^2)$$

Subtracting:

$$x\dot{y} - y\dot{x} = x^2 + y^2 = r^2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \dot{\theta} = 1 \quad \text{(6)}$$

**Step 3: Solve radial equation.**

Fixed points of (5): $r = 0$ and $r = \sqrt{\mu}$ (only if $\mu > 0$).

For $\mu < 0$: only $r=0$ exists. $\dot{r} = r \cdot (\mu - r^2)$ — at $r=0$, $\dot{r}$ is negative for $r>0$ small (since $\mu<0$), so $r \to 0$. The origin is **stable**.

For $\mu > 0$: $r = 0$ is unstable ($\dot{r}>0$ near origin since $\mu-r^2>0$); $r = \sqrt{\mu}$ is a stable limit cycle.

**Step 4: Conclusion.** The system exhibits a **supercritical Hopf bifurcation** at $\mu_c = 0$ with limit cycle radius $r^* = \sqrt{\mu}$ and frequency $\omega_c = 1$ rad/s.

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **Hopf normal form** — every supercritical Hopf bifurcation is locally equivalent to this system via centre-manifold reduction. Always quote the *normal form* in KTU answers to score full marks.

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — The Van der Pol Oscillator (Full Derivation)

The **Van der Pol equation** (1926), originally describing triode oscillator circuits and later applied to cardiac/biological rhythms:

$$\ddot{x} - \mu(1 - x^2)\dot{x} + x = 0 \quad \text{(7)}$$

where $\mu \geq 0$ is the **nonlinearity parameter**.

**Step 1: Convert to a 1st-order system** by setting $y = \dot{x}$:

$$\dot{x} = y \quad \text{(8)}$$
$$\dot{y} = \mu(1 - x^2) y - x \quad \text{(9)}$$

**Step 2: Find the nullclines.**

- $x$-nullcline: $y = 0$ (the $x$-axis).
- $y$-nullcline: $\mu(1-x^2) y = x \Rightarrow y = \dfrac{x}{\mu(1-x^2)}$ — has vertical asymptotes at $x = \pm 1$.

**Step 3: Find the only fixed point.** Intersection of $y=0$ and $y = x/[\mu(1-x^2)]$: substituting $y=0$ gives $x = 0$. So the unique fixed point is the **origin** $(0,0)$.

**Step 4: Linearise at origin.**

$$J(0,0) = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -1 & \mu \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 5: Eigenvalues.** Characteristic equation:

$$\lambda^2 - \mu \lambda + 1 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \lambda_{1,2} = \frac{\mu \pm \sqrt{\mu^2 - 4}}{2}$$

- For $\mu \in (0, 2)$: $\mu^2 - 4 < 0$, eigenvalues are **complex conjugates** with
$$\alpha = \Re(\lambda) = \frac{\mu}{2} > 0, \quad \omega = \Im(\lambda) = \frac{\sqrt{4 - \mu^2}}{2}$$
So origin is an **unstable spiral**.

- For $\mu > 2$: real positive eigenvalues, origin is an **unstable node**.

**Step 6: Hopf bifurcation at $\mu_c = 0$.** Note: for $\mu < 0$, eigenvalues have negative real part (stable spiral). The transition at $\mu = 0$ from stable spiral ($\mu<0$) to unstable spiral ($\mu>0$) is a **Hopf bifurcation** with critical frequency $\omega_c = 1$ rad/s.

**Step 7: Energy / Liénard analysis to prove limit cycle.**

Multiply (7) by $x$:

$$x\ddot{x} - \mu(1-x^2)x\dot{x} + x^2 = 0$$

$$\frac{d}{dt}\!\left[\frac{\dot{x}^2 + x^2}{2}\right] = \mu(1-x^2)\dot{x}^2 \quad \text{(10)}$$

The right side is a **Rayleigh dissipation function**:
- For $\vert x \vert < 1$: $\mu(1-x^2) > 0$ → energy *grows* (negative damping).
- For $\vert x \vert > 1$: $\mu(1-x^2) < 0$ → energy *decays* (positive damping).
- At $\vert x \vert = 1$: pure conservation, $dE/dt=0$.

This energy pump-decay mechanism guarantees an attracting closed orbit — a **limit cycle**.

**Step 8: Liénard's Theorem conditions.**

Liénard's system form: $\dot{x} = y - F(x), \ \dot{y} = -x$, where $F'(x) = f(x) = \mu(x^2 - 1)$.

So $F(x) = \mu\!\left(\dfrac{x^3}{3} - x\right)$.

Check Liénard's conditions for the existence of **exactly one stable limit cycle**:
1. $F(x)$ and $f(x)$ are continuously differentiable ✓
2. $F(x)$ is **odd** ✓ (since $x^3 - 3x$ is odd)
3. $F(x) < 0$ for $0 < x < \sqrt{3}$ and $F(x) > 0$ for $x > \sqrt{3}$ ✓
4. $F(x) \to \infty$ as $x \to \infty$ ✓

By **Liénard's Theorem**, the system has a **unique, stable limit cycle** for all $\mu > 0$.

**Step 9: Period of the limit cycle.**

For $\mu \to 0$ (weakly nonlinear), use perturbation:
$$T(\mu) \approx 2\pi\!\left(1 - \frac{\mu^2}{16} - \frac{5\mu^4}{768} + \cdots\right) \quad \text{(11)}$$

For $\mu \to \infty$ (relaxation limit), the cycle becomes a relaxation oscillator with period $T \sim \mu(3 - 2\ln 2)$.

## 3.4 Connection to Physiology

| Biological System | Limit Cycle Phenomenon | Relevant Model |
|-------------------|------------------------|----------------|
| SA-node cardiac pacemaker | Self-sustained beating | Modified Van der Pol / Bonhoeffer–Van der Pol |
| Squid giant axon | Action potential | FitzHugh–Nagumo (Van der Pol cousin) |
| Pancreatic $\beta$-cells | Bursting electrical activity | Hopf bifurcation in 3-D extension |
| Locomotion CPG | Rhythmic gait | Hopf bifurcation in half-centre oscillators |
| Respiratory rhythm | Eupnoea vs. gasping | Hopf/saddle-node on invariant circle |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mark-winning strategy for KTU answers:** Always finish a phase-plane or limit-cycle problem with a *physiological interpretation* sentence — examiners award full CO-mapping marks for this.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Phase-Plane Flow (Nullclines → Fixed Point → Limit Cycle)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start: 2D Autonomous System ẋ = f(x,y), ẏ = g(x,y)"] --> B["Find x-nullcline: f = 0"]
    A --> C["Find y-nullcline: g = 0"]
    B --> D["Intersect Nullclines"]
    C --> D
    D --> E["Locate Fixed Points (x*, y*)"]
    E --> F["Compute Jacobian J at (x*, y*)"]
    F --> G["Eigenvalues λ₁, λ₂ of J"]
    G --> H{"τ = 0 and Δ > 0?"}
    H -- "Yes" --> I["Hopf Bifurcation: Limit Cycle Born"]
    H -- "No" --> J["Classify: Node / Spiral / Saddle"]
    I --> K{"μ > μ_c (Supercritical)?"}
    K -- "Yes" --> L["Stable Limit Cycle Attracts Nearby Trajectories"]
    K -- "No" --> M["Unstable Limit Cycle Repels Trajectories"]
    L --> N["Physiological Interpretation: Self-Sustained Rhythm (e.g. heartbeat)"]
    M --> O["Pathological: Sudden Jump to Large Amplitude (e.g. seizure)"]
    J --> P["No Oscillation: Monotonic Decay or Growth"]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Van der Pol Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT["External Drive / Initial Condition"]
        IC["Initial State (x₀, ẋ₀)"]
    end

    IC --> INTEG1["Integrator 1: ∫ y dt → x"]

    INTEG1 --> NL["Nonlinear Block: μ(1 - x²)·y"]
    INTEG1 --> FB1["Linear Feedback: -x"]
    NL --> SUM["Summing Junction"]
    FB1 --> SUM
    SUM --> INTEG2["Integrator 2: ∫ (·) dt → y"]
    INTEG2 --> INTEG1
    INTEG2 --> OUT["Phase Trajectory Output (x, y)"]

    style NL fill:#fdd,stroke:#900
    style FB1 fill:#dfd,stroke:#090
    style INTEG1 fill:#dff,stroke:#009
    style INTEG2 fill:#dff,stroke:#009
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Limit-Cycle Stability Picture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph STABLE["Stable Limit Cycle (Supercritical Hopf)"]
        S1["Outer Trajectory: Spirals INWARD toward cycle"]
        S2["Inner Trajectory: Spirals OUTWARD toward cycle"]
        S3["Cycle γ: Isolated Closed Orbit"]
        S1 --> S3
        S2 --> S3
    end

    subgraph UNSTABLE["Unstable Limit Cycle (Subcritical Hopf)"]
        U1["Outer Trajectory: Spirals OUTWARD to large attractor"]
        U2["Inner Trajectory: Spirals INWARD to fixed point"]
        U3["Cycle γ_u: Repels on both sides"]
        U1 --> UA["Large-amplitude pathological state"]
        U2 --> UF["Stable fixed point (quiescence)"]
    end
```

## 4.4 Schematic — Energy Pump-Decay Mechanism in Van der Pol

```
  E = ½ (ẋ² + x²)

      ENERGY  │   dE/dt > 0 (NEGATIVE DAMPING, |x|<1)
       PUMP   │   ⇧ Energy grows, trajectory pushed outward
              │
    ──────────┼──────────  x-axis (state)
              │
      ENERGY  │   dE/dt < 0 (POSITIVE DAMPING, |x|>1)
       DECAY  │   ⇩ Energy dissipates, trajectory pulled inward
              │
  EQUILIBRIUM │   Net effect → Self-sustained OSCILLATION (limit cycle)
```

> [!NOTE]
> This energy pump-decay picture is the **single most important physical insight** behind relaxation oscillators. The student who draws this in a 14-mark KTU answer for Van der Pol usually scores full marks.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define nullclines and explain how they are used in phase-plane analysis.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Nullclines are curves in the phase plane along which one of the state derivatives vanishes. For a 2-D system $\dot{x}=f(x,y),\ \dot{y}=g(x,y)$, the $x$-nullcline is $f(x,y)=0$ and the $y$-nullcline is $g(x,y)=0$. Trajectories cross the $x$-nullcline *vertically* and the $y$-nullcline *horizontally*. Their intersections identify **fixed points** of the system. Nullclines partition the plane into regions of uniform flow direction, which together with linearisation at the fixed points, completely determine the qualitative phase portrait. **(3 marks)**

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**State the Poincaré–Bendixson theorem. Why is it significant for 2-D systems but not higher dimensions?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
