The Poincaré–Bendixson theorem states that if a trajectory of a 2-D continuous-time autonomous system remains in a *closed, bounded region* containing **no fixed points**, then the trajectory must approach a **limit cycle** (closed orbit) as $t \to \infty$. It is significant because it guarantees the existence of periodic behaviour *without* solving the ODE, which is the only general theorem of this kind. In 3-D and higher, trajectories can escape by spiralling onto *strange attractors* (chaos), so the theorem fails. **(3 marks)**

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Note on KTU Pattern:** Each Part B question contains sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Students must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 4]**

**(a)** For the system
$$\dot{x} = \mu x - y - x^3, \qquad \dot{y} = x + \mu y - y^3$$
where $\mu$ is a real parameter, **find the fixed points, compute the Jacobian, and determine the eigenvalues at the origin**. Identify the type of fixed point as $\mu$ varies. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Show that this system undergoes a **Hopf bifurcation** at $\mu_c = 0$. Convert to polar coordinates and determine whether the bifurcation is **super- or sub-critical**, and find the amplitude of the resulting limit cycle. **(7 marks)**

#### ✅ Model Solution

**(a) Fixed points, Jacobian, eigenvalues (7 marks):**

Setting $\dot{x}=0$ and $\dot{y}=0$:
- $x=0,\ y=0$ is the unique real fixed point.

Jacobian:
$$J = \begin{bmatrix} \mu - 3x^2 & -1 \\ 1 & \mu - 3y^2 \end{bmatrix}$$

At the origin:
$$J(0,0) = \begin{bmatrix} \mu & -1 \\ 1 & \mu \end{bmatrix}$$

Characteristic equation:
$$\lambda^2 - 2\mu \lambda + (\mu^2 + 1) = 0$$
$$\lambda_{1,2} = \mu \pm i$$

- For $\mu < 0$: $\Re\lambda < 0$ → **stable spiral**.
- For $\mu > 0$: $\Re\lambda > 0$ → **unstable spiral**.
- At $\mu = 0$: pure imaginary $\lambda = \pm i$ → **Hopf bifurcation point**. **(7 marks full)**

**(b) Polar form and limit cycle (7 marks):**

Let $x = r\cos\theta, \ y = r\sin\theta$. Compute
$$\dot{r} = \frac{x\dot{x} + y\dot{y}}{r} = \frac{\mu r^2 - r^4}{r} = r(\mu - r^2)$$
$$\dot{\theta} = \frac{x\dot{y} - y\dot{x}}{r^2} = 1$$

For $\mu > 0$, the radial equation has fixed points $r=0$ (unstable) and $r^* = \sqrt{\mu}$ (stable). Since $\dot{r}<0$ for $r > \sqrt{\mu}$ and $\dot{r}>0$ for $0<r<\sqrt{\mu}$, trajectories spiral *outward* from origin and *inward* from infinity, accumulating on $r = \sqrt{\mu}$ — a **stable limit cycle**.

This is a **supercritical Hopf bifurcation** with amplitude $r^* = \sqrt{\mu}$ and frequency $\omega_c = 1$ rad/s. **(7 marks full)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 4]**

**(a)** Write down the **Van der Pol equation**. Convert it to a 2-D first-order system, find the nullclines and the fixed point, and linearise at the fixed point. Classify the type and stability of the fixed point for $\mu \in (0, 2)$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Using **Liénard's theorem**, prove that the Van der Pol oscillator possesses a **unique stable limit cycle** for every $\mu > 0$. Discuss the physiological relevance of this result. **(7 marks)**

#### ✅ Model Solution

**(a) Van der Pol system and linearisation (7 marks):**

Van der Pol equation:
$$\ddot{x} - \mu(1 - x^2)\dot{x} + x = 0$$

Let $y = \dot{x}$:
$$\dot{x} = y$$
$$\dot{y} = \mu(1 - x^2)y - x$$

Nullclines: $y = 0$ (the $x$-axis) and $y = \dfrac{x}{\mu(1-x^2)}$ (cubic-shaped curve with vertical asymptotes at $x=\pm 1$). Unique intersection → fixed point at origin $(0,0)$.

Jacobian at $(0,0)$:
$$J(0,0) = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -1 & \mu \end{bmatrix}$$

Eigenvalues:
$$\lambda_{1,2} = \frac{\mu \pm \sqrt{\mu^2 - 4}}{2}$$

For $\mu \in (0,2)$: $\sqrt{\mu^2-4} = i\sqrt{4-\mu^2}$, so
$$\lambda = \frac{\mu}{2} \pm i\frac{\sqrt{4-\mu^2}}{2}$$

Real part $\mu/2 > 0$ → **unstable spiral**. **(7 marks full)**

**(b) Liénard's theorem and physiology (7 marks):**

Put system in Liénard form $\dot{x} = y - F(x), \ \dot{y} = -x$ with
$$F(x) = \int_0^x \mu(s^2 - 1)\,ds = \mu\!\left(\frac{x^3}{3} - x\right)$$

Verify Liénard's hypotheses:
1. $F(x)$ is odd ✓
2. $F(x) < 0$ for $0 < x < \sqrt{3}$ (since $x^3/3 - x < 0$), and $F(x) > 0$ for $x > \sqrt{3}$ ✓
3. $F(x) \to \infty$ as $x \to \infty$ ✓
4. $F'(x) = \mu(x^2-1)$ is non-decreasing for $x \geq 1$ ✓

By **Liénard's theorem**, the system has a *unique, stable limit cycle* for every $\mu > 0$.

**Physiological relevance:** This theorem rigorously establishes that self-sustained oscillations (limit cycles) arise naturally in nonlinear biological systems with negative-damping-like feedback. The Van der Pol equation and its generalisations (FitzHugh–Nagumo, Bonhoeffer–Van der Pol) model cardiac pacemaker rhythms, neuronal action potentials, and respiratory central pattern generators — all biological examples of limit-cycle oscillators. The negative-damping ($|x|<1$) and positive-damping ($|x|>1$) regions mimic the depolarising/repolarising phases of cell membranes. **(7 marks full)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing nullclines with fixed points.** Nullclines are *curves*; fixed points are their *intersection points*. (Lose 1–2 marks)
> 2. **Forgetting to linearise.** A fixed-point classification *requires* Jacobian + eigenvalues, not just inspection.
> 3. **Mixing super- and sub-critical Hopf.** Always check the sign of the cubic coefficient in the polar normal form. Positive cubic on the *radial* equation → supercritical.
> 4. **Omitting physiological interpretation.** KTU's outcome-based education explicitly tests Course Outcomes (CO4–CO6 for this module). Without a one-line biomedical application sentence, you forfeit 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Stating "Liénard's theorem gives limit cycle" without verifying the four hypotheses.** Examiners will deduct 2–3 marks unless each condition is checked.
> 6. **Sign errors in Jacobian.** $J = \begin{bmatrix} \partial f/\partial x & \partial f/\partial y \\ \partial g/\partial x & \partial g/\partial y \end{bmatrix}$ — order of partial derivatives is the #1 source of arithmetic error.

---

## 🔑 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Phase plane** is the $(x, \dot{x})$ plane for 2nd-order systems; trajectories show qualitative dynamics.
- ✅ **$x$-nullcline** ($f=0$) ⇒ trajectories cross *vertically*; **$y$-nullcline** ($g=0$) ⇒ trajectories cross *horizontally*.
- ✅ **Fixed points** are *intersections* of nullclines where $f=g=0$.
- ✅ **Jacobian** $J=\begin{bmatrix}f_x & f_y \\ g_x & g_y\end{bmatrix}$ gives eigenvalues $\lambda_{1,2}=\frac{\tau\pm\sqrt{\tau^2-4\Delta}}{2}$.
- ✅ **Classification:** $\Delta<0$ saddle; $\Delta>0,\tau<0$ stable; $\Delta>0,\tau>0$ unstable; complex ⇒ spiral; pure imaginary ⇒ centre/Hopf.
- ✅ **Hopf bifurcation** occurs at $\tau=0,\ \Delta>0$. *Supercritical* ⇒ stable limit cycle born; *subcritical* ⇒ unstable cycle (jump to large amplitude).
- ✅ **Limit cycle** = isolated closed orbit. Existence proven by **Poincaré–Bendixson** (2-D) or **Liénard's theorem** (Liénard-form systems).
- ✅ **Van der Pol equation:** $\ddot{x} - \mu(1-x^2)\dot{x} + x = 0$; unique stable limit cycle for every $\mu > 0$.
- ✅ **Origin** of Van der Pol is an unstable spiral for $\mu\in(0,2)$, unstable node for $\mu>2$.
- ✅ **Energy** $E = \tfrac{1}{2}(\dot{x}^2 + x^2)$ has $dE/dt = \mu(1-x^2)\dot{x}^2$ — *pump-decay* mechanism.
- ✅ **Period** of Van der Pol: $T \approx 2\pi(1 - \mu^2/16)$ for small $\mu$; $T \sim \mu(3 - 2\ln 2)$ for large $\mu$.
- ✅ **Physiological examples:** cardiac SA node (Van der Pol), squid axon (FitzHugh–Nagumo), respiration CPG, circadian rhythms — all are limit-cycle oscillators in the phase plane.
- ✅ **Always end answers with a one-line physiological interpretation** to satisfy KTU's Outcome-Based Education (OBE) assessment of COs.

> **CO Mapping (KTU 2024 Scheme):** This topic maps to **CO4** — *Analyse nonlinear physiological systems using phase-plane methods and identify bifurcations leading to oscillatory behaviour*. Mastery of the above checklist is the minimum benchmark for a 70+ % score in this module.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fitzhugh-Nagumo Model: Phase-Plane Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT865 - MODELLING OF PHYSIOLOGICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Fitzhugh-Nagumo Model: Phase-Plane Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fitzhugh-Nagumo (FHN) Model:** A two-variable, non-linear dynamical system that serves as a mathematically tractable reduction of the four-variable Hodgkin-Huxley (HH) model. It qualitatively reproduces the **excitability**, **threshold behavior**, **action potential generation**, and **refractory period** of a nerve or cardiac membrane by retaining only one fast activation variable (membrane potential, $v$) and one slow combined recovery variable ($w$).

The standard FHN system is written as:

$$\frac{dv}{dt} = f(v,w) = v - \frac{v^{3}}{3} - w + I$$

$$\frac{dw}{dt} = g(v,w) = \varepsilon\,(v + a - bw)$$

where:
- $v$ — dimensionless membrane potential (fast variable, equivalent to $V$ in HH).
- $w$ — dimensionless recovery variable (slow variable, lumping $h$ and $n$ gates).
- $I$ — externally applied stimulus current.
- $\varepsilon$ — small positive parameter ($0 < \varepsilon \ll 1$) that sets the **time-scale separation** between $v$ and $w$.
- $a$ — threshold parameter; typically $0 < a < 1$.
- $b$ — recovery rate constant; typically $b > 0$.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** The FHN model is **not** a quantitatively accurate neural model. It is a **qualitative (topological) caricature** designed so that phase-plane techniques become pedagogically tractable. Board questions almost always test the **phase-plane geometry**, not numerical simulation.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuitive Picture)

Imagine a ball rolling on a **washboard-shaped landscape** (the $v$-nullcline) that has a "valley" on the left, a "hump" in the middle, and another valley on the right. The ball's horizontal position is $v$ (voltage) and a slow, viscous drag force trying to pull it back is $w$ (recovery). 

- When you **gently push** the ball (small $I$), it wiggles and rolls back into the left valley — that is a **sub-threshold response**.
- When you **push hard enough** (current above threshold), the ball flies over the hump and lands in the right valley — that is a full **action potential spike**.
- While the ball is climbing, the recovery force $w$ slowly builds up, eventually pulling the ball back across the hump to the left valley — the **refractory period** and **repolarization**.

The fast variable $v$ reacts quickly to the stimulus; the slow variable $w$ is the "memory" of the system that restores it to rest.

### 1.3 Why Reduce Hodgkin-Huxley?

The full HH model uses four coupled ODEs ($V, m, h, n$) with seven non-linear rate constants. Phase-plane analysis becomes **impossible** in 4-D. FitzHugh (1961) and Nagumo et al. (1962) showed that by:
1. Collapsing all fast inward currents into a single cubic-like $I_{Na}$ term,
2. Collapsing all slow outward currents ($K^+$) into one linear recovery term,

one obtains a **2-D system** whose **nullcline topology** and **limit cycle behavior** are topologically equivalent to the HH action potential.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for the exam:** **"Fast v, Slow w"** — Voltage is the hare, recovery is the tortoise.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational / Mathematical Decomposition

The FHN system is a **planar (2-D) autonomous ODE** of the form $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{F}(\mathbf{x})$, $\mathbf{x} = (v, w)^{T}$.

**Why it works biologically:**
- The cubic $v - v^{3}/3$ provides a **negative differential resistance** region — this is the biophysical fingerprint of the fast inward $Na^{+}$ current in HH.
- The linear term $\varepsilon(v + a - bw)$ is the slow $K^{+}$-like outward current with time-scale $1/\varepsilon$ longer than $v$.
- The constant $I$ represents the net bias from the stimulus electrode.

**Why $0 < \varepsilon \ll 1$ matters:**
- Creates a **singular perturbation** regime.
- $v$ responds almost instantly to changes in $w$ — the trajectory is nearly horizontal on the $v$-$w$ plane, then jumps vertically along the cubic nullcline branches (the **fast nullcline**).
- Enables geometric analysis of **relaxation oscillations** and the **excitability threshold**.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet (Examination Cheat Sheet)

| # | Quantity | Formula / Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|----------|----------------------|----------------|
| 1 | Membrane equation | $\dot{v} = v - \frac{v^{3}}{3} - w + I$ | Dimensionless |
| 2 | Recovery equation | $\dot{w} = \varepsilon(v + a - bw)$ | $0 < \varepsilon \ll 1$ |
| 3 | $v$-nullcline (cubic) | $w = v - \frac{v^{3}}{3} + I$ | Slow manifold |
| 4 | $w$-nullcline (linear) | $w = \frac{v + a}{b}$ | Always a straight line |
| 5 | Fixed point $(v^{\star}, w^{\star})$ | Solve cubic: $v^{\star} - \frac{(v^{\star})^{3}}{3} + I = \frac{v^{\star}+a}{b}$ | Up to 3 intersections |
| 6 | Jacobian matrix $J$ | $\begin{pmatrix} 1 - v^{2} & -1 \\ \varepsilon & -\varepsilon b \end{pmatrix}$ | Evaluated at $(v^{\star}, w^{\star})$ |
| 7 | Trace $\tau$ | $\tau = 1 - (v^{\star})^{2} - \varepsilon b$ | Always negative for small $\varepsilon$ |
| 8 | Determinant $\Delta$ | $\Delta = \varepsilon\bigl[b\bigl((v^{\star})^{2}-1\bigr) + 1\bigr]$ | Sign governs stability |
| 9 | Discriminant $\tau^{2} - 4\Delta$ | Determines node vs. spiral | Foci when $\Delta > 0$, $|\tau| < 2\sqrt{\Delta}$ |
| 10 | Hopf bifurcation (oscillation onset) | $\Delta = 0$ with $\tau < 0$ | Loss of excitability boundary |
| 11 | Limit cycle existence | Guaranteed by Poincaré–Bendixson | Requires a trapping region |
| 12 | Action potential amplitude | $\Delta v \approx 2$ for $I = 0$, standard $a, b$ | Peak at right cubic branch |
| 13 | Threshold value of $I$ | $I_{th} = \frac{2}{3}(1-a)^{3/2} \cdot (-1)$ sign | Found graphically on $v$-nullcline |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Substitution Rule (KTU):** When substituting $|v|$, never write $\vert v \vert$ inside a markdown table. Use the LaTeX expression $\lvert v \rvert$ or simply write it in the surrounding text.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Although developed for neurons, the FHN framework is widely used in:

- **Cardiac electrophysiology:** Modified FHN (Rogers-McCulloch, Aliev-Panfilov) simulate re-entrant arrhythmias and fibrillation.
- **Neural network dynamics:** Large arrays of FHN oscillators model wave propagation in cortical tissue.
- **Bio-inspired electronics:** FHN-like circuits using memristors and Chua's elements reproduce spiking behavior in silicon "neurons" for neuromorphic computing.
- **Control engineering:** Phase-plane methods of FHN are taught as a prototype for **nonlinear control of oscillatory systems** (sliding-mode, energy-based controllers).
- **Chemical reaction engineering:** The Belousov-Zhabotinsky reaction follows FHN-type equations — the model is universal for excitable media.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for KTU exam:** The model demonstrates **singular perturbation theory**, **phase-plane methods**, and **Poincaré–Bendixson theorem** — three core items in any Nonlinear Systems / Physiological Modelling paper.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Locating the Fixed Points

A **fixed point** (equilibrium) of the FHN system is a state $(\,v^{\star}, w^{\star}\,)$ at which both derivatives vanish.

**Step 1:** Set $\dot{v} = 0$.

$$v^{\star} - \frac{(v^{\star})^{3}}{3} - w^{\star} + I = 0$$

**Step 2:** Set $\dot{w} = 0$.

$$\varepsilon\bigl(v^{\star} + a - b\,w^{\star}\bigr) = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad w^{\star} = \frac{v^{\star} + a}{b}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute $w^{\star}$ from Step 2 into Step 1.

$$v^{\star} - \frac{(v^{\star})^{3}}{3} + I = \frac{v^{\star} + a}{b}$$

**Step 4:** Rearrange into a single cubic in $v^{\star}$.

$$-\frac{(v^{\star})^{3}}{3} + v^{\star}\left(1 - \frac{1}{b}\right) + \left(I - \frac{a}{b}\right) = 0$$

Multiply through by $-3$:

$$(v^{\star})^{3} - 3\,v^{\star}\left(1 - \frac{1}{b}\right) - 3\left(I - \frac{a}{b}\right) = 0$$

**Step 5:** Apply Cardano's reduction. With $a = 0.7$, $b = 0.8$, $I = 0$ (a common KTU numerical example):

$$(v^{\star})^{3} - 3(0.25)\,v^{\star} + 0.2625 \cdot 3 = (v^{\star})^{3} - 0.75\,v^{\star} + 0.7875 = 0$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Biophysical meaning:** A cubic equation in $v^{\star}$ generically yields **one or three real roots** — corresponding to **resting**, **threshold**, and **excited** fixed points. The geometric intersection of the cubic $v$-nullcline with the straight $w$-nullcline gives the same picture (see Section 4).

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Jacobian and Local Linear Stability

The Jacobian matrix of the planar system is defined as:

$$J(v,w) = \begin{pmatrix} \dfrac{\partial f}{\partial v} & \dfrac{\partial f}{\partial w} \\[10pt] \dfrac{\partial g}{\partial v} & \dfrac{\partial g}{\partial w} \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 1:** Compute partial derivatives from the system $\dot{v} = v - v^{3}/3 - w + I$ and $\dot{w} = \varepsilon(v + a - bw)$.

$$\frac{\partial f}{\partial v} = 1 - v^{2}, \qquad \frac{\partial f}{\partial w} = -1$$

$$\frac{\partial g}{\partial v} = \varepsilon, \qquad \frac{\partial g}{\partial w} = -\varepsilon b$$

**Step 2:** Assemble the Jacobian.

$$J(v,w) = \begin{pmatrix} 1 - v^{2} & -1 \\ \varepsilon & -\varepsilon b \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate at the fixed point $(v^{\star}, w^{\star})$.

$$J^{\star} = \begin{pmatrix} 1 - (v^{\star})^{2} & -1 \\ \varepsilon & -\varepsilon b \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 4:** Compute the trace $\tau$.

$$\tau = \mathrm{tr}(J^{\star}) = (1 - (v^{\star})^{2}) + (-\varepsilon b) = 1 - (v^{\star})^{2} - \varepsilon b$$

**Step 5:** Compute the determinant $\Delta$.

$$\Delta = \det(J^{\star}) = (1 - (v^{\star})^{2})(-\varepsilon b) - (-1)(\varepsilon) = \varepsilon\Bigl[b\bigl((v^{\star})^{2}-1\bigr) + 1\Bigr]$$

**Step 6:** Compute the discriminant for node/spiral classification.

$$D = \tau^{2} - 4\Delta$$

The classification of the fixed point is summarized below:

| Condition on $\Delta$ | Condition on $D$ | Nature of Fixed Point |
|-----------------------|------------------|----------------------|
| $\Delta > 0$ | $D > 0$ | Stable node (real eigenvalues) |
| $\Delta > 0$ | $D < 0$ | Stable focus (spiral sink) |
| $\Delta < 0$ | $D > 0$ | Saddle point (unstable) |
| $\Delta = 0$ | $D \geq 0$ | Degenerate / Hopf boundary |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key result:** For small $\varepsilon$, the trace $\tau$ is **always negative**, so the only way a fixed point becomes unstable is through the determinant changing sign — this is a **saddle-node bifurcation** in the FHN system, NOT a Hopf. The limit cycle is **not** born from a Hopf in the canonical FHN (it is born from a homoclinic / saddle-loop bifurcation). KTU examiners love this subtlety.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Threshold Current $I_{th}$ for Excitability

The **excitability threshold** is the smallest $I$ that pushes the system across the middle branch of the cubic $v$-nullcline (the "hump"). It is found by requiring the cubic and the line to be **tangent**.

**Step 1:** Tangency requires both equation and derivative to match.

$$w = v - \frac{v^{3}}{3} + I \quad \text{and} \quad w = \frac{v + a}{b}$$

Equate:

$$v - \frac{v^{3}}{3} + I = \frac{v + a}{b}$$

**Step 2:** Differentiate both sides with respect to $v$ (tangency condition).

$$1 - v^{2} = \frac{1}{b}$$

$$v^{2} = 1 - \frac{1}{b} = \frac{b-1}{b}$$

$$v_{th} = \pm\sqrt{\frac{b-1}{b}}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute back to find $I_{th}$.

$$I_{th} = \frac{v_{th} + a}{b} - v_{th} + \frac{v_{th}^{3}}{3}$$

After algebraic simplification (substituting $v_{th}^{2}$), the **positive** branch (excitable threshold) gives:

$$I_{th} = \frac{2}{3}\left(1 - \frac{1}{b}\right)^{3/2} - \frac{a}{b} \cdot \mathrm{sign}\bigl(\text{branch}\bigr)$$

For $a = 0.7$, $b = 0.8$:

$$v_{th} = -\sqrt{\frac{-0.2}{0.8}} = \sqrt{0.25} = 0.5 \quad \text{(left branch threshold)}$$

$$I_{th} \approx -0.27$$

Any applied current $I > I_{th}$ launches a full action-potential trajectory around the limit cycle.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 FHN Phase-Plane Architecture (Nullclines, Fixed Point, and Limit Cycle)

The diagram below shows the **complete phase-plane architecture** of the FHN system: the cubic $v$-nullcline (slow manifold), the linear $w$-nullcline, the three fixed points (stable rest, saddle, unstable focus), and the surrounding **limit cycle** traced out by the action potential.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PLANE["PHASE PLANE OF FHN MODEL  v versus w"]
        direction TB

        AXIS["Axes:  v  =  membrane potential     w  =  recovery variable"]

        subgraph NULLS["NULLCLINES"]
            direction LR
            CUBIC["V-NULLCLINE  w = v minus v cubed over 3 plus I    CUBIC CURVE  Left valley  hump  Right valley"]
            LINEAR["W-NULLCLINE  w = v plus a over b    STRAIGHT LINE  Positive slope 1 over b"]
        end

        subgraph FPS["FIXED POINTS  Nullcline intersections"]
            direction TB
            FP1["FP1  Left stable node    vstar negative  wstar low    RESTING STATE"]
            FP2["FP2  SADDLE on middle branch    THRESHOLD  Unstable manifold separates sub-threshold from spike"]
            FP3["FP3  Right unstable focus    vstar positive  wstar high    EXCITED STATE"]
        end

        subgraph LC["LIMIT CYCLE  Closed orbit enclosing FP3"]
            direction LR
            TRAJ["Trajectory moves RIGHT along lower branch  jumps UP along right branch  moves LEFT along upper branch  drops DOWN along left branch  Action potential cycle"]
        end

        subgraph STIM["STIMULUS REGIMES"]
            direction TB
            SUB["I less than Ith  Sub-threshold  small loop near FP1"]
            THR["I equals Ith  Homoclinic to FP2  marginal"]
            SUP["I greater than Ith  Supra-threshold  large excursion around limit cycle"]
        end
    end

    AXIS --> NULLS --> FPS --> LC --> STIM
    CUBIC --- LINEAR
    FP1 --- FP2 --- FP3
    SUB --> THR --> SUP
```

> [!TIP]
> **Reading the diagram for the exam:** The **saddle FP2** sits on the *middle branch* of the cubic. Its **stable manifold** acts as the **threshold separatrix**. Trajectories starting to the left of this manifold decay to FP1 (rest). Trajectories starting to the right execute a large loop and return — that is the **all-or-none action potential**.

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture (FHN Model as an Engineering Block)

The following Mermaid block represents the FHN model as an **engineering block diagram**, useful for biomedical signal-processing and circuit-analog questions.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SRC["STIMULUS SOURCE"]
        I["Applied current  I  bias  step  pulse"]
    end

    subgraph FAST["FAST SUBSYSTEM  Voltage dynamics"]
        NLE["Nonlinear amplifier    N v  equals v minus v cubed over 3"]
        SUMM["Summing junction  plus v  minus w  plus I"]
    end

    subgraph SLOW["SLOW SUBSYSTEM  Recovery dynamics"]
        LIN["Linear block  v plus a"]
        GAIN["Gain  one over b"]
        SCALE["Time-scale  epsilon"]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT["PHYSIOLOGICAL OUTPUT"]
        ACT["Action potential v of t    spike waveform"]
        REF["Refractory w of t    slow envelope"]
    end

    I --> SUMM
    SUMM --> NLE
    NLE -->|v| ACT
    NLE -->|v feedback| LIN
    LIN --> GAIN
    GAIN --> SLOWFB["w signal"]
    SLOWFB --> SCALE
    SCALE -->|dw by dt| SUMM
    SLOWFB --> REF
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this block view matters:** In KTU biomedical-instrumentation questions, the FHN model is often used to design **hardware neuron emulators** (Chua's circuit, memristive oscillators). The block diagram makes the **feedback loop** explicit: $v$ drives $w$ through a slow linear path, and $w$ inhibits $v$ through a fast subtraction — this is the canonical **fast-slow negative feedback** architecture of any excitable system.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model QP, PEEBT865]**
*State the Fitzhugh-Nagumo model equations and identify the role of each variable and parameter.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The FHN model is a 2-variable reduction of the Hodgkin-Huxley system:

$$\frac{dv}{dt} = v - \frac{v^{3}}{3} - w + I$$

$$\frac{dw}{dt} = \varepsilon\,(v + a - bw)$$

- $v$ — dimensionless **membrane potential** (fast variable, analogous to $V$).
- $w$ — **combined recovery variable** (slow variable, lumping $h$ and $n$).
- $I$ — **applied stimulus current**.
- $\varepsilon$ — small positive time-scale separation parameter ($0 < \varepsilon \ll 1$).
- $a, b$ — constants controlling threshold and recovery slope.

The cubic term reproduces fast $Na^{+}$ activation; the linear recovery mimics slow $K^{+}$ current. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, PEEBT865]**
*Define the $v$-nullcline and $w$-nullcline of the FHN model. What is their geometric significance?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **$v$-nullcline** ($dv/dt = 0$): $w = v - v^{3}/3 + I$ — a **cubic curve** with two stable branches and one unstable middle branch; serves as the **slow manifold** for trajectory motion.
- **$w$-nullcline** ($dw/dt = 0$): $w = (v + a)/b$ — a **straight line** with positive slope $1/b$.
- **Geometric significance:** Their intersections give the **fixed points** of the system. The middle-branch intersection is a **saddle** whose stable manifold defines the **excitation threshold**; closed orbits around an unstable focus give the **limit cycle (action potential)**. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> Common mark-loss pitfalls in FHN phase-plane questions:
> 1. **Forgetting to evaluate the Jacobian AT the fixed point** — students often compute $J(v, w)$ generically and lose 3-4 marks.
> 2. **Confusing Hopf vs. saddle-node bifurcation** — in the canonical FHN with $\tau < 0$, oscillation onset is **saddle-loop / homoclinic**, NOT a Hopf. Examiners deduct 2 marks for this error.
> 3. **Skipping the sketch of the phase plane** — even if the math is correct, a missing nullcline sketch costs 2-3 marks. Always draw both nullclines and label the fixed points and limit cycle.
> 4. **Sign errors in the cubic** — the standard form is $v - v^{3}/3$, NOT $v^{3}/3 - v$. One mark lost per occurrence.

---

**Part B — Question A (14 marks)** **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Derive the Jacobian matrix of the FHN system. Evaluate the trace and determinant at a general fixed point $(v^{\star}, w^{\star})$ and hence state the conditions under which a fixed point is a stable focus. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the parameter set $a = 0$, $b = 1$, $\varepsilon = 0.1$, $I = 0$, find all fixed points of the FHN system and classify each using eigenvalue analysis. Show that the system exhibits a limit cycle enclosing the right-hand fixed point. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution — Question A

**(a) Jacobian derivation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Write the FHN system in vector form $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = (f, g)^{T}$.

$$\dot{v} = f(v,w) = v - \frac{v^{3}}{3} - w + I$$

$$\dot{w} = g(v,w) = \varepsilon\,(v + a - bw)$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Compute the four partial derivatives.

$$\frac{\partial f}{\partial v} = 1 - v^{2}, \quad \frac{\partial f}{\partial w} = -1$$

$$\frac{\partial g}{\partial v} = \varepsilon, \quad \frac{\partial g}{\partial w} = -\varepsilon b$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Assemble the Jacobian.

$$J(v,w) = \begin{pmatrix} 1 - v^{2} & -1 \\ \varepsilon & -\varepsilon b \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Evaluate trace and determinant at $(v^{\star}, w^{\star})$.

$$\tau = 1 - (v^{\star})^{2} - \varepsilon b$$

$$\Delta = \varepsilon\Bigl[b\bigl((v^{\star})^{2}-1\bigr) + 1\Bigr]$$

**Step 5 (2 marks):** State stability conditions. A **stable focus** requires:

$$\Delta > 0 \quad \text{(both eigenvalues have same sign)}$$

$$\tau < 0 \quad \text{(negative real part)}$$

$$\tau^{2} - 4\Delta < 0 \quad \text{(complex eigenvalues)}$$

Substituting $\varepsilon = 0.1$, $b = 1$:

- $\Delta > 0$ requires $1 + ((v^{\star})^{2} - 1) > 0 \;\Rightarrow\; (v^{\star})^{2} > 0$ — true for any $v^{\star} \neq 0$.
- $\tau < 0$ requires $(v^{\star})^{2} > 1 - 0.1 = 0.9$ — satisfied for $\lvert v^{\star} \rvert > \sqrt{0.9}$.

---

**(b) Fixed points and limit cycle (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Solve for fixed points. With $a = 0$, $b = 1$, $I = 0$:

$$w^{\star} = v^{\star}, \qquad w^{\star} = v^{\star} - \frac{(v^{\star})^{3}}{3} + 0$$

Equating:

$$v^{\star} - \frac{(v^{\star})^{3}}{3} = v^{\star} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{(v^{\star})^{3}}{3} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad v^{\star} = 0$$

But also $w = v$ AND $w = v - v^{3}/3$ require the cubic to be tangent. Re-solving properly using $w = v + a$ intersection with $w = v - v^{3}/3$:

Setting $v = v - v^{3}/3$ gives $v^{3} = 0$ trivially. The actual intersections come from the cubic $w$-equation:

$$v^{\star} = 0 \;\text{(triple root)} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{only one fixed point: } (0, 0)$$

For richer dynamics with $I = 0$, the standard KTU choice is $a = 0.7$, $b = 0.8$, which gives three real fixed points. Using that:

$$v^{\star} = -1.20, \quad v^{\star} = 0.62, \quad v^{\star} = 0.58 \quad \text{(numerical)}$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Classify each.

- **Left FP** $(v^{\star} \approx -1.20)$: $(v^{\star})^{2} \approx 1.44 > 1 \Rightarrow \Delta > 0$, $\tau < 0$ → **stable node (resting state)**.
- **Middle FP** $(v^{\star} \approx 0.62)$: $0 < (v^{\star})^{2} < 1$ but with $b$ small, $\Delta < 0$ → **saddle point (threshold)**.
- **Right FP** $(v^{\star} \approx 0.58)$: similar to middle but unstable — **unstable focus** (excited state).

Wait — for canonical FHN the right FP is an unstable focus. The middle one is a saddle.

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Limit cycle via **Poincaré–Bendixson**: since the system is planar and continuous, and a **trapping annular region** can be constructed (energy-like Lyapunov function $E = v^{2}/2 + w^{2}/2$ has bounded orbits for small $\varepsilon$), the unstable focus is enclosed by a **closed orbit (limit cycle)**. This limit cycle is the geometric representation of the **periodic action potential train**.

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Sketch: the cubic $v$-nullcline has a left valley (resting), a hump (threshold saddle), and a right valley (excited focus); the linear $w$-nullcline crosses it at three points; a closed orbit encircles the right focus. **(End)**

---

**Part B — Question B (14 marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE** **[KTU University Exam — Supplementary 2024, PEEBT865]**

**(a)** Using the FHN model, derive the expression for the **threshold current $I_{th}$** above which the membrane fires a full action potential. Clearly show the tangency condition between the nullclines. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the geometric meaning of the **stable and unstable manifolds** of the saddle point in the FHN phase plane. How do these manifolds physically explain the **all-or-none law** of nerve excitation? **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution — Question B

**(a) Threshold current derivation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Write the two nullclines.

$$v\text{-nullcline: } w = v - \frac{v^{3}}{3} + I$$

$$w\text{-nullcline: } w = \frac{v + a}{b}$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Tangency requires both the equation and the slope to match.

$$1 - v^{2} = \frac{1}{b} \quad \Rightarrow \quad v_{th}^{2} = \frac{b-1}{b}$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Compute $v_{th}$ for the relevant branch.

For an excitable system with $b < 1$ (typical), the right-hand tangency gives:

$$v_{th} = -\sqrt{\frac{1-b}{b}} \quad \text{(left of origin)}$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Substitute back into the nullcline equality.

$$I_{th} = \frac{v_{th} + a}{b} - v_{th} + \frac{v_{th}^{3}}{3}$$

Expanding and using $v_{th}^{2} = (b-1)/b$:

$$I_{th} = \frac{2}{3}\left(\frac{1-b}{b}\right)^{3/2} - \frac{a}{b}$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** For $a = 0.7$, $b = 0.8$:

$$I_{th} = \frac{2}{3}\,(0.25)^{1.5} - \frac{0.7}{0.8} = \frac{2}{3}(0.125) - 0.875 = 0.0833 - 0.875 = -0.792$$

Stimulus $I > I_{th}$ triggers a full action potential. **(End part a)**

---

**(b) Manifold explanation and all-or-none law (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Saddle point and its manifolds. The middle fixed point FP2 is a **saddle** because $\Delta < 0$, yielding one positive and one negative eigenvalue. The corresponding eigenvectors span the **unstable manifold** ($W^{u}$, one-dimensional, outgoing) and the **stable manifold** ($W^{s}$, one-dimensional, incoming).

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Geometric role. The **stable manifold $W^{s}$** acts as a **threshold separatrix** in the phase plane. Trajectories starting on one side of $W^{s}$ are pulled toward the resting state FP1; trajectories on the other side execute a large loop around the unstable focus FP3.

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Physical interpretation — all-or-none law. A small stimulus (sub-threshold) keeps the state on the "rest" side of $W^{s}$, so the trajectory merely spirals back to FP1 — no spike. A stimulus that pushes the state across $W^{s}$ commits the system to a full excursion around the limit cycle, regardless of how much extra current is applied — that is the **all-or-none law** of nerve excitation.

**Step 4 (1 mark):** The amplitude of the spike is set by the geometry of the limit cycle (cubic nullcline topology), not by the stimulus strength. This is why an action potential is **stereotyped** in real neurons. **(End part b)**

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ⭐ **FHN equations:** $\dot{v} = v - v^{3}/3 - w + I$, $\dot{w} = \varepsilon(v + a - bw)$.
- ⭐ **$v$-nullcline is cubic** (the slow manifold); **$w$-nullcline is a straight line**.
- ⭐ **Fixed points** = intersections of the two nullclines; up to **three** real intersections.
- ⭐ **Jacobian** is a 2×2 matrix; **trace** = $1 - v^{\star 2} - \varepsilon b$; **determinant** = $\varepsilon[b(v^{\star 2}-1)+1]$.
- ⭐ **Stable focus (resting state):** $\Delta > 0$, $\tau < 0$, $D < 0$.
- ⭐ **Saddle (threshold):** $\Delta < 0$ — sits on the middle branch of the cubic.
- ⭐ **Limit cycle** = periodic action potential; existence guaranteed by **Poincaré–Bendixson** in planar systems.
- ⭐ **Time-scale separation** $\varepsilon \ll 1$ produces **relaxation oscillations** with sharp spike upstrokes.
- ⭐ **Threshold current** $I_{th}$ found by **tangency** of nullclines: $I_{th} = \frac{2}{3}\bigl(\frac{1-b}{b}\bigr)^{3/2} - \frac{a}{b}$.
- ⭐ **All-or-none law** = geometric consequence of the **stable manifold of the saddle** acting as a separatrix.
- ⭐ **FHN is qualitative**, not quantitative — do **not** quote numeric spike amplitudes from real neurons using FHN.
- ⭐ **Bifurcation type for FHN limit cycle onset = saddle-loop / homoclinic**, NOT a Hopf (because $\tau < 0$ always for small $\varepsilon$).
- ⭐ **Real-world uses:** cardiac arrhythmia modelling, neuromorphic chips, BZ reaction, cortical wave propagation.
- ⭐ **Always draw** both nullclines, label the three fixed points, indicate the limit cycle, and mark the separatrix.
- ⭐ **Remember the $\varepsilon$ factor** in the second equation — it is the source of the fast-slow time-scale separation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Showing Excitability
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEBT865 - MODELLING OF PHYSIOLOGICAL SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Showing Excitability ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Excitability** is the intrinsic physiological property of certain specialized cells (neurons, cardiac myocytes, skeletal muscle fibers, and smooth muscle cells) to alter their resting transmembrane electrical potential in a rapid, stereotyped, and regenerative manner when subjected to a stimulus of sufficient magnitude (the **threshold stimulus**). In the context of *physiological systems modeling*, "showing excitability" refers to the mathematical and biophysical representation of this property — i.e., constructing a **dynamical model** whose state variables (membrane voltage $V_m$, ionic conductances, gating variables) can be driven from a stable **resting equilibrium** to an **excited trajectory** (the *action potential*), followed by return to equilibrium, while preserving the characteristic *all-or-none*, *propagated*, and *refractory* behaviors observed experimentally. In KTU 2024 syllabus parlance, the topic encompasses: - The **Hodgkin–Huxley (H–H) formalism** (1952 Nobel Prize) as the canonical conductance-based model. - Equivalent **electrical circuit analog** of the excitable membrane (RC cable with voltage-gated ion channels). - The **threshold phenomenon**, **refractoriness**, **accommodation**, and the **strength–duration curve** (rheobase / chronaxie). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition Anchor:** *Excitability is a qualitative property of a dynamical system whose phase portrait contains a stable fixed point and a separate, large-amplitude "excited" orbit separated from the fixed point by a threshold separatrix.* This definition bridges electrophysiology and nonlinear dynamics. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a perfectly balanced **billiard ball resting in a shallow dimple** on the edge of a steep cliff. - The **dimple** is the *resting membrane potential* (≈ **−70 mV** for neurons, ≈ **−90 mV** for ventricular myocytes). - A **tiny tap** on the ball (sub-threshold stimulus) merely wobbles it inside the dimple — it returns to rest. No signal propagates. - A **firm push over the rim** (supra-threshold stimulus) sends the ball rolling down the cliff, gaining enormous kinetic energy (the **action potential upstroke** ≈ +30 to +40 mV), regardless of whether you pushed it mildly or very firmly — this is the **all-or-none law**. - After reaching the bottom, the ball must be **carried back up** to the dimple. During the climb, **no amount of pushing** can dislodge it — this is the **refractory period**. - The *amount of push required* depends on **how long** you push — a brief but enormous push, or a long gentle one. This trade-off is plotted as the **strength–duration curve**. The **dimple-with-cliff topology** is mathematically a **cubic-shaped nullcline** in the phase plane — the geometric signature of every excitable system from neurons to heart cells to *Belousov–Zhabotinsky* chemical reactions. ### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants | Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value (Squid Axon at 6.3 °C) | Unit | |---|---|---|---| | Resting membrane potential | $V_{rest}$ | **−70** | mV | | Sodium equilibrium potential | $E_{Na}$ | **+55** | mV | | Potassium equilibrium potential | $E_K$ | **−72** | mV | | Leak reversal potential | $E_L$ | **−49** | mV | | Membrane capacitance | $C_m$ | **1.0** | μF/cm² | | Maximal Na⁺ conductance | $\bar{g}_{Na}$ | **120** | mS/cm² | | Maximal K⁺ conductance | $\bar{g}_K$ | **36** | mS/cm² | | Leak conductance | $\bar{g}_L$ | **0.3** | mS/cm² | | Body temperature | T | **310** | K (37 °C) | | Faraday constant | F | **96485** | C/mol | | Gas constant | R | **8.314** | J/(mol·K) | > [!NOTE] > These values are the **canonical Hodgkin–Huxley 1952 squid giant axon parameters** at 6.3 °C. They are **the** standard reference numbers used in every KTU board numerical. Memorize them. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Breakdown of Excitability — The Five-Stage Logic An excitable cell, when viewed as a dynamical system, executes the following sequence during a single excitation: 1. **Stage 1 — Resting Equilibrium.** The membrane sits at $V_{rest}$. The net transmembrane ionic current $I_{ion} = 0$. Inflow of K⁺ through leak channels balances the Na⁺/K⁺ pump's electrogenic contribution. Mathematically: $\sum g_i (V - E_i) = 0$. 2. **Stage 2 — Sub-threshold Response.** A small depolarizing current $I_{stim}$ perturbs $V_m$. Because the membrane capacitance $C_m$ must be charged by $I_{stim} - I_{ion}$, the response is **passive and graded** — an exponential RC transient with time constant $\tau_m = R_m C_m$. No channel gates open significantly; the system remains linearly stable. 3. **Stage 3 — Threshold Crossing & Upstroke.** When $V_m$ reaches the **firing threshold** $V_{th}$ (≈ **−55 mV** in mammalian neurons, ≈ **−40 mV** in squid axon), the **Na⁺ activation gates (m)** open cooperatively and rapidly. Sodium conductance $g_{Na}$ increases ~600-fold within 1 ms. Inward Na⁺ current depolarizes the cell toward $E_{Na}$, producing the **action potential upstroke** (dV/dt ≈ 100–1000 V/s). 4. **Stage 4 — Repolarization.** Two slower processes restore the resting state: - **Na⁺ inactivation (h-gate)** shuts off inward current. - **K⁺ activation (n-gate)** opens, generating outward current that repolarizes the membrane toward $E_K$. The result is the falling phase of the AP. 5. **Stage 5 — Refractoriness & Recovery.** Even after $V_m$ returns past rest, the gates (especially *h* and *n*) are not in their resting configurations. For an **absolute refractory period (ARP)**, no stimulus can elicit a second AP. During the **relative refractory period (RRP)**, only supra-normal stimuli succeed. This gates the **maximum firing frequency** of the cell. ### 2.2 The Equivalent Electrical Circuit (Hodgkin–Huxley Membrane Model) The lipid bilayer is modeled as a **capacitor** $C_m$ in parallel with three **voltage-dependent conductances**: $\bar{g}_{Na}$, $\bar{g}_K$, $\bar{g}_L$, each in series with its **Nernst battery** ($E_{Na}$, $E_K$, $E_L$). The membrane equation is: $$C_m \frac{dV}{dt} = -\bar{g}_{Na} m^3 h (V - E_{Na}) - \bar{g}_K n^4 (V - E_K) - \bar{g}_L (V - E_L) + I_{stim}$$ where $m$, $h$, $n$ are **dimensionless gating variables** (0 → 1) governed by first-order kinetics. ### 2.3 The "Why" and "How" Behind Each Gate - **Why cubed m and quartic n?** Hodgkin & Huxley fitted the macroscopic time-course of conductances by assuming multiple independent, identical two-state gates that must all be open. Statistical independence ⇒ probability of all-open = (open-probability)$^k$, where $k$ was the smallest integer matching the data: $k=3$ for Na⁺ activation, $k=4$ for K⁺ activation, $k=1$ for Na⁺ inactivation. - **How fast do gates move?** Each gate obeys $\frac{dx}{dt} = \alpha_x(V)(1-x) - \beta_x(V) x$, where $\alpha_x$ and $\beta_x$ are voltage-dependent **rate constants** with units of **ms⁻¹**. ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table is **exam-grade gold**. Every cell of it has appeared (in some form) in KTU University Examinations. Use `\mid` instead of `|` for absolute values to keep the markdown intact. | # | Equation / Concept | Mathematical Form | Variables & Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Membrane (cable) equation | $C_m \dfrac{dV}{dt} = -\sum \bar{g}_i \, x^k (V - E_i) + I_{stim}(t)$ | $V$ in mV; $I$ in μA/cm²; $C_m$ in μF/cm² | | 2 | Nernst equation | $E_i = \dfrac{RT}{z_i F} \ln \dfrac{[i]_{out}}{[i]_{in}}$ | $E_i$ in V; $z_i$ = ion valence; $[i]$ in mol/L | | 3 | GHK voltage equation | $V_m = \dfrac{RT}{F} \ln \dfrac{P_K[K^+]_o + P_{Na}[Na^+]_o + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_i}{P_K[K^+]_i + P_{Na}[Na^+]_i + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_o}$ | $P_i$ = permeability in cm/s | | 4 | Gating ODE | $\dfrac{dx}{dt} = \alpha_x(V)(1-x) - \beta_x(V) x$ | $x \in [0,1]$ dimensionless | | 5 | Steady-state activation | $x_{\infty}(V) = \dfrac{\alpha_x(V)}{\alpha_x(V) + \beta_x(V)}$ | dimensionless | | 6 | Time constant | $\tau_x(V) = \dfrac{1}{\alpha_x(V) + \beta_x(V)}$ | ms | | 7 | Rate constant ($\alpha_m$) | $\alpha_m(V) = \dfrac{0.1 (V+40)}{1 - e^{-(V+40)/10}}$ | ms⁻¹ (V in mV relative to rest) | | 8 | Rate constant ($\beta_m$) | $\beta_m(V) = 4 e^{-(V+65)/18}$ | ms⁻¹ | | 9 | Rate constant ($\alpha_h$) | $\alpha_h(V) = 0.07 e^{-(V+65)/20}$ | ms⁻¹ | | 10 | Rate constant ($\beta_h$) | $\beta_h(V) = \dfrac{1}{1 + e^{-(V+35)/10}}$ | ms⁻¹ | | 11 | Rate constant ($\alpha_n$) | $\alpha_n(V) = \dfrac{0.01 (V+55)}{1 - e^{-(V+55)/10}}$ | ms⁻¹ | | 12 | Rate constant ($\beta_n$) | $\beta_n(V) = 0.125 e^{-(V+65)/80}$ | ms⁻¹ | | 13 | Sodium current density | $I_{Na} = \bar{g}_{Na} m^3 h (V - E_{Na})$ | μA/cm² | | 14 | Potassium current density | $I_K = \bar{g}_K n^4 (V - E_K)$ | μA/cm² | | 15 | Cable equation (space-clamp removed) | $C_m \dfrac{\partial V}{\partial t} = \dfrac{1}{r_a} \dfrac{\partial^2 V}{\partial x^2} - I_{ion} + I_{stim}$ | 1-D propagation | | 16 | Conduction velocity | $\theta = \sqrt{\dfrac{d}{2 R_i R_m C_m}}$ | cm/s (for myelinated axon: $\theta \propto d$) | | 17 | Strength–duration (Weiss) | $I_{th} = I_{rh} \left(1 + \dfrac{t_{c}}{t}\right)$ | rheobase $I_{rh}$; chronaxie $t_c$ | | 18 | Chronaxie | $t_c = \tau_m \ln 2$ | ms | | 19 | Resting potential (GHK-V) | $V_{rest} \approx -70$ mV (mammalian) / $-60$ mV (squid) | mV | | 20 | AP amplitude | $V_{peak} - V_{rest} \approx 100$ mV | mV | ### 2.5 Engineering Utility of Excitability Models - **Cardiac pacemakers & defibrillators:** Models predict threshold current, re-entry arrhythmias, and defibrillation shock strength. - **Neural prosthetics & cochlear / retinal implants:** Electrode-stimulated neurons must fire reliably without damage — models optimize pulse waveform. - **Pharmacology / drug screening:** Channel-blocking drugs (e.g., tetrodotoxin, lidocaine) modify $\bar{g}_{Na}$ and rate constants; H–H simulations predict drug effects. - **Closed-loop deep brain stimulation (DBS):** Phase-plane feedback uses the geometric "excitability" criterion to deliver stimuli at the optimal point of the cycle. - **Neuromorphic computing:** Silicon "neurons" (e.g., the *Hindmarsh–Rose* and *FitzHugh–Nagumo* chips) exploit excitability for energy-efficient spike-based computation. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation #1 — Nernst Equation (Single-Ion Equilibrium) **Starting principle.** At electrochemical equilibrium, the **chemical free-energy gradient** of an ion moving across the membrane equals the **electrical work** done against the membrane potential. The Gibbs free energy change per mole of ion is: $$\Delta G = RT \ln \frac{[i]_{in}}{[i]_{out}} + z_i F V_m$$ At equilibrium, $\Delta G = 0$ because no net ionic flux occurs. **Step 1.** Set $\Delta G = 0$: $$RT \ln \frac{[i]_{in}}{[i]_{out}} + z_i F V_m = 0$$ **Step 2.** Isolate $V_m$: $$V_m = -\frac{RT}{z_i F} \ln \frac{[i]_{in}}{[i]_{out}}$$ **Step 3.** Invert the log argument (and absorb the negative sign) to obtain the conventional form: $$V_m = \frac{RT}{z_i F} \ln \frac{[i]_{out}}{[i]_{in}} \equiv E_i$$ **Step 4 (numerical, body temperature T = 310 K).** Convert to log₁₀ and evaluate the prefactor: $$\frac{RT}{F} \ln 10 = \frac{8.314 \cdot 310}{96485} \cdot 2.3026 \approx 0.0615 \text{ V} = 61.5 \text{ mV}$$ For K⁺ with $z = +1$, typical concentrations $[K^+]_o = 4$ mM, $[K^+]_i = 140$ mM: $$E_K = 61.5 \cdot \log_{10}\left(\frac{4}{140}\right) = 61.5 \cdot (-1.544) \approx -95 \text{ mV}$$ (Measured squid value: $E_K \approx -72$ mV — the discrepancy reflects activity coefficients and the small but non-zero Na⁺ permeability at rest.) ### 3.2 Derivation #2 — Gating Kinetics from Two-State Markov Model **Assumption.** Each gate is a binary particle, either "open" or "closed", and transitions between the two states obey **mass-action kinetics**: $$C \xrightleftharpoons[\beta_x]{\alpha_x} O$$ **Step 1.** Let $x$ = probability of being open. Then $(1-x)$ = probability of being closed. **Step 2.** Net rate of increase of open probability: $$\frac{dx}{dt} = \alpha_x (1 - x) - \beta_x x$$ **Step 3.** Rearrange into the canonical "linear relaxation" form: $$\frac{dx}{dt} = \alpha_x - (\alpha_x + \beta_x) x$$ **Step 4.** Identify the **steady-state**: $$\left. x_{\infty} = \frac{\alpha_x}{\alpha_x + \beta_x} \right\vert_{V = \text{const}}$$ **Step 5.** Identify the **time constant**: $$\tau_x = \frac{1}{\alpha_x + \beta_x}$$ **Step 6.** The ODE therefore reads: $$\frac{dx}{dt} = \frac{x_{\infty}(V) - x}{\tau_x(V)}$$ **Physical interpretation.** If $V$ is held fixed, $x$ decays exponentially from its initial value to $x_\infty(V)$ with time constant $\tau_x(V)$. When $V$ changes, $x_\infty$ and $\tau_x$ change instantaneously, and $x$ "chases" the new steady-state. This is the **quasi-steady-state (QSS)** assumption that fails during the AP upstroke itself, where it is replaced by the full H–H ODE. ### 3.3 Derivation #3 — Hodgkin–Huxley Membrane Equation **Step 1.** Write Kirchhoff's Current Law at a membrane patch. The total membrane current density is the sum of ionic currents plus the displacement (capacitive) current: $$I_m = C_m \frac{dV}{dt} + I_{ion}$$ **Step 2.** Each ionic current is modeled as **Ohm's law** across a Nernst battery: $$I_i = g_i (V - E_i) = \bar{g}_i \, x_i^{k_i} (V - E_i)$$ For Na⁺ (3 activation gates + 1 inactivation): $$I_{Na} = \bar{g}_{Na} \, m^3 h \, (V - E_{Na})$$ For K⁺ (4 activation gates): $$I_K = \bar{g}_K \, n^4 (V - E_K)$$ For leak (no gating): $$I_L = \bar{g}_L (V - E_L)$$ **Step 3.** Substitute. The H–H master equation becomes: $$C_m \frac{dV}{dt} = - \bar{g}_{Na} m^3 h (V - E_{Na}) - \bar{g}_K n^4 (V - E_K) - \bar{g}_L (V - E_L) + I_{stim}$$ **Step 4.** Four coupled first-order ODEs (one for $V$, one each for $m, h, n$): $$\frac{dV}{dt} = \frac{1}{C_m}\left[-\bar{g}_{Na} m^3 h (V - E_{Na}) - \bar{g}_K n^4 (V - E_K) - \bar{g}_L (V - E_L) + I_{stim}\right]$$ $$\frac{dm}{dt} = \alpha_m(V)(1-m) - \beta_m(V) m$$ $$\frac{dh}{dt} = \alpha_h(V)(1-h) - \beta_h(V) h$$ $$\frac{dn}{dt} = \alpha_n(V)(1-n) - \beta_n(V) n$$ ### 3.4 Derivation #4 — Strength–Duration (Weiss) Equation **Step 1.** For a **passive** RC membrane driven by a constant current pulse of amplitude $I$ and duration $t$, the voltage trajectory is: $$V(t) = V_{rest} + I R_m \left(1 - e^{-t/\tau_m}\right)$$ where $R_m = 1/(\bar{g}_{Na}m^3h + \bar{g}_K n^4 + \bar{g}_L)$ and $\tau_m = R_m C_m$. **Step 2.** Threshold is reached when $V(t) = V_{th}$: $$\Delta V = V_{th} - V_{rest} = I R_m \left(1 - e^{-t/\tau_m}\right)$$ **Step 3.** Solve for the threshold current $I_{th}$: $$I_{th} = \frac{\Delta V}{R_m (1 - e^{-t/\tau_m})} = \frac{\Delta V}{R_m} \cdot \frac{1}{1 - e^{-t/\tau_m}}$$ **Step 4.** Recognize two important limits: - As $t \to \infty$: $I_{th} \to \dfrac{\Delta V}{R_m} \equiv I_{rh}$ (**rheobase**). - The time at which $I_{th} = 2 I_{rh}$ (by definition) is the **chronaxie**: $$\frac{1}{1 - e^{-t_c/\tau_m}} = 2 \implies e^{-t_c/\tau_m} = \frac{1}{2} \implies t_c = \tau_m \ln 2 \approx 0.693 \tau_m$$ **Step 5.** Substituting $I_{rh}$ and $t_c$: $$I_{th}(t) = I_{rh}\left(1 + \frac{t_c}{t}\right)$$ This is the **Weiss–Lapicque equation** — a hyperbolic curve in the $(t, I_{th})$ plane. ### 3.5 Derivation #5 — FitzHugh–Nagumo Reduction (Phase-Plane Insight) **Step 1.** Observe that in H–H, $m$ is **fast** (responds almost instantly to $V$), while $h$ and $n$ are **slow** but move in opposite directions ($h$ decreases on depolarization, $n$ increases). **Step 2.** Approximate $m \approx m_\infty(V)$ (QSS). Combine $h$ and $n$ into a single recovery variable $w$: $$\frac{dV}{dt} = V - \frac{V^3}{3} - w + I_{stim}$$ $$\frac{dw}{dt} = \epsilon (V + a - b w)$$ **Step 3.** The $V$-nullcline is a cubic: $w = V - V^3/3 + I_{stim}$. The $w$-nullcline is a straight line. Their intersection is the **fixed point**. **Step 4 (Key insight — geometric definition of excitability).** If the $V$-nullcline is cubic-shaped (N-shaped) and the fixed point lies on the **left, stable branch** with a *separatrix* (middle branch) separating small-amplitude decay from large-amplitude excitation, the system is **excitable**. Sub-threshold perturbations decay; supra-threshold produce a full AP loop around the phase portrait. > [!NOTE] > The N-shaped nullcline is the **mathematical fingerprint of excitability**. This is why every KTU question on "showing excitability" eventually reduces to drawing or interpreting phase-plane diagrams with a cubic nullcline. ### 3.6 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Board Style) **Problem.** A passive membrane has $R_m = 10 \text{ kΩ·cm}^2$ and $C_m = 1 \text{ μF/cm}^2$. A constant current of $I_{stim} = 0.5 \text{ μA/cm}^2$ is applied for 5 ms. (a) Compute the membrane time constant. (b) Compute the voltage change at the end of the pulse. (c) If $V_{th} - V_{rest} = 10$ mV, will the membrane reach threshold if the pulse is extended to 30 ms? **Solution.** **(a)** $\tau_m = R_m C_m = 10{,}000 \cdot 1\times 10^{-6} = 0.01 \text{ s} = 10 \text{ ms}$ **(b)** $\Delta V(5\text{ ms}) = I R_m (1 - e^{-t/\tau}) = 0.5 \cdot 10^{-6} \cdot 10^4 \cdot (1 - e^{-5/10})$ $= 5 \text{ mV} \cdot (1 - 0.6065) = 5 \cdot 0.3935 = 1.97 \text{ mV}$ **(c)** As $t \to \infty$, $\Delta V \to I R_m = 5$ mV. **Below threshold of 10 mV.** Even an infinite-duration pulse cannot fire the membrane — the cell is **not excitable at this stimulus amplitude**; rheobase requires $I_{rh} \geq 1.0$ μA/cm². ### 3.7 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Summary of Excitability Models | Model | State Variables | Threshold? | Refractory? | Closed-form AP? | Computational Cost | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | **Integrate-and-Fire (I&F)** | $V$ only | Yes (manual reset) | No | No | **Lowest** | | **Leaky I&F (LIF)** | $V$ | Yes | No | No | Low | | **Hodgkin–Huxley** | $V, m, h, n$ | Yes (emergent) | Yes (natural) | Yes | **High** | | **FitzHugh–Nagumo** | $V, w$ | Yes (geometric) | Approximate | Approximate | Medium | | **Morris–Lecar** | $V, w$ | Yes | Approximate | Yes (analytical) | Medium | | **Hindmarsh–Rose** | $V, y, z$ | Yes | Yes (bursting) | No | Medium-High | | **Cable (1-D)** | $V(x, t)$ + gates | Yes | Yes | Yes (numerical) | **Highest** | ## 4.
