KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Define the term *excitability* as applied to a biological membrane. List the four ionic currents that contribute to the Hodgkin–Huxley membrane model. **Model Answer (3 marks):** *Excitability (1 mark):* Excitability is the property of certain cell membranes (nerve, muscle) to undergo a rapid, all-or-none, regenerative change in transmembrane potential — the **action potential** — in response to a stimulus that exceeds a critical threshold value. *Four currents of the H–H model (2 marks):* 1. Sodium current $I_{Na} = \bar{g}_{Na} m^3 h (V - E_{Na})$ 2. Potassium current $I_K = \bar{g}_K n^4 (V - E_K)$ 3. Leak current $I_L = \bar{g}_L (V - E_L)$ 4. Stimulus / applied current $I_{stim}$ (and the capacitive current $C_m dV/dt$ that completes KCL). --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** What are **rheobase** and **chronaxie**? Write the Weiss–Lapicque equation relating them to the threshold current. **Model Answer (3 marks):** *Rheobase (1 mark):* The minimum current amplitude of **infinite duration** that just suffices to fire an excitable membrane. Denoted $I_{rh}$. *Chronaxie (1 mark):* The **minimum pulse duration** at which a current of amplitude equal to **twice the rheobase** ($2 I_{rh}$) is just sufficient to reach threshold. It is a measure of membrane excitability — chronaxie $= \tau_m \ln 2$. *Weiss–Lapicque equation (1 mark):* $$I_{th}(t) = I_{rh}\left(1 + \frac{t_c}{t}\right)$$ --- ### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice — 14 Marks) #### **Question A** (14 Marks) — Hodgkin–Huxley Membrane Model Derivation **[KTU University Exam – Model Question based on 2024 Scheme pattern]** **(a)** With the help of a neat circuit diagram, derive the **Hodgkin–Huxley membrane equation** for a space-clamped squid giant axon. Clearly state the role of each ionic conductance and its gating variables. (7 marks) **(b)** Using the Nernst equation, calculate the equilibrium potential for K⁺ at 310 K given $[K^+]_{out} = 4$ mM and $[K^+]_{in} = 140$ mM. Comment on why the measured resting potential ($V_{rest} \approx -70$ mV) is more positive than $E_K$. (7 marks) --- **Model Solution:** **(a)** *(Circuit (3 marks) + derivation (3 marks) + gating description (1 mark))* *Circuit.* The membrane is represented as a capacitor $C_m$ (the lipid bilayer) in parallel with three parallel branches: - A Na⁺ branch: variable conductance $\bar{g}_{Na} m^3 h$ in series with battery $E_{Na}$. - A K⁺ branch: variable conductance $\bar{g}_K n^4$ in series with battery $E_K$. - A leak branch: constant conductance $\bar{g}_L$ in series with battery $E_L$. - A stimulus current source $I_{stim}$ injected externally. *Membrane equation derivation.* Apply KCL at the membrane node (current outward positive convention): $$I_{stim} = C_m \frac{dV}{dt} + I_{Na} + I_K + I_L$$ Substitute Ohm's-law forms: $$\boxed{C_m \frac{dV}{dt} = -\bar{g}_{Na} m^3 h (V - E_{Na}) - \bar{g}_K n^4 (V - E_K) - \bar{g}_L (V - E_L) + I_{stim}}$$ *Gating variables.* $m$ (Na⁺ activation, fast, raised to 3rd power), $h$ (Na⁺ inactivation, slow), $n$ (K⁺ activation, slowest, raised to 4th power) — each obeys $\frac{dx}{dt} = \alpha_x(V)(1-x) - \beta_x(V) x$. --- **(b)** *(Nernst formula (2 marks) + numerical substitution (3 marks) + comment (2 marks))* **Step 1.** Nernst equation: $$E_K = \frac{RT}{zF} \ln \frac{[K^+]_{out}}{[K^+]_{in}} = \frac{8.314 \cdot 310}{1 \cdot 96485} \ln\left(\frac{4}{140}\right)$$ **Step 2.** Prefactor: $$\frac{RT}{F} = \frac{8.314 \cdot 310}{96485} = 0.02671 \text{ V} = 26.71 \text{ mV}$$ **Step 3.** Logarithm: $$\ln(4/140) = \ln(0.02857) = -3.555$$ **Step 4.** Multiply: $$E_K = 26.71 \cdot (-3.555) = -94.96 \text{ mV} \approx -95 \text{ mV}$$ **Comment (2 marks).** The measured resting potential ($V_{rest} \approx -70$ mV) is *less negative* than $E_K$ because: (i) At rest, the membrane is **slightly permeable to Na⁺** as well as K⁺, so the actual $V_{rest}$ is a weighted average — given by the **Goldman–Hodgkin–Katz (GHK) voltage equation** — of $E_K$, $E_{Na}$, and $E_{Cl}$. (ii) The electrogenic **Na⁺/K⁺ ATPase pump** (3 Na⁺ out / 2 K⁺ in per cycle) contributes an additional ~ −4 to −10 mV beyond a passive distribution. --- #### **Question B** (14 Marks) — Strength–Duration & Phase-Plane Excitability **[KTU University Exam – Model Question based on 2024 Scheme pattern]** **(a)** Derive the **Weiss–Lapicque strength–duration equation** starting from the passive RC charging of a membrane. Define rheobase and chronaxie. A membrane has $R_m = 5$ kΩ·cm² and $C_m = 1$ μF/cm². Compute the chronaxie. (7 marks) **(b)** Draw the **phase-plane portrait** of the FitzHugh–Nagumo model. Mark the V-nullcline, w-nullcline, fixed point, and separatrix. Explain geometrically why the system is "excitable". (7 marks) --- **Model Solution:** **(a)** *(Derivation (4 marks) + definitions (2 marks) + numerical (1 mark))* **Derivation.** Consider an excitable membrane approximated by a passive RC circuit with time constant $\tau_m = R_m C_m$. Under constant current $I$ applied at $t=0$: $$V(t) = V_{rest} + I R_m \left(1 - e^{-t/\tau_m}\right)$$ Threshold $V_{th}$ is reached at $t = t_{stim}$: $$\Delta V \equiv V_{th} - V_{rest} = I_{th} R_m \left(1 - e^{-t/\tau_m}\right)$$ Solving for $I_{th}$: $$I_{th}(t) = \frac{\Delta V}{R_m} \cdot \frac{1}{1 - e^{-t/\tau_m}}$$ In the limit $t \to \infty$: $I_{th} \to \dfrac{\Delta V}{R_m} \equiv I_{rh}$ (**rheobase**). The chronaxie $t_c$ is defined as the duration at which $I_{th} = 2 I_{rh}$. Then: $$\frac{1}{1 - e^{-t_c/\tau_m}} = 2 \implies e^{-t_c/\tau_m} = \tfrac{1}{2} \implies t_c = \tau_m \ln 2$$ Substituting: $$\boxed{I_{th}(t) = I_{rh}\left(1 + \frac{t_c}{t}\right)}$$ **Numerical:** $\tau_m = R_m C_m = 5{,}000 \cdot 1 \times 10^{-6} = 5$ ms. Chronaxie $t_c = 5 \cdot 0.693 = \mathbf{3.47 \text{ ms}}$. --- **(b)** *(Sketch (3 marks) + labelling (1 mark) + excitability argument (3 marks))* **Sketch description (verbal — draw on answer sheet):** A two-dimensional phase plane with horizontal axis $V$ (membrane potential) and vertical axis $w$ (recovery variable). The **V-nullcline** is a cubic curve $w = V - V^3/3 + I_{stim}$ that opens to the right and has an N-shape. The **w-nullcline** is a straight line $w = (V+a)/b$ crossing the cubic from upper-left to lower-right. **Key features to label:** - **Left intersection** = stable fixed point (resting state). - **Middle branch of cubic** = unstable separatrix; perturbations crossing it produce excitation. - **Right intersection** = stable excited state (depolarized plateau). - **Trajectory:** sub-threshold perturbations spiral into the left fixed point; supra-threshold make a **large clockwise loop** through the right branch, mimicking the AP, before returning. **Geometric definition of excitability (3 marks):** The system is excitable because: 1. The **V-nullcline is N-shaped (cubic)**, providing two stable branches separated by an unstable middle (separatrix) branch. 2. The resting fixed point lies on the **left stable branch** but the perturbation must cross the **separatrix** to produce a large-amplitude excursion. 3. The system possesses **refractoriness**: immediately after a large loop, the trajectory lies on the right stable branch where it cannot be re-excited until $w$ has recovered — geometrically encoding the relative refractory period. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where KTU Students Lose Marks on "Excitability" Questions:** > 1. **Forgetting units in the Nernst equation** — examiners deduct 1 full mark if you write $E = (RT/zF)\ln(\dots)$ without the prefactor in **mV** at T = 310 K. Always show the prefactor 61.5 log₁₀ or 26.7 ln form. > 2. **Confusing rheobase with threshold current** — rheobase is the **minimum current of infinite duration**, NOT the threshold current for a given pulse. They differ by the factor $(1 + t_c/t)$. > 3. **Mis-stating the H–H exponents** — sodium activation is $m^3$ (NOT $m^2$); potassium activation is $n^4$ (NOT $n^3$). This is a **favourite one-mark trap**. > 4. **Skipping the gating ODE form** — many students write the H–H membrane equation but forget to also write $\frac{dm}{dt}, \frac{dh}{dt}, \frac{dn}{dt}$ and the rate-constant formulas. A complete answer must include all **four coupled ODEs**. > 5. **Phase-plane missing arrows** — when drawing the FHN portrait, you **must** show the trajectory direction (clockwise around the cubic). A static picture with no arrows = −2 marks. > 6. **Forgetting the sign convention** — KTU's preferred convention is $I_{outward} = +I_{ion}$. Getting the sign wrong in the membrane equation loses **2 marks** outright. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - ✅ **Excitability** = stable rest + threshold + all-or-none AP + refractoriness. - ✅ H–H membrane equation: $C_m \dot{V} = -\bar{g}_{Na} m^3 h (V-E_{Na}) - \bar{g}_K n^4 (V-E_K) - \bar{g}_L (V-E_L) + I_{stim}$. - ✅ **Gating ODE**: $\dot{x} = \alpha_x(V)(1-x) - \beta_x(V) x$, $x_\infty = \alpha/(\alpha+\beta)$, $\tau_x = 1/(\alpha+\beta)$. - ✅ **Six α/β rate constants** for $m, h, n$ — each is a voltage-dependent function (see Formula Sheet rows 7–12). - ✅ **Nernst equation** at 37 °C: prefactor = **61.5 mV** (log₁₀) or **26.7 mV** (ln). - ✅ **GHK voltage equation** explains why $V_{rest}$ lies between $E_K$ and $E_{Na}$. - ✅ **Rheobase** = min current of infinite duration; **chronaxie** $t_c = \tau_m \ln 2 \approx 0.693 \tau_m$. - ✅ **Weiss equation**: $I_{th}(t) = I_{rh}(1 + t_c / t)$ — hyperbolic strength–duration curve. - ✅ **FHN model** gives geometric insight: cubic V-nullcline + linear w-nullcline ⇒ excitable. - ✅ **Absolute refractory period** = no AP possible (Na⁺ inactivated, K⁺ still open). - ✅ **Relative refractory period** = supra-normal stimulus needed (some gates recovered). - ✅ Conduction velocity in unmyelinated axon: $\theta = \sqrt{d/(2R_iR_mC_m)}$. - ✅ Conduction velocity in myelinated axon: $\theta \propto d$ (saltatory conduction). - ✅ Standard membrane time constant for neurons: $\tau_m \approx 1$–$10$ ms. - ✅ Standard squid axon AP duration: ~1–2 ms; cardiac AP: ~200–300 ms. > [!NOTE] > **Last-mile mnemonic:** **"MR. HEN"** — **M**embrane capacitance, **R**ate constants (α,β), **H**odgkin–Huxley **E**quations, **N**ernst. The five pillars of any excitability question on the KTU board exam.

topics.title: Bistability and Oscillations: Simulation Studies of Simplified Models (e.g., Izhikevich Neuron Models)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT865 - MODELLING OF PHYSIOLOGICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Bistability and Oscillations: Simulation Studies of Simplified Models (e.g., Izhikevich Neuron Models)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Bistability — The Two-Equilibrium Phenomenon

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Term):**
> *Bistability* is a property of a nonlinear dynamical system in which exactly **two distinct stable equilibrium states** coexist for the same set of parameters, separated by an unstable equilibrium (a *saddle* or *threshold*). In a neuronal context, the membrane potential *v* can settle in either a hyperpolarized *resting* state or a depolarized *up-state*, and the system retains memory of the most recent perturbation.

**Intuitive Analogy — The Double-Well Landscape:**
Imagine a ball on a hilly surface shaped like the letter "W" (two valleys separated by a small hill).

- The ball can rest in the **left valley** (low *v*) or the **right valley** (high *v*) — these are the two stable equilibria.
- The **small hill** between them is the unstable equilibrium (saddle).
- A **gentle push** on the ball returns it to its valley (local stability).
- A **firm push** over the hill flips it to the other valley — a *state transition* analogous to a neuron firing.

This geometric picture is formalized by **bifurcation theory**: as a parameter (e.g., input current *I*) crosses a critical value, the number/character of equilibria changes.

### 1.2 Oscillations — Limit-Cycle Behaviour

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:**
> *Oscillations* in a physiological system correspond to a **stable limit cycle** in phase space — a closed, isolated trajectory to which neighbouring trajectories converge in the limit. They underlie rhythms such as respiration, cardiac pacing, and central pattern generators for locomotion.

**Intuitive Analogy — The Pendulum Clock:**
A pendulum, once nudged, swings back and forth at a fixed frequency determined by its length (analogous to model parameters), independent of the size of the initial push. In neurons, this manifests as **repetitive spiking** at a characteristic frequency.

### 1.3 Why Simplified Models?

The **Hodgkin–Huxley (HH)** model uses four coupled ODEs and ≈ **20 parameters** — biologically rich but computationally expensive for network simulations. Simplified models retain the *qualitative* dynamics (spiking, bursting, bistability) at a fraction of the cost.

| Model | Dimension | Key Trait |
|---|---|---|
| Hodgkin–Huxley (1952) | 4D | Full ion-channel biophysics |
| FitzHugh–Nagumo (1961) | 2D | Cubic v-nullcline, qualitative |
| Morris–Lecar (1981) | 2D | Conductance-based, excitable |
| **Izhikevich (2003)** | **2D + reset** | **All known cortical firing patterns with only 4 parameters** |

### 1.4 The Izhikevich Neuron Model

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Izhikevich (2003):** A *2-D ordinary differential equation* coupled with a *hard reset* rule, designed so that — by varying four parameters (*a, b, c, d*) — it can reproduce virtually every firing pattern observed in mammalian cortex.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy for the Model:**
> Think of the membrane potential *v* as water level in a leaky bucket, and the recovery variable *u* as a "brake" that builds up as water flows in:
> 1. Bucket fills (current *I* flows in, sodium activation) → *v* rises.
> 2. The "brake" *u* pulls *v* back down (potassium-like repolarisation).
> 3. When the bucket overflows (*v* ≥ 30 mV) — a **spike** is emitted — and the bucket is instantaneously emptied to a low level *c*, and the brake is incremented by *d*.
> 4. The cycle repeats, generating rhythmic firing or settling into a stable rest, depending on *I* and the parameters.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Mathematical Statement of the Izhikevich Model

The continuous dynamics are governed by two coupled ODEs:

$$\frac{dv}{dt} = 0.04\,v^{2} + 5\,v + 140 - u + I$$

$$\frac{du}{dt} = a\,(b\,v - u)$$

A piecewise *reset* (after-spike) rule closes the loop:

$$\text{if } v \geq 30 \text{ mV, then } v \leftarrow c,\quad u \leftarrow u + d$$

### 2.2 Roles of Parameters (Logic Step-by-Step)

- **State variable *v* (mV):** Membrane potential of the neuron. The quadratic term $0.04v^2$ supplies the *fast positive feedback* (mimicking $\text{Na}^{+}$ activation) that produces the upstroke.
- **State variable *u* (a.u.):** Recovery (refractory) current. Provides the *slow negative feedback* (mimicking $\text{K}^{+}$ activation) that repolarises the cell.
- **Parameter *a*:** Time-scale of the recovery variable. Small *a* → slow *u* → adaptation; large *a* → fast *u* → no adaptation.
- **Parameter *b*:** Sensitivity of *u* to subthreshold fluctuations in *v*. Sets the slope of the *u*-nullcline $u = bv$.
- **Parameter *c* (mV):** Post-spike reset value of *v*. Models the after-hyperpolarisation.
- **Parameter *d* (a.u.):** Post-spike jump in *u*. Larger *d* → stronger spike-frequency adaptation.
- **Input *I* (a.u.):** Injected DC current; the bifurcation parameter.

### 2.3 The 'Why' Behind the Design

- The constants **0.04**, **5**, and **140** are *not arbitrary*; they were tuned by Izhikevich so that $v = -65$ mV and $u = 0$ sit near the resting state and $v = 30$ mV sits at the spike peak for typical cortical neurons.
- The **2-D structure** is the *minimal* dimension that can produce a *saddle-node* or *Hopf bifurcation* — i.e., bistability and oscillations.

### 2.4 Comparison: FitzHugh–Nagumo (Qualitative Cousin)

The **FitzHugh–Nagumo (FHN)** model shares the same 2-D skeleton:

$$\frac{dv}{dt} = v - \frac{v^{3}}{3} - w + I$$

$$\frac{dw}{dt} = \epsilon\,(v + \alpha - \beta\,w)$$

with small $0 < \epsilon \ll 1$ (separation of timescales). Its *cubic* v-nullcline creates the S-shaped phase portrait necessary for **bistability** (two intersections with the w-nullcline).

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Symbol / Equation | Meaning | Typical Value / Range |
|---|---|---|
| $dv/dt = 0.04v^{2} + 5v + 140 - u + I$ | Membrane potential ODE | — |
| $du/dt = a(bv - u)$ | Recovery ODE | — |
| $v \leftarrow c$ at $v \geq 30$ mV | Spike reset | $c \in [-65, -50]$ mV |
| $u \leftarrow u + d$ | Recovery jump | $d \in [0, 8]$ |
| $a$ | Recovery time-scale | $[0.01, 0.1]$ |
| $b$ | *u*–*v* coupling | $[0.2, 0.3]$ |
| $I$ | Input current | $[0, 20]$ a.u. |
| $u_{v\text{-null}}: u = 0.04v^{2} + 5v + 140 + I$ | v-nullcline (parabola) | vertex at $v = -62.5$ |
| $u_{u\text{-null}}: u = bv$ | u-nullcline (line) | slope $b$ |
| $0.04v^{*2} + (5-b)v^{*} + (140+I) = 0$ | Equilibrium equation | quadratic in $v^{*}$ |
| $D = (5-b)^{2} - 0.16(140+I)$ | Discriminant | $D > 0 \Rightarrow$ bistable |
| $J = \begin{pmatrix} 0.08v + 5 & -1 \\ ab & -a \end{pmatrix}$ | Jacobian | $2 \times 2$ |
| $\tau = \text{tr}(J) = 0.08v + 5 - a$ | Trace (stability) | $\tau < 0 \Rightarrow$ damped |
| $\Delta = \det(J) = a(b - 0.08v - 5)$ | Determinant | $\Delta > 0 \Rightarrow$ non-saddle |
| $\tau^{2} - 4\Delta > 0$ | Real eigenvalues (node) | $< 0 \Rightarrow$ focus |
| $0 < \epsilon \ll 1$ (FHN) | Timescale separation | enables relaxation oscillation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner's Tip:**
> Always quote the **reset threshold of 30 mV** (not 0 mV) and the **quadratic coefficients 0.04, 5, 140** verbatim — these are the most frequently mis-remembered values in the Izhikevich model.

### 2.6 Real-World Utility

- **Large-scale cortical simulations** (e.g., the *Blue Brain* and *Allen Brain Atlas* projects) use the Izhikevich model because it reproduces **20+ firing patterns** with O(1) cost per neuron, enabling simulations of **millions of neurons**.
- **Neuroprosthetic design:** Bistable Izhikevich units can serve as *flip-flop memory elements* in neural-state decoders.
- **Deep Brain Stimulation (DBS) therapy** uses external *I* to switch subthalamic neurons between rest and tonic firing — a direct exploitation of **bistability** in basal-ganglia models.
- **Closed-loop anaesthesia monitoring** relies on detecting transitions between oscillatory EEG regimes, modelled via reduced Hopf bifurcations.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Equilibrium Analysis of the Izhikevich Continuous Subsystem

We solve for fixed points $(v^{*}, u^{*})$ by setting both time derivatives to zero.

**Step 1 — Set the right-hand sides to zero:**

$$\frac{dv}{dt} = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; 0.04\,(v^{*})^{2} + 5\,v^{*} + 140 - u^{*} + I = 0 \quad (1)$$

$$\frac{du}{dt} = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; a\,(b\,v^{*} - u^{*}) = 0 \quad (2)$$

**Step 2 — Solve Equation (2) for $u^{*}$:**

Since $a \neq 0$ (otherwise the recovery variable is decoupled), we have:

$$u^{*} = b\,v^{*} \quad (3)$$

**Step 3 — Substitute Equation (3) into Equation (1):**

$$0.04\,(v^{*})^{2} + 5\,v^{*} + 140 - b\,v^{*} + I = 0$$

Collecting like terms in $v^{*}$:

$$0.04\,(v^{*})^{2} + (5 - b)\,v^{*} + (140 + I) = 0 \quad (4)$$

This is a **quadratic equation in $v^{*}$** with real coefficients.

**Step 4 — Apply the quadratic formula:**

$$v^{*} = \frac{-(5-b) \;\pm\; \sqrt{(5-b)^{2} - 4(0.04)(140+I)}}{2(0.04)} = \frac{-(5-b) \;\pm\; \sqrt{D}}{0.08} \quad (5)$$

where the **discriminant** is:

$$D = (5-b)^{2} - 0.16\,(140+I) \quad (6)$$

**Step 5 — Interpret the discriminant:**

- $D > 0$ → **two real equilibria** (bistability / saddle–node configuration)
- $D = 0$ → **one repeated equilibrium** (saddle-node bifurcation)
- $D < 0$ → **no real equilibria** from the continuous system; the neuron oscillates via the reset rule (tonic spiking)

Expanding (6) for clarity:

$$D = 25 - 10b + b^{2} - 22.4 - 0.16\,I = 2.6 - 10b + b^{2} - 0.16\,I$$

**Step 6 — Numerical example (bistable regime):**

Take $a = 0.1,\; b = 0.2,\; I = 0$:

$$0.04\,(v^{*})^{2} + 4.8\,v^{*} + 140 = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; (v^{*})^{2} + 120\,v^{*} + 3500 = 0$$

$$v^{*} = \frac{-120 \;\pm\; \sqrt{14400 - 14000}}{2} = \frac{-120 \;\pm\; 20}{2}$$

$$\boxed{v^{*}_{1} = -70 \text{ mV},\quad v^{*}_{2} = -50 \text{ mV}}$$

From (3): $u^{*}_{1} = -14$, $u^{*}_{2} = -10$.

---

### 3.2 Linear Stability via the Jacobian

To classify the equilibria we linearise. Define $f(v,u) = 0.04v^{2} + 5v + 140 - u + I$ and $g(v,u) = a(bv - u)$.

**Step 7 — Compute partial derivatives:**

$$\frac{\partial f}{\partial v} = 0.08\,v + 5, \qquad \frac{\partial f}{\partial u} = -1$$

$$\frac{\partial g}{\partial v} = a\,b, \qquad \frac{\partial g}{\partial u} = -a$$

**Step 8 — Assemble the Jacobian matrix:**

$$J(v^{*}) = \begin{pmatrix} 0.08\,v^{*} + 5 & -1 \\ a\,b & -a \end{pmatrix} \quad (7)$$

**Step 9 — Trace and determinant:**

$$\tau \;\equiv\; \text{tr}(J) = 0.08\,v^{*} + 5 - a \quad (8)$$

$$\Delta \;\equiv\; \det(J) = -a\,(0.08\,v^{*} + 5) - (-1)(ab) = a\,(b - 0.08\,v^{*} - 5) \quad (9)$$

**Step 10 — Apply Routh–Hurwitz stability conditions for a 2-D linear system:**

$$\Delta > 0 \quad \text{and} \quad \tau < 0 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \text{asymptotically stable (node or focus)} \quad (10)$$

**Step 11 — Apply to the bistable numerical example:**

At $v^{*}_{1} = -70$ (with $a = 0.1$, $b = 0.2$):

$$\tau_{1} = 0.08(-70) + 5 - 0.1 = -5.6 + 5 - 0.1 = -0.7 < 0$$

$$\Delta_{1} = 0.1\,(0.2 - 0.08(-70) - 5) = 0.1\,(0.2 + 5.6 - 5) = 0.1 \times 0.8 = 0.08 > 0$$

Stability: **Stable node** ✓

At $v^{*}_{2} = -50$:

$$\tau_{2} = 0.08(-50) + 5 - 0.1 = -4 + 5 - 0.1 = 0.9 > 0$$

$$\Delta_{2} = 0.1\,(0.2 - 0.08(-50) - 5) = 0.1\,(0.2 + 4 - 5) = -0.08 < 0$$

Stability: **Saddle (unstable)** — this acts as the **threshold** between the two valleys.

---

### 3.3 Birth of a Limit Cycle — The Hopf Picture

When *I* is increased so that $D < 0$, no fixed points exist; the trajectory is forced around the $v = 30$ mV reset manifold, producing a *piecewise-smooth limit cycle* (repetitive spiking). As *I* is further increased, the **spike frequency grows monotonically** — the *f–I* curve. This is a **homoclinic-like** transition mediated by the reset, not a classical Hopf bifurcation, but is often called "**spiking via threshold**".

**Step 12 — Discriminant as a function of I (bistability onset):**

Setting $D = 0$ gives the critical current at which equilibria appear:

$$I_{\text{crit}} = \frac{(5-b)^{2} - 22.4}{0.16} = 6.25(5-b)^{2} - 140 \quad (11)$$

For $b = 0.2$:

$$I_{\text{crit}} = 6.25 \times 23.04 - 140 = 144 - 140 = 4 \text{ a.u.}$$

So **for $I < 4$ a.u.** (with $a=0.1, b=0.2$) the system is **bistable**; **for $I > 4$ a.u.** it spikes repetitively.

### 3.4 Summary Matrix — Three Dynamical Regimes

| Regime | Discriminant | Equilibria | Behaviour | Clinical / Engineering Analogue |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Bistable** | $D > 0$ | 1 stable node + 1 saddle | Two stable rest states with threshold-mediated switching | Working memory; ON/OFF decision circuits |
| **Quiescent** | $D > 0$, low $I$ | 1 stable node | Stable resting potential | Sub-threshold neuron |
| **Tonic spiking** | $D < 0$ | None (continuous) | Periodic spiking via reset | Respiration pacemaker, CPG |
| **Saddle-node (bifurcation)** | $D = 0$ | Coalescing pair | Onset of spiking | Action-potential threshold |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Flowchart — Izhikevich Simulation Algorithm

The following block diagram captures the **step-by-step computational procedure** used to simulate an Izhikevich neuron (e.g., in MATLAB / Python / NEURON). It also serves as a block-level architecture of the model's information flow.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start1[Start Simulation t = 0] --> init1[Initialize State v = minus 65 mV, u = 0]
    init1 --> setp1[Set Parameters a, b, c, d, I and dt]
    setp1 --> loopA{For each time step}
    loopA --> compA[Compute dv/dt equal to 0.04 v squared plus 5v plus 140 minus u plus I]
    compA --> compB[Compute du/dt equal to a times b v minus u]
    compB --> integA[Integrate Euler step v new = v + dt times dv/dt]
    integA --> integB[Integrate Euler step u new = u + dt times du/dt]
    integB --> testA{Is v new greater than or equal to 30 mV}
    testA -- Yes --> resetA[Record spike at time t]
    resetA --> resetB[Reset v to c]
    resetB --> resetC[Reset u to u plus d]
    resetC --> recA[Store v and u to trace arrays]
    testA -- No --> recA
    recA --> advA[Advance t = t + dt]
    advA --> loopA
    loopA -- End of time --> plotA[Plot Voltage v versus t]
    plotA --> plotB[Plot Phase Plane u versus v]
    plotB --> plotC[Plot Firing Rate versus I F I curve]
    plotC --> endA[End]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Architecture — Izhikevich Neuron as a Control System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    in1[Input Current I] --> sum1[Summing Junction]
    sum1 --> dynA[Fast Subsystem dv/dt block]
    dynA --> out1[Membrane Potential v]
    out1 --> fb1[Feedback to Slow Subsystem]
    fb1 --> dynB[Slow Subsystem du/dt block]
    dynB --> fb2[Recovery u to Summing Junction]
    fb2 --> sum1
    out1 --> thr1[Threshold Detector v greater than or equal to 30]
    thr1 --> rst1[Reset Logic v equals c and u equals u plus d]
    rst1 --> out2[Spike Output]
    out1 --> out3[Continuous Voltage Output]
```

**How to read this diagram:**

- The **fast subsystem** $dv/dt$ and the **slow subsystem** $du/dt$ form a **2-D feedback loop** in continuous time.
- The **threshold detector** (a Schmitt-trigger-like block) fires when $v$ crosses 30 mV, at which point the **reset logic** snaps *v* to *c* and increments *u* by *d* — this is the **piecewise-reset** that makes the Izhikevich model a *hybrid* dynamical system.
- The two outputs (continuous *v* and discrete spike times) feed downstream readout modules.

### 4.3 Parameter → Firing-Pattern Selection Block

The following diagram maps the **role of each parameter** to the resulting firing behaviour — a high-yield "cheat-block" for KTU viva and derivations.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    a1[Parameter a small approximately 0.02] --> rsp[Regular Spiking RS with adaptation]
    a2[Parameter a large approximately 0.1] --> fsp[Fast Spiking FS no adaptation]
    b1[Parameter b approximately 0.2] --> rsp
    b2[Parameter b approximately 0.26] --> bst[Bistable Two Rest States]
    c1[Parameter c around minus 65 mV] --> rsp
    c2[Parameter c around minus 50 mV] --> cha[Chattering CH]
    d1[Parameter d large approximately 8] --> rsp
    d2[Parameter d small approximately 0] --> bst
    rsp --> osc1[Oscillatory Spiking Output]
    bst --> osc2[Bistable Switching Output]
    cha --> osc3[Bursting Output]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation for the examiner:**
> Setting $(a,b,c,d) = (0.1, 0.26, -60, 0)$ produces **bistability** — both the *resting* state and a *depolarised* state are stable, and the cell can be flipped between them by brief current pulses. This is the classic Izhikevich demonstration of **memory storage in a single neuron's phase plane**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**

Define **bistability** in a neuronal dynamical system. With the help of a *double-well potential* analogy, explain what is meant by the **unstable equilibrium (saddle)** that separates the two stable states.

**Model Answer:**

> **Bistability** is the property of a nonlinear dynamical system in which two distinct stable equilibrium states coexist under the same parameter values, separated by an unstable equilibrium.
>
> **Double-well analogy:** The energy landscape of a bistable system resembles a "W"-shaped curve with two valleys. A ball placed in the left valley (state A) or right valley (state B) will remain there under small perturbations — these are the **stable equilibria**. The hilltop between them is the **unstable equilibrium (saddle)**; it is the *threshold* the system must overcome to flip from one stable state to the other.
>
> In neurons, the two stable states typically correspond to (i) the *resting membrane potential* (≈ −70 mV) and (ii) a *depolarised up-state* (≈ −50 mV), with the saddle acting as the **action-potential threshold**.

**Mark split:** 1 mark for definition + 2 marks for analogy and neuronal interpretation. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

List **any four** distinct firing patterns that the **Izhikevich neuron model** can reproduce, and state the parameter set $(a, b, c, d)$ that yields the *Regular Spiking (RS)* pattern.

**Model Answer:**

Four firing patterns the Izhikevich model reproduces:

1. **Regular Spiking (RS)** — cortical pyramidal neurons
2. **Fast Spiking (FS)** — inhibitory interneurons
3. **Chattering (CH)** — oscillatory bursting
4. **Low-Threshold Spiking (LTS)** — thalamic relay neurons

(Other valid patterns: *intrinsic bursting (IB)*, *resonator*, *integrator*, *thalamic bursting*, *rebound spike*, *rebound burst*.)

Parameter set for **RS**: $\;(a, b, c, d) = (0.02,\; 0.2,\; -65\text{ mV},\; 8)$

**Mark split:** ½ mark × 4 = 2 marks for patterns + 1 mark for parameter set. **(3 marks)**

---

### Part B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Choice-Based)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam – Sample Model Paper 2024]**
>
> **(a)** For the Izhikevich neuron model, derive the **equilibrium points** of the continuous subsystem and obtain the **discriminant condition** that distinguishes the bistable, saddle-node, and tonic-spiking regimes. **[7 marks]**
>
> **(b)** Compute the **Jacobian** at an equilibrium point. Using the **Routh–Hurwitz criteria**, determine the **stability conditions** for the equilibrium, and show that the system exhibits a **saddle-node bifurcation** as the input current *I* crosses a critical value. **[7 marks]**

---

**Model Solution to A(a):**

**Step 1.** Set $\dfrac{dv}{dt} = 0$ and $\dfrac{du}{dt} = 0$:

$$0.04\,(v^{*})^{2} + 5\,v^{*} + 140 - u^{*} + I = 0 \quad (\text{i})$$

$$a\,(b\,v^{*} - u^{*}) = 0 \quad (\text{ii})$$

**Step 2.** From (ii), since $a \neq 0$, we have $u^{*} = b\,v^{*}$.

**Step 3.** Substitute into (i):

$$0.04\,(v^{*})^{2} + (5-b)\,v^{*} + (140 + I) = 0$$

**Step 4.** Apply the quadratic formula:

$$v^{*} = \frac{-(5-b) \pm \sqrt{D}}{0.08}, \quad D = (5-b)^{2} - 0.16(140+I)$$

**Step 5. Discriminant condition:**

- $D > 0$ → two real equilibria → **bistable** regime (one stable node + one saddle).
- $D = 0$ → one repeated root → **saddle-node bifurcation**.
- $D < 0$ → no real equilibria → **tonic spiking** via the reset rule.

**Step 6.** The critical input current for the saddle-node is obtained by setting $D = 0$:

$$I_{\text{crit}} = \frac{(5-b)^{2} - 22.4}{0.16}$$

(Showing the full calculation earns the remaining marks.) **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to A(b):**

**Step 1.** The Jacobian is:

$$J = \begin{pmatrix} 0.08\,v + 5 & -1 \\ a\,b & -a \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 2.** Trace and determinant:

$$\tau = 0.08\,v + 5 - a, \qquad \Delta = a(b - 0.08\,v - 5)$$

**Step 3.** Routh–Hurwitz for a 2-D linear system: an equilibrium is **asymptotically stable** iff

$$\Delta > 0 \quad \text{and} \quad \tau < 0$$

**Step 4.** Apply to the equilibrium $v^{*}_{1} = -70$ mV with $a = 0.1, b = 0.2$:

- $\tau = 0.08(-70) + 5 - 0.1 = -0.7 < 0$ ✓
- $\Delta = 0.1(0.2 + 5.6 - 5) = 0.08 > 0$ ✓ → **stable node**.

**Step 5.** At $v^{*}_{2} = -50$ mV: $\Delta = -0.08 < 0$ → **saddle (unstable)**, the threshold between the two valleys.

**Step 6. Bifurcation:** As *I* is increased, the two equilibria ($v^{*}_{1}$ stable and $v^{*}_{2}$ saddle) move closer; at $I = I_{\text{crit}}$ they coalesce ($\Delta \to 0$, $\tau \to 0$) — a **saddle-node bifurcation**. For $I > I_{\text{crit}}$ the system has no continuous equilibrium and exhibits **tonic spiking** via the reset rule. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam – Sample Model Paper 2024]**
>
> **(a)** Explain, with a **neat labelled sketch**, the **two state variables** and **four parameters** of the Izhikevich neuron model. Describe how the model is simulated numerically. **[7 marks]**
>
> **(b)** A *Regular Spiking* (RS) cortical pyramidal neuron is simulated with parameters $(a, b, c, d) = (0.02, 0.2, -65, 8)$ and a step current $I = 10$ a.u. starting from $v(0) = -65$ mV, $u(0) = 0$. Describe the **expected voltage trace**, the **phase-plane trajectory**, and the **firing pattern** produced. Justify your answer by computing the discriminant for this case. **[7 marks]**

---

**Model Solution to B(a):**

**Two state variables:**

- $v(t)$ — membrane potential (mV), fast variable.
- $u(t)$ — recovery current (a.u.), slow variable.

**Four parameters:**

| Parameter | Role | RS value |
|---|---|---|
| *a* | Time-scale of *u* (small → slow, gives adaptation) | 0.02 |
| *b* | Coupling of *u* to subthreshold *v* (slope of u-nullcline) | 0.2 |
| *c* | Post-spike reset of *v* (AHP depth) | −65 mV |
| *d* | Post-spike jump in *u* (adaptation strength) | 8 |

**Numerical simulation procedure** (block diagram from SECTION 4):

1. Initialise $v = -65$ mV, $u = 0$, $t = 0$.
2. At each time step, evaluate the RHS of the two ODEs.
3. Integrate using **forward Euler** (or RK4) with step $\Delta t \approx 0.5$ ms.
4. Test $v \geq 30$ mV; if true, record a spike, set $v \leftarrow c$, $u \leftarrow u + d$.
5. Store $(t, v, u)$ and repeat.

**Sketch:** Refer to the Mermaid block diagram in **SECTION 4.1 / 4.2**. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to B(b):**

**Step 1 — Compute the discriminant:**

$$D = (5 - 0.2)^{2} - 0.16\,(140 + 10) = 23.04 - 24 = -0.96 < 0$$

**Step 2 — Interpretation:** Since $D < 0$, the continuous subsystem has **no real equilibrium**. The trajectory is therefore pushed by the **v-nullcline** (an upward parabola) until $v$ reaches the threshold 30 mV, at which point the **reset rule** fires, snapping the system to $(v, u) = (c, u + d) = (-65, u + 8)$. The cycle repeats indefinitely.

**Step 3 — Expected voltage trace:** A train of **action-potential-like spikes** with:
- Rapid upstroke (driven by the $0.04v^2 + 5v$ fast term).
- Sharp repolarisation (driven by the reset).
- Interspike intervals that **lengthen progressively** because *u* accumulates (adaptation).
- Spike frequency ≈ 10–20 Hz for $I = 10$ a.u.

**Step 4 — Phase-plane trajectory:** A closed loop in the $(v, u)$-plane:
- Fast horizontal sweep rightward along the reset branch to $v = 30$ mV (spike upstroke).
- Near-vertical drop at the reset to $(c, u + d) = (-65, u+8)$.
- Slow drift leftward and downward as *u* decays, then upward along the v-nullcline to the threshold — closing the loop.

**Step 5 — Firing pattern:** **Regular Spiking (RS) with strong spike-frequency adaptation** — the hallmark of cortical pyramidal neurons. **(7 marks)**

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost marks in this topic:**
> 1. **Forgetting the reset rule** — The Izhikevich model is a *hybrid* (ODE + piecewise reset) system. Writing only the two ODEs and omitting "if $v \geq 30$ mV, $v \leftarrow c$, $u \leftarrow u + d$" will lose **at least 2 marks**.
> 2. **Quoting the threshold as 0 mV or 20 mV** — it is **30 mV**; this is a *high-yield* detail checked by examiners.
> 3. **Mixing up *a* and *b*** — *a* controls the *time-scale* of *u*; *b* controls the *slope* of the *u*-nullcline. Confusing them invalidates the stability analysis.
> 4. **Sign error in the Jacobian determinant** — the correct form is $\Delta = a(b - 0.08v - 5)$, not $-a(0.08v+5-b)$. This is the single most common algebraic slip in KTU scripts.
> 5. **Confusing bistability with oscillation** — Bistability requires *two stable fixed points separated by a saddle*; oscillation requires a *limit cycle with no fixed points*. They are mutually exclusive regimes in this model.
> 6. **Missing the "$-16$ to $-65$ mV" physiology anchor** — Examiners expect you to map model states to *biophysical* voltages (rest ≈ −65 mV, peak ≈ +30 mV).
> 7. **No sketch/diagram** — A 14-mark question on Izhikevich *without* a phase-plane sketch or simulation block diagram is considered incomplete; **lose 1–2 marks**.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Bistability** = two stable equilibria + one unstable saddle, separable by threshold-like perturbations; the geometric picture is a *double-well potential*.
- **Oscillation** = a stable *limit cycle* in phase space with no nearby fixed points.
- The **Izhikevich model** is a 2-D ODE plus a hard reset at $v = 30$ mV, using parameters $(a, b, c, d)$ to switch between firing regimes.
- **Continuous ODEs:** $dv/dt = 0.04v^{2} + 5v + 140 - u + I$ ; $\,du/dt = a(bv - u)$.
- **Reset:** $v \leftarrow c$, $u \leftarrow u + d$ whenever $v \geq 30$ mV.
- **Equilibria** satisfy the quadratic $0.04v^{*2} + (5-b)v^{*} + (140+I) = 0$, with **discriminant** $D = (5-b)^{2} - 0.16(140+I)$.
- **Bistability criterion:** $D > 0$. **Saddle-node bifurcation:** $D = 0$ at $I = I_{\text{crit}} = [(5-b)^{2} - 22.4] / 0.16$. **Tonic spiking:** $D < 0$.
- **Jacobian:** $J = \begin{pmatrix} 0.08v+5 & -1 \\ ab & -a \end{pmatrix}$; **stability** iff $\Delta > 0$ and $\tau < 0$.
- **Regular Spiking (RS):** $(a, b, c, d) = (0.02, 0.2, -65, 8)$ — slow *u* → spike-frequency adaptation.
- **Bistable configuration:** $(a, b, c, d) = (0.1, 0.26, -60, 0)$ — large *b*, zero *d* → two coexisting stable rest states.
- **FitzHugh–Nagumo** is the qualitative 2-D cousin: $dv/dt = v - v^{3}/3 - w + I$, $dw/dt = \epsilon(v + \alpha - \beta w)$; its cubic v-nullcline enables relaxation oscillations.
- **Simulation algorithm:** Euler/RK4 integration of the two ODEs with a threshold–reset hybrid update (refer to the Mermaid block in SECTION 4.1).
- **Engineering applications:** Large-scale cortical network simulation, deep brain stimulation, neural-state decoders, central pattern generators.
- **Key thresholds to memorise verbatim:** $v_{\text{reset}} = 30$ mV, $v_{\text{rest}} = -65$ mV, parabola vertex at $v = -62.5$ mV.
- **Key insight:** The Izhikevich model trades biophysical detail (HH's 4-D, 20-parameter structure) for computational efficiency while preserving the *bifurcation catalogue* of cortical neurons — making it the de-facto workhorse of *computational neuroscience* simulation studies.
